topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Backpropagation: The Engine of Deep Learning

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Backpropagation** (short for *backward propagation of errors*) is a supervised learning algorithm used to train artificial neural networks. Formally, it is an efficient method for computing the **gradient of the loss function** with respect to each weight in the network, by applying the **chain rule of calculus** iteratively from the output layer back to the input layer. These gradients are then used by an optimization algorithm (typically **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**) to update the weights and minimize the prediction error.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Backpropagation is the cornerstone algorithm for training multi-layer feedforward neural networks. It dynamically adjusts the synaptic weights by propagating the output error backward through the network layers, enabling the model to learn complex, non-linear input–output mappings.

---

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Blame Game" 🎯
Imagine you are the **head coach of a football team** that just lost a match 0–3. The blame is not equally distributed: the striker missed a goal, but only because the midfielder made a poor pass, which in turn happened because the defender lost possession. To improve, you must figure out *how much* each player contributed to the defeat.

**Backpropagation does exactly this for a neural network:**
- The **final loss (error)** is the lost match.
- The **weights (synapses)** are the players.
- Backpropagation walks **backwards** through the network, calculating the *share of blame* (gradient) for every single weight.
- Weights that contributed more to the error get a larger correction; weights that contributed less get a smaller one.

This is mathematically achieved by the **Chain Rule** of calculus — we "chain" the partial derivatives from the loss all the way to each weight.

---

### Key Terminology at a Glance
| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Forward Pass** | Input flows Input → Hidden → Output, producing a prediction. |
| **Backward Pass** | Error flows Output → Hidden → Input, computing gradients. |
| **Loss Function ($\mathcal{L}$)** | A scalar metric (e.g., MSE, Cross-Entropy) that quantifies prediction error. |
| **Gradient ($\nabla\mathcal{L}$)** | The vector of partial derivatives of $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. every weight. |
| **Learning Rate ($\eta$)** | A hyperparameter (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$) controlling step size. |
| **Epoch** | One complete forward + backward pass over the entire training dataset. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Backpropagation is *not* an optimization algorithm by itself. It is a **gradient computation algorithm**. The actual optimization is performed by gradient descent variants (SGD, Adam, RMSProp), which *use* the gradients produced by backpropagation.

---

## 2. Prerequisites: What Must Be in Place Before Backpropagation Works
1. **A defined network architecture** (layers, activation functions, weight matrices).
2. **A differentiable loss function** $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$.
3. **A differentiable activation function** at every neuron (Sigmoid, ReLU, Tanh, Softmax).
4. **Initial weights** (randomly initialized using He, Xavier, or uniform schemes).
5. **An input–output pair** $(x, y)$ from the training set.

Once these are ready, backpropagation proceeds in **four phases per training sample**:
1. **Forward Propagation** (compute activations).
2. **Loss Computation** (measure error).
3. **Backward Propagation** (compute gradients via the chain rule).
4. **Parameter Update** (adjust weights using gradient descent).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Engine: Chain Rule of Calculus

The entire edifice of backpropagation rests on one elegant identity from multivariable calculus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chain Rule (Single Variable Form):** If $z = f(y)$ and $y = g(x)$, then $\dfrac{dz}{dx} = \dfrac{dz}{dy} \cdot \dfrac{dy}{x}$.

In a neural network, the loss $\mathcal{L}$ is a *composite function* of the weights $w$ through many intermediate variables (layer activations, pre-activations). The chain rule lets us "unroll" this composition layer by layer.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic: The Four-Phase Pipeline

### Phase 1 — Forward Propagation
For each layer $\ell$, compute the **linear combination** (pre-activation) and then the **activation**:

$$z^{(\ell)} = W^{(\ell)} a^{(\ell-1)} + b^{(\ell)}$$

$$a^{(\ell)} = \sigma\!\left(z^{(\ell)}\right)$$

where $\sigma$ is the activation function, $W^{(\ell)}$ is the weight matrix, $b^{(\ell)}$ is the bias vector, and $a^{(0)} = x$ is the input.

### Phase 2 — Loss Computation
For a single training example $(x, y)$ with prediction $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$ at the output layer $L$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\left(y - a^{(L)}\right)^2 \quad \text{(Mean Squared Error)}$$

### Phase 3 — Backward Propagation (The Core)
Starting from the output layer, compute the **error signal** $\delta^{(\ell)}$ at each layer, then the **weight gradients**:

$$\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{a^{(L)}} \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'\!\left(z^{(L)}\right)$$

$$\delta^{(\ell)} = \left(W^{(\ell+1)\top} \delta^{(\ell+1)}\right) \odot \sigma'\!\left(z^{(\ell)}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)} \left(a^{(\ell-1)}\right)^\top$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}$$

Here, $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.

### Phase 4 — Parameter Update (Gradient Descent)
With learning rate $\eta$:

$$W^{(\ell)} \leftarrow W^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(\ell)}}$$

$$b^{(\ell)} \leftarrow b^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(\ell)}}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $z = W a + b$ | Pre-activation (linear step) | Vector / Matrix |
| 2 | $a = \sigma(z)$ | Activation (non-linear step) | Element-wise |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\sum(y - \hat{y})^2$ | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | Scalar |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y \log \hat{y}$ | Categorical Cross-Entropy | Scalar |
| 5 | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1 - \sigma(z)\big)$ | Sigmoid derivative | Element-wise |
| 6 | $\text{ReLU}'(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z \le 0 \end{cases}$ | ReLU derivative | Piecewise |
| 7 | $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$ | Output-layer error signal | Vector |
| 8 | $\delta^{(\ell)} = (W^{(\ell+1)\top}\delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$ | Hidden-layer error signal | Vector |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}(a^{(\ell-1)})^\top$ | Weight gradient | Matrix |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}$ | Bias gradient | Vector |
| 11 | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$ | Gradient descent update | Matrix |
| 12 | $\eta$ | Learning rate (hyperparameter) | $\in [10^{-4},\,10^{-1}]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation:** The Hadamard product $\odot$ is *not* a matrix multiplication. It is element-wise multiplication between two vectors of identical dimension. Misusing it is one of the most common mistakes KTU examiners penalize.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Backpropagation is the algorithm that made modern AI possible. Its utility spans:
- **Computer Vision:** Training CNNs (e.g., ResNet, YOLO) for medical imaging, autonomous vehicles, defect detection.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Powering transformers (BERT, GPT) for translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots.
- **Speech Recognition:** End-to-end ASR systems (Whisper, DeepSpeech).
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy gradient methods that train game-playing agents (AlphaGo, MuZero).
- **Scientific Discovery:** AlphaFold's protein-structure prediction, weather forecasting (GraphCast), materials design.

Without backpropagation, deep networks could not be trained efficiently — and "deep learning" as a discipline would not exist.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example: A 2–2–1 Network

To make the abstract theory concrete, let us train a tiny feedforward network on **one training sample** $(x_1, x_2) = (0.5,\, 1.0)$ with target $y = 0.8$.

### Network Architecture
- **Input layer:** 2 neurons ($x_1, x_2$)
- **Hidden layer:** 2 neurons with sigmoid activation
- **Output layer:** 1 neuron with sigmoid activation
- **Loss:** Mean Squared Error
- **Learning rate:** $\eta = 0.5$

### Given Initial Parameters (Randomly Initialized)
| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $w_{11}^{(1)}$ | 0.15 | $w_{12}^{(1)}$ | 0.20 |
| $w_{21}^{(1)}$ | 0.25 | $w_{22}^{(1)}$ | 0.30 |
| $b_{1}^{(1)}$ | 0.35 | $b_{2}^{(1)}$ | 0.35 |
| $w_{11}^{(2)}$ | 0.40 | $w_{21}^{(2)}$ | 0.45 |
| $b^{(2)}$ | 0.60 |

(For clarity, biases of the hidden neurons are kept equal; this is illustrative, not a constraint.)

---

## 3.2 Phase 1 — Forward Pass (Compute Activations)

### Hidden Layer Pre-Activations
$$z_1^{(1)} = w_{11}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{21}^{(1)} x_2 + b_1^{(1)}$$

$$z_1^{(1)} = (0.15)(0.5) + (0.25)(1.0) + 0.35 = 0.075 + 0.25 + 0.35 = 0.675$$

$$z_2^{(1)} = w_{12}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{22}^{(1)} x_2 + b_2^{(1)}$$

$$z_2^{(1)} = (0.20)(0.5) + (0.30)(1.0) + 0.35 = 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.35 = 0.75$$

### Hidden Layer Activations (Sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$)
$$a_1^{(1)} = \sigma(0.675) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.675}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5092} = 0.6626$$

$$a_2^{(1)} = \sigma(0.75) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.75}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4724} = 0.6792$$

### Output Layer Pre-Activation
$$z^{(2)} = w_{11}^{(2)} a_1^{(1)} + w_{21}^{(2)} a_2^{(1)} + b^{(2)}$$

$$z^{(2)} = (0.40)(0.6626) + (0.45)(0.6792) + 0.60$$

$$z^{(2)} = 0.2650 + 0.3056 + 0.60 = 1.1706$$

### Output Layer Activation
$$\hat{y} = a^{(2)} = \sigma(1.1706) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.1706}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.3102} = 0.7632$$

### Loss Computation
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\big(y - \hat{y}\big)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.8 - 0.7632)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.0368)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.001354) = 0.000677$$

---

## 3.3 Phase 2 — Backward Pass (Compute Gradients)

### Step 2.1 — Output-Layer Error Signal
$$\delta^{(2)} = (\hat{y} - y)\,\sigma'(z^{(2)})$$

where $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1 - \sigma(z)\big) = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$.

$$\sigma'(z^{(2)}) = 0.7632 \times (1 - 0.7632) = 0.7632 \times 0.2368 = 0.1807$$

$$\delta^{(2)} = (0.7632 - 0.8) \times 0.1807 = (-0.0368)(0.1807) = -0.00665$$

### Step 2.2 — Hidden-Layer Error Signals
$$\delta_1^{(1)} = w_{11}^{(2)} \, \delta^{(2)} \, \sigma'(z_1^{(1)})$$

$$\sigma'(z_1^{(1)}) = 0.6626 \times (1 - 0.6626) = 0.6626 \times 0.3374 = 0.2236$$

$$\delta_1^{(1)} = (0.40)(-0.00665)(0.2236) = -0.000595$$

$$\delta_2^{(1)} = w_{21}^{(2)} \, \delta^{(2)} \, \sigma'(z_2^{(1)})$$

$$\sigma'(z_2^{(1)}) = 0.6792 \times (1 - 0.6792) = 0.6792 \times 0.3208 = 0.2179$$

$$\delta_2^{(1)} = (0.45)(-0.00665)(0.2179) = -0.000652$$

### Step 2.3 — Weight Gradients
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{11}^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a_1^{(1)} = (-0.00665)(0.6626) = -0.00441$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{21}^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a_2^{(1)} = (-0.00665)(0.6792) = -0.00452$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = -0.00665$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{11}^{(1)}} = \delta_1^{(1)} \cdot x_1 = (-0.000595)(0.5) = -0.000297$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{21}^{(1)}} = \delta_1^{(1)} \cdot x_2 = (-0.000595)(1.0) = -0.000595$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{12}^{(1)}} = \delta_2^{(1)} \cdot x_1 = (-0.000652)(0.5) = -0.000326$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{22}^{(1)}} = \delta_2^{(1)} \cdot x_2 = (-0.000652)(1.0) = -0.000652$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_1^{(1)}} = \delta_1^{(1)} = -0.000595 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_2^{(1)}} = \delta_2^{(1)} = -0.000652$$

---

## 3.4 Phase 3 — Parameter Update (Gradient Descent, $\eta = 0.5$)

Using $w \leftarrow w - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}$:

| Weight | Old | Gradient | New Weight | Computation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $w_{11}^{(2)}$ | 0.40 | $-0.00441$ | **0.40220** | $0.40 - 0.5(-0.00441)$ |
| $w_{21}^{(2)}$ | 0.45 | $-0.00452$ | **0.45226** | $0.45 - 0.5(-0.00452)$ |
| $b^{(2)}$ | 0.60 | $-0.00665$ | **0.60333** | $0.60 - 0.5(-0.00665)$ |
| $w_{11}^{(1)}$ | 0.15 | $-0.000297$ | **0.15015** | $0.15 - 0.5(-0.000297)$ |
| $w_{21}^{(1)}$ | 0.25 | $-0.000595$ | **0.25030** | $0.25 - 0.5(-0.000595)$ |
| $w_{12}^{(1)}$ | 0.20 | $-0.000326$ | **0.20016** | $0.20 - 0.5(-0.000326)$ |
| $w_{22}^{(1)}$ | 0.30 | $-0.000652$ | **0.30033** | $0.30 - 0.5(-0.000652)$ |
| $b_1^{(1)}$ | 0.35 | $-0.000595$ | **0.35030** | $0.35 - 0.5(-0.000595)$ |
| $b_2^{(1)}$ | 0.35 | $-0.000652$ | **0.35033** | $0.35 - 0.5(-0.000652)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The network's predicted output ($\hat{y} = 0.7632$) was slightly *below* the target ($y = 0.8$), so the error signal $\delta^{(2)}$ is negative. Negative gradient means we *increase* the weights (because of the minus sign in the update rule), nudging the network toward a larger output next iteration. This is exactly the intended behavior.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Why Hidden Layers Are Different From Output

| Aspect | Output Layer | Hidden Layer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direct Access to Loss?** | Yes | No (must propagate error backward) |
| **Error Signal Formula** | $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$ | $\delta^{(\ell)} = (W^{(\ell+1)\top}\delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$ |
| **Chain Rule Length** | Short (one-step composition) | Long (multi-step composition through all later layers) |
| **Vanishing Gradient Risk** | Low | High (especially with sigmoid/tanh) |
| **Mitigation** | N/A | ReLU activation, batch normalization, residual connections |

---

## 3.6 General Vectorized Backpropagation (For Exam Theorems)

For an $L$-layer feedforward network with input $x$, target $y$, and predictions $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$:

$$\text{(a) } \delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{\hat{y}}\mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$$

$$\text{(b) } \delta^{(\ell)} = \left(W^{(\ell+1)\top}\delta^{(\ell+1)}\right) \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)}) \quad \text{for } \ell = L-1, L-2, \dots, 1$$

$$\text{(c) } \nabla_{W^{(\ell)}}\mathcal{L} = \delta^{(\ell)}\left(a^{(\ell-1)}\right)^\top$$

$$\text{(d) } \nabla_{b^{(\ell)}}\mathcal{L} = \delta^{(\ell)}$$

$$\text{(e) } W^{(\ell)} \leftarrow W^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \nabla_{W^{(\ell)}}\mathcal{L}$$

This compact five-equation system is the *complete mathematical specification* of backpropagation, valid for any feedforward architecture.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Backpropagation Cycle

The following diagram captures the **complete training cycle** — from raw input to weight update — as a functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input X and Target Y] --> B[Initialize Weights W and Biases b]
    B --> C[Forward Pass: Compute z and a layer by layer]
    C --> D[Compute Prediction y hat at Output Layer]
    D --> E[Compute Loss L using L equals half y minus y hat squared]
    E --> F{Is Loss Converged?}
    F -- No --> G[Backward Pass: Compute delta at Output Layer]
    G --> H[Propagate Error Backward: Compute delta at Hidden Layers]
    H --> I[Compute Gradients: Partial L over Partial W and Partial L over Partial b]
    I --> J[Update Weights: W new equals W minus eta times gradient]
    J --> K[Update Biases: b new equals b minus eta times gradient]
    K --> C
    F -- Yes --> L[Training Complete: Final Weights Ready]
```

---

## 4.2 Computational Graph for a Single Neuron

The following diagram shows the **forward flow** of values and the **backward flow** of gradients through one neuron, illustrating how the chain rule is applied in practice.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[X1] --> N1[Weighted Sum: w1 times X1 plus w2 times X2 plus b]
    X2[X2] --> N1
    N1 --> Z[Pre-activation: z]
    Z --> S[Activation: sigma of z equals a]
    S --> L[Loss: L equals half y minus a squared]
    L -.->|Backward: dL over da| S
    S -.->|Backward: dL over dz equals dL over da times sigma prime z| Z
    Z -.->|Backward: dL over dw1 equals dL over dz times X1| N1
    N1 -.->|Backward: dL over dw2 equals dL over dz times X2| N1
    N1 -.->|Backward: dL over db equals dL over dz| N1
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a 2-2-1 Backpropagation Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[INPUT LAYER]
        I1[Input Neuron X1]:::input
        I2[Input Neuron X2]:::input
    end
    subgraph H[HIDDEN LAYER: 2 Neurons Sigmoid]
        H1[Hidden Neuron 1: sigma of z1]:::hidden
        H2[Hidden Neuron 2: sigma of z2]:::hidden
    end
    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT LAYER: 1 Neuron Sigmoid]
        O1[Output Neuron: y hat equals sigma of z]:::output
    end
    subgraph LOSS[LOSS BLOCK]
        L1[Loss: L equals half y minus y hat squared]:::loss
    end
    I1 -->|w11| H1
    I2 -->|w21| H1
    I1 -->|w12| H2
    I2 -->|w22| H2
    H1 -->|w11 layer2| O1
    H2 -->|w21 layer2| O1
    O1 --> L1
    L1 -.->|delta2| O1
    O1 -.->|delta1 and delta2| H1
    O1 -.->|delta1 and delta2| H2

    classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1
    classDef hidden fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c
    classDef output fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#4a148c
    classDef loss fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#7f0000
```

---

## 4.4 Gradient Flow Through Layers (Directionality Map)

```mermaid
flowchart RL
    L[Loss L] -->|Backward: dL over dyhat| O[Output: y hat]
    O -->|Backward: delta2| W2[Weights W layer 2]
    W2 -->|Backward: delta1 equals W2 transpose times delta2| H[Hidden: a1 and a2]
    H -->|Backward| W1[Weights W layer 1]
    W1 -->|Backward: dL over dW1| X[Input: X1 and X2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Solid arrows represent **forward propagation** of activations. Dashed arrows represent **backward propagation** of gradients. The numerical example in Section 3 can be directly traced through this architecture.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] 
**Define the term "Backpropagation" in the context of training neural networks. State the role of the chain rule in this algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Backpropagation is a supervised learning algorithm used to train multi-layer neural networks by efficiently computing the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight in the network. **(1 mark)**

The algorithm operates in two phases: a forward pass where activations are computed, and a backward pass where the error is propagated from the output layer to the input layer. **(1 mark)**

The **chain rule of calculus** is the mathematical engine that enables backpropagation. It allows the gradient of the loss with respect to any weight (no matter how deep in the network) to be expressed as a product of local gradients at each layer. This recursive composition is what makes gradient computation tractable for deep networks. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary] 
**Differentiate between Forward Propagation and Backward Propagation in a neural network.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Propagation | Backward Propagation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction** | Input $\rightarrow$ Output | Output $\rightarrow$ Input |
| **Purpose** | Compute predictions and loss | Compute gradients of loss w.r.t. weights |
| **Information Flow** | Activations ($a^{(\ell)}$) | Error signals ($\delta^{(\ell)}$) |
| **Mathematical Tool** | Weighted sum + activation | Chain rule of differentiation |
| **Triggered By** | Input data | Loss value at the output |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each clear comparison. Full 3 marks for at least 3 correct distinguishing points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** (theory/conceptual) and **(b) 7 marks** (numerical/derivation). Internal choice is provided — both alternatives must be attempted-ready.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.A (a)** Explain the four phases of the backpropagation algorithm in detail. State the mathematical formulation for computing the error signal $\delta^{(\ell)}$ at any layer $\ell$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Forward Propagation:** Given an input $x$, the network computes layer-wise pre-activations $z^{(\ell)} = W^{(\ell)}a^{(\ell-1)} + b^{(\ell)}$ and activations $a^{(\ell)} = \sigma(z^{(\ell)})$ until the output $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$ is obtained. **(1.5 marks)**

**Phase 2 — Loss Computation:** A scalar loss $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ is computed, e.g., $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$ for regression. **(0.5 marks)**

**Phase 3 — Backward Propagation:** Starting from the output layer, the error signal is computed. For the output layer, $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$. For any hidden layer, $\delta^{(\ell)} = (W^{(\ell+1)\top}\delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$. The weight gradient is $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}(a^{(\ell-1)})^\top$ and the bias gradient is $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}$. **(3 marks)**

**Phase 4 — Parameter Update:** The weights and biases are updated using gradient descent: $W^{(\ell)} \leftarrow W^{(\ell)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(\ell)}}$ and $b^{(\ell)} \leftarrow b^{(\ell)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(\ell)}}$, where $\eta$ is the learning rate. **(1 mark)**

**Mathematical Justification of $\delta^{(\ell)}$:** The chain rule gives $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(\ell)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(\ell+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial z^{(\ell)}}$. Since $z^{(\ell+1)} = W^{(\ell+1)}a^{(\ell)} + b^{(\ell+1)} = W^{(\ell+1)}\sigma(z^{(\ell)}) + b^{(\ell+1)}$, we get $\frac{\partial z^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial z^{(\ell)}} = W^{(\ell+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$, yielding the recursive formula. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q.A (b)** Consider a neural network with 2 input neurons, 2 neurons in the hidden layer (sigmoid activation), and 1 output neuron (sigmoid activation). Given input $(0.5, 1.0)$, target $y = 0.8$, learning rate $\eta = 0.5$, and the initial weights and biases from the table in Section 3.1 above, compute the updated values of all weights and biases after **one iteration of backpropagation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Reference: Section 3 of these notes):**

**Step 1 — Forward Pass (1.5 marks):**
- $z_1^{(1)} = 0.675$, $a_1^{(1)} = \sigma(0.675) = 0.6626$
- $z_2^{(1)} = 0.75$, $a_2^{(1)} = \sigma(0.75) = 0.6792$
- $z^{(2)} = 1.1706$, $\hat{y} = \sigma(1.1706) = 0.7632$
- $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(0.8 - 0.7632)^2 = 0.000677$

**Step 2 — Output Error Signal (1 mark):**
- $\sigma'(z^{(2)}) = 0.7632 \times 0.2368 = 0.1807$
- $\delta^{(2)} = (0.7632 - 0.8)(0.1807) = -0.00665$

**Step 3 — Hidden Error Signals (1.5 marks):**
- $\sigma'(z_1^{(1)}) = 0.6626 \times 0.3374 = 0.2236$ ; $\delta_1^{(1)} = (0.40)(-0.00665)(0.2236) = -0.000595$
- $\sigma'(z_2^{(1)}) = 0.6792 \times 0.3208 = 0.2179$ ; $\delta_2^{(1)} = (0.45)(-0.00665)(0.2179) = -0.000652$

**Step 4 — Weight Gradients (1.5 marks):**
- $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w_{11}^{(2)} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a_1^{(1)} = -0.00441$
- $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w_{21}^{(2)} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a_2^{(1)} = -0.00452$
- $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w_{11}^{(1)} = \delta_1^{(1)} \cdot x_1 = -0.000297$ ; $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w_{21}^{(1)} = \delta_1^{(1)} \cdot x_2 = -0.000595$
- $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w_{12}^{(1)} = \delta_2^{(1)} \cdot x_1 = -0.000326$ ; $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w_{22}^{(1)} = \delta_2^{(1)} \cdot x_2 = -0.000652$

**Step 5 — Parameter Updates (1.5 marks):**
| Weight | Updated Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| $w_{11}^{(2)}$ | $0.40 - 0.5(-0.00441) = 0.40220$ |
| $w_{21}^{(2)}$ | $0.45 - 0.5(-0.00452) = 0.45226$ |
| $b^{(2)}$ | $0.60 - 0.5(-0.00665) = 0.60333$ |
| $w_{11}^{(1)}$ | $0.15 - 0.5(-0.000297) = 0.15015$ |
| $w_{21}^{(1)}$ | $0.25 - 0.5(-0.000595) = 0.25030$ |
| $w_{12}^{(1)}$ | $0.20 - 0.5(-0.000326) = 0.20016$ |
| $w_{22}^{(1)}$ | $0.30 - 0.5(-0.000652) = 0.30033$ |
| $b_1^{(1)}$ | $0.35 - 0.5(-0.000595) = 0.35030$ |
| $b_2^{(1)}$ | $0.35 - 0.5(-0.000652) = 0.35033$ |

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** Derive the backpropagation update rule for the weight $W^{(\ell)}$ of an $L$-layer feedforward neural network using the chain rule. Clearly state the meaning of the error term $\delta^{(\ell)}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We aim to minimize $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ where $\hat{y} = a^{(L)} = \sigma(z^{(L)})$ and $z^{(\ell)} = W^{(\ell)} a^{(\ell-1)} + b^{(\ell)}$.

**Step 1 — Define the error signal:** Let $\delta^{(\ell)} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(\ell)}}$. This is the rate of change of the loss with respect to the pre-activation at layer $\ell$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Output layer formula:** By the chain rule, $\delta^{(L)} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \nabla_{a^{(L)}}\mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$. For MSE loss, $\nabla_{a^{(L)}}\mathcal{L} = (\hat{y} - y)$, so $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Recursive formula for hidden layers:** Since $z^{(\ell+1)} = W^{(\ell+1)} a^{(\ell)} + b^{(\ell+1)} = W^{(\ell+1)} \sigma(z^{(\ell)}) + b^{(\ell+1)}$, we apply the chain rule:
$$\delta^{(\ell)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(\ell+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial z^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell+1)} \cdot W^{(\ell+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$$
Equivalently, $\delta^{(\ell)} = (W^{(\ell+1)\top} \delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Weight gradient:** Since $z^{(\ell)} = W^{(\ell)} a^{(\ell-1)} + b^{(\ell)}$:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(\ell)}}{\partial W^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)} (a^{(\ell-1)})^\top$$
Similarly, $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Update rule:** Apply gradient descent with learning rate $\eta$:
$$W^{(\ell)} \leftarrow W^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \delta^{(\ell)} (a^{(\ell-1)})^\top$$
$$b^{(\ell)} \leftarrow b^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \delta^{(\ell)}$$
This completes the derivation. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **Vanishing Gradient Problem** in the context of backpropagation. Discuss how the choice of activation function and network initialization strategy can mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Vanishing Gradient Problem occurs when gradients $\delta^{(\ell)}$ shrink exponentially as they are propagated from the output layer back to earlier layers. Since each layer's gradient is multiplied by $\sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$, and for sigmoid $\sigma'(z) \le 0.25$, the product across many layers becomes vanishingly small. As a result, weights in early layers barely update, and the network fails to learn meaningful low-level features.

**Mathematical Illustration (1.5 marks):** For a deep network with $L$ layers:
$$\delta^{(1)} = \left(\prod_{\ell=1}^{L-1} W^{(\ell+1)} \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})\right) \delta^{(L)}$$
If each factor is less than 1 in magnitude, the product decays exponentially with depth.

**Mitigation via Activation Functions (2 marks):**
- **ReLU** $\sigma(z) = \max(0, z)$ has derivative $1$ for $z > 0$, eliminating the multiplicative shrinkage.
- **Leaky ReLU** $f(z) = \max(0.01z, z)$ preserves a small gradient even for negative inputs.
- **ELU, Swish, GELU** offer smooth, non-saturating alternatives.

**Mitigation via Initialization (1 mark):**
- **Xavier (Glorot) Initialization:** $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}})$ — keeps variance stable across layers for sigmoid/tanh.
- **He Initialization:** $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \frac{2}{n_{in}})$ — designed for ReLU networks.

**Other Mitigations (0.5 marks):** Batch Normalization, Residual Connections (ResNets), and gradient clipping also help.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\sigma(z)$ with $\sigma'(z)$:** Many students use the *activation value* $\sigma(z)$ in the backward pass instead of the *derivative* $\sigma'(z)$. The sigmoid derivative is $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — never just $\sigma(z)$.
>
> 2. **Missing the Hadamard product:** The error signal $\delta^{(\ell)}$ requires *element-wise* multiplication ($\odot$), not matrix multiplication. Writing $\delta^{(\ell)} = W^{(\ell+1)\top} \delta^{(\ell+1)} \cdot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$ without the $\odot$ symbol loses at least 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the transpose:** The hidden-layer error formula requires $W^{(\ell+1)\top}$ (the *transpose* of the next layer's weights), not $W^{(\ell+1)}$ itself. Dimension mismatch errors are immediate red flags.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in gradient descent:** The update rule is $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_w \mathcal{L}$. A common slip is to write $w \leftarrow w + \eta \nabla_w \mathcal{L}$, which causes the loss to *diverge* instead of converge.
>
> 5. **Skipping the chain-rule explanation:** In derivations, you must *show* the chain rule explicitly, not just state the final formula. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the chain-rule step.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the learning rate:** When describing gradient descent, $\eta$ must be explicitly written and its typical range ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$) should be stated.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the bias:** Many students update only the weights $W$ and ignore the bias $b$. The bias has its own gradient $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial b^{(\ell)} = \delta^{(\ell)}$ and must be updated separately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **Backpropagation = Chain Rule + Dynamic Programming.** It efficiently computes gradients by reusing intermediate values from the forward pass.
- ✅ **It is NOT an optimizer.** Backpropagation only *computes* gradients; gradient descent (or Adam, RMSProp) *uses* them to update weights.
- ✅ **Two passes per training example:** Forward (compute activations) → Backward (compute gradients).
- ✅ **The four master equations** to memorize:
  1. $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$
  2. $\delta^{(\ell)} = (W^{(\ell+1)\top} \delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})$
  3. $\nabla_{W^{(\ell)}} \mathcal{L} = \delta^{(\ell)} (a^{(\ell-1)})^\top$
  4. $W^{(\ell)} \leftarrow W^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \nabla_{W^{(\ell)}} \mathcal{L}$
- ✅ **Sigmoid derivative is the most tested formula:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$. Derive it once and memorize it permanently.
- ✅ **The Hadamard product $\odot$ is element-wise**, not a dot product or matrix product.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradients** plague sigmoid/tanh networks; **exploding gradients** plague RNNs. Both are solved by careful activation choice and gradient clipping.
- ✅ **Learning rate $\eta$** is the single most important hyperparameter: too high → divergence; too low → slow convergence.
- ✅ **Mini-batch Gradient Descent** is the practical default: average gradients over $B$ examples per update to reduce noise.
- ✅ **Momentum, Adam, RMSProp** are advanced optimizers that *still rely* on backpropagation for their gradient input.
- ✅ **Xavier & He initialization** keep gradient magnitudes balanced across layers.
- ✅ **Numerical example mastery:** Practice the 2-2-1 worked example until you can reproduce all 9 weight updates from memory — this is the single highest-yield activity for KTU numerical questions.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Approximately 60–70% of Module 1 marks in the End Semester Exam (ESE) come from a single numerical problem requiring forward + backward pass on a small (2-2-1 or 2-3-1) network with sigmoid activation. Master Section 3.1–3.4 of these notes, and you will secure the bulk of your marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Layer Perceptron Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multi-Layer Perceptron Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Multi-Layer Perceptron Model

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** is a class of **feedforward artificial neural network** that consists of **at least three layers** of fully connected nodes (neurons): an **input layer**, **one or more hidden layers**, and an **output layer**. Except for the input nodes, every node is a neuron that applies a **non-linear activation function** to a weighted sum of its inputs, enabling the network to learn complex, non-linear mappings between inputs and outputs.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Diagnostic System"

Imagine a hospital that diagnoses diseases from patient reports:

- **Input Layer (Reception Desk):** Raw patient data is collected — age, blood pressure, sugar level, temperature. These are simply *passed forward*; no processing occurs.
- **Hidden Layer 1 (Junior Doctors):** They look at combinations of symptoms. For instance, "high BP + high sugar" together might mean something. They form **intermediate clinical patterns**.
- **Hidden Layer 2 (Senior Specialists):** They take the patterns from junior doctors and combine them into higher-order medical reasoning, such as "metabolic syndrome with cardiac risk."
- **Output Layer (Final Diagnosis):** A single diagnosis is produced — *Diabetic, Healthy, or Critical*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Each layer transforms the *representation* of the data into something more abstract and useful for the final decision. This **hierarchical feature learning** is the central idea of deep learning.

### 1.2 Structural Components of an MLP

| Component | Role | Activation? |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Layer** | Receives raw feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | **No** (linear pass-through) |
| **Hidden Layer(s)** | Learn non-linear intermediate representations | **Yes** (e.g., ReLU, sigmoid) |
| **Output Layer** | Produces prediction $\hat{y}$ | Depends on task (softmax/sigmoid/linear) |
| **Weights $W^{(l)}$** | Trainable parameters scaling inputs at layer $l$ | — |
| **Biases $b^{(l)}$** | Trainable offsets that shift activation boundaries | — |

**Standard KTU Notation:** An MLP with architecture $n - h_1 - h_2 - \cdots - h_k - m$ has $n$ input neurons, $k$ hidden layers of sizes $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k$, and $m$ output neurons.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Exam Hook:** "Why is the perceptron called *multi-layer*?" — Because it overcomes the limitation of the single-layer perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1958) which could only solve **linearly separable** problems (e.g., XOR could NOT be solved by a single-layer perceptron).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Forward Propagation

The MLP transforms input to output through a sequence of **affine transformations** followed by **non-linear activations**. For a network with $L$ layers indexed $l = 0, 1, \ldots, L$:

**Step 1 — Linear Combination at layer $l$:** Each neuron computes a weighted sum of activations from the previous layer plus a bias term.

**Step 2 — Non-linear Activation:** The linear sum is passed through a differentiable, non-linear function $f$ to introduce representational power.

**Step 3 — Cascading:** The output of layer $l$ becomes the input of layer $l+1$, forming a compositional function.

### 2.2 Why Hidden Layers Are Mathematically Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single-layer perceptron can only represent a **linear decision boundary** of the form $w^T x + b = 0$. The **Universal Approximation Theorem** (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik, 1991) states that an MLP with **even a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons** and a **non-linear activation** can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision. **Multiple hidden layers** enable this approximation more efficiently (with exponentially fewer neurons) and learn hierarchical features.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Common Form |
|---|---|---|
| $a^{(0)} = x$ | Input vector fed to network | $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ | Pre-activation at layer $l$ | $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_l \times h_{l-1}}$ |
| $a^{(l)} = f^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$ | Post-activation at layer $l$ | $a^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_l}$ |
| $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$ | Final output of the network | — |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output in $(0,1)$ |
| Tanh | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | Output in $(-1,1)$ |
| ReLU | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Output in $[0, \infty)$ |
| Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class output |
| MSE Loss | $L = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression |
| Cross-Entropy | $L = -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification |
| Universal Approx. | $\vert f(x) - \hat{f}(x) \vert < \epsilon$ | $\forall x \in K \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MLPs are the **backbone of deep learning** and form the foundational building block of modern architectures like CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers. They are deployed universally in **medical diagnosis, fraud detection, financial forecasting, NLP, computer vision, and reinforcement learning**.

### 2.4 Key Hyperparameters (Bolded for KTU Importance)

- **Learning rate $\eta$**: Controls step size of weight updates — typically $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$.
- **Batch size $B$**: Number of samples per gradient update.
- **Number of epochs $E$**: Full passes over the training set.
- **Network depth $L$**: Total number of layers (excluding input).
- **Network width $h_l$**: Number of neurons in layer $l$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Pass Through a 2-Hidden-Layer MLP

Consider an MLP with the architecture **Input(2) → Hidden1(3) → Hidden2(2) → Output(1)**. Let us compute the forward pass for a single input sample $x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 1 — Initialize activations with the input vector:**

$$
a^{(0)} = x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Reasoning:** By convention, the input layer is "layer 0" and is treated as the activation of the first computational layer.

**Step 2 — Compute the pre-activation of Hidden Layer 1:**

The pre-activation vector $z^{(1)}$ is the affine transformation of $a^{(0)}$.

$$
z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} a^{(0)} + b^{(1)}
$$

Here, $W^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$ and $b^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$.

Writing out the matrix multiplication explicitly:

$$
z^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(1)} & w_{12}^{(1)} \\ w_{21}^{(1)} & w_{22}^{(1)} \\ w_{31}^{(1)} & w_{32}^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1^{(1)} \\ b_2^{(1)} \\ b_3^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{12}^{(1)} x_2 + b_1^{(1)} \\ w_{21}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{22}^{(1)} x_2 + b_2^{(1)} \\ w_{31}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{32}^{(1)} x_2 + b_3^{(1)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply activation function to Hidden Layer 1:**

Using ReLU $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ for hidden layers:

$$
a^{(1)} = \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0, z_1^{(1)}) \\ \max(0, z_2^{(1)}) \\ \max(0, z_3^{(1)}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Reasoning:** ReLU introduces non-linearity; without it, the entire network collapses to a single linear transformation regardless of depth.

**Step 4 — Compute the pre-activation of Hidden Layer 2:**

Here $W^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 3}$ and $b^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 1}$.

$$
z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)}
$$

Expanding:

$$
z^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(2)} & w_{12}^{(2)} & w_{13}^{(2)} \\ w_{21}^{(2)} & w_{22}^{(2)} & w_{23}^{(2)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1^{(1)} \\ a_2^{(1)} \\ a_3^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1^{(2)} \\ b_2^{(2)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply activation function to Hidden Layer 2:**

$$
a^{(2)} = \text{ReLU}(z^{(2)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0, z_1^{(2)}) \\ \max(0, z_2^{(2)}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the output layer (regression task, linear activation):**

$$
z^{(3)} = W^{(3)} a^{(2)} + b^{(3)}
$$

With $W^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 2}$ and $b^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$
z^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(3)} & w_{12}^{(3)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1^{(2)} \\ a_2^{(2)} \end{bmatrix} + b^{(3)} = w_{11}^{(3)} a_1^{(2)} + w_{12}^{(3)} a_2^{(2)} + b^{(3)}
$$

$$
\hat{y} = a^{(3)} = z^{(3)}
$$

**Step 7 — Total parameter count:**

$$
\text{Params} = (2 \times 3 + 3) + (3 \times 2 + 2) + (2 \times 1 + 1) = 9 + 8 + 3 = 20
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization:** The forward pass for a network with $L$ layers can be compactly written as the **nested function**:
> $$\hat{y} = f^{(L)}\!\big(W^{(L)} f^{(L-1)}(\cdots f^{(1)}(W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}) \cdots ) + b^{(L)}\big)$$
> This composition is what gives MLPs their expressive power.

### 3.2 Loss Computation (Mean Squared Error for a Single Sample)

$$
L = (y - \hat{y})^2
$$

For a batch of $N$ samples:

$$
L = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

The training objective is:

$$
\min_{W, b} \; L(W, b)
$$

which is solved by **backpropagation + gradient descent** (covered in detail in Module 2).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MLP Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1((x1))
    in2((x2))
    in3((x3))

    h1((h1_1))
    h2((h1_2))
    h3((h1_3))
    h4((h1_4))

    k1((h2_1))
    k2((h2_2))
    k3((h2_3))

    out1((y1))
    out2((y2))

    in1 --> h1
    in1 --> h2
    in1 --> h3
    in1 --> h4
    in2 --> h1
    in2 --> h2
    in2 --> h3
    in2 --> h4
    in3 --> h1
    in3 --> h2
    in3 --> h3
    in3 --> h4

    h1 --> k1
    h1 --> k2
    h1 --> k3
    h2 --> k1
    h2 --> k2
    h2 --> k3
    h3 --> k1
    h3 --> k2
    h3 --> k3
    h4 --> k1
    h4 --> k2
    h4 --> k3

    k1 --> out1
    k1 --> out2
    k2 --> out1
    k2 --> out2
    k3 --> out1
    k3 --> out2
```

> **Figure 1.** A fully-connected MLP with 3 input neurons, two hidden layers (4 and 3 neurons), and 2 output neurons. Every neuron in layer $l$ is connected to every neuron in layer $l+1$ — this is the definition of a **dense / fully-connected** layer.

### 4.2 Forward Pass Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Vector x] --> B[Linear Step: W1 x + b1]
    B --> C[Activation f1 ReLU]
    C --> D[Linear Step: W2 a1 + b2]
    D --> E[Activation f2 ReLU]
    E --> F[Linear Step: W3 a2 + b3]
    F --> G[Output Activation: Softmax or Linear]
    G --> H[Predicted Output y_hat]
    H --> I[Loss Computation: L y, y_hat]
    I --> J[Backpropagation: Compute Gradients]
    J --> K[Update Weights via Gradient Descent]
```

> **Figure 2.** Functional flow of a single training iteration in an MLP, showing the alternation of **linear (affine)** and **non-linear (activation)** transformations, followed by loss evaluation and weight updates.

### 4.3 Layer-wise Information Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Data: Pixels, Tokens, Signals] --> low[Layer 1: Low-Level Features: Edges, Phonemes]
    low --> mid[Layer 2: Mid-Level Features: Shapes, Words]
    mid --> high[Layer 3: High-Level Features: Objects, Semantics]
    high --> pred[Prediction: Class Label, Value]
```

> **Figure 3.** Hierarchical feature learning in deep MLPs — each successive layer captures more abstract and task-relevant features. This is the **central reason** depth matters in deep learning.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define a Multi-Layer Perceptron. Why is it called "multi-layer"?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) is a **feedforward neural network** consisting of an **input layer**, one or more **hidden layers**, and an **output layer**, where each neuron (except input) applies a **non-linear activation** to a weighted sum of its inputs.
> It is called "multi-layer" because, unlike the **single-layer perceptron** of Rosenblatt (1958) which had only input and output layers and could only learn **linearly separable** functions, the MLP adds **hidden layers** between input and output, enabling it to learn **non-linear** decision boundaries (e.g., solving the **XOR problem**).
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for hidden layer role, 1 mark for non-linear separability.)*

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** List any three activation functions used in MLPs and state one advantage of ReLU over sigmoid.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Three activation functions:
> 1. **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ — squashes output to $(0, 1)$, used for binary output.
> 2. **Tanh:** $\tanh(z)$ — squashes output to $(-1, 1)$, zero-centered.
> 3. **ReLU:** $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ — outputs positive linear values, computationally simple.
>
> **Advantage of ReLU over sigmoid:** ReLU mitigates the **vanishing gradient problem** because its gradient is exactly **1** for positive inputs, whereas sigmoid's gradient becomes extremely small (≈ 0) for large positive or negative inputs, slowing down learning in deep networks.
> *(Award 1 mark each for three activations, 1 mark for vanishing gradient reasoning.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Paper]`** (14 Marks)
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Multi-Layer Perceptron. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the mathematical expressions for forward propagation in a 2-hidden-layer MLP using ReLU activation in the hidden layers and sigmoid at the output. **(7 marks)**

**OR**

> **Q3B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** (14 Marks)
> **(a)** State and explain the **Universal Approximation Theorem**. What is its significance in deep learning? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare sigmoid, tanh, and ReLU activation functions across at least four criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q3A

**(a) MLP Architecture (7 marks):**

An MLP is an **organized arrangement of neurons in layers**, where signals flow strictly in one direction — from **input → hidden → output** — with **no cycles or feedback loops** (hence *feedforward*).

**Architecture Description (3 marks):**

- **Input Layer:** Holds the raw feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. No computation; it merely distributes inputs.
- **Hidden Layer(s):** Each neuron computes $z = W x + b$ and then applies a non-linear activation $f$. Hidden layers transform the input into a new **feature space** where the problem becomes easier to solve.
- **Output Layer:** Produces the final prediction. For **binary classification**, a single sigmoid neuron is used; for **multi-class**, a softmax layer of $C$ neurons; for **regression**, a linear output neuron.
- **Connections:** Every neuron in layer $l$ is connected to **every** neuron in layer $l+1$ — these are called **dense** or **fully-connected** layers.
- **Trainable parameters:** Weight matrices $W^{(l)}$ and bias vectors $b^{(l)}$ for every layer.

**Neat Diagram (2 marks):**

```
  Input         Hidden Layer 1     Hidden Layer 2     Output
   x1 ----+------> h1 ------+--------> k1 -----+----> y1
          |    +--> h2 ----+ |   +----> k2 ---+ |
   x2 ----+--->  h3 ----+ | |   |   +-> (etc) | |
          |   |          | | |   |   |        | |
   x3 ----+---+--------->+ | |   |   |        | |
                          v v v   v   v        v v
```

(Refer Figure 1 in Section 4 for the Mermaid equivalent.)

**Key Properties (2 marks):**

- **Feedforward:** No loops; computation flows strictly forward.
- **Fully connected:** Dense weight matrices between consecutive layers.
- **Non-linear:** Non-linear activations enable learning of complex patterns.
- **Depth = representational power:** More hidden layers → more abstract features.

---

**(b) Forward Propagation Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the MLP have input dimension $n$, hidden layer 1 of size $h_1$, hidden layer 2 of size $h_2$, and output dimension $m$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Initialize the network input:

$$
a^{(0)} = x
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Hidden Layer 1 — pre-activation and ReLU activation:

$$
z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} a^{(0)} + b^{(1)} = W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}
$$

$$
a^{(1)} = f^{(1)}(z^{(1)}) = \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)}) = \max(0, z^{(1)})
$$

where $W^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_1 \times n}$ and $b^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_1}$.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Hidden Layer 2 — pre-activation and ReLU activation:

$$
z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)}
$$

$$
a^{(2)} = f^{(2)}(z^{(2)}) = \text{ReLU}(z^{(2)}) = \max(0, z^{(2)})
$$

where $W^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_2 \times h_1}$ and $b^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_2}$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Output layer — pre-activation and sigmoid activation:

$$
z^{(3)} = W^{(3)} a^{(2)} + b^{(3)}
$$

$$
\hat{y} = a^{(3)} = \sigma(z^{(3)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(3)}}}
$$

where $W^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times h_2}$ and $b^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For a single-output binary classifier ($m=1$), $\hat{y} \in (0, 1)$ represents the predicted probability of class 1, and the predicted class is $\hat{y}_{\text{class}} = 1$ if $\hat{y} > 0.5$, else $0$.

**Step 6 (0.5 marks):** Compact nested-function form:

$$
\hat{y} = \sigma\!\left(W^{(3)} \cdot \text{ReLU}\!\left(W^{(2)} \cdot \text{ReLU}\!\left(W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}\right) + b^{(2)}\right) + b^{(3)}\right)
$$

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q3B

**(a) Universal Approximation Theorem (7 marks):**

**Statement (3 marks):** The Universal Approximation Theorem states that a **feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons** can approximate **any continuous function** $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ defined on a **compact subset** $K \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to **arbitrary precision** $\epsilon > 0$, provided the activation function is **non-constant, bounded, and monotonically increasing** (e.g., sigmoid).

Formally, for any such $f$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, there exist parameters $W, b$ such that:

$$
\sup_{x \in K} \vert f(x) - \hat{f}(x) \vert < \epsilon
$$

**Key Intuition (2 marks):** This theorem guarantees that MLPs are *theoretically* **universal function approximators** — no matter how complex the underlying mapping (image recognition, language translation, control), there exists *some* MLP that can approximate it to any desired accuracy.

**Significance in Deep Learning (2 marks):**
1. **Theoretical Foundation:** It provides the mathematical justification for using neural networks to model any real-world function.
2. **Limitation:** The theorem guarantees *existence* but not *learnability* — finding the right weights via training remains the practical challenge.
3. **Motivation for Depth:** While one hidden layer is enough in theory, **deeper networks** approximate complex functions with **exponentially fewer neurons**, making depth practically essential.

---

**(b) Comparison of Activation Functions (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Form** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\tanh(z)$ | $\max(0, z)$ |
| **Output Range** | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| **Zero-Centered** | No | **Yes** | No |
| **Vanishing Gradient** | **Severe** (saturates both sides) | Moderate (saturates both sides) | **None for $z > 0$** |
| **Computational Cost** | High (exponential) | High (exponential) | **Low** (just thresholding) |
| **Derivative** | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | $0$ if $z<0$, else $1$ |
| **Dying Neuron Issue** | No | No | **Yes** (neurons stuck at 0) |

**Best Practice Recommendation (1 mark):** ReLU is the **default choice** for hidden layers in modern deep networks due to its computational efficiency and gradient flow. Sigmoid/softmax is reserved for the **output layer** in classification tasks; pure linear activation is used for **regression** outputs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "multi-layer" with "deep":** A 3-layer MLP is *not* deep by modern standards; "deep" typically implies ≥ 5–10 layers. KTU expects this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting biases in derivation:** Always include $b^{(l)}$ in the affine step. Many students omit it, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong activation at output:** ReLU at the output of a **classification** task is *wrong* — use sigmoid (binary) or softmax (multi-class).
> 4. **Stating "more layers = always better":** Deeper networks suffer from vanishing gradients, overfitting, and higher computational cost. Always mention trade-offs.
> 5. **Mixing up forward and backward pass:** The forward pass computes predictions; **backpropagation** (a separate topic in Module 2) computes gradients. KTU examiners deduct marks for this confusion.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MLP** = feedforward, fully-connected neural network with $\geq 1$ hidden layer and **non-linear activations**.
- **Core computation** at each layer: $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$, then $a^{(l)} = f^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$.
- **Architectural parameters:** depth (number of layers), width (neurons per layer), activation choice.
- **XOR problem** is the classical motivation for adding hidden layers — single-layer perceptron cannot solve it.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** even 1 hidden layer is enough in theory, but **depth helps in practice** (exponentially fewer neurons).
- **Activation function rules of thumb:**
  - Hidden layers → **ReLU** (default), sometimes tanh.
  - Binary output → **sigmoid**.
  - Multi-class output → **softmax**.
  - Regression output → **linear (identity)**.
- **Loss function rules of thumb:**
  - Regression → **MSE**.
  - Classification → **Cross-Entropy**.
- **Forward pass** is the prediction step; **backward pass** (backpropagation) updates weights using gradients.
- **Trainable parameters** = all weights $W^{(l)}$ and biases $b^{(l)}$ across all layers.
- **Fully-connected** means every neuron in layer $l$ connects to every neuron in layer $l+1$.
- **Total parameter count formula:** $\sum_{l=1}^{L} (h_{l-1} \cdot h_l + h_l)$, where $h_0 = n$ (input dimension) and $h_L = m$ (output dimension).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activation Functions and Loss Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Activation Functions and Loss Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS & LOSS FUNCTIONS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Activation Functions** are non-linear mathematical transformations applied to the weighted sum of inputs (the pre-activation or "logit") of a neuron in a neural network. They introduce non-linearity into the model, enabling the network to learn complex, hierarchical patterns. Without them, a deep network collapses mathematically into a single linear layer.
>
> **Loss Functions** (also called Cost Functions or Objective Functions) are scalar-valued mathematical functions that quantify the discrepancy between the network's predicted output ($\hat{y}$) and the actual ground-truth label ($y$). They form the foundation of the gradient-based learning process, since their gradient with respect to model parameters drives the weight update rule.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Coffee Machine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Coffee Machine):**
> Imagine a coffee machine that receives raw inputs (water pressure, bean quantity, grind size). The **activation function** is like the *thermostat switch* inside the machine: it decides whether the heating element should fire at full power, stay off, or run at a partial setting based on a threshold. Just as a thermostat introduces a *decision boundary* (ON/OFF), an activation function introduces a *non-linear decision boundary* in the network.
>
> The **loss function** is like the *digital weighing scale* that compares the produced coffee's actual taste score to the customer's expected taste score. The larger the gap (the "loss"), the louder the alarm that the machine's internal dials (weights) need adjustment.

### Why These Functions Are the Heart of Deep Learning

| Concept | Why It Matters |
|---------|---------------|
| Non-linearity | Stacking linear layers without activation = a single linear transformation. No universal approximation. |
| Differentiability | Both activations and losses must be differentiable to enable backpropagation. |
| Bounded vs Unbounded | Choice of activation determines gradient flow stability (vanishing/exploding gradients). |
| Probabilistic Interpretation | Softmax + Cross-Entropy gives a valid probability distribution over classes. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking: *"Why can't we use a linear activation function in hidden layers?"* The textbook answer is: *A composition of linear functions is itself linear, so the network loses its expressive power and cannot solve non-linearly separable problems like XOR.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1.2.1 Taxonomy of Activation Functions

Activation functions are classified by their **range** and **monotonicity**:

1. **Sigmoid (Logistic)** — squashes input to $(0, 1)$
2. **Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent)** — squashes input to $(-1, 1)$
3. **ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)** — half-wave rectifier, range $[0, \infty)$
4. **Leaky ReLU / PReLU** — fixes the "dying ReLU" problem
5. **Softmax** — multi-class probability distribution
6. **Swish / GELU** — modern smooth non-monotonic activations

## 1.2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula you must memorize** for Module 1. All derivatives assume the standard scalar case; vectorized Jacobian forms are noted separately.

| Function | Forward Equation $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Output Range | Key Property |
|----------|-------------------------|---------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | $\sigma(x)\bigl(1 - \sigma(x)\bigr)$ | $(0, 1)$ | Smooth, differentiable, **vanishing gradient** for $\vert x \vert > 2$ |
| Tanh | $\tanh(x) = \dfrac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$ | $1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Zero-centered, still saturates |
| ReLU | $\max(0, x)$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x < 0 \end{cases}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Computationally cheap, **dying ReLU** issue |
| Leaky ReLU | $\max(\alpha x, x)$, $\alpha \approx 0.01$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ \alpha & x < 0 \end{cases}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Prevents dead neurons |
| Softmax (vector) | $S_{i} = \dfrac{e^{z_{i}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{j}}}$ | $\dfrac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{j}} = S_{i}(\delta_{ij} - S_{j})$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to 1 | Used in **output layer** for multi-class |
| Swish | $x \cdot \sigma(x)$ | $\sigma(x) + x \cdot \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ | $(\approx -0.28, \infty)$ | Self-gated, smooth |
| Linear (Identity) | $x$ | $1$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Output layer for **regression** |

### Loss Function Cheat Sheet

| Loss Function | Formula | Derivative w.r.t. $\hat{y}$ | Use Case |
|---------------|---------|----------------------------|----------|
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $L = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}$ | $-\dfrac{2}{n}(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})$ | Regression |
| Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $L = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i} \vert$ | $-\dfrac{1}{n}\,\text{sign}(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})$ | Robust regression |
| Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) | $L = -\bigl[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\bigr]$ | $-\dfrac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$ | Binary classification |
| Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE) | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_{i} \log \hat{y}_{i}$ | $-\dfrac{y_{i}}{\hat{y}_{i}}$ | Multi-class classification |
| Hinge Loss (SVM-style) | $L = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot \hat{y})$ | $\begin{cases} -y & y\hat{y} < 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Max-margin classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In production AI systems (medical imaging, autonomous driving, NLP), the choice of activation in the *hidden* layers (almost always ReLU family) is decoupled from the *output* activation (Sigmoid for binary, Softmax for multi-class, Linear for regression). The loss is **always** matched to the output activation — this pairing is a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 1.2.3 Critical Engineering Properties to Remember

- **Vanishing Gradient:** Sigmoid/Tanh saturate → derivative $\to 0$ → early layers stop learning.
- **Exploding Gradient:** Unbounded activations (ReLU, Linear) can compound → use gradient clipping.
- **Zero-Centered Output:** Tanh and ReLU (with biases) give faster convergence than Sigmoid.
- **Probabilistic Consistency:** Softmax + CCE forms the canonical maximum-likelihood estimator for multinomial classification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Directive:** Derivations of activation function derivatives, the Softmax + Cross-Entropy combined gradient, and MSE gradient are **highest-priority questions** for the 14-mark module questions. Each derivation below is shown in full, line-by-line.

## 1.3.1 Derivation 1 — Sigmoid Derivative $\sigma'(x)$

We begin with the standard form:

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$$

**Step 1:** Rewrite using a negative exponent for cleaner differentiation.

$$\sigma(x) = (1 + e^{-x})^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule $\dfrac{d}{dx}\bigl[u(x)^{-1}\bigr] = -u(x)^{-2} \cdot u'(x)$.

$$\sigma'(x) = -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-x})^{-2} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(1 + e^{-x})$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate the inner function. The derivative of $1$ is $0$, and the derivative of $e^{-x}$ is $-e^{-x}$.

$$\sigma'(x) = -(1 + e^{-x})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-x})$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the sign and rearrange.

$$\sigma'(x) = \frac{e^{-x}}{(1 + e^{-x})^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (Elegant Form):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{x}$:

$$\sigma'(x) = \frac{1}{(1 + e^{-x})(1 + e^{x})}$$

**Step 6:** Notice that $\dfrac{1}{1 + e^{x}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}} = 1 - \sigma(x)$.

$$\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x) \cdot \bigl(1 - \sigma(x)\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant form is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU Deep Learning papers. Memorize the algebraic form: $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$.

## 1.3.2 Derivation 2 — Tanh Derivative

The hyperbolic tangent can be written in terms of exponentials:

$$\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the quotient rule: $\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)' = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^{2}}$.

$$u = e^{x} - e^{-x}, \quad u' = e^{x} + e^{-x}$$

$$v = e^{x} + e^{-x}, \quad v' = e^{x} - e^{-x}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$\tanh'(x) = \frac{(e^{x} + e^{-x})(e^{x} + e^{-x}) - (e^{x} - e^{-x})(e^{x} - e^{-x})}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize numerator as a difference of squares: $(a+b)^{2} - (a-b)^{2} = 4ab$ where $a = e^{x}$ and $b = e^{-x}$.

$$\text{Numerator} = (e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2} - (e^{x} - e^{-x})^{2} = 4 e^{x} e^{-x} = 4$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$\tanh'(x) = \frac{4}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (Elegant Form):** Divide numerator and denominator by $(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}$:

$$\tanh'(x) = 1 - \left(\frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}\right)^{2} = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$$

$$\boxed{\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)}$$

## 1.3.3 Derivation 3 — ReLU Derivative (Sub-gradient)

ReLU is piecewise linear:

$$f(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} x, & x > 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

Differentiating each branch:

$$f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x > 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

At $x = 0$, the function is **non-differentiable** in the classical sense, but the sub-gradient set is $[0, 1]$. In practice, deep learning frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) define $f'(0) = 0$ by convention.

## 1.3.4 Derivation 4 — Softmax Derivative (Jacobian)

The Softmax for class $i$:

$$S_{i} = \frac{e^{z_{i}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_{k}}}$$

For notational brevity, let $Z = \sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_{k}}$, so $S_{i} = e^{z_{i}} / Z$.

**Case 1:** $i = j$ (diagonal element).

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{i}} = \frac{e^{z_{i}} \cdot Z - e^{z_{i}} \cdot e^{z_{i}}}{Z^{2}} = \frac{e^{z_{i}}}{Z} - \left(\frac{e^{z_{i}}}{Z}\right)^{2} = S_{i} - S_{i}^{2} = S_{i}(1 - S_{i})$$

**Case 2:** $i \neq j$ (off-diagonal element).

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{j}} = \frac{0 \cdot Z - e^{z_{i}} \cdot e^{z_{j}}}{Z^{2}} = -\frac{e^{z_{i}}}{Z} \cdot \frac{e^{z_{j}}}{Z} = -S_{i} S_{j}$$

**Unified form using Kronecker delta $\delta_{ij}$:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{j}} = S_{i}(\delta_{ij} - S_{j})}$$

## 1.3.5 Derivation 5 — MSE Gradient

The Mean Squared Error for $n$ samples:

$$L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}$$

Differentiate with respect to a single prediction $\hat{y}_{j}$:

**Step 1:** Pull the constant $1/n$ and the summation inside.

$$L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}$$

**Step 2:** All terms with $i \neq j$ vanish under partial differentiation. Apply the chain rule on the $i = j$ term.

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}_{j}} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot 2(y_{j} - \hat{y}_{j}) \cdot (-1)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}_{j}} = -\frac{2}{n}(y_{j} - \hat{y}_{j})}$$

## 1.3.6 Derivation 6 — The Famous Softmax + Cross-Entropy Combined Gradient

This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 1, because it shows why the Softmax + CCE pairing is numerically and mathematically elegant.

**Setup:** $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_{i} \log S_{i}$, where $S_{i}$ is Softmax output.

**Step 1:** Apply the chain rule.

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_{j}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_{i} \cdot \frac{1}{S_{i}} \cdot \frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{j}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the Softmax Jacobian from Section 1.3.4.

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_{j}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_{i} \cdot \frac{1}{S_{i}} \cdot S_{i}(\delta_{ij} - S_{j})$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $S_{i}$ in numerator and denominator.

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_{j}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_{i}(\delta_{ij} - S_{j})$$

**Step 4:** Split the sum using the property of the Kronecker delta: $\sum_{i} y_{i} \delta_{ij} = y_{j}$.

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_{j}} = -\left(y_{j} - S_{j}\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_{i}\right)$$

**Step 5:** For a one-hot ground truth label, $\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_{i} = 1$.

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_{j}} = S_{j} - y_{j} = \hat{y}_{j} - y_{j}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this is a Board-Favorite:** The result is *ridiculously simple* — the gradient of CCE w.r.t. the *pre-activation* (logit) is just the difference between predicted probability and true label. No messy Jacobian, no numerical instability. This is why Softmax + CCE is the canonical multi-class output pair.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1.4.1 Functional Flow: A Neuron with Activation in the Forward Pass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Vector X] --> weightA[Weighted Sum WX plus Bias B]
    weightA --> preactA[Pre activation Z]
    preactA --> actA{Activation Function F}
    actA --> outA[Output A]
    outA --> lossA[Loss Function L]
    labelA[Ground Truth Y] --> lossA
    lossA --> gradA[Gradient Computation]
    gradA --> backA[Backpropagation Update]
    backA --> weightA
```

## 1.4.2 Architecture: Block-Level Functional Mapping of Softmax + Cross-Entropy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rawA[Raw Logits Z1 Z2 Z3] --> expA[Exponentiation E to the Z]
    expA --> sumA[Sum Across Classes]
    sumA --> divA[Normalize Division]
    divA --> probA[Probability Vector S]
    probA --> logA[Logarithm]
    labelB[One Hot Label Y] --> logA
    logA --> mulA[Element Wise Multiply with Y]
    mulA --> sumB[Sum and Negate]
    sumB --> lossB[Final Loss L]
    probA --> gradB[Gradient equals S minus Y]
    gradB --> updateA[Weight Update Step]
```

## 1.4.3 Decision Diagram: Choosing the Right Activation & Loss Pair

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Problem Type] --> q1A{Binary Classification}
    q1A -->|Yes| s1A[Output Sigmoid]
    s1A --> l1A[Loss Binary Cross Entropy]
    q1A -->|No| q2A{Multi Class Classification}
    q2A -->|Yes| s2A[Output Softmax]
    s2A --> l2A[Loss Categorical Cross Entropy]
    q2A -->|No| q3A{Regression}
    q3A -->|Yes| s3A[Output Linear]
    s3A --> l3A{Loss Type}
    l3A -->|Outliers Present| m1A[MAE]
    l3A -->|Smooth Gradient| m2A[MSE]
    l3A -->|Max Margin SVM| m3A[Hinge Loss]
```

## 1.4.4 Comparative Graphical Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1A[Sigmoid] --> p1A[Range Zero to One]
    a2A[Tanh] --> p2A[Range Negative One to One]
    a3A[ReLU] --> p3A[Range Zero to Infinity]
    a4A[Leaky ReLU] --> p4A[Range Negative Infinity to Infinity]
    a5A[Softmax] --> p5A[Probabilities Sum to One]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a transformation stage. The arrows indicate the *direction of data flow* during a single training iteration. The "Ground Truth Y → Loss" branch is the supervision signal that closes the loop and enables learning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of activation functions in a neural network. Why is a non-linear activation function necessary in hidden layers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An activation function is a non-linear mathematical transformation applied to a neuron's pre-activation value $z = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + b$, producing the output $a = f(z)$.
- **Role (1 mark):** It introduces **non-linearity** into the network, allowing it to approximate complex, non-linear mappings (Universal Approximation Theorem).
- **Why non-linear (1 mark):** If $f$ is linear, then a stack of $L$ layers collapses to a single linear transformation $\hat{y} = \mathbf{W}'\mathbf{x} + b'$, making depth useless. Non-linear activations preserve the expressive power of depth and enable learning of XOR, image features, language patterns, etc.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Compare Sigmoid and ReLU activation functions in terms of gradient behavior.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Sigmoid (1.5 marks):** $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$. Maximum value is $0.25$ at $x = 0$. For $\vert x \vert > 2$, the gradient saturates to nearly $0$, causing the **vanishing gradient problem** and stalling learning in deep networks.
- **ReLU (1.5 marks):** $f'(x) = 1$ for $x > 0$ and $0$ for $x < 0$. The gradient is constant (does not vanish) for all positive inputs, leading to faster convergence. However, neurons can become "dead" (always output $0$) if they get stuck in the negative region — known as the **dying ReLU problem**.

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) Derive the gradient of the Sigmoid activation function. Show that $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Starting from $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$:

1. Rewrite as $\sigma(x) = (1 + e^{-x})^{-1}$ (1 mark)
2. Apply chain rule: $\sigma'(x) = -(1 + e^{-x})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-x}) = \dfrac{e^{-x}}{(1 + e^{-x})^{2}}$ (2 marks)
3. Multiply top and bottom by $e^{x}$: $\sigma'(x) = \dfrac{1}{e^{x}(1 + e^{-x})(1 + e^{-x}) \cdot e^{-x}}$... simplify to $\dfrac{1}{(1 + e^{-x})(1 + e^{x})}$ (2 marks)
4. Recognize $1 - \sigma(x) = \dfrac{1 + e^{-x} - 1}{1 + e^{-x}} = \dfrac{e^{-x}}{1 + e^{-x}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{x}}$ and use $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ to get $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$ (2 marks)

**(b) Explain the vanishing gradient problem. How does ReLU address it? Describe one limitation of ReLU and its fix.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution Outline:**

- **Vanishing Gradient Definition (2 marks):** When backpropagating through many layers, gradients are multiplied by small derivatives (e.g., $\sigma'(x) \leq 0.25$ for Sigmoid). For a 10-layer network, the effective gradient reaching layer 1 can be as small as $0.25^{10} \approx 10^{-6}$, making early layers learn negligibly.
- **How ReLU Solves It (2 marks):** For $x > 0$, $\text{ReLU}'(x) = 1$, so the gradient flows unchanged through positive neurons regardless of depth. This enables training of very deep networks (ResNet-152, GPT, etc.).
- **Dying ReLU Problem (1.5 marks):** If a neuron's bias shifts so that its pre-activation is always negative, the gradient is permanently $0$ and the neuron never updates.
- **Fix: Leaky ReLU (1.5 marks):** $f(x) = \max(\alpha x, x)$ with $\alpha \approx 0.01$. The derivative is $\alpha$ for $x < 0$, ensuring a small but non-zero gradient keeps the neuron alive.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) For a $K$-class classification problem, derive the combined gradient of the Softmax output and Categorical Cross-Entropy loss with respect to the pre-activation logits $z_j$.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Given $S_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{k} e^{z_k}}$ and $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log S_i$:

1. Write chain rule: $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = -\sum_{i} y_i \cdot \dfrac{1}{S_i} \cdot \dfrac{\partial S_i}{\partial z_j}$ (1 mark)
2. Derive Softmax Jacobian (1 mark): $\dfrac{\partial S_i}{\partial z_j} = S_i(\delta_{ij} - S_j)$
3. Substitute and cancel $S_i$: $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = -\sum_i y_i (\delta_{ij} - S_j)$ (2 marks)
4. Split using $\sum_i y_i \delta_{ij} = y_j$: $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = -y_j + S_j \sum_i y_i$ (2 marks)
5. Apply one-hot constraint $\sum_i y_i = 1$: $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = S_j - y_j$ (1 mark)

**(b) Mean Squared Error loss is given by $L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. Derive the gradient w.r.t. $\hat{y}_j$ and explain why MSE is unsuitable for classification tasks.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution Outline:**

- **Gradient Derivation (4 marks):** Pull the constant $\dfrac{1}{n}$ outside, keep only the $i = j$ term under partial differentiation, apply chain rule with derivative of inner function being $-1$:
  $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}_j} = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot 2(y_j - \hat{y}_j) \cdot (-1) = -\dfrac{2}{n}(y_j - \hat{y}_j)$
- **Why MSE is poor for classification (3 marks):**
  1. **Non-probabilistic:** MSE treats outputs as continuous values, not class probabilities — no interpretable confidence.
  2. **Flat gradients near saturation:** When paired with Sigmoid output, MSE's gradient is small even when the prediction is very wrong, leading to slow learning.
  3. **Wrong loss landscape:** Cross-Entropy has a much steeper gradient for confidently wrong predictions, providing stronger learning signals. MSE treats a misclassification of "cat" as "dog" the same as predicting "cat" as "truck" — categorical information is lost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the chain rule** when differentiating composite functions like Softmax — most students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Confusing the Sigmoid derivative forms** — writing $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x) - \sigma^2(x)$ is correct, but $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x) + \sigma^2(x)$ is a *fatal* error.
> 3. **Missing the $1/n$ factor** in MSE gradient derivations — examiners are strict about this.
> 4. **Pairing wrong loss with wrong activation:** Using MSE with Softmax, or BCE with Softmax multi-class — this shows conceptual gap and attracts negative marking.
> 5. **In Softmax derivation:** Forgetting that the denominator $Z$ depends on $z_j$ — must use quotient rule, not simple differentiation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Activation & Loss Functions: 10 Things You MUST Know**

- **Sigmoid output range:** $(0, 1)$; **Tanh output range:** $(-1, 1)$; **ReLU output range:** $[0, \infty)$.
- **Sigmoid derivative identity:** $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$ — this is the most-frequently-asked derivation.
- **Tanh derivative identity:** $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$.
- **ReLU derivative:** $1$ if $x > 0$, else $0$ — non-differentiable at $x = 0$ (sub-gradient used).
- **Softmax + Cross-Entropy combined gradient:** $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = S_j - y_j$ — clean, elegant, and the most board-relevant.
- **MSE gradient:** $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}_j} = -\dfrac{2}{n}(y_j - \hat{y}_j)$.
- **Vanishing gradient cause:** Sigmoid/Tanh saturation; **fix:** ReLU family activations.
- **Dying ReLU cause:** Permanently negative pre-activations; **fix:** Leaky ReLU with $\alpha \approx 0.01$.
- **Canonical pairings:** Sigmoid + BCE (binary), Softmax + CCE (multi-class), Linear + MSE (regression).
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A neural network with at least one hidden layer and a non-linear activation can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary precision — this justifies the entire use of activation functions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Deep Learning**, **Optimization** refers to the mathematical and computational process of finding the set of model parameters (weights $\mathbf{W}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}$) that minimize (or maximize) a predefined objective function, conventionally known as the **Loss Function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ or **Cost Function** $J(\theta)$, where $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ represents the parameter vector of the neural network.

Formally, the optimization problem is expressed as:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}} \mathcal{L}(\theta)$$

where $\theta^{*}$ is the optimal parameter configuration that yields the global minimum of the loss landscape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) for PBCAT604, optimization is treated as the **backbone of model training**.
