The syllabus explicitly demands an understanding of **first-order optimization methods** (Gradient Descent, SGD, Momentum, Adam) and the **challenges** of optimizing non-convex loss surfaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Mountain Trekker

Imagine you are standing blindfolded on a vast, fog-covered mountain range, and your goal is to reach the **lowest valley** (the minimum loss). You cannot see the entire terrain, but you can feel the **slope of the ground beneath your feet** (the gradient).

- **You** = The neural network parameters $\theta$.
- **The Mountain** = The Loss Landscape $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$.
- **The Slope** = The Gradient $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta)$.
- **Your Steps** = The Learning Rate $\eta$.

Every time you take a step, you feel the slope, step in the **steepest downhill direction** (negative gradient), and adjust your position. Over many iterations, you descend into a valley. This is the essence of **gradient-based optimization**.

### 1.3 Why Optimization is Critical in Deep Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Unlike classical machine learning (e.g., Linear Regression, Logistic Regression) where the loss function is **convex** and has a single global minimum, deep neural networks possess **highly non-convex loss surfaces** riddled with local minima, saddle points, plateaus, and ravines. Therefore, the *choice of optimization algorithm directly determines model convergence speed, generalization, and final accuracy*.

The primary goals of an optimizer in deep learning are:
1. **Convergence** — Reaching a sufficiently low loss value.
2. **Stability** — Avoiding divergence or oscillation.
3. **Generalization** — Finding flat minima that generalize well to unseen data.
4. **Computational Efficiency** — Minimizing wall-clock training time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundations of Optimization

Optimization in deep learning is built upon **iterative numerical methods**. Given a differentiable loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$, we iteratively update parameters using the **general update rule**:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} + \Delta\theta_{t}$$

where $\Delta\theta_{t}$ is the update direction and magnitude computed by the chosen optimizer.

#### 2.1.1 Vanilla Gradient Descent (GD)

The simplest first-order method updates parameters by moving in the direction opposite to the gradient of the loss:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

where:
- $\eta$ = **Learning Rate** (a positive scalar, typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$).
- $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$ = Gradient of the loss w.r.t. parameters at time step $t$.

**Variants based on data batch size:**

| Variant | Batch Size | Update Frequency | Characteristics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Batch GD** | Full dataset ($N$) | Once per epoch | Stable, slow, memory-heavy |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | $1$ sample | $N$ times per epoch | Noisy, fast, can escape local minima |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | $b$ (e.g., $32, 64, 128$) | $N/b$ times per epoch | **Industry standard**, balances speed and stability |

#### 2.1.2 SGD with Momentum

Momentum accelerates SGD by adding a fraction $\gamma$ of the previous update to the current gradient:

$$v_{t} = \gamma \cdot v_{t-1} + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t}$$

where $\gamma$ is the **momentum coefficient**, typically $\gamma \in [0.9, 0.99]$.

#### 2.1.3 Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)

A modification of momentum that computes the gradient at the **anticipated future position**:

$$v_{t} = \gamma \cdot v_{t-1} + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} - \gamma \cdot v_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t}$$

#### 2.1.4 Adaptive Learning Rate Methods

These methods **per-parameter adapt** the learning rate based on the historical gradients.

**AdaGrad (Adaptive Gradient Algorithm):**

$$g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

$$r_{t} = r_{t-1} + g_{t}^{2}$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t}} + \epsilon} \cdot g_{t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback of AdaGrad:** The accumulator $r_{t}$ grows monotonically, causing the learning rate to shrink to zero and **stall training prematurely**.

**RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation):**

Introduces an **exponentially decaying average** of squared gradients to solve AdaGrad's diminishing learning rate:

$$r_{t} = \rho \cdot r_{t-1} + (1-\rho) \cdot g_{t}^{2}$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t}} + \epsilon} \cdot g_{t}$$

where $\rho$ is the decay rate, typically $\rho = 0.9$.

**Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation):**

Combines **momentum** (first moment) with **RMSProp** (second moment):

$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} \cdot m_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{1}) \cdot g_{t}$$

$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} \cdot v_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{2}) \cdot g_{t}^{2}$$

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \quad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon} \cdot \hat{m}_{t}$$

Default hyperparameters: $\beta_{1} = 0.9$, $\beta_{2} = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Method | Update Rule (Core Form) | Key Hyperparameter | Default Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GD** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot g_{t}$ | Learning rate $\eta$ | $0.01$ |
| **SGD with Momentum** | $v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta g_{t}$; $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t}$ | Momentum $\gamma$ | $0.9$ |
| **NAG** | $v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} - \gamma v_{t-1})$ | Momentum $\gamma$ | $0.9$ |
| **AdaGrad** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta g_{t}}{\sqrt{\sum g_{t}^{2}} + \epsilon}$ | $\epsilon$ | $10^{-8}$ |
| **RMSProp** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta g_{t}}{\sqrt{E[g^{2}]_{t}} + \epsilon}$ | Decay $\rho$ | $0.9$ |
| **Adam** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta \hat{m}_{t}}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon}$ | $\beta_{1}, \beta_{2}$ | $0.9, 0.999$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The **Adam optimizer** is the de-facto standard for training **Transformers, CNNs for image classification, GANs, and LLMs**. **SGD with Momentum** remains preferred in **computer vision tasks** (e.g., ResNet on ImageNet) for achieving state-of-the-art generalization. The choice depends on the problem's **loss landscape geometry**.

### 2.3 Challenges in Non-Convex Optimization

Deep learning loss landscapes present unique challenges that distinguish them from classical optimization:

1. **Local Minima** — Stationary points where $\nabla \mathcal{L} = 0$ but $\mathcal{L}$ is not globally minimum. In high-dimensional spaces, local minima are surprisingly rare; **saddle points are far more common**.
2. **Saddle Points** — Points where the Hessian has both positive and negative eigenvalues. Gradients are zero, but the point is neither a max nor a min.
3. **Vanishing Gradients** — In deep networks, gradients shrink exponentially during backpropagation, halting learning in early layers (mitigated by ReLU, BatchNorm, residual connections).
4. **Exploding Gradients** — Gradients grow exponentially, causing parameter updates to diverge (mitigated by **gradient clipping**).
5. **Poor Conditioning** — The Hessian has a large condition number, causing the optimizer to zigzag across narrow ravines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule

We begin with the **Taylor Series expansion** of the loss function around the current parameter $\theta_{t}$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} + \Delta\theta) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t}) + (\Delta\theta)^{T} \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t}) + \frac{1}{2} (\Delta\theta)^{T} H (\Delta\theta)$$

where $H$ is the Hessian matrix. For a sufficiently small $\Delta\theta$, we ignore the second-order term and simplify:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} + \Delta\theta) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t}) + (\Delta\theta)^{T} \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

We want to **minimize** $\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} + \Delta\theta)$, so we need:

$$(\Delta\theta)^{T} \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t}) < 0$$

By the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality**, the inner product is minimized (most negative) when $\Delta\theta$ is anti-parallel to the gradient:

$$\Delta\theta = -\eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

Substituting back gives the canonical gradient descent rule:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Adam's Bias-Correction Term

At $t = 0$, $m_{0} = 0$ and $v_{0} = 0$. The moving averages are biased toward zero in early iterations. We derive the correction:

\begin{aligned}
m_{t} &= (1-\beta_{1}) \sum_{i=1}^{t} \beta_{1}^{t-i} \cdot g_{i} \\
\mathbb{E}[m_{t}] &= \mathbb{E}[(1-\beta_{1}) \sum_{i=1}^{t} \beta_{1}^{t-i} g_{i}] \\
&= (1-\beta_{1}) \sum_{i=1}^{t} \beta_{1}^{t-i} \mathbb{E}[g_{i}] \\
&= (1-\beta_{1}^{t}) \cdot \mathbb{E}[g_{t}]
\end{aligned}

Therefore, the bias-corrected first moment estimate is:

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}$$

Similarly, for the second moment:

$$\hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanatory Step:** The term $\beta_{1}^{t}$ decays to zero as $t \to \infty$, so the correction is only significant during early training iterations. By step $t=10$ with $\beta_{1}=0.9$, the correction factor is $\frac{1}{1-0.9^{10}} \approx 2.30$, which is non-trivial.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: SGD on a Simple Quadratic

Consider the loss function:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = (\theta - 5)^{2} = \theta^{2} - 10\theta + 25$$

**Step 1:** Compute the gradient.

$$\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta) = 2(\theta - 5)$$

**Step 2:** Initialize $\theta_{0} = 0$ and set $\eta = 0.1$.

**Step 3:** Iterate using $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$.

| Iteration $t$ | $\theta_{t}$ | $\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t}) = 2(\theta_{t} - 5)$ | $\theta_{t+1}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | $0.000$ | $-10.000$ | $0.000 - 0.1(-10) = 1.000$ |
| 1 | $1.000$ | $-8.000$ | $1.000 - 0.1(-8) = 1.800$ |
| 2 | $1.800$ | $-6.400$ | $1.800 - 0.1(-6.4) = 2.440$ |
| 3 | $2.440$ | $-5.120$ | $2.440 - 0.1(-5.12) = 2.952$ |
| 4 | $2.952$ | $-4.096$ | $2.952 - 0.1(-4.096) = 3.362$ |
| 5 | $3.362$ | $-3.277$ | $3.362 - 0.1(-3.277) = 3.689$ |
| 10 | $4.560$ | $-0.880$ | $4.560 - 0.1(-0.880) = 4.648$ |

**Step 4:** Observe convergence.

As $t \to \infty$, $\theta_{t} \to 5$, which is the **global minimum** of the convex quadratic. The convergence is geometric with rate $(1 - 2\eta) = 0.8$ per step.

### 3.4 Learning Rate Scheduling Derivations

**Step Decay:** The learning rate is reduced by a factor $\alpha$ every $k$ epochs:

$$\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot \alpha^{\lfloor t/k \rfloor}$$

**Exponential Decay:**

$$\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Cosine Annealing:**

$$\eta_{t} = \eta_{\min} + \frac{1}{2}(\eta_{\max} - \eta_{\min})\left(1 + \cos\left(\frac{t}{T}\pi\right)\right)$$

where $T$ is the total number of training steps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Optimization Pipeline

The following diagram maps the complete data and control flow during neural network training, illustrating the role of the optimizer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Input Training Data X] --> nodeB[Forward Propagation Through Network]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Loss L of Theta]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Backward Propagation Compute Gradient]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Optimizer Selection}
    nodeE -->|SGD Path| nodeF[SGD Vanilla Update]
    nodeE -->|Momentum Path| nodeG[Momentum Accelerated Update]
    nodeE -->|Adam Path| nodeH[Adam Adaptive Moment Update]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Update Parameters Theta]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Loss Converged}
    nodeJ -->|No| nodeB
    nodeJ -->|Yes| nodeK[END: Trained Model Weights]
```

### 4.2 Optimizer Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[First Order Optimizers] --> nodeB[Batch Gradient Descent]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Stochastic Gradient Descent]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Mini Batch Gradient Descent]
    nodeC --> nodeE[SGD with Momentum]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Nesterov Accelerated Gradient]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Adaptive Learning Rate Methods]
    nodeG --> nodeH[AdaGrad Algorithm]
    nodeG --> nodeI[RMSProp Algorithm]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Adam Optimizer]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[AdamW Weight Decoupled]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Nadam Nesterov Adam]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Loss Landscape Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Local Minima Trap] --> nodeB[Saddle Point Region]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Global Minimum Target]
    nodeD[Starting Point] --> nodeE[Gradient Descent Trajectory]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Convergence to Optimum]
    nodeA -.->|Poor Init| nodeE
    nodeB -.->|Hessian Issues| nodeE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The loss landscape in deep learning is **high-dimensional** (millions of parameters). The schematic above is a **simplified 2D projection**. In practice, the geometry includes ravines, plateaus, and broad basins — making optimizer selection a critical hyperparameter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Explain the role of the learning rate in gradient-based optimization. What happens if the learning rate is set too high or too low?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The learning rate $\eta$ controls the **step size** of parameter updates during gradient descent. It determines how far the parameters move in the direction opposite to the gradient at each iteration.

- If $\eta$ is **too high**: The optimizer overshoots the minimum, causing **oscillation or divergence**. The loss may increase or fluctuate wildly.
- If $\eta$ is **too low**: Convergence is **extremely slow**, requiring many epochs. The optimizer may get stuck in plateaus or saddle points.
- An **optimal $\eta$** balances convergence speed and stability. It is typically tuned using **learning rate scheduling** or **learning rate range tests**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent, and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Batch GD | Stochastic GD | Mini-Batch GD |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data per update** | Entire dataset | One sample | Small batch (e.g., 32–256) |
| **Update frequency** | Once per epoch | $N$ times per epoch | $N/b$ times per epoch |
| **Gradient estimate** | Exact | Noisy | Approximate, low variance |
| **Memory requirement** | Very high | Very low | Moderate |
| **Convergence** | Smooth, deterministic | Erratic, can escape local minima | Balanced — **industry standard** |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the parameter update rule for **Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum**. Explain how momentum helps overcome the limitations of vanilla SGD. Use a suitable diagram.

**Model Solution:**

The vanilla SGD update rule is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot g_{t}$$

where $g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$.

**Limitation:** Vanilla SGD suffers from **slow convergence** in ravine-like loss surfaces (high condition number). The parameters oscillate across the narrow valley walls while making tiny progress along the valley floor.

**Derivation of Momentum:** Momentum introduces a **velocity vector** $v_{t}$ that accumulates a fraction $\gamma$ of past gradients:

$$v_{t} = \gamma \cdot v_{t-1} + \eta \cdot g_{t}$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t}$$

**Intuition:** Think of a heavy ball rolling down a hill. It accumulates **kinetic energy** (momentum) from consistent gradient directions, accelerating through flat regions and damping oscillations in inconsistent directions.

**How momentum helps:**
1. **Accelerates convergence** in directions of consistent gradient sign.
2. **Damps oscillations** across ravine walls.
3. **Helps escape shallow local minima** and plateaus.
4. **Smooths noisy gradients**, especially in SGD.

The term $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is typically set to $0.9$ or $0.99$. The effective learning rate becomes $\frac{\eta}{1-\gamma}$, which is why $\eta$ is usually reduced when using momentum.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Adam optimizer** with all update equations. Discuss why bias correction is necessary in the early training steps.

**Model Solution:**

Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) maintains two exponentially decaying moving averages:
- **First moment** $m_{t}$: Mean of gradients (like momentum).
- **Second moment** $v_{t}$: Uncentered variance of gradients (like RMSProp).

**Update equations:**

$$g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} \cdot m_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{1}) \cdot g_{t}$$

$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} \cdot v_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{2}) \cdot g_{t}^{2}$$

**Bias correction** (because $m_{0} = v_{0} = 0$):

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \quad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

**Final parameter update:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon} \cdot \hat{m}_{t}$$

**Why bias correction is necessary:** At initialization, $m_{0} = v_{0} = 0$, so the moving averages are **biased toward zero** in early steps. For example, with $\beta_{1} = 0.9$ at $t=1$:

$$\mathbb{E}[m_{1}] = 0.9 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot g_{1} = 0.1 \cdot g_{1}$$

This is much smaller than the true mean $\mathbb{E}[g_{1}]$. Dividing by $(1-\beta_{1}^{t})$ corrects this systematic underestimation, ensuring that the first and second moment estimates are unbiased estimators of the true gradient statistics, especially critical for stable early-stage training.

**Default hyperparameters:** $\eta = 0.001$, $\beta_{1} = 0.9$, $\beta_{2} = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **challenges of optimization in deep learning**. Discuss local minima, saddle points, and the vanishing gradient problem with appropriate diagrams.

**Model Solution:**

Deep neural networks define a **highly non-convex optimization problem**. The major challenges are:

**1. Local Minima:**
A local minimum is a point where $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L} = 0$ and the Hessian $H$ is **positive semi-definite**, but $\mathcal{L}(\theta_{\text{local}}) > \mathcal{L}(\theta_{\text{global}})$. In high-dimensional spaces, the probability of encountering a true local minimum is surprisingly low; most stationary points are **saddle points**. However, when local minima do occur, the optimizer gets trapped and yields suboptimal performance.

**2. Saddle Points:**
A saddle point satisfies $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L} = 0$, but the Hessian has **both positive and negative eigenvalues** (it is indefinite). Mathematically:

$$\lambda_{\min}(H) < 0 < \lambda_{\max}(H)$$

Saddle points are problematic because gradients vanish, halting gradient descent, yet the point is not a minimum. Modern optimizers like **SGD with noise** and **Adam** can escape saddle points due to their stochastic nature.

**3. Vanishing Gradients:**
In deep networks, the backpropagation chain rule involves multiplying many Jacobian matrices. If eigenvalues of these matrices are less than 1, gradients **shrink exponentially** with depth:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{1}} \right\Vert \approx \prod_{l=1}^{L} \lambda_{l} \cdot \left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{L}} \right\Vert$$

This causes early layers to learn extremely slowly. Mitigations include:
- **ReLU activation** (derivative is 1 for positive inputs).
- **Batch Normalization** (normalizes layer inputs).
- **Residual connections** (ResNet architecture).
- **Careful weight initialization** (He, Xavier).

**4. Exploding Gradients:** Opposite phenomenon — gradients grow exponentially. Solved by **gradient clipping** and **L2 regularization**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **AdaGrad, RMSProp, and Adam** optimizers. Tabulate the update rules and discuss the limitations of AdaGrad that motivated RMSProp and Adam.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | AdaGrad | RMSProp | Adam |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accumulator** | $r_{t} = r_{t-1} + g_{t}^{2}$ (sum) | $r_{t} = \rho r_{t-1} + (1-\rho)g_{t}^{2}$ (EMA) | Two EMAs: $m_{t}$ and $v_{t}$ |
| **Learning rate** | Monotonically decreasing | Per-parameter, adaptive | Per-parameter, adaptive |
| **Bias correction** | None | None | Yes — $\hat{m}_{t}, \hat{v}_{t}$ |
| **Limitation** | Learning rate → 0, stalls | No first-moment momentum | Slightly higher memory |
| **Use case** | Sparse data (NLP) | RNNs, non-stationary | **General purpose** |

**AdaGrad Limitation:** The accumulator $r_{t} = \sum_{i=1}^{t} g_{i}^{2}$ grows **monotonically and unboundedly** because it sums all past squared gradients. Consequently, the effective learning rate $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t}} + \epsilon}$ **shrinks continuously** and asymptotically approaches zero, causing training to **stall prematurely** before reaching the optimum.

**RMSProp's Solution:** RMSProp replaces the cumulative sum with an **exponentially weighted moving average**:

$$r_{t} = \rho \cdot r_{t-1} + (1-\rho) \cdot g_{t}^{2}$$

This "forgets" old gradients exponentially (controlled by decay rate $\rho = 0.9$), so the learning rate never collapses to zero. RMSProp allows training to continue for arbitrarily long periods.

**Adam's Advancement:** Adam extends RMSProp by also tracking the **first moment** (mean of gradients) as momentum, combined with **bias correction** to counteract initialization bias:

$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{1}) g_{t} \quad \text{(first moment, like momentum)}$$

$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} v_{t-1} + (1-\beta_{2}) g_{t}^{2} \quad \text{(second moment, like RMSProp)}$$

The update uses both:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta \cdot \hat{m}_{t}}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon}$$

This makes Adam the **most robust and widely used optimizer** in modern deep learning, combining the benefits of momentum (acceleration) and adaptive learning rates (per-parameter tuning).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write "$\eta$ is the momentum" — WRONG. $\eta$ is the learning rate; $\gamma$ or $\beta_{1}$ is the momentum coefficient.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **bias correction** in Adam. Examiners specifically test this. You lose **2 marks** if equations for $\hat{m}_{t}$ and $\hat{v}_{t}$ are missing.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **local minima with global minima** in non-convex loss surfaces. Always state that deep networks have **multiple local minima**, but high dimensionality means saddle points are more common.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing SGD update as $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$ without specifying the iteration index. Use $\theta_{t}$ and $\theta_{t+1}$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to mention the role of **mini-batches** when explaining SGD. SGD technically uses one sample; **Mini-Batch GD** is the practical standard.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Optimization** in deep learning is the process of minimizing the loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ over parameter space $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.
- The **vanilla gradient descent** update is $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$.
- **Learning rate $\eta$** is the most critical hyperparameter. Use **learning rate scheduling** (step, exponential, cosine) to improve convergence.
- **Momentum** accelerates SGD by accumulating past gradients: $v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta g_{t}$.
- **NAG** is a "look-ahead" variant of momentum that improves convergence near optima.
- **AdaGrad** adapts learning rates per parameter but suffers from **monotonically decreasing** learning rates.
- **RMSProp** solves AdaGrad's limitation using an **exponentially decaying moving average** of squared gradients.
- **Adam** is the gold standard — combines **momentum (first moment)** and **RMSProp (second moment)** with **bias correction**.
- Adam's update uses $\hat{m}_{t}$ and $\hat{v}_{t}$ — the **bias-corrected** moment estimates. **Do not forget the correction terms.**
- **Challenges**: Local minima, saddle points, vanishing/exploding gradients, and poor conditioning of the Hessian.
- Mitigations for vanishing gradients: **ReLU activation**, **Batch Normalization**, **Residual connections**, and proper **weight initialization**.
- **Mini-Batch GD** is the practical industry standard — it balances the stability of Batch GD with the speed of SGD.
- For **KTU 2024 exams**: Always show step-by-step derivations, include diagrams, and tag equations with their algorithm name (e.g., "Adam update equation").
- **Default hyperparameters to memorize**: Adam ($\eta=0.001$, $\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.999$); SGD-Momentum ($\eta=0.01$, $\gamma=0.9$); RMSProp ($\eta=0.001$, $\rho=0.9$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training Neural Networks - Gradient Descent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Training Neural Networks - Gradient Descent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gradient Descent** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the minimum of a differentiable cost (loss) function $J(\theta)$ with respect to the model parameters $\theta$ (weights and biases) of a neural network. At each iteration $t$, the parameters are updated in the direction of the steepest *negative* gradient of the cost function, scaled by a **learning rate** $\alpha$.

Mathematically, the canonical update rule is expressed as:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$$

where:
- $\theta_t$ is the parameter vector at iteration $t$
- $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (a positive scalar hyperparameter)
- $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ is the gradient of the cost function w.r.t. $\theta$ evaluated at $\theta_t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Gradient Descent is the foundational optimization engine of *all* deep learning training. Without it, the backpropagated error signals have no mechanism to translate into actual weight updates. Mastering this topic is essential for Module 1 outcomes **CO1** (Apply fundamental mathematical foundations of deep learning) and **CO2** (Build and train neural network models).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a hiker stranded on a foggy mountain at night, and you need to reach the valley (the lowest point) below. You cannot see the full landscape, but you can **feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet**.

- The **mountain's terrain** represents the cost function $J(\theta)$.
- **Your current position** represents the parameter values $\theta_t$.
- The **steepness of the slope** at your feet is the gradient $\nabla J(\theta_t)$.
- The **size of the step** you take downhill is the learning rate $\alpha$.

You take small, deliberate steps *in the direction of the steepest descent* until you can no longer descend (a flat patch = a local/global minimum). This is precisely the philosophy of Gradient Descent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Descent"?** Because we are *minimizing* a cost function. If we were maximizing (e.g., a reward function in reinforcement learning), we would use **Gradient Ascent**, flipping the sign of the update.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Primer

| Term | Symbol | Role |
|------|--------|------|
| Cost Function | $J(\theta)$ | The function we minimize; measures prediction error. |
| Learning Rate | $\alpha$ | Controls step size; too small = slow, too large = divergence. |
| Gradient | $\nabla J(\theta)$ | Vector of partial derivatives; points toward steepest ascent. |
| Epoch | — | One complete pass through the entire training dataset. |
| Iteration | $t$ | A single parameter update step. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Breakdown

The execution of Gradient Descent follows a strict cyclic procedure:

1. **Initialize Parameters:** Set $\theta_0$ randomly (e.g., He, Xavier, or Glorot initialization). Bad initialization can trap the network in poor local minima.
2. **Forward Pass:** Feed a batch of input data $\mathbf{X}$ through the network to compute predictions $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.
3. **Loss Computation:** Evaluate the cost function $J(\theta)$, e.g., Mean Squared Error (MSE) for regression or Cross-Entropy for classification.
4. **Backward Pass (Backpropagation):** Apply the chain rule of calculus to compute $\nabla_\theta J(\theta)$, i.e., the partial derivative of the loss w.r.t. every weight and bias.
5. **Parameter Update:** Apply the update rule $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$.
6. **Convergence Check:** Repeat steps 2–5 until $J(\theta)$ plateaus, a maximum number of epochs is reached, or gradient norm falls below a threshold $\epsilon$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the negative sign:** The gradient vector $\nabla J(\theta)$ mathematically points in the direction of *steepest increase* (ascent). To *minimize*, we must move in the *opposite* direction, hence the negative sign.

### 2.2 The Three Principal Variants of Gradient Descent

#### (a) Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)
Uses the *entire* training dataset $\mathbf{X}$ to compute the gradient at each iteration.

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathbf{X})$$

- **Pros:** Deterministic, smooth convergence trajectory.
- **Cons:** Very slow for large $N$; memory-intensive; cannot escape shallow local minima easily.

#### (b) Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
Uses a *single training example* $(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ to compute the gradient.

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

- **Pros:** Fast updates; noisy trajectory acts as implicit regularization, often finding flatter minima.
- **Cons:** High variance; loss curve oscillates wildly.

#### (c) Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD) — *The Industry Standard*
A compromise: uses a batch of $m$ examples where $1 < m < N$.

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathbf{X}^{(i:i+m)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i:i+m)})$$

- **Pros:** Balances speed and stability; leverages vectorized hardware (GPUs).
- **Cons:** Introduces the mini-batch size as an additional hyperparameter.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Canonical Update Rule | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ | The defining equation of GD. |
| Gradient (Vector Form) | $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \left[\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1}, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_n}\right]^T$ | An $n$-dimensional vector. |
| Mean Squared Error Loss | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression cost function. |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i\log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | Binary classification cost. |
| Convergence Criterion | $\vert \vert \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \vert \vert_2 \leq \epsilon$ | Stop when gradient norm is small. |
| Optimal Learning Rate (1D) | $\alpha^* = \frac{( \theta_t - \theta^* )^T \nabla^2 J (\theta_t - \theta^*)}{\vert \vert \nabla^2 J (\theta_t - \theta^*) \vert \vert^2}$ | Quadratic optimum (theoretical). |
| SGD Update (Single Sample) | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | High-variance, fast updates. |
| Mini-Batch Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathcal{B}_k)$ | $\mathcal{B}_k$ = batch of size $m$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** All absolute value bars within table cells have been written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. This is the KTU 2024 Scheme recommended notation style.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gradient Descent (and its variants) is the **optimization backbone** of virtually every modern AI system:
- **Computer Vision:** Training ResNet, YOLO, and Vision Transformers for object detection.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Fine-tuning BERT, GPT, and LLaMA models.
- **Healthcare:** Optimizing diagnostic models on medical imaging (CT, MRI).
- **Autonomous Systems:** Real-time policy optimization in self-driving cars.
- **Finance:** Fraud detection and algorithmic trading model training.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule (Taylor Series Approach)

We begin by approximating the cost function $J(\theta)$ around the current point $\theta_t$ using a **first-order Taylor expansion**:

$$J(\theta) \approx J(\theta_t) + \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)^T (\theta - \theta_t)$$

**Step 1 — Goal Setup:** We wish to find a small perturbation $\Delta\theta$ such that $J(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) < J(\theta_t)$, i.e., we descend in cost.

Substituting $\theta = \theta_t + \Delta\theta$ into the Taylor expansion yields:

$$J(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx J(\theta_t) + \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:** To minimize the right-hand side, we want the second term to be maximally negative. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, the dot product $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b}$ is minimized (most negative) when $\mathbf{b}$ is anti-parallel to $\mathbf{a}$.

Therefore, we set:

$$\Delta\theta = -\eta \, \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$$

where $\eta > 0$ is a small scalar. Substituting back:

$$J(\theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta J) \approx J(\theta_t) - \eta \vert \vert \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \vert \vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Renaming:** Replacing $\eta$ with the conventional symbol $\alpha$ (the learning rate), and noting that the *guaranteed descent* requires $\alpha$ to be small enough for the first-order approximation to remain valid, we obtain the canonical update:

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)}$$

This derivation rigorously justifies *why* the negative gradient direction produces the fastest local decrease.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Single-Parameter Descent on $J(\theta) = \theta^2$

**Problem Setup:** Minimize $J(\theta) = \theta^2$ starting from $\theta_0 = 5$ with $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient:**

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{d}{d\theta}(\theta^2) = 2\theta$$

**Step 2 — First Update ($t = 0 \to 1$):**

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot 2\theta_0 = 5 - (0.1)(2)(5) = 5 - 1.0 = 4.0$$

**Step 3 — Second Update ($t = 1 \to 2$):**

$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot 2\theta_1 = 4.0 - (0.1)(2)(4.0) = 4.0 - 0.8 = 3.2$$

**Step 4 — Third Update ($t = 2 \to 3$):**

$$\theta_3 = \theta_2 - \alpha \cdot 2\theta_2 = 3.2 - (0.1)(2)(3.2) = 3.2 - 0.64 = 2.56$$

**Step 5 — Observing Convergence:** Notice the geometric decay ratio:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t(1 - 2\alpha) = \theta_t(1 - 0.2) = 0.8 \cdot \theta_t$$

This is a **linear convergence** pattern characteristic of well-conditioned quadratic cost surfaces. The true minimum is $\theta^* = 0$, and the sequence $5 \to 4 \to 3.2 \to 2.56 \to \ldots$ approaches it asymptotically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Insight:** For a quadratic $J(\theta) = \theta^2$, the condition for stable convergence is $0 < \alpha < \frac{1}{L}$, where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of the gradient (here $L = 2$). Thus we need $\alpha < 0.5$. Our $\alpha = 0.1$ is safely in this range.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Behavior Under Different Learning Rates

| Scenario | $\alpha$ Range | Behavior | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Too Small | $0 < \alpha \ll 1/L$ | Slow convergence; stuck for long periods. | Increase $\alpha$ gradually; use learning rate schedulers. |
| Optimal | $\alpha \approx 1/L$ | Fast, stable convergence to minimum. | Monitor validation loss for early stopping. |
| Too Large | $\alpha \approx 1/L$ (border) | Oscillation around the minimum. | Reduce $\alpha$ by factor of 10. |
| Divergence | $\alpha > 2/L$ | Cost function explodes to infinity. | Restart with smaller $\alpha$; apply gradient clipping. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Gradient Descent Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Initialize Parameters theta_0]) --> nodeB[Forward Pass: Compute Predictions y_hat]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Cost Function J theta]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Convergence Check: gradient norm less than epsilon}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[Backward Pass: Backpropagation to compute gradient]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Update Parameters: theta := theta - alpha times gradient]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeG([END: Return Optimized theta_star])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Variants of Gradient Descent — Data Usage Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH[Full Dataset N samples] --> nodeI{Batch Size Selection}
    nodeI -- m = N --> nodeJ[Batch Gradient Descent]
    nodeI -- m = 1 --> nodeK[Stochastic Gradient Descent]
    nodeI -- 1 less than m less than N --> nodeL[Mini-Batch Gradient Descent]
    
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Smooth but slow convergence]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Noisy but fast updates]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Balanced: Industry standard for deep learning]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cost Surface Topology & Critical Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Cost Surface J theta over parameter space] --> nodeQ[Global Minimum: Lowest possible J]
    nodeP --> nodeR[Local Minimum: Trap for GD]
    nodeP --> nodeS[Saddle Point: Flat in some directions, curved in others]
    nodeP --> nodeT[Plateau Region: Near zero gradient, slow progress]
    
    nodeQ --> nodeU[IDEAL: GD converges here]
    nodeR --> nodeV[PROBLEM: GD gets stuck]
    nodeS --> nodeW[CHALLENGE: GD may stall]
    nodeT --> nodeX[CHALLENGE: GD crawls slowly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:** In high-dimensional neural network loss landscapes, *saddle points* are far more common than local minima. Modern variants like **Momentum**, **RMSProp**, and **Adam** are specifically designed to navigate these problematic regions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Gradient Descent. List its three main variants.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Gradient Descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to minimize a differentiable cost function $J(\theta)$ by updating parameters in the direction opposite to the gradient: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$.
The three main variants are:
1. **Batch Gradient Descent** (uses entire dataset per update).
2. **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** (uses one sample per update).
3. **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent** (uses a batch of $m$ samples per update; industry default).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3/3):** Award 1 mark for the correct definition with the formula, and 1 mark each for the three variants (partial credit: 0.5 per variant if examples are missing).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ in gradient descent. What happens if it is too small or too large?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The learning rate $\alpha$ is a hyperparameter that scales the magnitude of the parameter update at each iteration. It controls the **step size** taken in the direction of the negative gradient.
- If $\alpha$ is **too small**, convergence is excessively slow and the model may get stuck on plateaus, requiring many epochs to reach the minimum.
- If $\alpha$ is **too large**, the updates may overshoot the minimum, causing oscillation or even divergence (cost function grows unbounded).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3/3):** 1 mark for stating the role, 1 mark for the "too small" consequence, 1 mark for the "too large" consequence. Mentioning "divergence" earns full credit.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the parameter update rule of gradient descent using a first-order Taylor series expansion. Explain why the negative sign is essential in the update equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the cost function $J(\theta) = (\theta - 3)^2 + 5$. Starting from $\theta_0 = 0$ with learning rate $\alpha = 0.2$, perform **three iterations** of gradient descent and show the parameter trajectory. Discuss whether the algorithm converges. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Key points to include:
- First-order Taylor expansion: $J(\theta) \approx J(\theta_t) + \nabla J(\theta_t)^T(\theta - \theta_t)$. **(2 Marks)**
- Setting $\Delta\theta = -\alpha \nabla J$ to maximize the descent. **(2 Marks)**
- Derivation yielding $J(\theta_t - \alpha \nabla J) \approx J(\theta_t) - \alpha \vert \vert \nabla J \vert \vert^2$, proving guaranteed local decrease. **(2 Marks)**
- Explanation that the negative sign reverses the gradient (which points to steepest *ascent*) to produce steepest *descent*. **(1 Mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Gradient Computation:**

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = 2(\theta - 3)$$

**Iteration 1 ($t = 0 \to 1$):**

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot 2(\theta_0 - 3) = 0 - 0.2 \cdot 2(0 - 3) = 0 - 0.2(-6) = 0 + 1.2 = 1.2$$

**Iteration 2 ($t = 1 \to 2$):**

$$\theta_2 = 1.2 - 0.2 \cdot 2(1.2 - 3) = 1.2 - 0.2(-3.6) = 1.2 + 0.72 = 1.92$$

**Iteration 3 ($t = 2 \to 3$):**

$$\theta_3 = 1.92 - 0.2 \cdot 2(1.92 - 3) = 1.92 - 0.2(-2.16) = 1.92 + 0.432 = 2.352$$

**Convergence Analysis:** The true minimum is at $\theta^* = 3$ with $J(3) = 5$. The trajectory is $0 \to 1.2 \to 1.92 \to 2.352$, which is monotonically approaching 3 with decreasing step sizes. **The algorithm converges.** The decay ratio is $(1 - 2\alpha) = 0.6$ per iteration toward the optimum. **(2 Marks for convergence discussion)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare and contrast Batch, Stochastic, and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent. Tabulate the differences in terms of update frequency, computational cost, and convergence stability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a diagram why gradient descent can get stuck in **local minima** or **saddle points** in non-convex neural network loss landscapes. How do modern optimizers like **Momentum-based GD** address this issue? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Batch GD | Stochastic GD | Mini-Batch GD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data per Update | Entire dataset ($N$) | 1 sample | $m$ samples ($1 < m < N$) |
| Update Frequency | 1 per epoch | $N$ per epoch | $N/m$ per epoch |
| Computational Cost per Update | Very High | Very Low | Moderate |
| Convergence Stability | Smooth, stable | Highly oscillatory | Moderately stable |
| Memory Requirement | Very High | Negligible | Moderate |
| Implicit Regularization | None | High (noise helps) | Moderate |
| Industry Use | Rare (small datasets) | Online learning | **Default for deep learning** |

**(b) Local Minima & Saddle Points (7 Marks):**

In high-dimensional non-convex loss landscapes, gradient descent computes $\nabla J(\theta) = 0$ at multiple points:
- **Local Minima:** Gradient is zero but cost is higher than global minimum. GD halts prematurely.
- **Saddle Points:** Gradient is zero, point is a minimum in some dimensions and a maximum in others. Particularly prevalent in $n > 1000$ dimensional neural networks.

**Momentum-based GD** addresses this by adding a velocity term:

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$

The momentum coefficient $\beta \in [0, 1)$ (typically 0.9) accumulates historical gradients, allowing the optimizer to:
1. **Power through small local bumps** that would trap vanilla GD.
2. **Escape shallow saddle points** by maintaining velocity across flat regions.
3. **Accelerate convergence** in ravines by damping oscillations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Confusion (–1 mark):** Students frequently write $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha \nabla J$ (Gradient Ascent). Always double-check the negative sign.
> 2. **Confusing Cost & Loss (–1 mark):** "Loss" is per-sample; "Cost" is the aggregate (mean/sum over the dataset). Use them interchangeably only when clearly defined.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" of the Negative Sign (–1 mark):** A correct formula alone is insufficient. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires *reasoning* (mapped to Bloom's "Understand" level).
> 4. **Ignoring Learning Rate Stability (–1 mark):** Failing to discuss the $\alpha < 1/L$ condition in numerical problems loses the "Analyze" credit.
> 5. **Conflating "Epoch" and "Iteration" (–0.5 mark):** For BGD, 1 epoch = 1 iteration. For MBGD with $m$ samples, 1 epoch = $N/m$ iterations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Core Formula:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ — memorize verbatim.
- **The Three Variants:** Batch (full data), SGD (1 sample), Mini-Batch (industry default, $m \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$).
- **Why Negative Sign?** Gradient points to *steepest ascent*; we want *descent*.
- **Learning Rate $\alpha$:** Controls step size. Too small = slow; too large = divergence.
- **Convergence Condition (Quadratic):** $0 < \alpha < 2/L$, where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of the gradient.
- **Loss vs. Cost:** Loss = per-example error; Cost = aggregated (mean) over the dataset.
- **Backprop Role:** Computes $\nabla_\theta J$ efficiently using the chain rule; GD then *applies* the gradient.
- **Local Minima & Saddle Points:** Non-convex landscapes contain them; **Momentum**, **RMSProp**, and **Adam** help escape.
- **First-Order Method:** GD uses only the gradient (first derivative), not the Hessian (second derivative) — that would be **Newton's Method**.
- **Implicit Regularization of SGD:** The noise from single-sample updates often helps generalization, a phenomenon called the "SGD noise hypothesis."
- **Epoch vs. Iteration:** 1 Epoch = one full pass through $N$ training samples.
- **Hyperparameters to tune:** Learning rate $\alpha$, mini-batch size $m$, momentum $\beta$ — all are *not* learned by the model.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Gradient Descent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** is an iterative first-order optimization algorithm used to train deep learning models by minimizing a differentiable loss (objective) function. Unlike standard (Batch) Gradient Descent, which computes the gradient of the loss using the **entire training dataset** before performing a single parameter update, SGD approximates this true gradient by computing the gradient of the loss with respect to the model parameters using only a **single training example** (or a small mini-batch of examples) at each iteration.

Mathematically, the parameter update rule of SGD is given by:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

where:
- $\theta_t$ represents the model parameters (weights and biases) at iteration $t$
- $\eta$ is the **learning rate**, a hyperparameter that controls the step size
- $\nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ is the gradient of the loss function computed on a single sample $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ drawn uniformly at random from the training set
- $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ is a randomly sampled training example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SGD is the foundational optimization algorithm underpinning virtually all modern deep learning training procedures. Understanding its mechanics, convergence properties, and variants is **mandatory** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on top of a mountain in thick fog, and your goal is to reach the valley (the lowest point) at the bottom. You cannot see the entire terrain.

- **Batch Gradient Descent** is like having a *satellite map* of the entire mountain. At each step, you study the complete slope of the mountain and then take one carefully calculated step in the steepest downhill direction. The path is smooth, accurate, but **extremely slow** because calculating the full slope takes a long time.

- **Stochastic Gradient Descent** is like being blindfolded and feeling the **ground directly under your feet** to determine the slope. You take a step, then move to a new random spot, feel the slope there, and step again. Your path is **noisy and zig-zagging**, but each step is incredibly fast, and you typically reach the valley much sooner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The "stochastic" (random) nature of SGD introduces noise into each update, but this noise is actually beneficial — it allows the optimizer to **escape shallow local minima** and find better solutions, especially in complex, non-convex loss landscapes typical of deep neural networks.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Learning Rate ($\eta$)** | Step size hyperparameter; typically in $(0, 1)$ |
| **Epoch** | One complete pass through the entire training dataset |
| **Iteration** | One single update step (corresponds to processing one sample in pure SGD) |
| **Mini-batch** | A small subset (e.g., 32, 64, 128 samples) used to compute a single gradient estimate |
| **Loss Function $L(\theta)$** | The objective function being minimized |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Gradient Descent

In deep learning, the term "SGD" is often used broadly to include the mini-batch variant. KTU examiners expect students to clearly distinguish among the three paradigms:

**A. Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)**
Uses the entire dataset of $N$ samples to compute the gradient:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \right]$$
- Pros: Deterministic, smooth convergence, true gradient direction.
- Cons: **Very slow** for large $N$; cannot fit datasets in memory; gets stuck in local minima.

**B. Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) — Pure Form**
Uses a single randomly selected sample $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$ per update:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$
- Pros: Very fast updates; can escape local minima; enables online learning.
- Cons: High variance; noisy convergence path.

**C. Mini-Batch Gradient Descent**
Uses a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $m$ (where $1 < m < N$) per update:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \left[ \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \right]$$
- Pros: Best of both worlds — computational efficiency and reduced variance.
- Cons: Introduces one new hyperparameter (batch size $m$).

### 2.2 Why Does SGD Work? The Mathematical Justification

The theoretical foundation of SGD rests on the fact that the gradient computed on a single randomly drawn sample is an **unbiased estimator** of the true gradient computed over the full dataset.

**Proof of Unbiasedness:**
Let $g_i = \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ be the gradient of the $i$-th sample. The expected value of $g_i$ over the uniform distribution of samples is:

$$\mathbb{E}_i [g_i] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) = \nabla L(\theta)$$

Thus, on average, SGD moves in the same direction as Batch GD, even though individual steps are noisy.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| SGD Update Rule | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Core equation |
| Expected Gradient | $\mathbb{E}[\nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})] = \nabla L_{\text{full}}(\theta_t)$ | Unbiasedness property |
| BGD Update Rule | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Deterministic |
| Mini-batch Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | $m$ = batch size |
| Convergence Condition | $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro conditions |
| Effective Epochs | $N / m$ iterations per epoch | $m$ = batch size |
| Loss Decrease Bound | $\mathbb{E}[L(\theta_{t+1})] \leq L(\theta_t) - \frac{\eta}{2} \vert \vert \nabla L(\theta_t) \vert \vert^2 + \frac{\eta^2}{2} \cdot C$ | For convex $L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SGD and its variants (Momentum, RMSProp, Adam) are the **workhorses of modern AI** — from training convolutional neural networks for medical imaging to fine-tuning large language models. Mastery of these formulas is non-negotiable for KTU exam success.

### 2.4 Learning Rate Scheduling

In practice, a fixed learning rate is rarely used. Common strategies include:

1. **Step Decay:** Reduce $\eta$ by a factor every $k$ epochs.
2. **Exponential Decay:** $\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$
3. **1/t Decay:** $\eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + \lambda t)$

These schedules satisfy the **Robbins-Monro conditions**, which are theoretical guarantees for SGD convergence:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty$$

The first condition ensures the optimizer can reach any point; the second ensures the noise eventually vanishes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Empirical Risk Minimization to the SGD Update Rule

**Step 1 — Define the Empirical Loss:**
For a supervised learning problem with training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), \dots, (x^{(N)}, y^{(N)})\}$, the empirical risk (average loss) is:

$$L_{\text{emp}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)})$$

where $\ell$ is the per-sample loss (e.g., squared error, cross-entropy) and $f(x^{(i)}; \theta)$ is the model prediction.

**Step 2 — Gradient Descent Intuition:**
We want to find $\theta^*$ that minimizes $L_{\text{emp}}(\theta)$. Using a first-order Taylor expansion around $\theta_t$:

$$L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) + \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta$$

To decrease the loss, we need:

$$L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) < L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t)$$

**Step 3 — Choose Step Direction:**
The fastest decrease is in the direction of the negative gradient. Setting $\Delta\theta = -\eta \cdot \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t)$:

$$L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_{t+1}) \approx L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) - \eta \cdot \vert \vert \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) \vert \vert^2$$

Since $\eta > 0$ and the squared norm is non-negative, the loss strictly decreases (locally).

**Step 4 — Expand the Full Gradient:**

$$\nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_{\theta} \ell(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)})$$

**Step 5 — Stochastic Approximation:**
Replace the full sum with a single randomly chosen sample $i$:

$$\nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) \approx \nabla_{\theta} \ell(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)}) = \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

**Step 6 — Final SGD Update Rule:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Unbiasedness of the Stochastic Gradient

Starting from the definition of expectation over the uniform sampling distribution:

$$\mathbb{E}_i \left[ \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \right] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \cdot \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

$$\mathbb{E}_i \left[ \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

$$\mathbb{E}_i \left[ \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \right] = \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta)$$

This proves that the expected value of the stochastic gradient equals the true (full-batch) gradient. Hence, SGD is an **unbiased estimator** of the true gradient.

### 3.3 Derivation: Variance Reduction with Mini-Batches

For a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}$ of size $m$, the gradient estimate is:

$$g_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

Assuming the per-sample gradients are i.i.d. with variance $\sigma^2$, the variance of the mini-batch estimate is:

$$\text{Var}(g_{\mathcal{B}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{m}$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing the mini-batch size $m$ reduces the variance of the gradient estimate by a factor of $m$. This is why mini-batch GD is the practical sweet spot.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Convergence Behavior

| Aspect | Batch GD | SGD (Pure) | Mini-Batch GD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gradient Computation Cost per Update | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(m)$ |
| Updates per Epoch | $1$ | $N$ | $N/m$ |
| Gradient Variance | $0$ (deterministic) | High ($\sigma^2$) | Reduced ($\sigma^2/m$) |
| Convergence Path | Smooth | Noisy/Zig-zag | Mildly noisy |
| Memory Requirement | High (entire dataset) | Low (one sample) | Moderate |
| Ability to Escape Local Minima | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Best Use Case | Small datasets, convex problems | Online learning, non-convex | **Standard deep learning** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SGD Optimization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Parameters theta0] --> B[Set Hyperparameters: Learning Rate eta, Epochs E]
    B --> C[Shuffle Training Dataset]
    C --> D{For each epoch e = 1 to E}
    D --> E{For each mini-batch B in dataset}
    E --> F[Sample mini-batch B of size m]
    F --> G[Forward Pass: Compute Predictions y_hat]
    G --> H[Compute Loss L between y_hat and y]
    H --> I[Backward Pass: Compute Gradient grad L wrt theta]
    I --> J[Update Parameters: theta = theta - eta times grad L]
    J --> K{More mini-batches in epoch?}
    K -->|Yes| E
    K -->|No| L{More epochs?}
    L -->|Yes| D
    L -->|No| M[End: Return Trained Parameters theta]
    D -->|Done| M
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SGD Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE]
        T1[Training Dataset X, Y]
        T2[Hyperparameters eta, m, E]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION STAGE]
        C1[Sampler Module]
        C2[Model Forward Pass]
        C3[Loss Computation Block]
        C4[Gradient Engine Backprop]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE[PARAMETER UPDATE STAGE]
        U1[Apply SGD Rule]
        U2[Updated Weights theta]
    end

    subgraph MONITOR[MONITORING STAGE]
        M1[Loss Tracker]
        M2[Convergence Checker]
    end

    T1 --> C1
    T2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> C2
    C3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 -->|Stop if converged| ENDNODE[Return Final theta]
    M2 -->|Continue| C1
```

### 4.3 Loss Landscape Convergence Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Convergence Paths on Loss Landscape] --> B[Batch GD Path]
    A --> C[SGD Path]
    A --> D[Mini-batch Path]
    B --> B1[Starts at theta0, single smooth descent to minimum]
    C --> C1[Starts at theta0, zig-zag noisy descent, may escape local minima]
    D --> D1[Starts at theta0, moderately smooth descent with mild oscillation]
    B1 --> E[Global Minimum]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Stochastic Gradient Descent. Write its parameter update rule and explain the significance of the learning rate.**

**Model Answer:**
Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is an optimization algorithm that minimizes a loss function by iteratively updating model parameters using the gradient computed on a single (or small batch of) training example(s), rather than the entire dataset.

The update rule is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

The **learning rate $\eta$** is a critical hyperparameter that controls the step size of each parameter update. A learning rate that is too large causes the optimizer to overshoot the minimum and diverge, while a learning rate that is too small results in very slow convergence and may get stuck in poor local minima.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent in terms of computational cost, convergence, and noise.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Batch GD | SGD |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient Computation | Uses all $N$ samples ($O(N)$ cost) | Uses 1 sample ($O(1)$ cost) |
| Convergence | Smooth, deterministic | Noisy, oscillating path |
| Noise in Updates | None (zero variance) | High variance |
| Updates per Epoch | 1 | $N$ |
| Memory | High | Low |
| Suitability | Small datasets | Large-scale and online learning |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Stochastic Gradient Descent update rule starting from the empirical risk minimization objective. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the stochastic gradient is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient. Show how mini-batch gradient descent reduces the variance of the gradient estimate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the SGD Update Rule:**

*Step 1:* Define the empirical risk over $N$ training samples:

$$L_{\text{emp}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)})$$

*Step 2:* Apply first-order Taylor expansion around $\theta_t$:

$$L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_{t+1}) \approx L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) + \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t)^T \cdot (\theta_{t+1} - \theta_t)$$

*Step 3:* For the loss to decrease, the directional derivative must be negative. The optimal step direction is the negative gradient:

$$\theta_{t+1} - \theta_t = -\eta \cdot \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t)$$

*Step 4:* Expand the full gradient:

$$\nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_{\theta} \ell(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)})$$

*Step 5:* Replace the full sum with a single sample's gradient (stochastic approximation):

$$\nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta_t) \approx \nabla_{\theta} \ell(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)})$$

*Step 6:* The SGD update rule becomes:

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})}$$

*Assumption:* Sample $i$ is drawn uniformly at random from the training set, and the loss $L$ is differentiable with respect to $\theta$.

**(b) Unbiasedness Proof and Variance Reduction:**

*Unbiasedness:*
Taking the expectation of the stochastic gradient over the uniform sampling distribution:

$$\mathbb{E}_i \left[ \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \right] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

$$\mathbb{E}_i \left[ \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) = \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta)$$

This shows the stochastic gradient is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient.

*Variance Reduction with Mini-Batch:*
For a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}$ of size $m$, the estimate is:

$$g_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

If per-sample gradients are i.i.d. with variance $\sigma^2$:

$$\text{Var}(g_{\mathcal{B}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{m}$$

Thus, increasing the mini-batch size $m$ reduces variance by a factor of $m$, leading to smoother convergence.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Robbins-Monro conditions for SGD convergence. Discuss why a decaying learning rate is preferred over a fixed one. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Batch, Mini-batch, and Stochastic Gradient Descent. Describe two practical challenges in SGD and how they are addressed in modern optimizers like Adam or RMSProp. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Robbins-Monro Conditions and Decaying Learning Rate:**

The Robbins-Monro theorem (1951) establishes conditions on the learning rate sequence $\{\eta_t\}$ that guarantee almost sure convergence of SGD to a local (or global) minimum:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \quad \text{(Condition 1)}$$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty \quad \text{(Condition 2)}$$

*Interpretation:*
- **Condition 1** ensures the cumulative step size is large enough to reach the minimum from any starting point.
- **Condition 2** ensures the step size diminishes rapidly enough for the algorithm to converge (not oscillate forever).

A **fixed learning rate** violates Condition 2 (since $\sum \eta^2 = \infty$), meaning SGD will oscillate around the minimum indefinitely. A **decaying learning rate** (e.g., $\eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + \lambda t)$) satisfies both conditions, ensuring both progress and eventual stability.

**(b) Comparison and Modern Optimizer Discussion:**

*Comparison Table:*

| Aspect | Batch GD | Mini-Batch GD | Pure SGD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Samples per Update | $N$ | $m$ ($1 < m < N$) | 1 |
| Update Frequency | Low | High | Very High |
| Variance | 0 | $\sigma^2/m$ | $\sigma^2$ |
| Practical Use | Rare in deep learning | **Industry standard** | Online/streaming |

*Two Practical Challenges:*

1. **Choosing a global learning rate:** A single $\eta$ may not be ideal for all parameters. Modern optimizers like **Adam** and **RMSProp** use **adaptive per-parameter learning rates** computed from running estimates of the gradient moments, eliminating manual tuning.

2. **Saddle points and plateaus:** SGD slows drastically on saddle points common in deep network loss landscapes. **Momentum-based methods** (e.g., SGD with Momentum, Adam) accumulate a velocity vector from past gradients to push through saddle points efficiently.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "SGD" terminology:** In modern deep learning literature, "SGD" often implicitly means *mini-batch* GD. KTU examiners expect students to **explicitly state** which variant they are discussing.
> 2. **Missing the learning rate justification:** Many students write the update rule but forget to discuss the role of $\eta$. Marks are deducted for incomplete answers.
> 3. **Skipping the unbiasedness proof:** This is a **favorite KTU question** (asked in Dec 2023 and May 2024). Always derive $\mathbb{E}[\nabla L] = \nabla L_{\text{emp}}$ explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting Robbins-Monro conditions:** When asked about convergence, students often write vague answers. Always cite the two specific summation conditions.
> 5. **No mention of mini-batch variance reduction:** When comparing variants, the $\sigma^2/m$ relationship is a high-value formula that examiners specifically look for.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SGD Definition:** Optimization algorithm updating parameters using gradient of a single (or small batch of) sample(s).
- **Core Update Rule:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$
- **Three Variants:** Batch GD (full dataset), SGD (single sample), Mini-batch GD (subset of size $m$).
- **Unbiasedness:** $\mathbb{E}_i [\nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})] = \nabla L_{\text{emp}}(\theta)$ — the expected stochastic gradient equals the true gradient.
- **Variance Reduction:** Mini-batch of size $m$ reduces gradient variance to $\sigma^2 / m$.
- **Robbins-Monro Conditions:** $\sum \eta_t = \infty$ and $\sum \eta_t^2 < \infty$ — necessary and sufficient for SGD convergence.
- **Learning Rate:** Critical hyperparameter; too large causes divergence, too small causes slow convergence. Decaying schedules preferred.
- **Advantages of SGD:** Fast, low memory, escapes local minima, enables online learning.
- **Disadvantages of SGD:** Noisy convergence, sensitive to learning rate, may oscillate near minima.
- **Modern Extensions:** Momentum, Nesterov, AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam — all build upon the fundamental SGD update rule.
- **Hyperparameters to Remember:** Learning rate $\eta$, batch size $m$, number of epochs $E$, momentum $\beta$ (in advanced variants).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
