In the realistic case where base models have some correlation $\rho$ with one another, the formula generalizes to:

$$\text{Var}\left(\bar{\hat{f}}(x)\right) = \frac{1}{N}\sigma^2\left[\rho + \frac{1-\rho}{N}\right]$$

This shows that variance reduction is maximized when **diversity (low correlation) is high**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU ESE 2024 — every formula below is examinable.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions & Units | Applicable Task |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Ensemble Average (Regression) | $\hat{F}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{f}_i(x)$ | $N$ = number of base models (dimensionless) | Regression |
| 2 | Weighted Ensemble | $\hat{F}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \hat{f}_i(x),\; \sum w_i = 1$ | $w_i$ = weight of model $i$ (dimensionless) | Regression / Classification |
| 3 | Soft Voting (Classification) | $\hat{F}(x) = \arg\max_{c}\;\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i(c \mid x)$ | $P_i(c \mid x)$ = probability of class $c$ from model $i$ | Classification |
| 4 | Hard Voting (Classification) | $\hat{F}(x) = \text{mode}\{\hat{f}_1(x), \hat{f}_2(x), \ldots, \hat{f}_N(x)\}$ | `mode` = statistical mode of labels | Classification |
| 5 | Bias–Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | All terms in (error)² units | Theoretical Analysis |
| 6 | Variance of Independent Mean | $\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \sigma^2 / N$ | $\sigma^2$ = variance of single model | Variance Reduction Proof |
| 7 | Variance with Correlation | $\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}[\rho + \frac{1-\rho}{N}]$ | $\rho \in [0,1]$ = pairwise correlation | Realistic Ensembles |
| 8 | Condorcet Majority Bound | $P_{\text{majority}} = \sum_{k=\lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$ | $p$ = single-model accuracy | Majority Voting |
| 9 | Boosting Weighted Error | $\epsilon_t = \sum_{i:\,h_t(x_i)\neq y_i} w_i^{(t)}$ | $w_i^{(t)}$ = sample weight at round $t$ | AdaBoost |
| 10 | AdaBoost Final Classifier | $H(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$ | $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}$ | Boosting |
| 11 | Bagging Out-of-Bag Error | $\text{OOB} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_i^{\text{oob}} \neq y_i]$ | $n$ = dataset size | Bagging Validation |
| 12 | Stacking Meta-Feature | $z_i = [\hat{f}_1(x_i), \hat{f}_2(x_i), \ldots, \hat{f}_N(x_i)]^{\top}$ | $z_i \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ | Stacking |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Model ensembles are not merely academic curiosities — they are deployed in mission-critical engineering systems:

- **Medical Imaging:** Cancer detection systems in hospitals often ensemble 5–10 CNNs to reduce false negatives.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Self-driving cars ensemble object detectors, lane detectors, and depth estimators for redundancy.
- **Financial Forecasting:** Stock market prediction systems combine LSTMs, GRUs, and Transformers via weighted averaging.
- **Kaggle Competitions:** Almost every winning solution on Kaggle uses some form of ensembling — it is considered the single most reliable technique to push leaderboard scores higher.
- **Production ML Pipelines:** Google's, Meta's, and OpenAI's deployed models often use ensembles internally to balance accuracy and latency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Variance Reduction via Simple Averaging

**Problem Statement:** Given $N$ base models $\hat{f}_1(x), \hat{f}_2(x), \ldots, \hat{f}_N(x)$, each with prediction variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$ between any two distinct models, derive the variance of the averaged ensemble prediction $\bar{\hat{f}}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{f}_i(x)$.

**Step 1 — Setup the Variance of a Sum:**
The variance of a sum of $N$ random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N$ is:

$$\text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}(X_i) + 2\sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} \text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Ensemble Setup:**
Let each $X_i = \hat{f}_i(x)$. Then:

$$\text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{f}_i(x)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}(\hat{f}_i(x)) + 2\sum_{i<j} \text{Cov}(\hat{f}_i(x), \hat{f}_j(x))$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Correlation Structure:**
For $N$ identically distributed models with $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_i) = \sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$ (so $\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_i, \hat{f}_j) = \rho\sigma^2$ for $i \neq j$):

$$\text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{f}_i(x)\right) = N\sigma^2 + 2 \cdot \binom{N}{2} \cdot \rho\sigma^2$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the Combinatorial Term:**
Recall $\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$, so:

$$2 \cdot \binom{N}{2} \cdot \rho\sigma^2 = N(N-1)\rho\sigma^2$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{f}_i(x)\right) = N\sigma^2 + N(N-1)\rho\sigma^2 = N\sigma^2\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Scaling Factor $\frac{1}{N}$ for the Mean:**

$$\text{Var}\left(\bar{\hat{f}}(x)\right) = \frac{1}{N^2} \cdot N\sigma^2\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right] = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right]$$

**Step 6 — Reformat into the Standard Form:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Var}\left(\bar{\hat{f}}(x)\right) = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{N}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Special Case Analysis:**
- If $\rho = 0$ (independent models): $\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$ → variance shrinks as $1/N$.
- If $\rho = 1$ (perfectly correlated, i.e., identical models): $\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \sigma^2$ → **no benefit from ensembling** (a key insight for KTU).
- If $0 < \rho < 1$ (realistic case): variance is reduced, but the benefit is bounded below by $\rho\sigma^2$ as $N \to \infty$.

**Engineering Interpretation:** The lower the correlation $\rho$ between base models, the greater the benefit of ensembling. **This is why KTU-evaluated ensemble projects must use diverse training strategies** (different seeds, dropout masks, data augmentations) rather than training identical models.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Condorcet Jury Theorem for Majority Voting

**Problem Statement:** Derive the probability that a majority-vote ensemble of $N$ independent classifiers (each with accuracy $p > 0.5$) correctly classifies a sample.

**Step 1 — Setup the Binomial Structure:**
Each of the $N$ classifiers makes a correct prediction independently with probability $p$. The number of correct votes $K$ follows a Binomial distribution:

$$K \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$$

**Step 2 — Define the Ensemble Decision Rule:**
The ensemble predicts correctly if and only if at least $\lceil N/2 \rceil$ classifiers vote correctly. For odd $N$, this is strictly $> N/2$; for even $N$, ties may be broken arbitrarily (we assume ties favor the correct class with probability $0.5$ for simplicity, though KTU assumes no ties for clean derivations).

**Step 3 — Write the Cumulative Probability:**

$$P_{\text{ensemble correct}} = \sum_{k=\lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} P(K = k) = \sum_{k=\lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$$

**Step 4 — Special Case $N = 3$:**
Compute explicitly to build intuition:

$$P_{\text{correct}} = \binom{3}{2}p^2(1-p) + \binom{3}{3}p^3 = 3p^2(1-p) + p^3$$

$$= 3p^2 - 3p^3 + p^3 = 3p^2 - 2p^3$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Limit Behavior:**

$$\lim_{p \to 1} P_{\text{correct}} = 3(1) - 2(1) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\lim_{p \to 0.5} P_{\text{correct}} = 3(0.25) - 2(0.125) = 0.75 - 0.25 = 0.5 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Verification for KTU-type Question:**
Suppose $N = 5$, $p = 0.7$. Compute:

$$P_{\text{correct}} = \sum_{k=3}^{5} \binom{5}{k}(0.7)^k(0.3)^{5-k}$$

$$= \binom{5}{3}(0.7)^3(0.3)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.7)^4(0.3)^1 + \binom{5}{5}(0.7)^5$$

$$= 10(0.343)(0.09) + 5(0.2401)(0.3) + 1(0.16807)$$

$$= 10(0.03087) + 5(0.07203) + 0.16807$$

$$= 0.3087 + 0.36015 + 0.16807 = 0.83692$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{\text{ensemble correct}} \approx 0.837\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A single model with 70% accuracy, when ensembled with 4 other independent 70%-accurate models via majority vote, jumps to **83.7% accuracy**. This is the **Condorcet Jury Theorem** in action, and it is a frequently tested KTU result.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ensemble Error Upper Bound (Hansen–Salamon Result)

**Problem Statement:** Derive an upper bound for the expected error of an ensemble of $N$ neural networks with individual error rates $\epsilon_i$ and pairwise correlations $\rho_{ij}$.

**Step 1 — Setup:**
The mean squared error of the ensemble prediction $\bar{\hat{f}}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \hat{f}_i(x)$ can be bounded using the variance result above. The total expected error decomposes as:

$$E_{\text{ensemble}} = E\left[\left(y - \bar{\hat{f}}(x)\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bias–Variance Decomposition to the Ensemble:**

$$E_{\text{ensemble}} = \text{Bias}^2(\bar{\hat{f}}) + \text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}}$$

**Step 3 — For Identical Architectures Trained Differently:**
Assume all base models have the same bias $b$ (they share architecture) but different random initializations. Then:

$$\text{Bias}^2(\bar{\hat{f}}) = b^2 \quad \text{(unchanged from a single model)}$$

$$\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right]$$

**Step 4 — Final Bound:**

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{ensemble}} = b^2 + \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right] + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Single Model Error:**
A single model's expected error is $E_{\text{single}} = b^2 + \sigma^2 + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}}$.

The improvement from ensembling is:

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{single}} - E_{\text{ensemble}} = \sigma^2 - \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right] = \sigma^2 \cdot \frac{(N-1)(1-\rho)}{N}$$

**Step 6 — Asymptotic Behavior:**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \Delta E = \sigma^2(1 - \rho)$$

This confirms: **The maximum achievable reduction in error is $\sigma^2(1-\rho)$**, achieved only in the limit of infinite ensemble size, and it is bounded by how uncorrelated the base models are.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Ensemble Strategies at a Glance

| Strategy | Mechanism | Reduces Bias? | Reduces Variance? | Computational Cost | Best Use Case |
|----------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Bagging** | Bootstrap samples + averaging | No (slight) | **Yes (large)** | Medium (parallel) | High-variance models (deep NNs) |
| **Boosting (AdaBoost)** | Sequential reweighting | **Yes (large)** | Yes (small) | High (sequential) | High-bias models (shallow trees) |
| **Stacking** | Meta-learner on base outputs | Yes | Yes | High (two-stage) | Heterogeneous base models |
| **Snapshot Ensembles** | Cyclic LR + checkpoints | No | **Yes (medium)** | **Low (single training)** | Budget-constrained scenarios |
| **Voting (Hard/Soft)** | Mode/Mean of predictions | No | **Yes (medium)** | Low | Quick deployment |
| **Mixture of Experts** | Gated sub-networks | **Yes (large)** | Yes | High (gating network) | Massive-scale models |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Ensemble Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data X] --> B[Bootstrap Sampler 1]
    A --> C[Bootstrap Sampler 2]
    A --> D[Bootstrap Sampler 3]
    A --> E[Bootstrap Sampler N]
    B --> F1[Base Model M1]
    C --> F2[Base Model M2]
    D --> F3[Base Model M3]
    E --> FN[Base Model MN]
    F1 --> G[Aggregator: Voting or Averaging]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    FN --> G
    G --> H[Final Prediction Yhat]
    I[Diversity Source: Random Seeds Dropout Data Augmentation] --> F1
    I --> F2
    I --> F3
    I --> FN
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Soft Voting Ensemble for Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image x] --> B1[CNN Model 1]
    A --> B2[CNN Model 2]
    A --> B3[CNN Model 3]
    A --> B4[CNN Model 4]
    A --> B5[CNN Model 5]
    B1 --> C1[Prob Vector P1]
    B2 --> C2[Prob Vector P2]
    B3 --> C3[Prob Vector P3]
    B4 --> C4[Prob Vector P4]
    B5 --> C5[Prob Vector P5]
    C1 --> D[Soft Voting Aggregator]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Final Class Label]
    D --> F[Confidence Score]
```

## 4.3 Sequential Boosting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Equal Weights w1] --> B[Train Model M1]
    B --> C[Compute Weighted Error epsilon1]
    C --> D[Compute Alpha1 = 0.5 ln 1-eps1 over eps1]
    D --> E[Update Sample Weights w2]
    E --> F[Train Model M2]
    F --> G[Compute Weighted Error epsilon2]
    G --> H[Compute Alpha2 = 0.5 ln 1-eps2 over eps2]
    H --> I[Update Sample Weights w3]
    I --> J[Train Model MT]
    J --> K[Final Classifier Hx = sign Sum AlphaT hT x]
```

## 4.4 Stacking Architecture — Two-Stage Meta-Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data] --> B1[Base Learner L1]
    A --> B2[Base Learner L2]
    A --> B3[Base Learner L3]
    B1 --> C[Out-of-Fold Predictions]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Meta Features Matrix Z]
    D --> E[Meta Learner: Logistic Regression or XGBoost]
    E --> F[Stacked Final Prediction]
    G[Validation Set] --> F
```

## 4.5 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Which Ensemble Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need Higher Accuracy] --> B{Is Single Model Overfitting}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Bagging or Snapshot Ensembles]
    B -- No --> D{Is Single Model Underfitting}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Boosting]
    D -- No --> F{Are Base Models Heterogeneous}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Stacking]
    F -- No --> H{Need Fast Deployment}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Soft Voting with 3 to 5 Models]
    H -- No --> J[Use Weighted Ensemble with Optimized Weights]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Model Ensembles. List any two advantages of using ensembles in deep learning.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A Model Ensemble is a machine learning technique in which multiple base models (learners) are trained and their predictions are combined using an aggregation strategy (such as averaging, voting, or stacking) to produce a final prediction that is typically more accurate and robust than any individual model in the ensemble.

**Two Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Variance Reduction:** By averaging the predictions of multiple independently trained models, the variance of the final prediction is reduced, which mitigates overfitting. For $N$ independent models with variance $\sigma^2$, the ensemble variance drops to $\sigma^2/N$.

2. **Improved Generalization:** Ensembles leverage the *Wisdom of the Crowd* principle — the diversity among base models ensures that their individual errors tend to cancel out, leading to better performance on unseen data. This is critical in deep learning where single models often exhibit high variance due to stochastic training.

---

### **Question 2: What is the difference between Bagging and Boosting?**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bagging | Boosting |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **Training Style** | Parallel — models trained independently (1 mark) | Sequential — each model depends on previous (1 mark) |
| **Primary Goal** | Reduce **variance** | Reduce **bias** (0.5 marks) |
| **Data Sampling** | Bootstrap samples (with replacement) | Reweighted samples (focus on errors) (0.5 marks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: Complete Discussion on Model Ensembles**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **Bias–Variance decomposition** of model error. Using this decomposition, derive the variance of the ensemble average when $N$ base models with pairwise correlation $\rho$ are combined. Discuss how correlation $\rho$ affects ensemble performance.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Bias–Variance Decomposition (3 marks):**

For a model $\hat{f}(x)$ trained on dataset $\mathcal{D}$ with true target $y = f(x) + \epsilon$ (where $\epsilon$ is noise with $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$), the expected squared prediction error at a point $x$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\right)^2 + \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right] + \sigma^2$$

- The first term is **Bias²**: how far the average prediction is from the truth.
- The second term is **Variance**: how much predictions fluctuate across datasets.
- The third term is **irreducible noise** $\sigma^2$.

**Derivation of Ensemble Variance (3 marks):**

Let $\bar{\hat{f}}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\hat{f}_i(x)$ with $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_i) = \sigma^2$ and $\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_i, \hat{f}_j) = \rho\sigma^2$ for $i \neq j$.

$$\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \frac{1}{N^2}\left[N\sigma^2 + N(N-1)\rho\sigma^2\right] = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{N}$$

**Effect of Correlation (1 mark):**
- $\rho = 0$: Maximum benefit, variance $= \sigma^2/N$.
- $\rho = 1$: No benefit, variance $= \sigma^2$ (models are identical).
- $0 < \rho < 1$: Diminishing returns; asymptotic floor at $\rho\sigma^2$.

Hence, **diversity** (low $\rho$) is essential for ensemble effectiveness.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Condorcet Jury Theorem**. Compute the probability that a 5-model majority-vote ensemble (each model with 70% accuracy, independent predictions) correctly classifies a sample. Comment on the result.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Theorem Statement (2 marks):** The Condorcet Jury Theorem states that if a group of $N$ independent voters (or classifiers) each make a correct decision with probability $p > 0.5$, then the probability that the **majority vote** of the group is correct approaches **1** as $N \to \infty$. Conversely, if $p < 0.5$, the majority vote becomes arbitrarily wrong as $N$ grows.

**Setup (1 mark):** Let $K$ = number of correct votes among $N$ models. Then $K \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$. The ensemble is correct if $K \ge \lceil N/2 \rceil$.

**Computation for $N = 5$, $p = 0.7$ (3 marks):**

$$P_{\text{correct}} = \sum_{k=3}^{5}\binom{5}{k}(0.7)^k(0.3)^{5-k}$$

$$= \binom{5}{3}(0.7)^3(0.3)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.7)^4(0.3)^1 + \binom{5}{5}(0.7)^5(0.3)^0$$

$$= 10(0.343)(0.09) + 5(0.2401)(0.3) + 1(0.16807)(1)$$

$$= 10(0.03087) + 5(0.07203) + 0.16807$$

$$= 0.3087 + 0.36015 + 0.16807 = 0.83692$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{ensemble correct}} \approx 0.837 = 83.7\%}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** A single model with 70% accuracy, when ensembled with 4 other independent 70%-accurate models, jumps to **83.7% accuracy** — a **13.7 percentage-point gain** with no change in underlying model quality. This demonstrates the immense practical value of ensembling and is a key reason why production-grade deep learning systems virtually always use ensembles.

---

### **Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question (Ensemble Strategies & Stacking)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare and contrast the three principal ensemble strategies: **Bagging, Boosting, and Stacking**. For each, describe the training mechanism, the data sampling approach, and a representative algorithm.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) — 2.5 marks:**
- *Mechanism:* Trains $N$ base models **in parallel** on $N$ different bootstrap samples (drawn with replacement from the training set).
- *Sampling:* Each model sees ~63.2% unique samples; the remaining ~36.8% forms the Out-of-Bag (OOB) validation set.
- *Aggregation:* Predictions are combined via averaging (regression) or majority voting (classification).
- *Representative Algorithm:* **Random Forest** (bagging applied to decision trees with random feature subsets).
- *Strength:* Excellent for reducing variance in unstable, high-variance base learners.

**2. Boosting — 2.5 marks:**
- *Mechanism:* Trains base models **sequentially**. Each new model focuses on the samples that previous models misclassified, with sample weights updated after each round.
- *Sampling:* Weighted resampling or reweighting — no bootstrap.
- *Aggregation:* Weighted vote, where each model's weight $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}$ depends on its accuracy.
- *Representative Algorithm:* **AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, LightGBM**.
- *Strength:* Excellent for reducing bias; turns weak learners into a strong learner.

**3. Stacking (Stacked Generalization) — 2 marks:**
- *Mechanism:* Two-stage learning. Level-0 base learners generate predictions; a Level-1 **meta-learner** (e.g., logistic regression) is trained on these predictions (often using out-of-fold predictions to avoid leakage).
- *Sampling:* Standard k-fold cross-validation is used to generate meta-features without data leakage.
- *Aggregation:* The meta-learner optimally combines base predictions, learning nonlinear or weighted combinations automatically.
- *Representative Algorithm:* **Stacked Generalization (Wolpert, 1992)**, often implemented with diverse base models (SVM + RF + NN).
- *Strength:* Captures complementary strengths of heterogeneous base models.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A deep learning engineer trains 5 ResNet-18 classifiers on the CIFAR-10 dataset, each with a different random seed and dropout mask. The individual test accuracies are: 91.2%, 90.8%, 91.5%, 91.0%, 91.3%. Compute the soft-voting ensemble accuracy assuming the ensemble is **calibrated** and the joint errors are negligible. If the engineer then uses **weighted averaging** with weights proportional to individual accuracies, derive the new weights and the weighted ensemble prediction formula.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Average Accuracy (1 mark):**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{91.2 + 90.8 + 91.5 + 91.0 + 91.3}{5} = \frac{455.8}{5} = 91.16\%$$

The soft-voting ensemble accuracy, assuming calibrated probabilities and negligible joint errors, is approximately **91.16%** with a small upward bias due to error cancellation (the true value is typically 0.5–1.5% higher, around 91.5–92.5%).

**Step 2 — Deriving Weighted Ensemble Weights (3 marks):**

Let the individual accuracies be $a_1 = 0.912$, $a_2 = 0.908$, $a_3 = 0.915$, $a_4 = 0.910$, $a_5 = 0.913$.

Weights are proportional to accuracy: $w_i \propto a_i$. To normalize:

$$w_i = \frac{a_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{5} a_j}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sum of Accuracies (1 mark):**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{5} a_j = 0.912 + 0.908 + 0.915 + 0.910 + 0.913 = 4.558$$

**Step 4 — Individual Weights (1.5 marks):**

$$w_1 = \frac{0.912}{4.558} = 0.2001, \quad w_2 = \frac{0.908}{4.558} = 0.1992$$

$$w_3 = \frac{0.915}{4.558} = 0.2008, \quad w_4 = \frac{0.910}{4.558} = 0.1996$$

$$w_5 = \frac{0.913}{4.558} = 0.2003$$

Verification: $\sum w_i = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 5 — Weighted Prediction Formula (0.5 mark):**

$$\hat{F}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \hat{f}_i(x) = 0.2001\,\hat{f}_1(x) + 0.1992\,\hat{f}_2(x) + 0.2008\,\hat{f}_3(x) + 0.1996\,\hat{f}_4(x) + 0.2003\,\hat{f}_5(x)$$

**Engineering Insight:** When accuracies are close (within 1% of each other), weighted averaging yields nearly identical results to simple averaging. Weighted schemes become substantially more useful when base models have **significantly different accuracies** (e.g., 85% vs. 95%).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Writing $\rho = 0$ for "real-world" ensembles without justification** (–2 marks). Real neural networks trained on the same data are *never* fully independent. State explicitly: "Assuming approximate independence for simplicity...".
>
> 2. **Confusing soft voting with hard voting** (–1 mark). Soft voting averages *probabilities*; hard voting counts *labels*. These give different numerical results.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to normalize weights in weighted averaging** (–2 marks). KTU examiners specifically check that $\sum w_i = 1$. Always verify this constraint.
>
> 4. **Treating Bagging and Boosting as identical** (–1 mark). Bagging is *parallel, variance-reducing*; Boosting is *sequential, bias-reducing*. This distinction is a **favorite KTU question**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Condorcet theorem derivation** (–3 marks). Even if a question does not explicitly ask for the theorem, applying it to compute ensemble accuracy is considered a *high-value step* in 14-mark answers.
>
> 6. **Stacking "uses the same training data for meta-learner as for base learners"** (–2 marks). Always mention **out-of-fold predictions** to prevent data leakage. This is a classic trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE 2024:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Ensemble = multiple base models + aggregation strategy.
- ✅ **Primary Benefit:** Variance reduction (not bias reduction, generally).
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $\text{Var}(\bar{\hat{f}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left[1 + (N-1)\rho\right]$ — memorize this.
- ✅ **Special Cases:** $\rho = 0$ → $\sigma^2/N$; $\rho = 1$ → $\sigma^2$.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Bagging (variance ↓, parallel), Boosting (bias ↓, sequential), Stacking (meta-learning, two-stage).
- ✅ **Condorcet Jury Theorem:** Majority vote of $N$ models with $p > 0.5$ accuracy → ensemble accuracy → 1 as $N \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Diversity is King:** Use different seeds, dropout, augmentations, architectures to ensure low $\rho$.
- ✅ **Voting Variants:** Hard voting = mode of labels; Soft voting = average of probabilities (soft is usually better).
- ✅ **Stacking Requirement:** Use **out-of-fold predictions** to avoid data leakage in meta-learner training.
- ✅ **AdaBoost Weight:** $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}$ — increases weight of accurate models.
- ✅ **Bagging Validation:** OOB error provides a free validation estimate without a held-out set.
- ✅ **Snapshot Ensembles:** Cyclic learning rate + checkpointing = ensemble from single training run (cost-efficient).
- ✅ **Practical Limit:** Beyond $N \approx 10$ base models, returns diminish. KTU projects typically use $N = 5$ to $N = 10$.
- ✅ **Computational Cost:** Ensembles multiply inference cost by $N$ — consider distillation to compress.
- ✅ **Knowledge Distillation Connection:** A single student model trained on ensemble predictions often matches ensemble accuracy at a fraction of the cost (Hinton et al.).
- ✅ **Real-world Deployment:** Medical imaging, autonomous driving, financial forecasting, and competitive ML all use ensembles as a standard tool.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Deep Learning: Deep Feedforward Networks and Training Deep Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Deep Learning: Deep Feedforward Networks and Training Deep Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Deep Feedforward Network?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Definition (Goodfellow et al., Deep Learning, 2016):**
> A **Deep Feedforward Network** (also called a *Multilayer Perceptron / MLP* or *Deep Neural Network / DNN*) is a parametric mathematical model that approximates a target function $f^{\star}(\mathbf{x})$ by learning a mapping $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ through a composition of $L$ successive linear and non-linear transformations, called *layers*, where information flows strictly in one direction—from the input layer through zero or more **hidden layers** to the output layer—without any feedback connections or internal memory.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Feedforward** | No cycles; output of a layer feeds only into the next layer. |
| **Deep** | Contains **two or more hidden layers** (depth $L \geq 2$ in hidden units). |
| **Network** | A chain of differentiable, parameterized functions. |

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) 🧠

Imagine you are a bank manager deciding whether to approve a loan. You do not use a single rule like "income > ₹50,000 → approve." Instead, you use a **chain of reasoning**:

1. Layer 1 checks raw signals (income, age, credit history).
2. Layer 2 combines these into higher-level features (repayment capacity, risk profile).
3. Layer 3 makes a final yes/no decision.

Each layer refines the *representation* of the input into something more useful for the final task. This is exactly what a **deep feedforward network** does—each layer is a *representation learner*, and depth allows the construction of **increasingly abstract features**. The network is *trained* by showing it many examples of past loan decisions and adjusting internal weights until its answers match reality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Deep" matters:** According to the **Universal Approximation Theorem**, even a single hidden layer *can* approximate any continuous function, but a *deep* network can do so with **exponentially fewer parameters** by exploiting the hierarchical composition of features (Bengio et al., 2007; Goodfellow, Ch. 6).

---

## 1.2 What does "Training a Deep Model" Mean?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Definition:**
> **Training a Deep Model** is the iterative optimization process of finding the optimal set of parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}, \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L}$ of a deep network that minimize a *loss function* $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ computed over a training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$, typically via **gradient-based optimization** (e.g., Stochastic Gradient Descent) and the **backpropagation algorithm** for efficient gradient computation.

### The Big Picture (Plain English)

Training = **learning from mistakes**.

1. The network makes a **prediction** $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ for an input $\mathbf{x}$ (the *forward pass*).
2. We measure how wrong it is using a **loss function** (the "mistake meter").
3. We compute **how much each weight contributed to the mistake** (the *backward pass* / backpropagation).
4. We **nudge each weight** slightly in the direction that reduces the mistake.
5. Repeat millions of times until the network is good enough.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three pillars of training deep models** (KTP 2024 Module 2 high-yield):
> 1. **Loss function** — defines the objective to minimize.
> 2. **Backpropagation** — efficiently computes gradients via the chain rule.
> 3. **Optimization algorithm** — uses gradients to update parameters (SGD, Adam, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Architecture of a Deep Feedforward Network

Let the network have an input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_0}$, $L$ hidden layers, and an output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{L+1}}$. Each layer $l$ produces a **pre-activation** $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$ and an **activation** $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ (also called $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$ in some texts).

### Forward Propagation Equations (Layer-wise)

**Step 1 — Linear transformation (affine map):**

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}
$$

- $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$ — weight matrix of layer $l$
- $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ — bias vector of layer $l$
- $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ (input acts as the activation of the "zeroth" layer)

**Step 2 — Non-linear activation:**

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(l)}\right)
$$

where $g^{(l)}$ is the element-wise **activation function** of layer $l$.

**Step 3 — Output layer:**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = g^{(L+1)}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(L+1)}\right)
$$

The whole network is a **function composition**:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = g^{(L+1)} \circ \mathbf{W}^{(L+1)} \, g^{(L)} \circ \mathbf{W}^{(L)} \cdots g^{(1)} \circ \mathbf{W}^{(1)}\, (\mathbf{x})
$$

---

## 2.2 Activation Functions (Hidden Units)

| Activation | Formula $g(z)$ | Derivative $g'(z)$ | Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $g(z)(1-g(z))$ | $(0,1)$ | Saturates; suffers vanishing gradient. |
| **Tanh** | $\dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $1-g(z)^{2}$ | $(-1,1)$ | Zero-centered; still saturates. |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0,z)$ | $\begin{cases}1 & z>0\\0 & z\le 0\end{cases}$ | $[0,\infty)$ | Default for modern nets; fast, no saturation for $z>0$. |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z)$ | $\begin{cases}1 & z>0\\ \alpha & z\le 0\end{cases}$ | $(-\infty,\infty)$ | Fixes "dying ReLU" with small slope $\alpha$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Favorite:** ReLU is preferred in deep networks because its gradient is exactly $1$ for $z>0$, which prevents the **vanishing gradient problem** in deep architectures.

---

## 2.3 Output Units and Associated Loss Functions

| Task Type | Output Activation $g$ | Loss Function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear regression** (continuous $\mathbf{y}$) | Identity: $g(z)=z$ | Mean Squared Error: $\frac{1}{m}\sum ( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} )^2$ |
| **Binary classification** | Sigmoid | Binary Cross-Entropy: $-\frac{1}{m}\sum [y\log\hat{y}+(1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ |
| **Multi-class classification** | Softmax | Categorical Cross-Entropy: $-\frac{1}{m}\sum \sum_{k} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ |

> Where $m$ is the number of training samples.

---

## 2.4 The Backpropagation Algorithm (Why it Works)

Backpropagation is simply the **chain rule of calculus** applied recursively from the output layer back to the input. It efficiently computes $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$ for every layer in **one backward sweep**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Output error signal** | $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L+1)} = \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} J \odot g'^{(L+1)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L+1)})$ | Gradient w.r.t. last pre-activation. |
| 2 | **Hidden error signal (layer $l$)** | $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \left(\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)\top} \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}\right) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | Recursively propagates error backward. |
| 3 | **Gradient w.r.t. weights** | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \, \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)\top}$ | Outer product of error and previous activation. |
| 4 | **Gradient w.r.t. biases** | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$ | Just the error signal. |
| 5 | **SGD Update Rule** | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Move weights opposite to gradient; $\eta$ = learning rate. |
| 6 | **Momentum Update** | $\mathbf{v}_t = \beta \mathbf{v}_{t-1} + \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J;\quad \boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \mathbf{v}_t$ | Smooths updates; $\beta \approx 0.9$. |
| 7 | **MSE Loss** | $J_{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^{2}$ | Used for regression. |
| 8 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $J_{CE} = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{k} y_k^{(i)} \log \hat{y}_k^{(i)}$ | Used for classification. |
| 9 | **L2 Regularization** | $J_{reg} = J + \dfrac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{l}\Vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \Vert_{F}^{2}$ | Penalizes large weights; prevents overfitting. |
| 10 | **Dropout (inverted)** | During training: keep unit with prob $p$; scale activations by $1/p$ at test time. | Stochastic regularization. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\odot$ (Hadamard / element-wise product)?** Because the derivative of an element-wise non-linearity acts independently on each neuron, so we multiply component-wise.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Network Used | Engineering Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Predictive Maintenance** | MLP / DNN | Predicts RUL (Remaining Useful Life) of turbines, motors. |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Deep MLP on tabular data | Detects diabetes, heart disease from EHR features. |
| **Financial Fraud Detection** | Deep classifier | Flags anomalous credit-card transactions. |
| **Process Control** | MLP as a soft sensor | Estimates unmeasurable variables in chemical plants. |
| **Speech & Image Pre-networks** | MLP as front-end encoder | Pre-2020 ASR and vision pipelines. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No jumps, no shortcuts. This is the derivation a KTU board examiner expects for full marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Pass for a 2-Layer Network

Consider a network with **one hidden layer** for regression with linear output. Let:

- Input: $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$
- Hidden layer size: $n_1 = 2$
- Output: $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}$ (scalar regression)

**Step 1 — Hidden layer linear step:**

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(1)} & w_{12}^{(1)} \\ w_{21}^{(1)} & w_{22}^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1^{(1)} \\ b_2^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{12}^{(1)} x_2 + b_1^{(1)} \\ w_{21}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{22}^{(1)} x_2 + b_2^{(1)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Hidden layer non-linear activation (tanh):**

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \tanh(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \tanh(z_1^{(1)}) \\ \tanh(z_2^{(1)}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Output layer (linear):**

$$
\hat{y} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = w_1^{(2)} h_1^{(1)} + w_2^{(2)} h_2^{(1)} + b^{(2)}
$$

This is the full forward pass for one input sample.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backpropagation for the 2-Layer Network Above

Loss function (Mean Squared Error for a single sample):

$$
J = \tfrac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^{2}
$$

### (a) Gradient w.r.t. Output Layer Weights $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$

**Step 1 — Derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\hat{y}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{y}} = (\hat{y} - y)
$$

**Step 2 — Derivative of $\hat{y}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{z}^{(2)}$** (linear output, so $g^{(2)}(z) = z$, $g'(z) = 1$):

$$
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(2)}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Error signal at output:**

$$
\delta^{(2)} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot g'^{(2)}(\mathbf{z}^{(2)}) = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot 1 = (\hat{y} - y)
$$

**Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$ using chain rule:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot \mathbf{h}^{(1)\top} = (\hat{y} - y) \begin{bmatrix} h_1^{(1)} & h_2^{(1)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $b^{(2)}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = (\hat{y} - y)
$$

### (b) Gradient w.r.t. Hidden Layer Weights $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$

**Step 1 — Derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(1)}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)\top} \cdot \delta^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_1^{(2)} \\ w_2^{(2)} \end{bmatrix} (\hat{y} - y)
$$

**Step 2 — Derivative of $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{z}^{(1)}$** (tanh, element-wise):

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}} = 1 - \mathbf{h}^{(1)} \odot \mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - (h_1^{(1)})^2 \\ 1 - (h_2^{(1)})^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Hidden error signal:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} = \left(\mathbf{W}^{(2)\top} \delta^{(2)}\right) \odot g'^{(1)}(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})
$$

Expanding element-wise:

$$
\delta_1^{(1)} = w_1^{(2)} (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \bigl(1 - (h_1^{(1)})^2\bigr)
$$

$$
\delta_2^{(1)} = w_2^{(2)} (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \bigl(1 - (h_2^{(1)})^2\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} \, \mathbf{x}^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta_1^{(1)} \\ \delta_2^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta_1^{(1)} x_1 & \delta_1^{(1)} x_2 \\ \delta_2^{(1)} x_1 & \delta_2^{(1)} x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{b}^{(1)}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(1)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta_1^{(1)} \\ \delta_2^{(1)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### (c) SGD Parameter Update (with learning rate $\eta$)

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(1)} - \eta \, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}
$$

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(2)} - \eta \, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}}
$$

$$
\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{(1)} - \eta \, \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)}
$$

$$
b^{(2)} \leftarrow b^{(2)} - \eta \, (\hat{y} - y)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The backward pass reuses computations from the forward pass (e.g., $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$, $g'^{(1)}$). This *reuse* is what makes backpropagation **computationally efficient** — its cost is roughly the same as the forward pass (a factor of ~2×). This is the *back-propagation of errors* paradigm from Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vanishing Gradient in Sigmoid Networks (Why ReLU Wins)

For a sigmoid unit, the derivative is bounded:

$$
0 \leq g'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \leq \tfrac{1}{4}
$$

For a network of depth $L$, the magnitude of the gradient w.r.t. an early-layer weight involves a product of $L$ such derivatives:

$$
\left\Vert \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \right\Vert \propto \prod_{l=1}^{L} \left\Vert \mathbf{W}^{(l+1)\top} \right\Vert \cdot \prod_{l=1}^{L} \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \prod_{l=1}^{L} \tfrac{\Vert \mathbf{W}^{(l+1)} \Vert}{4}
$$

If $\Vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \Vert < 4$ (a very common case after initialization), then each factor is $< 1$, and the product **decays exponentially** with $L$ → **vanishing gradient**. For ReLU, $g'(z) = 1$ for $z>0$, so the product no longer shrinks. Hence ReLU enables training of very deep networks (He et al., 2015).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls in Training

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Standard Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Training loss plateaus high | Learning rate too small or activation saturation | Increase $\eta$; switch to ReLU. |
| Training loss oscillates / diverges | Learning rate too large | Decrease $\eta$; use gradient clipping. |
| Train loss $\downarrow$ but val loss $\uparrow$ | Overfitting | Add dropout, L2 regularization, early stopping. |
| All hidden units stuck at 0 | Dying ReLU (large negative bias) | Use Leaky ReLU / PReLU / lower learning rate. |
| Loss = NaN | Exploding gradient | Apply **gradient clipping**: $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \mathbf{g} \cdot \min(1, \frac{c}{\Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert})$. |
| Slow convergence on deep nets | Poor initialization | Use **Xavier (Glorot)** or **He initialization**. |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Deep Feedforward Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["INPUT LAYER\nx1 x2 x3 x4"] --> H1["HIDDEN LAYER 1\n5 neurons + ReLU"]
    H1 --> H2["HIDDEN LAYER 2\n4 neurons + ReLU"]
    H2 --> H3["HIDDEN LAYER 3\n3 neurons + ReLU"]
    H3 --> O["OUTPUT LAYER\n2 neurons + Softmax"]
    O --> P["PREDICTION\ny-hat class A or B"]
```

> *Caption:* A 4→5→4→3→2 deep MLP. Each arrow is a fully connected weight matrix $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Training Data D\nx_i, y_i"] --> B["FORWARD PASS\nCompute z and h\nlayer by layer"]
    B --> C["OUTPUT PREDICTION\ny-hat = f of x and theta"]
    C --> D["LOSS FUNCTION\nJ of theta"]
    D --> E["BACKWARD PASS\nCompute delta via chain rule"]
    E --> F["GRADIENT W of J\nw.r.t. all weights"]
    F --> G{"Gradient > 0?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["PARAMETER UPDATE\ntheta minus eta * grad"]
    H --> I{"Epochs done?"}
    I -- No --> B
    I -- Yes --> J["TRAINED MODEL\nf of x and theta*"]
    G -- No --> J
```

> *Caption:* The end-to-end training loop. Note the **re-use of intermediate activations** $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ cached during the forward pass — this is what makes backprop efficient.

---

## 4.3 Schematic of Information Flow in a Single Neuron

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1["x1"] --> W1["w1"]
    X2["x2"] --> W2["w2"]
    X3["x3"] --> W3["w3"]
    W1 --> S["SUMMATION\nz = sum w_i x_i + b"]
    W2 --> S
    W3 --> S
    S --> G["ACTIVATION\ng of z"]
    G --> OUT["h = g of z"]
```

> *Caption:* A single artificial neuron — the building block of deep feedforward networks. Biological inspiration: McCulloch–Pitts (1943), Rosenblatt (1958).

---

## 4.4 Gradient Flow Comparison: Sigmoid vs. ReLU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIG["SIGMOID NETWORK — VANISHING GRADIENT"]
        direction LR
        S1["Layer 1\ngrad 1.0"] --> S2["Layer 2\ngrad 0.25"] --> S3["Layer 3\ngrad 0.0625"] --> S4["Layer 4\ngrad 0.0156"]
    end
    subgraph REL["RELU NETWORK — HEALTHY GRADIENT"]
        direction LR
        R1["Layer 1\ngrad 1.0"] --> R2["Layer 2\ngrad 1.0"] --> R3["Layer 3\ngrad 1.0"] --> R4["Layer 4\ngrad 1.0"]
    end
```

> *Caption:* With sigmoid, gradient decays as $(1/4)^{L}$; with ReLU (for $z>0$), it stays constant. This is *the* fundamental reason ReLU enabled very deep networks post-2010.
