# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "deep" with "wide":** A wide network has *many neurons per layer*; a deep network has *many layers*. KTU questions often test this distinction. (–1 mark if mixed up.)
> 2. **Forgetting activation in backprop:** Many students write $\delta^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l+1)\top}\delta^{(l+1)}$ **without** the $\odot g'(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ term. (–2 marks — partial credit only.)
> 3. **Confusing MSE with cross-entropy:** Regression ↔ MSE; Classification ↔ Cross-Entropy. Mixing them up costs full marks on output unit sub-parts.
> 4. **Writing $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}\mathbf{h}^{(l)\top}$ without explaining dimensions:** Always state *outer product* and verify $\delta^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$, $\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{l-1}}$, so the result is $n_l \times n_{l-1}$ as required.
> 5. **Skipping forward-pass caching:** In long-derivation questions, the examiner will check that you explicitly say *which* values from the forward pass are reused. Mention $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$ and $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ when computing $\delta^{(l)}$.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 2]**
*Define a deep feedforward network. How does depth (more layers) differ from width (more neurons per layer) in terms of representational power?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A deep feedforward network is a multilayer model of the form $f(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta}) = g^{(L+1)}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(L+1)}g^{(L)}(\cdots g^{(1)}(\mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}^{(1)})\cdots)\right)$ that maps an input $\mathbf{x}$ to an output $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ with information flowing in one direction only. **Width** refers to the number of neurons in a single layer; **depth** refers to the number of layers. Theoretically, both are capable of universal approximation, but depth provides an *exponential advantage* in representational efficiency for hierarchical functions (functions that can be decomposed into compositions of simpler functions), because each layer can build progressively more abstract features from the previous layer's outputs.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 2]**
*State the Universal Approximation Theorem and mention one limitation of its single-hidden-layer form.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Universal Approximation Theorem** (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik, 1991) states that a feedforward network with a single hidden layer containing a *finite* number of neurons, with any non-linear "squashing" activation (e.g., sigmoid, tanh), can approximate *any continuous function on a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$* to arbitrary accuracy, given a sufficient (possibly exponential) number of hidden units. **Limitation:** The theorem only guarantees *existence* of such a network; it does not say the network can be *trained* by gradient descent, nor does it bound the number of hidden units (which may grow exponentially with input dimension). Deeper architectures achieve similar representational power with far fewer parameters.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — KTU Internal Choice

> *Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both are 14 marks total, with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Forward + Backward Pass Derivation**

**(a)** Derive the forward pass equations for a deep feedforward network with input dimension $n_0$, $L$ hidden layers of sizes $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_L$, and output dimension $n_{L+1}$. Clearly define all symbols. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the chain rule of calculus, derive the backpropagation equations to compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$ and $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}}$ for every layer $l$. State explicitly how the error signal $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$ is computed at the output and hidden layers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward Pass (7 marks):**

Define the input as the activation of the zeroth layer: $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_0}$.

For each layer $l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$ (hidden layers):

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Affine transformation:**

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}
$$

where $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Non-linear activation:**

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Output layer (final layer $L+1$):**

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(L+1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(L+1)} \mathbf{h}^{(L)} + \mathbf{b}^{(L+1)}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = g^{(L+1)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L+1)}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{L+1}}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compact form:**

$$
f(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta}) = g^{(L+1)}\circ \mathbf{W}^{(L+1)} \circ g^{(L)} \circ \mathbf{W}^{(L)} \cdots g^{(1)} \circ \mathbf{W}^{(1)} (\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Symbol table:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}$ | Input vector | $n_0 \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ | Weight matrix layer $l$ | $n_l \times n_{l-1}$ |
| $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ | Bias vector layer $l$ | $n_l \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$ | Pre-activation | $n_l \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ | Activation | $n_l \times 1$ |
| $g^{(l)}$ | Activation function | element-wise |

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Forward-pass caching:** For efficient backprop, the values $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$ and $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ are stored at every layer for re-use in the backward pass.

**(b) Backpropagation Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the loss derivative w.r.t. output:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} = \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} J \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{L+1}}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Output error signal** $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L+1)}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L+1)} = \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} J \odot g'^{(L+1)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L+1)}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{L+1}}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Recursion for hidden error signal** (chain rule, layer $l = L, L-1, \ldots, 1$):

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \left(\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)\top} \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}\right) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Justification via chain rule:**

Starting from $J = J(\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\mathbf{z}^{(L+1)}(\mathbf{h}^{(L)}(\cdots))))$,

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(l)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)\top} \mathbf{W}^{(l+1)} \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})
$$

which, in vector form, yields the recursion above.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Gradients w.r.t. weights:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \, \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Gradients w.r.t. biases:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — SGD update:**

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}, \qquad \mathbf{b}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}}
$$

The whole procedure is a backward sweep through the layers, reusing cached $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$, $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$, and $g'^{(l)}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Activation, Loss & Training Challenges**

**(a)** Compare Sigmoid, Tanh, and ReLU activation functions in terms of their mathematical form, gradient behaviour, and effect on deep-network training. Justify why ReLU is the default choice in modern deep networks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **vanishing gradient problem** with a mathematical illustration. Describe two practical techniques used to train deep networks effectively. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Activation Function Comparison (7 marks):**

| Property (1 mark each row in table below) | **Sigmoid** | **Tanh** | **ReLU** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formula $g(z)$ | $\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\frac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $\max(0,z)$ |
| Range | $(0,1)$ | $(-1,1)$ | $[0,\infty)$ |
| Derivative $g'(z)$ | $g(z)(1-g(z))$ — max $1/4$ | $1-g(z)^{2}$ — max $1$ | $1$ for $z>0$, $0$ for $z\leq 0$ |
| Zero-centered | No | Yes | No |
| Saturation | Yes (both tails) | Yes (both tails) | No (one-sided) |
| Computational cost | Expensive | Expensive | Cheap |

**Justification (2 marks):** ReLU is the default because (i) its gradient is exactly $1$ for positive pre-activations, eliminating the vanishing-gradient decay across deep layers; (ii) it is computationally trivial (a single threshold at $0$); (iii) it induces **sparsity** (many neurons output $0$), which improves generalization. Drawback: *dying ReLU* problem (neurons can become permanently inactive if $b$ is very negative), remedied by **Leaky ReLU** $\max(\alpha z, z)$ with $\alpha \approx 0.01$.

**(b) Vanishing Gradient Problem (7 marks):**

**Mathematical illustration (3 marks):**
For sigmoid, $g'(z) \leq \tfrac{1}{4}$. In a deep network of $L$ layers, the gradient w.r.t. an early weight is a product of layer-wise Jacobians:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L+1)} \odot \prod_{l=2}^{L+1} \left( \mathbf{W}^{(l)\top} \odot g'(\mathbf{z}^{(l-1)}) \right) \cdot \mathbf{h}^{(0)\top}
$$

If all $\Vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \Vert < 4$, each factor contributes $\Vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \Vert/4 < 1$, and the product decays **exponentially** in $L$:

$$
\left\Vert \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \right\Vert \leq \prod_{l} \frac{\Vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \Vert}{4} \to 0 \text{ as } L \to \infty
$$

Consequently, early-layer weights update negligibly and the network fails to learn low-level features.

**Two practical remedies (2 marks each):**

1. **Use ReLU activations** — derivative is $1$ for $z>0$, so the multiplicative decay disappears for healthy (positive) neurons.
2. **Careful weight initialization** — e.g., **He initialization** for ReLU: $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{l-1}}\right)$, which keeps the variance of activations stable across layers.
3. *(Acceptable third)* **Batch Normalization** (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) — normalizes pre-activations within each mini-batch to have zero mean and unit variance, then applies learnable scale and shift; this drastically mitigates internal covariate shift and gradient decay.
4. *(Acceptable fourth)* **Residual connections** (He et al., ResNet 2015) — adding skip connections $\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = g(\mathbf{z}^{(l)}) + \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)}$ creates a near-identity path for gradients to flow backward.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density, KTU 2024 exam-aligned):**

- [ ] A **deep feedforward network** is a chain of layers with strictly one-way information flow, mapping $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ via $f(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
- [ ] Depth = number of layers; Width = number of neurons per layer. Depth gives **exponential representational efficiency** for hierarchical features.
- [ ] **Universal Approximation Theorem**: a one-hidden-layer net *can* approximate any continuous function, but depth is more parameter-efficient.
- [ ] Forward pass: $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$; $\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$.
- [ ] **Loss** measures prediction error: MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification (with Softmax for multi-class, Sigmoid for binary).
- [ ] **Backpropagation** is the recursive chain rule: $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \left(\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)\top}\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}\right) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$.
- [ ] **Gradient w.r.t. weights**: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)\top}$ (outer product).
- [ ] **SGD update**: $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J$.
- [ ] **ReLU** $\max(0,z)$ is preferred for deep nets because its gradient is $1$ for $z>0$, eliminating vanishing gradient.
- [ ] **Vanishing gradient** = exponential decay of $\partial J / \partial \mathbf{W}^{(\text{early})}$ with depth; **exploding gradient** = exponential growth. Fixes: ReLU, He init, BatchNorm, gradient clipping, residual connections.
- [ ] **Common regularizers** for deep nets: L2 weight decay, Dropout, early stopping, data augmentation.
- [ ] **Initialization matters**: Xavier (Glorot) for tanh/sigmoid ($\sigma^{2} = \frac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$); He for ReLU ($\sigma^{2} = \frac{2}{n_{in}}$).
- [ ] Backprop is **computationally efficient** — cost is roughly twice a forward pass because it reuses cached activations.
- [ ] **Dying ReLU** = neuron permanently outputs $0$ because $b$ is too negative → use Leaky ReLU / PReLU.
- [ ] KTU frequently asks: *derive backprop equations* (full marks for showing chain rule + recursion + outer-product gradient).
- [ ] KTU frequently asks: *compare activations* — use a table; mention vanishing gradient and sparsity.
- [ ] KTU frequently asks: *loss function selection* — match loss with output activation and task type.
- [ ] The **chain rule of calculus** is the single most important mathematical tool behind training deep models — memorize its form and its recursive application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Techniques: Gradient Descent, GD with Momentum, Stochastic GD, AdaGrad, RMSProp, and Adam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optimization Techniques: Gradient Descent, GD with Momentum, Stochastic GD, AdaGrad, RMSProp, and Adam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization in Deep Learning** is the mathematical process of iteratively updating the learnable parameters (weights $\theta$ and biases $b$) of a neural network in order to **minimize a loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ (or equivalently, navigate the parameter space towards a local or global minimum).

In the context of neural network training, the optimization problem is formally stated as:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta)$$

where $\theta^{*} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the optimal parameter vector, $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ is a differentiable loss function, and the search is restricted to a high-dimensional, non-convex landscape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need different optimizers?**
> The loss surface of a deep network is highly non-convex with **saddle points, ravines, and plateaus**. A single naive update rule fails to converge efficiently. Hence, we require sophisticated optimization algorithms that handle:
> - **Sparse gradients** (rare features)
> - **Vanishing / exploding gradients** (deep architectures)
> - **Non-stationary objectives** (changing loss surfaces)
> - **Ill-conditioned Hessian** (curvature anisotropy)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Hiker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:**
> Imagine you are a **blindfolded hiker** on a foggy, mountainous terrain (the loss landscape). Your goal is to reach the **lowest valley** (global minimum). At every step, you can only feel the **slope of the ground beneath your feet** (the gradient). 
> - **Gradient Descent** = You take a step strictly in the steepest downward direction. You may oscillate and take forever to reach the bottom.
> - **Momentum** = You gain speed from past steps, like a rolling ball, helping you push through small bumps.
> - **SGD** = Instead of surveying the entire mountain, you check just one random spot. It's noisy but fast.
> - **AdaGrad / RMSProp** = You become aware that the mountain is steep in some directions and gentle in others, so you slow down on steep slopes.
> - **Adam** = The best hiker — combines speed (momentum) and terrain awareness (adaptive steps).

### 1.3 Core Standard Metrics Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ | Learning rate (step size) | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $\beta, \beta_1$ | Momentum decay / 1st moment decay | $0.9$ |
| $\beta_2$ | 2nd moment decay (Adam) | $0.999$ |
| $\epsilon$ | Numerical stability constant | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-8}$ |
| $\gamma$ | RMSProp decay rate | $0.9$ |
| $m$ | Mini-batch size | $32, 64, 128, 256$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Optimization Variants — A Comparative View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently test the **algorithmic difference** between these six techniques. Memorize the **update rule** of each — it is the single most high-yield fact in this module.

#### **Variant 1: Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)**
- Uses the **entire training set** to compute the gradient at every iteration.
- Update rule: 
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta)$$
- **Pros:** Deterministic, stable convergence.
- **Cons:** Very slow for large datasets; cannot escape shallow local minima easily; one update per epoch.

#### **Variant 2: GD with Momentum**
- Addresses BGD's slow convergence in ravines by adding a velocity term.
- Velocity update:
$$v_{t} = \beta \cdot v_{t-1} + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$
- Parameter update:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t}$$
- **Physical interpretation:** The parameter vector $\theta$ behaves like a **heavy ball rolling down the loss surface**, gaining speed over iterations.

#### **Variant 3: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**
- Computes the gradient using **a single randomly chosen sample** $x^{(i)}$.
- Update rule:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}\left(\theta_{t}; x^{(i)}; y^{(i)}\right)$$
- **Pros:** Fast updates; noise helps escape shallow minima; can be applied online.
- **Cons:** High variance updates; oscillates near minima.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mini-Batch GD** is the practical middle ground:
> $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}\left(\theta_{t}; x^{(i:i+m)}; y^{(i:i+m)}\right)$$
> where $m$ is the mini-batch size.

#### **Variant 4: AdaGrad (Adaptive Gradient Algorithm)**
- Adapts the learning rate **per parameter** based on historical gradients.
- Accumulator:
$$G_{t} = G_{t-1} + \left(\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})\right)^{2}$$
- Update:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t}} + \epsilon} \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$
- **Why divide by $\sqrt{G_{t}}$?** Parameters that have received **large gradients** (frequently updated) get smaller effective learning rates, while **sparse features** (infrequent, large gradient when they do occur) get amplified.
- **Drawback:** $G_{t}$ monotonically increases ⇒ learning rate shrinks to 0 ⇒ training **stops prematurely**.

#### **Variant 5: RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation)**
- Solves AdaGrad's monotonic decay by using an **exponential moving average (EMA)** of squared gradients.
- EMA update:
$$E[g^{2}]_{t} = \gamma \cdot E[g^{2}]_{t-1} + (1 - \gamma) \cdot \left(\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})\right)^{2}$$
- Parameter update:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^{2}]_{t}} + \epsilon} \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$
- **Key Idea:** Old squared gradients are exponentially forgotten, so the effective learning rate never dies out.

#### **Variant 6: Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation)**
- Combines the **first moment** (mean, like Momentum) and **second moment** (uncentered variance, like RMSProp).
- **Step 1 — Biased 1st moment:**
$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} \cdot m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{1}) \cdot g_{t}$$
- **Step 2 — Biased 2nd moment:**
$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} \cdot v_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{2}) \cdot g_{t}^{2}$$
- **Step 3 — Bias correction (because $m_{t}, v_{t}$ are initialized to 0):**
$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \quad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$
- **Step 4 — Parameter update:**
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon} \cdot \hat{m}_{t}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Algorithm | Core Update Equation | Learning Rate Behaviour | Key Hyperparameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BGD** | $\theta = \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$ | **Fixed** for all params | $\eta$ |
| **Momentum** | $v = \beta v + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L};\ \theta = \theta - v$ | Fixed | $\beta$ |
| **SGD** | $\theta = \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Fixed, per-sample | $\eta$ |
| **AdaGrad** | $\theta = \theta - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G} + \epsilon}\nabla \mathcal{L}$ | **Per-param, decreasing** | $\eta, \epsilon$ |
| **RMSProp** | $\theta = \theta - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^{2}]} + \epsilon}\nabla \mathcal{L}$ | Per-param, controlled by EMA | $\eta, \gamma$ |
| **Adam** | $\theta = \theta - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}} + \epsilon}\hat{m}$ | Per-param, adaptive + bias-corrected | $\eta, \beta_{1}, \beta_{2}, \epsilon$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Theorem (Standard Result):** For convex, $L$-smooth loss functions, SGD with step-size $\eta_{t} = \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{t})$ achieves a sub-linear convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ in $T$ iterations.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision (ResNet, VGG):** Adam & SGD-with-momentum dominate.
- **Natural Language Processing (BERT, GPT):** AdamW (a decoupled-weight-decay variant of Adam) is the de-facto standard.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** RMSProp / Adam preferred due to non-stationary reward distributions.
- **Generative Models (GANs, Diffusion):** Adam is the workhorse for the generator and discriminator.
- **Recommendation Systems:** AdaGrad is used when data is extremely sparse (e.g., click prediction).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Adam's Bias Correction Works

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **KTU favourite**. The 7-mark question on Adam frequently demands this exact bias-correction math.

**Step 1 — Define the gradient at time $t$:**
Let $g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$. We treat $g_{t}$ as a random variable drawn from a distribution with true mean $\mathbb{E}[g_{t}] = \mu$ and true second moment $\mathbb{E}[g_{t}^{2}] = \zeta$.

**Step 2 — Expand the 1st moment recursion:**
We have:
$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{1}) g_{t}$$

Unrolling the recursion from initialization $m_{0} = 0$:

$$m_{t} = (1 - \beta_{1})\sum_{i=1}^{t} \beta_{1}^{t-i} g_{i}$$

**Step 3 — Take expectation on both sides:**
Since $m_{0} = 0$, the initialization introduces a bias. Taking expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[m_{t}] = (1 - \beta_{1})\sum_{i=1}^{t} \beta_{1}^{t-i} \mathbb{E}[g_{i}] = (1 - \beta_{1})\mu \sum_{i=1}^{t} \beta_{1}^{t-i}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the geometric series:**
The sum is a finite geometric series:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{t} \beta_{1}^{t-i} = \beta_{1}^{t-1} + \beta_{1}^{t-2} + \cdots + \beta_{1}^{0} = \frac{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**
Therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}[m_{t}] = (1 - \beta_{1}) \cdot \mu \cdot \frac{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}} = \mu(1 - \beta_{1}^{t})$$

**Step 6 — Compute the bias-corrected estimator:**
To unbias the estimator, we divide by $(1 - \beta_{1}^{t})$:

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}$$

Now, $\mathbb{E}[\hat{m}_{t}] = \mu$, which is the **unbiased first-moment estimate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** The bias $(1 - \beta_{1}^{t}) \to 0$ as $t \to 0$ (early iterations) and $(1 - \beta_{1}^{t}) \to 1$ as $t \to \infty$ (steady state). Thus the correction is most critical at the start of training.

The same derivation applies identically to $v_{t}$ with $\beta_{2}$ in place of $\beta_{1}$:

$$\hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: RMSProp Effective Learning Rate Bound

Given the EMA recursion $E[g^{2}]_{t} = \gamma E[g^{2}]_{t-1} + (1-\gamma)g_{t}^{2}$, the stationary solution is:

$$E[g^{2}]_{\infty} = \mathbb{E}[g_{t}^{2}]$$

Therefore, the effective per-parameter learning rate is:

$$\eta_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[g^{2}]_{\infty}} + \epsilon} \approx \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[g^{2}]_{\infty}}}$$

This shows that RMSProp **normalizes the gradient to unit RMS magnitude**, making the algorithm scale-invariant.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Single Iteration of Adam

> [!NOTE]
> KTU numerical questions on Adam typically give you 1 or 2 iterations. Use this worked example as your model.

**Given:**
- $\theta_{0} = 0.5$, $m_{0} = 0$, $v_{0} = 0$
- $\eta = 0.1$, $\beta_{1} = 0.9$, $\beta_{2} = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$
- $g_{0} = 0.2$

**Step 1 — 1st moment:**
$$m_{0} = 0.9(0) + 0.1(0.2) = 0.02$$

**Step 2 — 2nd moment:**
$$v_{0} = 0.999(0) + 0.001(0.2)^{2} = 0.001 \times 0.04 = 4 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 — Bias correction (1st moment):**
$$\hat{m}_{0} = \frac{0.02}{1 - 0.9^{1}} = \frac{0.02}{0.1} = 0.2$$

**Step 4 — Bias correction (2nd moment):**
$$\hat{v}_{0} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{1 - 0.999^{1}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.001} = 0.04$$

**Step 5 — Parameter update:**
$$\theta_{1} = 0.5 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{0.04} + 10^{-8}} \times 0.2 = 0.5 - \frac{0.1}{0.2} \times 0.2 = 0.5 - 0.1 = 0.4$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to Use What"

| Scenario | Recommended Optimizer | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Sparse data (NLP vocab, recommender) | **AdaGrad / Adam** | Per-parameter adaptation amplifies rare features |
| Computer vision (CNN training) | **SGD with Momentum** | Better generalization, smoother convergence |
| RNN / LSTM training | **RMSProp / Adam** | Handles exploding gradients effectively |
| GANs | **Adam** (with $\beta_{1} = 0.5$) | Non-stationary min-max objective |
| Reinforcement Learning | **RMSProp / Adam** | High variance, non-stationary reward |
| Quick prototyping | **Adam** | Robust default, minimal hyperparameter tuning |
| Best test accuracy (production) | **SGD with Momentum** + learning-rate decay | Empirically best generalization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Family Tree of Optimizers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Base Optimization Problem: minimize L theta"]
    rootB["First-Order Methods (use only gradient)"]
    rootA --> rootB
    rootB --> nodeA["Fixed LR Methods"]
    rootB --> nodeB["Adaptive LR Methods"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Batch GD"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["SGD"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Momentum GD"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Nesterov Accelerated"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["AdaGrad"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["RMSProp"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["Adam"]
    nodeB --> nodeJ["AdamW"]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Inside the Adam Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["Gradient gt at time t"] --> cmpA["Compute 1st moment: mt = b1 mt-1 + 1-b1 gt"]
    inpA --> cmpB["Compute 2nd moment: vt = b2 vt-1 + 1-b2 gt squared"]
    cmpA --> biasA["Bias correct: mhat = mt divided by 1 minus b1 power t"]
    cmpB --> biasB["Bias correct: vhat = vt divided by 1 minus b2 power t"]
    biasA --> updA["Combine: theta new = theta - eta mhat divided by sqrt vhat plus epsilon"]
    biasB --> updA
    updA --> outA["Updated parameter theta t+1"]
```

### 4.3 Trajectory Comparison on a Ravine-Shaped Loss Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCENE["Loss Surface: Narrow Ravine along W2, Gentle Slope along W1"]
        sgdA["SGD: zig-zag oscillations, slow descent"] --> sgdB["Reaches minimum in 1000+ iterations"]
    end
    subgraph SCENE2["Momentum: smoother path, overshoots then corrects"]
        momA["Accelerates along ravine"] --> momB["Reaches minimum in ~500 iterations"]
    end
    subgraph SCENE3["Adam: direct descent, normalized updates"]
        adamA["Adaptive per-axis scaling"] --> adamB["Reaches minimum in ~200 iterations"]
    end
```

### 4.4 SGD vs Mini-Batch vs Full-Batch — Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ds["Full Dataset D of size N"] --> optA{"Optimizer Type"}
    optA -->|Full Batch GD| fb["Compute gradient over all N samples per update"]
    optA -->|Mini-Batch GD| mb["Sample m of N samples per update: m in 32 64 128 256"]
    optA -->|Pure SGD| sg["Sample 1 sample per update: m equals 1"]
    fb --> fb1["1 update per epoch"]
    mb --> mb1["N/m updates per epoch"]
    sg --> sg1["N updates per epoch"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Stochastic Gradient Descent. Mention two advantages it has over Batch Gradient Descent.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is a variant of gradient descent in which the gradient of the loss function is computed using **only a single randomly chosen training example** $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ at each iteration, rather than the entire training set.
- **Mathematical form (0.5 mark):** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t}; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$
- **Advantages (1 mark):**
  1. Much **faster updates** since only one sample needs to be processed — essential for large-scale datasets.
  2. The **noisy updates introduce stochasticity** that helps the algorithm escape shallow local minima and saddle points.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Write the update equations of the Adam optimizer. Why is bias correction necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equations (2 marks):**

$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{1}) g_{t}, \quad v_{t} = \beta_{2} v_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{2}) g_{t}^{2}$$

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \quad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon} \hat{m}_{t}$$

- **Why bias correction is necessary (1 mark):** Since $m_{0}$ and $v_{0}$ are initialized to $0$, the moment estimates $m_{t}$ and $v_{t}$ are **biased towards zero**, especially during the initial iterations. The bias correction factor $(1 - \beta_{i}^{t})$ compensates for this initialization bias, ensuring the first and second moment estimates are **unbiased estimators** of the true gradient mean and variance.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Writing AdaGrad's update as $\theta = \theta - \eta \nabla\mathcal{L}$ (forgetting the division by $\sqrt{G_{t}}$) → **deduct 2 marks**.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Confusing Adam's $\beta_1$ (momentum) with RMSProp's $\gamma$ — they are **different hyperparameters with different defaults** ($\beta_1=0.9$, $\gamma=0.9$ in RMSProp, but they govern different things).
> - **Common mistake #3:** Omitting the bias-correction step in Adam — examiners treat it as **mandatory** for full marks.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Writing SGD's update as if it uses the full batch — that is **Batch GD**, not SGD. Strictly per-sample.
> - **Common mistake #5:** Forgetting to mention the role of $\epsilon$ — it is **not optional**; it prevents division by zero and must appear in every adaptive optimizer's update rule.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the parameter update rule of the **Adam optimizer** step by step, clearly explaining the role of first moment, second moment, and bias correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **AdaGrad**, **RMSProp**, and **Adam** optimizers in terms of (i) accumulator behavior, (ii) handling of sparse gradients, (iii) tendency for learning rate to vanish, and (iv) typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Adam Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Setup (1 mark):** At time $t$, gradient is $g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$. We seek an estimator that captures both the **mean direction** (1st moment) and the **variance magnitude** (2nd moment) of the gradient.

2. **First moment — exponential moving average of gradient (1.5 marks):**
$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{1}) g_{t}, \quad m_{0} = 0$$
This is analogous to Momentum — it accumulates an exponentially decayed history of gradients, giving a "rolling average" of the descent direction.

3. **Second moment — EMA of squared gradient (1.5 marks):**
$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} v_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{2}) g_{t}^{2}, \quad v_{0} = 0$$
This captures the **squared magnitude** history, analogous to RMSProp, and is used for per-parameter learning-rate scaling.

4. **Bias correction — derivation (2 marks):** Since $m_{0} = v_{0} = 0$, the expectations are biased. Unrolling $m_{t}$:
$$m_{t} = (1 - \beta_{1})\sum_{i=1}^{t}\beta_{1}^{t-i}g_{i}$$
Taking expectations: $\mathbb{E}[m_{t}] = \mu(1 - \beta_{1}^{t})$. To unbias, divide by $(1-\beta_{1}^{t})$:
$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \quad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

5. **Final update (1 mark):**
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon}\hat{m}_{t}$$

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.75 marks each row):**

| Criterion | AdaGrad | RMSProp | Adam |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accumulator behaviour** | Sum of all past squared gradients (monotonic) | EMA of squared gradients (decaying) | Two EMAs: $m_t$ and $v_t$ |
| **Sparse gradients** | Excellent — rare features get amplified | Good | Excellent (combines both moments) |
| **Learning-rate vanishing** | Yes — $G_t$ grows indefinitely | No — EMA prevents this | No — bias correction + EMA prevent this |
| **Typical use cases** | NLP, sparse recommender systems | RNN training, non-stationary objectives | Default for most deep learning tasks |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Gradient Descent with Momentum** algorithm in detail. Derive the velocity update equation and explain how momentum helps in navigating ravines in the loss surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $\theta_{0} = 1.0$, $v_{0} = 0$, learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, momentum $\beta = 0.9$, and gradients $g_{0} = 0.5, g_{1} = 0.3, g_{2} = 0.2$, compute $\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \theta_{3}$ using **GD with Momentum**. Show all working. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Momentum GD Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Motivation (1 mark):** Standard GD oscillates heavily in narrow ravines and is slow to converge. Momentum addresses this by **accumulating past gradients** into a velocity vector, analogous to the momentum of a physical object.

2. **Velocity update (2 marks):**
$$v_{t} = \beta v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$
where $\beta \in [0, 1)$ is the momentum coefficient (typically $\beta = 0.9$). The term $\beta v_{t-1}$ retains a decaying history of past gradients.

3. **Parameter update (1 mark):**
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t}$$

4. **Ravine navigation (2 marks):** In a narrow ravine, the gradient oscillates perpendicularly to the ravine axis. Momentum **dampens the perpendicular oscillations** (because they cancel out over time) while **amplifying motion along the ravine axis** (because gradients reinforce each other). This leads to faster convergence along the principal direction of descent.

5. **Physical analogy (1 mark):** The parameter vector behaves like a **heavy ball rolling down the loss surface** — friction (momentum decay $\beta$) prevents infinite acceleration, while gravity (gradient) pulls it towards the minimum.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Using $v_{t} = \beta v_{t-1} + \eta g_{t}$ and $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t}$:

**Iteration 1 (t=0):**
$$v_{0} = 0.9(0) + 0.1(0.5) = 0.05$$
$$\theta_{1} = 1.0 - 0.05 = 0.95$$

**Iteration 2 (t=1):**
$$v_{1} = 0.9(0.05) + 0.1(0.3) = 0.045 + 0.03 = 0.075$$
$$\theta_{2} = 0.95 - 0.075 = 0.875$$

**Iteration 3 (t=2):**
$$v_{2} = 0.9(0.075) + 0.1(0.2) = 0.0675 + 0.02 = 0.0875$$
$$\theta_{3} = 0.875 - 0.0875 = 0.7875$$

**Final answer:** $\theta_{1} = 0.95$, $\theta_{2} = 0.875$, $\theta_{3} = 0.7875$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- [ ] **BGD** uses full dataset per update; **SGD** uses one sample; **Mini-Batch** uses $m$ samples.
- [ ] **Momentum** adds a velocity term $v_{t} = \beta v_{t-1} + \eta g_{t}$; default $\beta = 0.9$.
- [ ] **AdaGrad** uses accumulator $G_{t} = G_{t-1} + g_{t}^{2}$; **suffers from vanishing LR**.
- [ ] **RMSProp** uses EMA $E[g^{2}]_{t} = \gamma E[g^{2}]_{t-1} + (1-\gamma)g_{t}^{2}$; default $\gamma = 0.9$.
- [ ] **Adam** combines Momentum ($m_t$, $\beta_1 = 0.9$) and RMSProp ($v_t$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$) with **bias correction**.
- [ ] **Bias correction** in Adam: $\hat{m}_{t} = m_{t}/(1-\beta_{1}^{t})$, $\hat{v}_{t} = v_{t}/(1-\beta_{2}^{t})$.
- [ ] **$\epsilon$** is mandatory in all adaptive methods to prevent division by zero; typical value $10^{-8}$.
- [ ] Adam is the **default choice** for most deep learning; SGD + Momentum often gives **better test accuracy** with longer training.
- [ ] AdaGrad: best for **sparse data**; RMSProp: best for **RNNs**; Adam: best **all-rounder**.
- [ ] **Convergence order:** BGD converges linearly for strongly convex; SGD converges sub-linearly at $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$.
- [ ] Adam's update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \hat{m}_t / (\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon)$ — memorize verbatim.
- [ ] Nesterov Momentum is a variant that looks ahead of the current position before computing the gradient.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regularization Methods: L1 and L2, Early Stopping, Dataset Augmentation, Parameter Sharing and Tying, Input Noise Injection, Ensemble Methods, Dropout, and Parameter Initialization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regularization Methods: L1 and L2, Early Stopping, Dataset Augmentation, Parameter Sharing and Tying, Input Noise Injection, Ensemble Methods, Dropout, and Parameter Initialization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Regularization? (The Master Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Regularization* is any modification we make to a learning algorithm that is intended to **reduce its generalization error** but **not its training error**. In deep learning, regularization is a central tool because explicit constraints on model parameters (like norm penalties) interact intimately with the underlying optimization problem.

**Intuition (The "Student Cramming vs. Real Learning" Analogy):**
Imagine a student preparing for an exam.
- A student who **memorizes every answer from last 10 years' question papers** (including the exact wording, page numbers, and even the typos) has **zero training error** but will fail on a new question paper. This is **overfitting**.
- A student who **understands the underlying concepts and principles** can answer both old and brand-new questions. This is **good generalization**.
- Regularization is like telling the student: *"Don't memorize — understand the principles. The patterns matter more than the exact words."*

In neural network terms: **regularization prevents the network from memorizing the training data and forces it to learn the underlying patterns.**

---

## 1.2 L1 Regularization (Lasso)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** L1 regularization adds a penalty equal to the **sum of the absolute values** of the weights to the loss function.

**The Modified Loss Function:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{L1}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{data}(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i} \vert w_i \vert$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{L}_{data}(\theta)$ = the original (data) loss
- $\lambda$ = the **regularization strength** (hyperparameter, $\lambda \geq 0$)
- $w_i$ = individual weight parameters
- $\vert w_i \vert$ = absolute value of weight

**Intuition (The "Sparsity Sheriff" Analogy):**
Think of L1 as a strict budget manager. For every weight it asks, *"How much does this cost?"* It charges equally whether the weight is large or small. Because the penalty is *linear* in weight magnitude, the optimization finds it cheaper to **kill off (zero out) unnecessary weights entirely** rather than keep them small. The result? A **sparse network** where many weights are exactly zero — like a forest where the budget manager has cut down most trees, leaving only the most important ones.

**Key Property:** L1 produces **sparse models** (feature selection).

---

## 1.3 L2 Regularization (Ridge / Weight Decay)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** L2 regularization adds a penalty equal to the **sum of the squared values** of the weights to the loss function.

**The Modified Loss Function:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{L2}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{data}(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i} w_i^{2}$$

**Intuition (The "Gentle Shrinker" Analogy):**
L2 is like a wise elder who says, *"Don't let any single weight get too powerful."* Because the penalty is *quadratic*, large weights are punished **disproportionately** (10× the weight = 100× the penalty), while small weights are barely affected. So L2 doesn't kill weights — it **shrinks them proportionally**. The result? All weights remain non-zero but small, like a well-distributed team where no single member dominates.

**Key Property:** L2 produces **small, distributed weights** and is also called **Weight Decay** in deep learning.

---

## 1.4 Early Stopping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Early stopping* is a regularization strategy in which training is halted at the point where performance on a **validation set** starts to degrade, even though performance on the training set continues to improve.

**Intuition (The "Runway Lights" Analogy):**
Imagine a pilot landing a plane in fog. Training error is the altitude (lower = closer to ground = "better"). But if the pilot keeps descending past the runway, they crash. The validation error acts as **runway lights** — when the validation error starts rising, the pilot has gone past the safe point. **Stop training here.** The "altitude at stop time" is the *optimal capacity*.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$t^{*} = \arg\min_{t} \mathcal{L}_{val}(\theta_{t})$$

Where $t$ is the training iteration/step and $\mathcal{L}_{val}$ is the validation loss.

---

## 1.5 Dataset Augmentation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Dataset augmentation* is a regularization technique that artificially **increases the size and diversity of the training set** by applying label-preserving transformations to existing examples.

**Intuition (The "Photo Album Variety" Analogy):**
If you only ever saw your friend in one specific photo, you'd struggle to recognize them in real life from different angles. But if you saw 100 photos — smiling, serious, with hat, without hat, from left, from right — you'd recognize them anywhere. **Dataset augmentation creates those extra photos for the network.**

**Common Transformations (by domain):**
- **Images:** rotation, flipping, cropping, scaling, color jitter, cutout
- **Audio:** pitch shift, time stretch, add background noise
- **Text:** synonym replacement, back-translation, random insertion/deletion

---

## 1.6 Parameter Sharing & Parameter Tying

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Parameter sharing* refers to using the **same parameter** in multiple parts of the model. *Parameter tying* forces different parameters to be **close to each other** (but not identical).

**Intuition (The "Reusable Lego Brick" Analogy):**
- **Parameter Sharing:** A factory uses the *same mold* to make 1,000 identical car doors. The mold is shared — so are the doors. This works because all car doors should look the same.
- **Parameter Tying:** A factory has two similar molds that should produce *almost identical* parts (say, left and right car doors). The molds are tied — slightly different but constrained to be similar.

**Classic Examples:**
- **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs):** Same kernel slides across the image → parameter sharing.
- **Siamese Networks:** Two networks with tied weights process different inputs → parameter tying.

---

## 1.7 Input Noise Injection

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Input noise injection* (also called *input perturbation*) is a regularization technique that adds **random noise to the training inputs** to improve generalization.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$\tilde{x} = x + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2} I)$$

**Intuition (The "Pencil Sketch vs. Masterpiece" Analogy):**
If an art student only ever studies perfect masterpieces, they will struggle to recognize rough pencil sketches. But if you **show them noisy, slightly corrupted versions**, they learn the *essence* of the art — the core structure — rather than memorizing the exact pixel-perfect details. The noise forces robustness.

**Why It Works:** It can be shown (under certain conditions) that input noise injection is **mathematically equivalent to a Tikhonov regularizer** with penalty on the gradient of the model.

---

## 1.8 Ensemble Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An *ensemble* combines **multiple models** (often called "base learners" or "experts") to produce a single prediction, typically resulting in better generalization than any individual model.

**Intuition (The "Jury of Experts" Analogy):**
One doctor can misdiagnose. A **panel of 10 doctors, voting on the diagnosis**, will likely be more accurate — even if some are mediocre — because their *uncorrelated mistakes cancel out*. Ensembles apply the same principle: train many (diverse) models and combine their predictions.

**Mathematical Form (Regression / Averaging):**
$$\hat{y} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} f_{m}(x)$$

For classification, **majority voting** or **probability averaging** is used.

**Key Variants:**
- **Bagging** (Bootstrap Aggregating)
- **Boosting** (AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting)
- **Stacking** (meta-learner)
- **Snapshot Ensembles** in deep learning

---

## 1.9 Dropout

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Dropout* is a regularization technique where, during each training iteration, each neuron (unit) is **temporarily "dropped out"** (set to 0) with probability $p$ (the "dropout rate"), independently. At test time, all neurons are used, but their outgoing weights are **scaled down by $(1-p)$** (inverted dropout) or activations are scaled (standard dropout).

**Mathematical Form (Training Time):**
$$\tilde{h}_i = h_i \cdot r_i, \quad r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$$

Where $r_i$ is a binary mask.

**Intuition (The "Surprise Quiz" Analogy):**
Imagine a sports team where **at every practice, the coach randomly benches 30% of the players**. The remaining players must:
1. **Cover for missing teammates** (no single player can be lazy).
2. **Learn all positions** (not specialize too narrowly).
3. **Develop multiple strategies** (robustness).

The team becomes stronger because **no single player becomes indispensable**. Dropout does the same for neural networks — it prevents **co-adaptation** of features.

**Why It Works (Three Interpretations):**
1. **Co-adaptation prevention:** Neurons can't rely on specific other neurons.
2. **Model averaging:** Dropout approximates averaging an exponential number of "thinned" networks.
3. **Noise robustness:** Acts as noise injection to the hidden layer activations.

---

## 1.10 Parameter Initialization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Parameter initialization* refers to the strategy used to set the **initial values of the weights and biases** of a neural network before training begins. Good initialization is critical because it affects convergence speed, the ability to escape poor local minima, and generalization.

**Intuition (The "First Impression" Analogy):**
A student's **first day of class** sets the tone. If a math student starts believing they're terrible at math, that bias is hard to overcome. Similarly, a neural network's **initial weights shape its entire learning trajectory**. Too small → vanishing gradients, no learning. Too large → exploding gradients, instability. Just right → fast, stable learning.

**Major Initialization Schemes:**
- **Zero Initialization:** $\text{BAD}$ — causes symmetry breaking failure.
- **Random Initialization:** $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$ or $\mathcal{U}(-a, a)$.
- **Xavier (Glorot) Initialization:** Variance $\propto 1/n_{in}$.
- **He Initialization:** Variance $\propto 2/n_{in}$ (for ReLU).
- **LeCun Initialization:** Variance $\propto 1/n_{in}$.

Where $n_{in}$ is the number of inputs to a layer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Many students confuse **regularization** with **optimization**. Remember: **Optimization minimizes training error; Regularization trades training error for lower generalization error.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Norm Penalties (L1 and L2) — Operational Logic

The general framework of **parameter norm penalty** regularization:
$$\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{data}(\theta) + \alpha \cdot \Omega(\theta)$$

Where:
- $\alpha \in [0, \infty)$ is the **hyperparameter** weighting the penalty
- $\Omega(\theta)$ is the **penalty function** acting on parameters

### 2.1.1 L2 Regularization — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient of the regularized loss:**

$$\nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{L2}(w) = \nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{data}(w) + 2\lambda w$$

**Step 2 — Apply the gradient descent update:**

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{L2}(w)$$

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta(\nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{data}(w) + 2\lambda w)$$

$$w \leftarrow (1 - 2\eta\lambda)w - \eta \nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{data}(w)$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:** Every gradient step **multiplies the weight by $(1 - 2\eta\lambda)$** *before* applying the data-driven update. This is **weight decay** — weights "decay" toward zero by a constant factor at each step.

**Geometric Interpretation:** L2 penalty = spherical constraint → solution lies on a **sphere** → **all weights shrink proportionally** (no sparsity).

### 2.1.2 L1 Regularization — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient of the regularized loss:**

$$\nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{L1}(w) = \nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{data}(w) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w)$$

Where $\text{sign}(w) = +1$ if $w > 0$, $-1$ if $w < 0$, and $0$ if $w = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply the gradient descent update:**

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{data}(w) - \eta\lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w)$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:** Unlike L2, the L1 penalty is a **constant magnitude push toward zero**, regardless of weight size. Large and small weights are penalized equally. The result: weights are driven to **exactly zero** (sparsity).

**Geometric Interpretation:** L1 penalty = diamond-shaped constraint → solution tends to lie on **axes** → **sparse weights**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Method** | **Core Equation / Formula** | **Key Property** | **Hyperparameter** |
|:-----:|:-----------|:----------------------------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | L2 (Weight Decay) | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{data} + \lambda \sum_i w_i^{2}$ | Shrinks all weights, no sparsity | $\lambda$ |
| 2 | L1 (Lasso) | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{data} + \lambda \sum_i \vert w_i \vert$ | Produces sparse weights (feature selection) | $\lambda$ |
| 3 | Early Stopping | $t^{*} = \arg\min_t \mathcal{L}_{val}(\theta_t)$ | Stops at best validation performance | Patience / max epochs |
| 4 | Dataset Augmentation | $\tilde{x} = T(x)$, $T \in$ {transform set} | Increases effective dataset size | Augmentation strength |
| 5 | Parameter Sharing | $W^{(l,k)} = W^{(l,j)}$ for some $j \neq k$ | Reduces parameter count | Architecture choice |
| 6 | Parameter Tying | $\Vert W_{1} - W_{2} \Vert_{F}^{2}$ added to loss | Forces similar weights, allows them to differ | Tying coefficient |
| 7 | Input Noise | $\tilde{x} = x + \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2} I)$ | Equivalent to Tikhonov regularization | Noise variance $\sigma^{2}$ |
| 8 | Ensemble (Average) | $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} f_m(x)$ | Reduces variance via model averaging | Number of models $M$ |
| 9 | Dropout (Train) | $\tilde{h}_i = h_i \cdot r_i$, $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$ | Prevents co-adaptation | Dropout rate $p$ |
| 10 | Dropout (Test) | $h_i^{test} = (1-p) \cdot h_i$  *(inverted dropout: no scaling needed)* | Uses full network | — |
| 11 | Xavier Init | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}\right)$ | Maintains variance across layers | $n_{in}, n_{out}$ |
| 12 | He Init | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in}}\right)$ | Optimal for ReLU activations | $n_{in}$ |
| 13 | LeCun Init | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{1}{n_{in}}\right)$ | Optimal for SELU / linear | $n_{in}$ |

---

## 2.3 Why Regularization Works — The Bias-Variance Tradeoff

The expected test error decomposes as:
$$\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \text{Noise}$$

- **L1/L2, Dropout, Early Stopping** → reduce **variance** (at small cost to bias)
- **Dataset Augmentation** → reduces both bias and variance
- **Ensemble** → reduces variance ($O(1/M)$ for independent models)
- **Good Initialization** → reduces optimization difficulty and improves both

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In practice, deep networks are **over-parameterized** (millions of parameters, fewer training samples). Without regularization, they would memorize training data and fail on new data. Regularization is the **single most important practical ingredient** in modern deep learning success.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Application Domain** | **Regularization Used** | **Why** |
|:-----------------------|:------------------------|:--------|
| Medical Imaging (tumor detection) | L2 + Dropout + Augmentation | Small datasets, high cost of false negatives |
| Self-driving cars (object detection) | L1 + Augmentation + Ensembles | Safety-critical, need robust predictions |
| NLP (language models) | Dropout + Weight Tying (embeddings) | Massive parameter counts, prone to overfit |
| Speech Recognition | Input Noise Injection + SpecAugment | Real-world audio is noisy |
| Recommender Systems | L2 + Early Stopping | Collaborative filtering overfits quickly |
| Generative Models (GANs) | Spectral Norm + Dropout | Stabilize adversarial training |
| Transfer Learning | Parameter Tying (multi-task) | Share knowledge across related tasks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why L2 is Called "Weight Decay"

**Starting Point:** L2-regularized loss function for a single weight $w$ (omitting bias for clarity):
$$\mathcal{L}_{L2}(w) = \mathcal{L}_{data}(w) + \lambda w^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Take the gradient with respect to $w$:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{L2}}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w} + 2\lambda w$$

**Step 2 — Apply standard gradient descent update rule (with learning rate $\eta$):**
$$w_{new} = w - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{L2}}{\partial w}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the gradient:**
$$w_{new} = w - \eta \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w} + 2\lambda w\right)$$

**Step 4 — Distribute and group the terms involving $w$:**
$$w_{new} = w - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w} - 2\eta\lambda w$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $w$ from the rightmost terms:**
$$w_{new} = (1 - 2\eta\lambda) \cdot w - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- The factor $(1 - 2\eta\lambda)$ is called the **decay factor**.
- Assuming $\eta\lambda$ is small (typical in practice), $(1 - 2\eta\lambda) \approx 1 - 2\eta\lambda < 1$.
- **At every gradient step, the weight is multiplied by a number slightly less than 1 BEFORE the data-driven update.**
- This is mathematically equivalent to the weight "decaying" toward zero at each step.
- Hence the name **"Weight Decay."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on terminology:** In some frameworks (e.g., PyTorch with decoupled weight decay), "weight decay" refers to adding the operation $w \leftarrow w - \eta\lambda w$ **directly**, not through the loss. These are equivalent only for vanilla SGD, but differ for adaptive optimizers like Adam.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why L1 Produces Sparsity (Geometric View)

**Problem Setup:** Minimize $\mathcal{L}_{data}(w)$ subject to the L1 constraint $\sum_i \vert w_i \vert \leq c$.

This is a **constrained optimization** problem (equivalent to adding the penalty to the loss for an appropriate $\lambda$).

**Step 1 — Assume we are at the optimal point $w^{*}$.**
The KKT (Karush-Kuhn-Tucker) conditions state that for an active constraint, the **gradient of the loss is parallel (anti-parallel) to the gradient of the constraint**.

**Step 2 — Gradient of the loss at optimum:**
$$\nabla_{w} \mathcal{L}_{data}(w^{*}) = \mu \cdot \nabla_{w} \Omega(w^{*})$$

Where $\mu$ is a scalar (the Lagrange multiplier) and $\Omega(w) = \sum_i \vert w_i \vert$.

**Step 3 — Compute the subgradient of the L1 penalty:**

For each component $w_i$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_i} \vert w_i \vert = \text{sign}(w_i) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } w_i > 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } w_i < 0 \\ [-1, +1] & \text{if } w_i = 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — KKT condition per component:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w_i} = \mu \cdot \text{sign}(w_i)$$

**Step 5 — Key observation:**
The right-hand side $\mu \cdot \text{sign}(w_i)$ is **bounded** in magnitude by $\vert\mu\vert$. 

If $\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w_i} \right\vert < \vert\mu\vert$, then the equation **cannot be satisfied** for $w_i \neq 0$.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
The only way to satisfy KKT is $w_i = 0$ (using the subgradient interval $[-1, +1]$). 

Therefore: **Whenever the data-driven gradient is small (in magnitude) compared to $\mu$, L1 forces the weight to be exactly zero.** This is the **mathematical origin of sparsity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Intuition (for the visual learner):** The L1 constraint region $\sum_i \vert w_i \vert \leq c$ is a **diamond (rhombus)** in 2D with **sharp corners on the axes**. The contours of the loss function are smooth ellipses. As you expand the diamond, the first point where it touches the ellipses is almost always a **corner** — i.e., a point where one weight is zero. For L2, the constraint region is a **circle** with no corners, so contact occurs at smooth points where all weights are non-zero.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Xavier Initialization (Variance Preservation)

**Problem:** Choose the variance of the initial weights $W$ at layer $l$ so that the **variance of activations** is preserved across layers.

**Setup:** Consider a layer with $n_{in}$ inputs, weights $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{out} \times n_{in}}$, and linear activation (approx. for tanh/sigmoid near zero):
$$s = W \cdot x, \quad h_i = \text{activation}(s_i)$$

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
- Inputs $x_j$ are i.i.d. with mean 0 and variance $\text{Var}(x_j) = \sigma_x^{2}$.
- Weights $W_{ij}$ are i.i.d. with mean 0 and variance $\text{Var}(W_{ij}) = \sigma_w^{2}$.
- $W_{ij}$ and $x_j$ are independent.

**Step 2 — Compute variance of pre-activation $s_i$:**
$$s_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n_{in}} W_{ij} x_j$$

Using the variance of a sum of independent products:
$$\text{Var}(s_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_{in}} \text{Var}(W_{ij} x_j) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_{in}} \text{Var}(W_{ij}) \cdot \text{Var}(x_j)$$

$$= \sum_{j=1}^{n_{in}} \sigma_w^{2} \cdot \sigma_x^{2} = n_{in} \cdot \sigma_w^{2} \cdot \sigma_x^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Condition for variance preservation (forward pass):**
We want $\text{Var}(s_i) = \text{Var}(x_j)$, i.e., $\sigma_x^{2}$:
$$n_{in} \cdot \sigma_w^{2} \cdot \sigma_x^{2} = \sigma_x^{2}$$
$$n_{in} \cdot \sigma_w^{2} = 1$$
$$\sigma_w^{2} = \frac{1}{n_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Condition for variance preservation (backward pass):**
By symmetric argument (gradient flows back), we want:
$$n_{out} \cdot \sigma_w^{2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_w^{2} = \frac{1}{n_{out}}$$

**Step 5 — Xavier's compromise (the average):**
Glorot & Bengio (2010) used the **harmonic / arithmetic mean** of the two:
$$\sigma_w^{2} = \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}$$

**Step 6 — Final initialization formula (Xavier/Glorot):**
$$W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad W \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left(-\sqrt{\frac{6}{n_{in}+n_{out}}}, \sqrt{\frac{6}{n_{in}+n_{out}}}\right)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: He Initialization (for ReLU)

**Difference from Xavier:** ReLU "kills" half the neurons (sets to 0), effectively halving the variance.

**Step 1 — Forward pass variance for ReLU:**
ReLU output: $h_i = \max(0, s_i)$. Assuming $s_i$ is symmetric around 0, exactly **half the variance is preserved**:
$$\text{Var}(h_i) = \frac{1}{2} \text{Var}(s_i) = \frac{1}{2} n_{in} \sigma_w^{2} \sigma_x^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Set equal to $\sigma_x^{2}$:**
$$\frac{1}{2} n_{in} \sigma_w^{2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_w^{2} = \frac{2}{n_{in}}$$

**Step 3 — Final He initialization formula:**
$$W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why He has a factor of 2:** The factor 2 compensates for ReLU killing half the activations, so the surviving half maintains the original variance. **This is a strict requirement for training very deep ReLU networks** (e.g., ResNet-50+).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Guide for Choosing Regularization

| **Symptom / Situation** | **Recommended Regularization** | **Reasoning** |
|:------------------------|:-------------------------------|:--------------|
| Weights are very large (e.g., $> 10$) | L2 (Weight Decay) | Penalizes large weights quadratically |
| Need feature selection / interpretability | L1 | Produces sparse weights |
| Validation loss rises while training loss falls | Early Stopping | Classic overfitting signature |
| Small training dataset (e.g., $< 10^4$ samples) | Dataset Augmentation + Dropout | Both reduce variance dramatically |
| CNN architecture | Parameter Sharing (built-in via convolution) | Translation equivariance for free |
| Multi-task learning | Parameter Tying | Share knowledge across tasks |
| Input data is noisy / sensor data | Input Noise Injection | Robustness to perturbation |
| Need maximum accuracy, compute is cheap | Ensemble | Variance reduction is guaranteed |
| Hidden units are highly correlated | Dropout | Breaks co-adaptation |
| Network fails to train (loss not decreasing) | He/Xavier Initialization | Bad init is the #1 cause of training failure |
| GAN training is unstable | Spectral Norm + Dropout | Stabilize adversarial dynamics |
| Recurrent network (RNN) with exploding gradients | L2 + Gradient Clipping | Constrain weight magnitude |

---

## 3.6 Mathematical Proof: Dropout as Ensemble Averaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **key KTU 14-mark derivable result.** Master this!

**Setup:** A network has $n$ units in a hidden layer. With dropout rate $p$ (probability of being dropped), there are $2^n$ possible binary masks $r = (r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n)$.

**Step 1 — Define a "thinned" network:**
For each mask $r$, define a sub-network $f_r(x)$ obtained by keeping only units with $r_i = 1$.

**Step 2 — At test time, we want the expected output of the ensemble of all $2^n$ thinned networks:**
$$\mathbb{E}_{r}[f_r(x)] = \sum_{r \in \{0,1\}^{n}} p^{\sum r_i}(1-p)^{n - \sum r_i} f_r(x)$$

**Step 3 — Key claim:** Using the **independence** of the Bernoulli masks, this expected output can be computed efficiently:

For a single unit $i$ with pre-dropout activation $h_i(x)$:
$$\mathbb{E}_r[h_i(x) \cdot r_i] = h_i(x) \cdot \mathbb{E}[r_i] = h_i(x) \cdot (1-p)$$

**Step 4 — Inverted Dropout Implementation:**
At **training time**, divide activations by $(1-p)$:
$$\tilde{h}_i = \frac{r_i \cdot h_i}{1-p}$$

Then $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = h_i$ — the expected training-time output equals the test-time output. **No scaling is needed at test time.**

**Step 5 — Approximation of the Ensemble:**
The expected output of the exponential $2^n$ ensemble is approximated by the single forward pass at test time (with all units active):
$$f_{\text{ensemble}}(x) \approx \mathbb{E}_r[f_r(x)] \approx f_{\text{full}}(x)$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Dropout is an **efficient approximation** of averaging the predictions of an exponential number of "thinned" networks. This is the formal reason it works as a powerful regularizer — **variance reduction via implicit ensembling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical refinement:** Srivastava et al. (2014) — the original Dropout paper — showed empirically that dropout provides regularization benefits exceeding those of standard L2 in many cases, and approximates Bayesian variational inference (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) — connecting dropout to Bayesian deep learning.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - For **L1 vs L2 questions**, students often forget to mention that L1 produces **sparse weights** while L2 does not. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in 14-mark questions.
> - For **Dropout derivations**, students frequently skip the **inverted dropout scaling** step. Examiners expect it.
> - For **Initialization questions**, students often write Xavier and He formulas **without stating which activation they apply to**. Always state the activation.
> - For **Early Stopping**, students forget to define the **patience hyperparameter**.
> - For **Ensembles**, students confuse **bagging** (parallel, independent) with **boosting** (sequential, dependent). Be precise.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain L1 and L2 regularization with their respective loss functions. How do they differ in the effect on model parameters?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**L1 Regularization (Lasso):** Adds a penalty proportional to the **sum of the absolute values** of the weights.

$$\mathcal{L}_{L1} = \mathcal{L}_{data} + \lambda \sum_{i} \vert w_i \vert$$

**L2 Regularization (Ridge / Weight Decay):** Adds a penalty proportional to the **sum of the squared values** of the weights.

$$\mathcal{L}_{L2} = \mathcal{L}_{data} + \lambda \sum_{i} w_i^{2}$$

**Key Difference:**
- L1 produces **sparse weights** (many weights become exactly zero), acting as an **implicit feature selector**.
- L2 produces **small, distributed, non-zero weights** (all weights shrink but none become exactly zero), providing **smooth shrinkage**.
- Geometrically, L1's diamond-shaped constraint touches the loss contour at axis corners (sparsity), while L2's circular constraint touches at smooth points (no sparsity).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is Dropout? Briefly explain how it acts as a regularizer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Dropout** is a regularization technique in which, during each training iteration, every neuron in the network is **temporarily "dropped out" (set to zero) with probability $p$** (the dropout rate), independently. At test time, all neurons are kept active.

**How it regularizes:**
1. **Prevents co-adaptation:** No neuron can rely on the presence of specific other neurons, forcing each unit to learn useful features independently.
2. **Implicit ensemble:** Dropout approximates averaging the predictions of an **exponential number of thinned sub-networks** ($2^n$ for $n$ units), which reduces prediction variance.
3. **Noise injection:** Acts as multiplicative noise on hidden activations, making the network robust to perturbations.

Mathematically (inverted dropout):
$$\tilde{h}_i = \frac{r_i h_i}{1-p}, \quad r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$$

This provides powerful regularization, often outperforming L2 penalty in deep networks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry internal choice. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient update rule for L2 regularization. Show mathematically why it is also called "weight decay." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain dataset augmentation as a regularization technique. List at least 5 common image augmentation operations and discuss why augmentation reduces overfitting. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Write the L2-regularized loss function (1 mark):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{L2}(w) = \mathcal{L}_{data}(w) + \lambda \sum_{i} w_i^{2}$$

where $\lambda$ is the regularization hyperparameter.

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient with respect to weights (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{L2}}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w} + 2\lambda w$$

**Step 3 — Apply the gradient descent update rule with learning rate $\eta$ (1 mark):**
$$w_{new} = w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{L2}}{\partial w}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the gradient (1 mark):**
$$w_{new} = w - \eta \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w} + 2\lambda w\right)$$

**Step 5 — Expand and group terms (1 mark):**
$$w_{new} = w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w} - 2\eta\lambda w$$

**Step 6 — Factor $w$ from the appropriate terms (1 mark):**
$$w_{new} = (1 - 2\eta\lambda) w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{data}}{\partial w}$$

**Step 7 — Conclude why it is "weight decay" (1 mark):**
The factor $(1 - 2\eta\lambda)$ is the **decay factor**. For $\eta\lambda$ small, this factor is less than 1. At each gradient step, the weight is **multiplied by a number slightly less than 1** before the data-driven update. Hence the weight *decays* (shrinks) toward zero geometrically at each iteration, which is why L2 regularization is also called **weight decay**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Dataset augmentation is a regularization strategy that **artificially increases the size and diversity of the training set** by applying label-preserving transformations to existing training examples. The label of the transformed image is unchanged because the semantic content is preserved.

**Five common image augmentation operations (2.5 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Horizontal Flip:** Mirror the image left-right. A cat facing left and a cat facing right are semantically the same.
2. **Random Crop:** Take a random sub-region of the image. Forces the network to recognize objects regardless of position.
3. **Color Jitter:** Randomly adjust brightness, contrast, saturation, and hue. Simulates varying lighting conditions.
4. **Rotation (small angles, e.g., ±15°):** Rotates the image by a small angle. Handles minor orientation variations.
5. **Gaussian Noise Injection:** Adds small random pixel noise. Improves robustness to sensor noise and acts as input noise regularization.
   *(Other valid: scaling, shearing, CutOut, Mixup, CutMix.)*

**Why augmentation reduces overfitting (2.5 marks):**
- **Increases effective dataset size:** The model sees more "examples" per epoch, reducing memorization.
- **Introduces invariance:** The model learns that the class is invariant to certain transformations (e.g., a cat is still a cat if flipped).
- **Smooths the decision boundary:** The model cannot memorize exact pixel patterns because they keep changing, forcing it to learn robust features.
- **Reduces variance:** Like any data-increasing technique, augmentation reduces the variance component of the generalization error.
- **Acts as implicit regularization:** Equivalent (approximately) to injecting structured noise into the input, similar to Tikhonov-style smoothing of the model function.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is Dropout? Derive why Dropout acts as an approximation to ensemble averaging. Explain inverted dropout. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the importance of parameter initialization. Derive the Xavier initialization formula and state its limitation for ReLU activations. How does He initialization address this? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition of Dropout (1 mark):**
Dropout is a regularization technique where, during each training iteration, each unit in the network is **dropped (set to 0) with probability $p$** independently. At test time, all units are used, but their outgoing weights are scaled (in standard dropout) or no scaling is needed (inverted dropout).

**Dropout as Ensemble Approximation (4 marks):**

Consider a network with $n$ units in a hidden layer. With dropout, there are $2^n$ possible binary masks $r \in \{0,1\}^n$. Each mask defines a "thinned" sub-network $f_r(x)$.

The expected output over all sub-networks is:
$$\mathbb{E}_r[f_r(x)] = \sum_{r} p^{\sum r_i}(1-p)^{n-\sum r_i} f_r(x)$$

For independent Bernoulli masks, this expected value can be computed efficiently. For unit $i$ with pre-dropout activation $h_i(x)$:
$$\mathbb{E}_r[r_i h_i(x)] = h_i(x) \cdot \mathbb{E}[r_i] = h_i(x) \cdot (1-p)$$

The single forward pass at test time with all units active gives an output equal to this expected value (under the approximation that the linear combinations are independent), thereby **approximating the average over an exponential number of sub-networks**. This implicit ensembling **reduces variance** and is the formal reason Dropout works as a powerful regularizer.

**Inverted Dropout (2 marks):**
To make the test-time computation simple, we **scale up** the activations at training time:
$$\tilde{h}_i = \frac{r_i h_i}{1-p}$$

Then:
$$\mathbb{E}_r[\tilde{h}_i] = \frac{h_i \cdot (1-p)}{1-p} = h_i$$

So the expected training-time output equals the test-time output. **No scaling is needed at test time.** This is called "inverted" dropout and is the standard implementation in modern frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Importance of Parameter Initialization (1 mark):**
- **Avoids vanishing/exploding gradients:** Proper scaling keeps activations and gradients in a stable range.
- **Breaks symmetry:** If all weights are equal, all neurons compute the same function and gradient — the network cannot learn diverse features.
- **Speeds up convergence:** A good starting point reduces training time.
- **Determines trainability:** Very deep networks (50+ layers) may not train at all with poor initialization.

**Derivation of Xavier Initialization (3 marks):**

Consider a layer with $n_{in}$ inputs, weights $W$, and pre-activation $s = W \cdot x$.

Assumptions: $x_j$ i.i.d. with mean 0, variance $\sigma_x^{2}$. $W_{ij}$ i.i.d. with mean 0, variance $\sigma_w^{2}$. $W$ and $x$ are independent.

$$\text{Var}(s_i) = \text{Var}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n_{in}} W_{ij} x_j\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_{in}} \text{Var}(W_{ij}) \text{Var}(x_j) = n_{in} \sigma_w^{2} \sigma_x^{2}$$

For **forward pass variance preservation**: $\text{Var}(s_i) = \text{Var}(x_j)$:
$$n_{in} \sigma_w^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow \sigma_w^{2} = \frac{1}{n_{in}}$$

For **backward pass variance preservation** (symmetric argument on the gradient):
$$\sigma_w^{2} = \frac{1}{n_{out}}$$

**Xavier (Glorot) takes the harmonic/average compromise:**
$$\sigma_w^{2} = \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}$$

$$W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}\right)$$

**Limitation for ReLU (1 mark):**
ReLU sets half the activations to zero, halving the variance. Xavier's formula does NOT account for this, so with ReLU activations, the variance **decays** layer by layer, leading to **vanishing activations and gradients in deep networks**.

**He Initialization (2 marks):**
He et al. (2015) modified the derivation to account for ReLU's halving effect:
$$\text{Var}(h_i) = \frac{1}{2} n_{in} \sigma_w^{2} \sigma_x^{2}$$

Setting $\text{Var}(h_i) = \sigma_x^{2}$:
$$\frac{1}{2} n_{in} \sigma_w^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow \sigma_w^{2} = \frac{2}{n_{in}}$$

$$W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in}}\right)$$

The factor of 2 compensates for ReLU's zero-killing behavior, **ensuring stable training of very deep ReLU networks** (e.g., ResNets).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must memorize for KTU exams:**

- ☐ **L1 = Lasso** → penalty = $\lambda \sum \vert w_i \vert$ → **sparse weights** (sparsity)
- ☐ **L2 = Ridge = Weight Decay** → penalty = $\lambda \sum w_i^{2}$ → **shrinks but non-zero** weights
- ☐ **L2 update:** $w_{new} = (1 - 2\eta\lambda)w - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_{data}$ → the decay factor is $(1 - 2\eta\lambda)$
- ☐ **L1 gradient:** $\nabla \mathcal{L}_{L1} = \nabla \mathcal{L}_{data} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w)$ → constant magnitude push
- ☐ **Early stopping** → stop when $\mathcal{L}_{val}$ stops improving; use **patience** hyperparameter
- ☐ **Dataset augmentation** → label-preserving transformations; effectively increases dataset size
- ☐ **Parameter sharing** (CNN) → same kernel used across spatial positions
- ☐ **Parameter tying** (Siamese) → two networks with $\Vert W_1 - W_2 \Vert^{2}$ penalty
- ☐ **Input noise** → $\tilde{x} = x + \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$ → equivalent to Tikhonov regularization
- ☐ **Ensemble** → $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{M}\sum f_m(x)$ → reduces variance, $O(1/M)$ for independent models
- ☐ **Bagging = parallel, independent**; **Boosting = sequential, weighted**
- ☐ **Dropout formula (train):** $\tilde{h}_i = r_i h_i / (1-p)$, $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$
- ☐ **Dropout ≈ averaging $2^n$ thinned networks** (variance reduction)
- ☐ **Inverted dropout:** scale at training time → no scaling at test time
- ☐ **Xavier (Glorot):** $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2/(n_{in} + n_{out}))$ → for **tanh/sigmoid**
- ☐ **He initialization:** $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2/n_{in})$ → for **ReLU**
- ☐ **LeCun initialization:** $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1/n_{in})$ → for **SELU / linear**
- ☐ **Zero initialization = BAD** → symmetry breaking failure
- ☐ **Bias regularization** → usually NOT done (single parameter per unit, low impact)
- ☐ **Augmentation in CNNs** → essential for small datasets (medical, satellite)
- ☐ **Dropout in RNNs** → apply only to non-recurrent connections (standard practice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, **always include a diagram** (e.g., Draw a network with dropout mask, or Draw the L1 vs L2 constraint region). Examiners allocate 1-2 marks specifically for diagrams. Use the **exact formula notation** from this notesheet — copy it precisely to avoid formatting errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolutional Neural Networks: Convolution Layer, Pooling Layer, Fully Connected Layer, and ConvNet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Convolutional Neural Networks: Convolution Layer, Pooling Layer, Fully Connected Layer, and ConvNet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN / ConvNet) — Formal Definition
