- **Image Domain:** rotation, flipping, cropping, scaling, color jitter, cutout, mixup.
- **Text Domain:** synonym replacement, back-translation, random insertion.
- **Audio Domain:** noise injection, time-stretching, pitch-shifting.

### 2.7.2 Why It Works
- Effectively **increases dataset size** without collecting new data.
- Forces the network to learn **invariances** (e.g., a cat is a cat even when flipped).

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **one-stop reference** for the KTU 2024 board exam. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Technique | Regularized Objective / Update Rule | Hyperparameter | Primary Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **L2 (Weight Decay)** | $J + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum \theta_j^2$ ; update: $\theta_j(1-\eta\lambda) - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | $\lambda$ | Shrinks weights smoothly |
| 2 | **L1 (Lasso)** | $J + \lambda\sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ ; subgradient: $\text{sign}(\theta_j)$ | $\lambda$ | Sparse weights, exact zeros |
| 3 | **Dropout** | Mask $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$; $\tilde{a}_i = r_i a_i / p$ | $p \in [0,1]$ (often **0.5**) | Prevents co-adaptation |
| 4 | **Batch Norm** | $\hat{x} = (x-\mu)/\sqrt{\sigma^2+\epsilon}$; $y = \gamma\hat{x}+\beta$ | $\gamma, \beta$ (learnable), $\epsilon$ | Stable training, mild regularization |
| 5 | **Early Stopping** | Stop when $E_{val}$ stops improving for $k$ epochs | Patience $k$ | Implicit parameter norm control |
| 6 | **Data Augmentation** | $\mathcal{D}_{aug} = \{T(x) : x \in \mathcal{D}\}$ | Number/type of transforms | Larger effective dataset |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Regularization Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging (CT/MRI)** | Data Augmentation + Dropout | Limited labelled data; prevent false positives |
| **Autonomous Driving (Tesla)** | L2 + Dropout + Augmentation | Safety-critical generalization to unseen scenarios |
| **NLP Transformers (BERT/GPT)** | L2 + Label Smoothing + Dropout | Massive parameter count, easy to overfit |
| **Speech Recognition (DeepSpeech)** | Batch Norm + SpecAugment | Real-time, robust to noise |
| **Recommender Systems** | L2 + Early Stopping | Sparse, high-dimensional user-item data |
| **Industrial Defect Detection** | Heavy Augmentation + Dropout | Tiny dataset of rare defect classes |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 3 Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. No shortcuts. Multi-line derivations use `\begin{aligned}...\end{aligned}` blocks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: L2 Regularization Gradient and Update Rule

### Step 1: Start with the Empirical Loss
We minimize the empirical risk over $m$ training samples:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} L\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\right)$$

### Step 2: Add the L2 Penalty Term
The L2 regularized objective is:

$$J_{L2}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2$$

where $n$ is the number of parameters.

### Step 3: Differentiate with respect to $\theta_j$
Using the linearity of differentiation:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\left(\theta_j^2\right)$$

Since $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}(\theta_j^2) = 2\theta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda \theta_j$$

### Step 4: Apply Gradient Descent Update

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_j^{(t+1)} &= \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial \theta_j} \\
&= \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta\left(\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda \theta_j^{(t)}\right) \\
&= \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} - \eta\lambda\theta_j^{(t)} \\
&= \theta_j^{(t)}(1 - \eta\lambda) - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
The factor $(1 - \eta\lambda)$ **multiplicatively shrinks** the weight on every step before applying the data-driven gradient — this is the essence of **weight decay**. If $\eta\lambda$ is small, the weight decays **exponentially** toward zero (in the absence of the data term), explaining the name.

### Step 6: Steady-State Analysis (No Data Term)
If we ignore the data term $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$:

$$\theta_j^{(t+1)} = \theta_j^{(t)}(1-\eta\lambda)$$

Iterating from $t=0$:

$$\theta_j^{(t)} = \theta_j^{(0)}(1-\eta\lambda)^t$$

Since $0 < (1-\eta\lambda) < 1$, the weight decays **exponentially** to zero — confirming the "weight decay" interpretation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: L1 Regularization Sub-Gradient

### Step 1: L1 Objective

$$J_{L1}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert \theta_j \vert$$

### Step 2: Sub-Derivative of $|\theta_j|$
The absolute value function is **not differentiable at zero**. The sub-derivative is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\vert \theta_j \vert = \text{sign}(\theta_j) = \begin{cases} +1, & \theta_j > 0 \\ -1, & \theta_j < 0 \\ [-1, 1], & \theta_j = 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 3: Gradient of L1 Objective

$$\frac{\partial J_{L1}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j)$$

### Step 4: Update Rule

$$\theta_j^{(t+1)} = \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta \left[\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j^{(t)})\right]$$

### Step 5: Why L1 Produces Sparsity
Unlike L2, the penalty on L1 is **constant magnitude** regardless of weight size. So once a weight is small, the L2 penalty becomes negligible (proportional to $\theta$), but the L1 penalty remains **constant** (proportional to sign). This constant push toward zero **dominates** the data term for small weights, driving them to exactly zero.

Formally, consider a small weight $\theta_j$ where the data gradient is also small. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_j^{(t+1)} &\approx \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta\lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j^{(t)}) \\
&\approx \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta\lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j^{(t)})
\end{aligned}$$

If $\theta_j > 0$, then $\theta_j^{(t+1)} = \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta\lambda$. The minimum value it can reach is $0$ (not negative, because then $\text{sign}$ flips and pushes it back). Hence exact zero is **stable** for L1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dropout Expected Output

### Step 1: Activation Before Dropout
Let $a_i$ be the activation of neuron $i$ computed deterministically.

### Step 2: Sample Bernoulli Mask
$$r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p), \quad P(r_i = 1) = p, \quad P(r_i = 0) = 1-p$$

### Step 3: Apply Inverted Dropout

$$\tilde{a}_i = \frac{r_i a_i}{p}$$

### Step 4: Compute Expected Value of Output

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\tilde{a}_i] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{r_i a_i}{p}\right] \\
&= \frac{a_i}{p}\mathbb{E}[r_i] \\
&= \frac{a_i}{p} \cdot p \\
&= a_i
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms that **the expected activation is preserved** — this is why inverted dropout does not require rescaling at inference.

### Step 5: Variance of Output
Since $r_i$ and $a_i$ are independent:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\tilde{a}_i) &= \text{Var}\left(\frac{r_i a_i}{p}\right) \\
&= \frac{a_i^2}{p^2} \cdot p(1-p) \\
&= \frac{a_i^2(1-p)}{p}
\end{aligned}$$

For $p = 0.5$, this gives $\text{Var}(\tilde{a}_i) = a_i^2$, doubling the original variance. This added noise is what drives the regularization effect.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Batch Normalization Forward Pass

### Step 1: Mini-Batch Mean

$$\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$$

### Step 2: Mini-Batch Variance

$$\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$$

### Step 3: Normalize
Add $\epsilon$ inside the square root for numerical stability:

$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$$

### Step 4: Scale and Shift (Learnable)

$$y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$$

### Step 5: Verify the Distribution of $\hat{x}_i$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean: } \mathbb{E}[\hat{x}_i] &= \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}} \\
&= \frac{1}{m\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) \\
&= \frac{1}{m\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m}x_i - m\mu_{\mathcal{B}}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{m\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}\left(m\mu_{\mathcal{B}} - m\mu_{\mathcal{B}}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Variance: } \text{Var}[\hat{x}_i] &= \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}\right)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{m(\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2 \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2}{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon} \approx 1
\end{aligned}$$

Thus, $\hat{x}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ approximately — the **desired normalization property** holds.

### Step 6: Why $\gamma$ and $\beta$ Are Needed
If pure zero-mean-unit-variance were optimal for the network, we could skip $\gamma$ and $\beta$. But sometimes the network needs the activations to be in a different distribution (e.g., for ReLU, having a non-zero mean is helpful). The learnable $\gamma$ and $\beta$ **restore representational power** — note that if the network sets $\gamma = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}$ and $\beta = \mu_{\mathcal{B}}$, then $y_i = x_i$, recovering the original (un-normalized) transform.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Regularizer

| Technique | When to Use | Computational Cost | Sparsity? | Best With |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L2** | Default choice; large dense networks | Very low (one term) | No | Almost any model |
| **L1** | Need feature selection, interpretable model | Very low | Yes (exact zeros) | Linear models, embeddings |
| **Dropout** | Large fully-connected layers | Moderate (2x training) | No (implicit) | MLPs, CNNs, RNNs |
| **Batch Norm** | Deep CNNs, training instability | Low (per-batch stats) | No | CNNs, very deep networks |
| **Early Stopping** | Limited compute, want free regularization | Free (just monitoring) | No | Any model |
| **Data Augmentation** | Limited data, vision/audio tasks | Moderate (preprocessing) | No | Vision, audio, sometimes NLP |

flowchart TD

    B1 --> F[Produces Sparse Weights]

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Interaction of Regularizers Within a Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Batch X]:::in --> B[Layer L Forward Pass]
    B --> C{Dropout Layer}
    C -->|Keep p prob| D[Activation A L]
    C -->|Drop 1-p| E[Zeroed Out]
    D --> F[Batch Normalization]
    F --> G[Activation Function ReLU]
    G --> H[Next Layer L plus 1]
    H --> I[Final Output Yhat]
    I --> J[Loss Computation]
    J --> K[Add L2 Penalty]
    K --> L[Regularized Loss]
    L --> M[Backpropagation]
    M --> N[Weight Update with Decay]
    N --> O[Updated Weights]
    O --> B
    P[Validation Set] --> Q[Early Stopping Monitor]
    Q -->|Patience Exhausted| R[Stop and Restore Best]
    Q -->|Improving| O

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** This figure shows how a single training iteration combines **Dropout** (in the layer), **Batch Normalization** (post-activation), **L2 weight decay** (in the loss), and **Early Stopping** (outer loop), all working together. KTU often asks students to "explain how two regularizers interact" — this diagram is your answer template.

## 4.3 Dropout Sub-Architecture (Training vs Inference)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Training_Phase["TRAINING PHASE"]
        direction LR
        X1[Input Neurons]:::in --> M1[Mask Bernoulli p]
        M1 -->|Multiply| A1[Active Neurons fraction p]
        M1 -->|Zero out| Z1[Dropped Neurons fraction 1-p]
        A1 --> S1[Scale by 1 over p]
        S1 --> O1[Output Activations]
    end

    subgraph Inference_Phase["INFERENCE PHASE"]
        direction LR
        X2[Input Neurons]:::in --> F2[Full Network No Dropout]

    end

    O1 --> Compare[Expected Output Equals Deterministic Output]

    classDef in fill:#c00000,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Batch Normalization Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Mini Batch Activations X] --> M[Compute Mean mu B]
    X --> V[Compute Variance sigma squared B]
    M --> N[Normalize x minus mu over sqrt sigma squared plus epsilon]
    V --> N
    N --> SC[Scale by gamma learnable]
    SC --> SH[Shift by beta learnable]
    SH --> Y[Output Y]

    M --> RM[Update Running Mean]
    V --> RV[Update Running Variance]
    RM -.Inference.-> INF[Test Time Use Running Averages]
    RV -.Inference.-> INF
    INF --> Y

    Y --> NEXT[Feed to Next Layer]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Define regularization in deep learning. Why is it necessary, and what role does the hyperparameter $\lambda$ play?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Regularization is a set of techniques used to **reduce the generalization error** of a deep learning model by imposing additional constraints on the parameters or the training process. **(1 mark)**

It is necessary because deep networks with millions of parameters tend to **overfit** training data, learning noise rather than underlying patterns. Regularization prevents this by introducing controlled bias to significantly reduce variance, leading to better performance on unseen data. **(1 mark)**

The hyperparameter $\lambda \ge 0$ controls the **strength** of the regularization penalty $\Omega(\theta)$. A larger $\lambda$ imposes stronger constraints (less overfitting but possibly underfitting), while $\lambda = 0$ removes regularization entirely. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Differentiate between L1 and L2 regularization in terms of their objective functions, gradients, and effect on weight sparsity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Weight Decay) |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | $J + \lambda\sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ | $J + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum \theta_j^2$ |
| **Gradient** | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j)$ | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda \theta_j$ |
| **Sparsity** | Produces **exact zeros** (sparse) | Shrinks all weights smoothly (dense) |

**(1 mark per row — total 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - When answering regularization derivations, **always start from the full loss function** before adding the penalty — students lose 2 marks for jumping directly to the gradient.
> - For Dropout, students often forget to mention **inverted dropout scaling** (factor of $\frac{1}{p}$). This is worth **1 mark** explicitly.
> - In Batch Norm derivations, mentioning **running averages at inference** is mandatory — failing to do so costs **2 marks**.
> - Never confuse L1's penalty ($\lambda \vert \theta \vert$) with L2's ($\lambda \theta^2/2$). This single mistake forfeits **3 marks** in 14-mark problems.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the gradient descent update rule for **L2 regularization** starting from the regularized objective function. Show that the update can be interpreted as **weight decay** by isolating the factor $(1 - \eta\lambda)$.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Dropout** regularization technique in detail. Derive the expected value of the output of a dropped neuron using the Bernoulli mask, and explain why inverted dropout avoids rescaling at inference time.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Write the regularized objective [1 mark]**

$$J_{L2}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to $\theta_j$ [1 mark]**

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda\theta_j$$

**Step 3: Apply gradient descent [1 mark]**

$$\theta_j^{(t+1)} = \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta\left(\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda\theta_j^{(t)}\right)$$

**Step 4: Isolate the weight decay term [2 marks]**

$$\theta_j^{(t+1)} = \theta_j^{(t)}(1-\eta\lambda) - \eta\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$$

The factor $(1-\eta\lambda)$ shrinks the weight multiplicatively on every step, which is the **weight decay interpretation**.

**Step 5: Steady-state analysis [1 mark]**

Ignoring the data term, the weight evolves as $\theta_j^{(t)} = \theta_j^{(0)}(1-\eta\lambda)^t$, decaying exponentially to zero — confirming "weight decay".

**Step 6: Engineering significance [1 mark]**

L2 regularization penalizes large weights, encouraging smoother decision boundaries and improving generalization. It is the **default regularizer** in most deep learning frameworks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Concept [1 mark]**
Dropout randomly deactivates neurons with probability $(1-p)$ during training, preventing co-adaptation and acting as an ensemble of thinned networks.

**Step 2: Bernoulli mask [1 mark]**
For each neuron $i$, sample $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$.

**Step 3: Apply mask with inversion [1 mark]**

$$\tilde{a}_i = \frac{r_i a_i}{p}$$

**Step 4: Derive expected value [2 marks]**

$$\mathbb{E}[\tilde{a}_i] = \frac{a_i}{p}\mathbb{E}[r_i] = \frac{a_i}{p}\cdot p = a_i$$

**Step 5: Inference simplification [1 mark]**
Because $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{a}_i] = a_i$ during training, the expected output at inference matches the deterministic output — no rescaling needed.

**Step 6: Why it regularizes [1 mark]**
Dropout prevents neurons from co-adapting, encourages redundancy, and can be viewed as **model averaging** over $2^n$ sub-networks, all of which reduces overfitting and improves generalization.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain **Batch Normalization** in detail. Derive the forward-pass equations for a mini-batch and show that the normalized activations have approximately **zero mean and unit variance**.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss **Early Stopping** and **Data Augmentation** as regularization techniques. Compare them in terms of mechanism, cost, and effect on the bias-variance trade-off.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Motivation [1 mark]**
Batch Normalization (BN) addresses the **internal covariate shift** problem — the change in distribution of layer inputs during training — by normalizing activations across a mini-batch.

**Step 2: Mini-batch mean [1 mark]**

$$\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}x_i$$

**Step 3: Mini-batch variance [1 mark]**

$$\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i-\mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$$

**Step 4: Normalize with stability constant [1 mark]**

$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$$

**Step 5: Scale and shift [1 mark]**

$$y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$$

**Step 6: Prove zero mean [1 mark]**

$$\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\hat{x}_i = \frac{1}{m\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i-\mu_{\mathcal{B}}) = 0$$

**Step 7: Prove unit variance [1 mark]**

$$\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\hat{x}_i^2 = \frac{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2}{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon} \approx 1$$

(For $\epsilon \ll \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$, the variance tends to 1.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Early Stopping — Mechanism [2 marks]**
Early stopping monitors validation loss after every epoch. When the validation loss stops improving for $k$ consecutive epochs (patience), training is terminated and the best-performing parameters are restored. It implicitly restricts the number of optimization steps, controlling the model's effective capacity.

**Step 2: Early Stopping — Pros/Cons [1 mark]**
- **Pro:** Free, requires no change to model or loss; computationally simple.
- **Con:** Requires a separate validation set; may stop too early or too late.

**Step 3: Data Augmentation — Mechanism [2 marks]**
Data Augmentation artificially enlarges the training set by applying label-preserving transformations $T(x)$ (rotations, flips, crops for images; synonym replacement for text). The effective dataset becomes $\mathcal{D}_{aug} = \{(T(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})\}$.

**Step 4: Data Augmentation — Pros/Cons [1 mark]**
- **Pro:** Increases effective dataset size, reduces variance, encourages invariance.
- **Con:** Requires domain knowledge to choose meaningful transformations; may add computational overhead.

**Step 5: Bias-Variance comparison [1 mark]**
Both techniques **reduce variance** at the cost of slightly **increased bias** (Early Stopping: limits the optimization trajectory; Augmentation: regularizes via more data). However, both are non-intrusive — they do not modify the model architecture or loss function explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Regularization**

- [x] **Definition:** Regularization = techniques to reduce generalization error by constraining model capacity.
- [x] **Overfitting Symptom:** Large gap between training and validation error; low bias + high variance.
- [x] **L2 Penalty:** $\frac{\lambda}{2}\sum \theta_j^2$; update has factor $(1-\eta\lambda)$ — weight decay.
- [x] **L1 Penalty:** $\lambda\sum \vert \theta_j \vert$; sub-gradient is $\text{sign}(\theta_j)$; produces sparsity.
- [x] **Dropout Formula:** $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$; $\tilde{a}_i = r_i a_i / p$; expected output equals $a_i$.
- [x] **Dropout Inference:** Use the full network **without rescaling** (inverted dropout).
- [x] **Batch Norm Train:** Compute $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ and $\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$ per mini-batch, normalize, then scale/shift.
- [x] **Batch Norm Inference:** Use running averages of $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ (momentum $\alpha \in \{0.9, 0.99\}$).
- [x] **Early Stopping:** Monitors validation loss; restores best weights when patience $k$ is exhausted.
- [x] **Data Augmentation:** Label-preserving transformations expand $\mathcal{D}$ → reduces variance.
- [x] **Bias-Variance Trade-off:** Regularization increases bias to dramatically reduce variance.
- [x] **Key Hyperparameters:** $\lambda$ (penalty), $p$ (dropout keep prob, often **0.5**), $\epsilon$ (**$10^{-5}$**), patience $k$.
- [x] **Default Choices:** L2 + Dropout (FC layers) + Batch Norm (CNNs) + Early Stopping (universal).
- [x] **Sparsity Distinction:** Only **L1** produces exact zeros; L2 shrinks smoothly; Dropout is implicit.
- [x] **Ensemble View of Dropout:** Equivalent to $2^n$ sub-networks sharing parameters.
- [x] **Numerical Stability:** Always include $\epsilon$ in $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *"explain any two regularization techniques"*, present L2 + Dropout (most common) or L2 + Batch Norm (trending in modern papers). Always state: (i) the formula, (ii) the gradient/update, (iii) the regularization effect, (iv) a real-world example. This 4-point structure guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Augmentation: Zooming, Rotation, Cropping, Blurring, and Noise Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Augmentation: Zooming, Rotation, Cropping, Blurring, and Noise Addition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Augmentation** is a strategic regularization and generalization technique in deep learning wherein the effective size and diversity of a training dataset are artificially expanded by applying a series of label-preserving geometric, photometric, and stochastic transformations to the original input samples. In the context of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Computer Vision tasks, augmentation operates as an inductive bias — it forces the model to learn **invariance properties** against nuisance variations such as scale, orientation, translation, illumination, and sensor noise.

Formally, given an input image $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ with label $y$, an augmentation function family $\mathcal{T} = \{T_1, T_2, \dots, T_K\}$ generates a synthetic dataset:

$$\mathcal{D}_{aug} = \mathcal{D}_{orig} \cup \{ (T_k(\mathbf{X}_i), y_i) \mid \mathbf{X}_i \in \mathcal{D}_{orig}, \, T_k \in \mathcal{T} \}$$

The expected empirical risk is minimized over the augmented set:

$$\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{X}, y) \sim \mathcal{D}_{aug}} \left[ \mathcal{L}\big(f_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}), y\big) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Data augmentation is listed under *Module 3 – CNN and Computer Vision Applications*. Students must know the **five canonical geometric/photometric augmentations** — Zooming, Rotation, Cropping, Blurring, and Noise Addition — along with their effect on tensor dimensions and model invariance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine teaching a child to recognize a *chair*. If the child sees a chair **only from the front**, they will fail to recognize it when seen from the side, top, or partially hidden behind a table. Data augmentation is the equivalent of showing the child the same chair from **many different angles, distances, lighting conditions, and with mild obstructions**. The underlying semantic content (it is still a chair) remains unchanged — only the *appearance* is perturbed.

- **Zooming** ≈ The child walks closer to / further from the chair.
- **Rotation** ≈ The child turns around while still looking at the chair.
- **Cropping** ≈ The child sees only the chair leg, or only the backrest.
- **Blurring** ≈ The child looks at the chair through a foggy window.
- **Noise Addition** ≈ The child's eyes are slightly strained, adding grain to the vision.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Invariances (in bold)

- **Scale Invariance** — learned through *Zooming*
- **Rotation Invariance** — learned through *Rotation*
- **Translation Invariance** — learned through *Cropping*
- **Blur Robustness** — learned through *Blurring*
- **Noise Robustness** — learned through *Noise Addition*

> [!NOTE]
> A network trained **without** augmentation is essentially memorizing the training set pixel patterns (a phenomenon called *shortcut learning*). Augmentation breaks this memorization and forces the network to learn **semantic features**.

### 1.4 Five Canonical Augmentation Families

| # | Augmentation | Category | Type of Invariance Gained |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Zooming | Geometric (Scale) | Scale |
| 2 | Rotation | Geometric (Orientation) | Rotation |
| 3 | Cropping | Geometric (Spatial) | Translation / Occlusion |
| 4 | Blurring | Photometric (Low-pass filter) | Defocus robustness |
| 5 | Noise Addition | Photometric (Stochastic) | Sensor / quantization noise |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Zooming (Scale Transformation)

**Definition:** Zooming modifies the spatial extent of an object within the image by either magnifying (zoom-in) or shrinking (zoom-out) a region of interest, while the output canvas size remains fixed.

**Mathematical Formulation:**
Given an image $\mathbf{X}$ of dimensions $H \times W$, a zoom factor $z > 0$ produces a rescaled image $\mathbf{X}'$ where the new dimensions are:

$$H' = \left\lfloor \frac{H}{z} \right\rfloor, \quad W' = \left\lfloor \frac{W}{z} \right\rfloor$$

The rescaled image is then either:
- **Zoom-out ($z > 1$):** Padded with the chosen *fill mode* (constant, reflect, wrap) to match $H \times W$.
- **Zoom-in ($z < 1$):** Cropped centrally to $H \times W$.

**Why it works:** Forces the CNN to detect features at **multiple receptive field scales**, mitigating overfitting on objects of a single fixed size.

**How it works (step-by-step):**
1. Extract a sub-region of size $H' \times W'$ from the original image.
2. Resize this sub-region back to $H \times W$ using bilinear or bicubic interpolation.
3. For zoom-out, pad the periphery with the chosen fill mode.

> [!TIP]
> Standard practice: $z \in [0.8, 1.2]$ — aggressive zooms ($z < 0.5$ or $z > 2.0$) distort the semantic content.

### 2.2 Rotation

**Definition:** Rotation rotates the image around a pivot point (typically the geometric center) by an angle $\theta \in [0°, 360°]$.

**Mathematical Formulation:**
The 2D rotation matrix is:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

For each pixel coordinate $(x, y)$ in the output, the source coordinate is computed via the **inverse mapping**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_{src} \\ y_{src} \end{bmatrix} = R(-\theta) \begin{bmatrix} x_{dst} - c_x \\ y_{dst} - c_y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} c_x \\ c_y \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(c_x, c_y) = \left( \frac{W-1}{2}, \frac{H-1}{2} \right)$ is the image center.

**Why it works:** CNNs are **not inherently rotation-invariant**. A digit "6" rotated 180° looks like a "9". By training on rotated samples, the network learns rotational symmetry.

**How it works:**
1. Compute the inverse rotation matrix $R(-\theta)$.
2. For each output pixel, map back to source coordinates.
3. Use bilinear interpolation to compute pixel values at non-integer source coordinates.
4. Fill the resulting corner gaps (created because rotation enlarges the bounding box) using the chosen fill mode.

> [!WARNING]
> Rotating by arbitrary angles (e.g., 37°) is computationally expensive due to non-integer pixel mapping. Most frameworks snap to common angles: 90°, 180°, 270° for lossless integer-pixel rotation, or use small angles ($\pm 15°$) for fine-grained invariance.

### 2.3 Cropping

**Definition:** Cropping extracts a sub-region of the image, discarding the periphery. It simulates **partial observability** and **object translation** within the frame.

**Mathematical Formulation:**
A crop is defined by a bounding box $(x_0, y_0, w_c, h_c)$ where:
- $(x_0, y_0)$ is the top-left corner.
- $w_c$ and $h_c$ are the crop width and height.

Constraints:
$$0 \leq x_0 \leq W - w_c, \quad 0 \leq y_0 \leq H - h_c$$

The cropped image is then **resized** back to the target dimensions $H \times W$ to maintain batch tensor compatibility.

**Variants:**
- **Center Crop:** $x_0 = \lfloor (W - w_c)/2 \rfloor$, $y_0 = \lfloor (H - h_c)/2 \rfloor$.
- **Random Crop:** $(x_0, y_0)$ sampled uniformly from the feasible region.
- **Five-Crop / Ten-Crop:** Extract 4 corners + center (and their horizontal flips for Ten-Crop) — common in evaluation protocols.
- **Random Resized Crop (RRC):** Used by *torchvision* and *timm*; crop a random region with area $\in [a, 1]$ of the original, then resize.

**Why it works:** The CNN learns to recognize objects even when they are **partially occluded or shifted** within the frame, emulating translation invariance that pure weight-sharing convolutions only partially provide.

### 2.4 Blurring

**Definition:** Blurring applies a low-pass filter to the image, suppressing high-frequency components (edges, fine textures) and preserving low-frequency content (overall shapes, color blobs).

**Mathematical Formulation — 2D Convolution:**

$$\mathbf{X}'(i, j) = \sum_{m=-k}^{k} \sum_{n=-k}^{k} \mathbf{K}(m, n) \cdot \mathbf{X}(i+m, j+n)$$

where $\mathbf{K}$ is a $(2k+1) \times (2k+1)$ kernel.

**Common Blur Kernels:**

**Gaussian Blur** (most common):

$$\mathbf{K}_{Gauss}(m, n) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

with normalization $\sum_{m,n} \mathbf{K}(m, n) = 1$.

**Box / Average Blur:**

$$\mathbf{K}_{box}(m, n) = \frac{1}{(2k+1)^2}$$

**Median Blur:** Replaces each pixel with the median of its neighborhood — excellent for **salt-and-pepper noise** removal.

**Why it works:** Real-world images are often out-of-focus or captured under motion. Training on blurred versions forces the model to rely on **macro-structures** rather than fine pixel-level patterns, improving robustness to defocus and motion blur.

### 2.5 Noise Addition

**Definition:** Noise addition injects stochastic perturbations into the image, simulating sensor noise, quantization artifacts, and transmission errors.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\mathbf{X}'(i, j) = \mathbf{X}(i, j) + \boldsymbol{\eta}(i, j)$$

where $\boldsymbol{\eta}$ is the noise tensor.

**Common Noise Models:**

**Gaussian Noise:**

$$\boldsymbol{\eta}(i, j) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2)$$

PDF: $p(\eta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_n} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\eta^2}{2\sigma_n^2}\right)$

**Salt-and-Pepper Noise (Impulse Noise):**

$$P(\boldsymbol{\eta} = 255) = P(\boldsymbol{\eta} = 0) = \frac{p}{2}, \quad P(\boldsymbol{\eta} = 0) = 1 - p$$

where $p \in [0, 1]$ is the noise density.

**Shot / Poisson Noise:** Modeled as $\boldsymbol{\eta}(i,j) \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$ where $\lambda$ is the expected photon count — represents real sensor noise.

**Speckle Noise (Multiplicative):**

$$\mathbf{X}'(i, j) = \mathbf{X}(i, j) \cdot (1 + \boldsymbol{\eta}(i, j)), \quad \boldsymbol{\eta} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

**Why it works:** Augmenting with noise acts as a form of **data-driven regularization**, similar in spirit to dropout but applied at the input layer. The model is forced to denoise implicitly, learning smoother decision boundaries.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All variables below are exam-ready. Memorize the table — at least 2 questions per ESE cycle reference these directly.

| Augmentation | Core Equation | Key Parameter | Output Dim | Standard Range |
|--------------|---------------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| Zooming | $H' = \lfloor H/z \rfloor$ | Zoom factor $z$ | $H \times W$ (preserved) | $z \in [0.8, 1.2]$ |
| Rotation | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | Angle $\theta$ | $H \times W$ (preserved, padded) | $\theta \in [-30°, 30°]$ |
| Cropping | $0 \le x_0 \le W - w_c$ | Crop size $w_c, h_c$ | Resized to $H \times W$ | $w_c = 0.6W$ to $0.9W$ |
| Blurring (Gaussian) | $\mathbf{K}(m,n) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} e^{-\frac{m^2+n^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Std. dev. $\sigma$ | $H \times W$ (preserved) | $\sigma \in [0.5, 2.0]$ |
| Blurring (Box) | $\mathbf{K}(m,n) = \frac{1}{(2k+1)^2}$ | Kernel size $k$ | $H \times W$ (preserved) | $k \in \{3, 5, 7\}$ |
| Noise (Gaussian) | $\boldsymbol{\eta} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2)$ | Noise std. $\sigma_n$ | $H \times W$ (preserved) | $\sigma_n \in [0.01, 0.1]$ |
| Noise (S&P) | $P(\boldsymbol{\eta}=255)=P(\boldsymbol{\eta}=0)=p/2$ | Density $p$ | $H \times W$ (preserved) | $p \in [0.01, 0.05]$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (X-ray, MRI):** Limited labeled data — augmentation is mandatory. Rotation and noise addition simulate different scanner calibrations and patient positions.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Cropping simulates partial occlusions; blur simulates fog, defocus, motion; noise simulates low-light sensor noise.
- **Satellite / Remote Sensing:** Rotation and zoom simulate arbitrary satellite pass orientations and altitudes.
- **Industrial Defect Detection:** Noise addition simulates real factory-floor sensor noise; blur simulates out-of-focus conveyor-belt cameras.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Bilinear Interpolation in Zooming

When zooming, the output image is generated by resampling the input. For an output pixel at fractional source coordinate $(x_{src}, y_{src})$, we need to compute the pixel value via interpolation.

Let $x_1 = \lfloor x_{src} \rfloor$, $x_2 = x_1 + 1$, and fractional part $\alpha = x_{src} - x_1$. Similarly for the y-axis: $y_1, y_2, \beta$.

**Step 1:** Linear interpolation along x-axis (top row):

$$I_{top} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mathbf{X}(x_1, y_1) + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{X}(x_2, y_1)$$

**Step 2:** Linear interpolation along x-axis (bottom row):

$$I_{bot} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mathbf{X}(x_1, y_2) + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{X}(x_2, y_2)$$

**Step 3:** Linear interpolation along y-axis (final result):

$$\mathbf{X}'(x_{src}, y_{src}) = (1 - \beta) \cdot I_{top} + \beta \cdot I_{bot}$$

**Combining into a single equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}'(x_{src}, y_{src}) = \; & (1-\beta)(1-\alpha)\mathbf{X}(x_1, y_1) + (1-\beta)\alpha\mathbf{X}(x_2, y_1) \\
& + \beta(1-\alpha)\mathbf{X}(x_1, y_2) + \beta\alpha\mathbf{X}(x_2, y_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Step 1 fixes the y-coordinate and interpolates horizontally using the two nearest x-neighbors. Step 2 does the same for the next row down. Step 3 then blends the two horizontally-interpolated rows vertically. This is the **separable property** of bilinear interpolation — it equals 2 sequential 1D linear interpolations.

### 3.2 Derivation — Inverse Mapping for Rotation

A naive forward mapping (scan source pixels, push to destination) creates holes. Standard practice uses **inverse mapping**: scan destination pixels, pull from source.

**Step 1:** For each destination pixel $(x_{dst}, y_{dst})$, translate to image-centered coordinates:

$$x_c = x_{dst} - c_x, \quad y_c = y_{dst} - c_y$$

**Step 2:** Apply the inverse rotation $R(-\theta)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{src, c} &= x_c \cos\theta + y_c \sin\theta \\
y_{src, c} &= -x_c \sin\theta + y_c \cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Translate back to image-origin coordinates:

$$x_{src} = x_{src, c} + c_x, \quad y_{src} = y_{src, c} + c_y$$

**Step 4:** Boundary check — if $(x_{src}, y_{src})$ lies outside the original image, fill with the chosen padding strategy (constant 0, reflect, replicate, wrap).

**Step 5:** If $(x_{src}, y_{src})$ is non-integer, apply bilinear interpolation (see §3.1).

**Conversion Logic:** This guarantees every destination pixel gets a defined value (no holes). The use of $R(-\theta)$ instead of $R(\theta)$ is the key trick that makes the mapping **bijective** for the output domain — we ask "where did this output pixel come from?" rather than "where does this source pixel go?".

### 3.3 Derivation — Gaussian Kernel Normalization

A raw Gaussian function $G(m, n) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ sums to a value $S > 1$. To use it as a convolution kernel (which must preserve image brightness), we normalize:

**Step 1:** Compute the raw sum:

$$S = \sum_{m=-k}^{k} \sum_{n=-k}^{k} \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Divide by $S$ to obtain a normalized kernel:

$$\mathbf{K}(m, n) = \frac{1}{S} \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Verify the constraint:

$$\sum_{m=-k}^{k} \sum_{n=-k}^{k} \mathbf{K}(m, n) = 1$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Gaussian kernel acts as a **weighted average** of neighboring pixels. If the weights did not sum to 1, blurring would change the overall image brightness (a DC offset). Normalization ensures **energy preservation**.

### 3.4 Derivation — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for Noise Augmentation

When training with added noise, the relevant figure of merit is the SNR:

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{\sigma_{signal}^2}{\sigma_{noise}^2} = 20 \log_{10} \frac{\sigma_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}$$

For a normalized image with $\sigma_{signal} \approx 0.5$ (after scaling to $[0,1]$):

| $\sigma_{noise}$ | SNR (dB) | Perceptual Quality |
|------------------|----------|---------------------|
| 0.01 | 34.0 dB | Pristine |
| 0.05 | 20.0 dB | Slight grain |
| 0.10 | 14.0 dB | Visible noise |
| 0.20 | 8.0 dB | Heavy noise |
| 0.30 | 4.4 dB | Severe degradation |

**Conversion Logic:** $\log_{10}$ of a power ratio gives bels; multiplying by 10 converts to decibels. The factor of 20 (instead of 10) appears when comparing **amplitudes** (voltages) rather than powers.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Augmentation Effect on Feature Maps

For a CNN layer with input feature map $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ and a stride-1 convolution with $3 \times 3$ kernel, augmentation effect on the **receptive field** is:

| Augmentation | Effect on Feature Map | Receptive Field Implication |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Zoom-in ($z < 1$) | Object occupies more pixels | Larger effective receptive field needed |
| Zoom-out ($z > 1$) | Object occupies fewer pixels | Smaller features must still be detected |
| Rotation | Pixel coordinates permuted | Weights must be equivariant OR data must cover all angles |
| Cropping | Spatial extent reduced | Forces partial-occlusion robustness |
| Blurring | High-frequency content suppressed | Forces reliance on low-frequency content |
| Noise | Per-pixel perturbation | Smooths loss landscape, regularization effect |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Data Augmentation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Image X] --> B[Augmentation Manager]
    B --> C1[Zooming Module]
    B --> C2[Rotation Module]
    B --> C3[Cropping Module]
    B --> C4[Blurring Module]
    B --> C5[Noise Addition Module]
    C1 --> D1[Rescaled Tensor HxW]
    C2 --> D2[Rotated Tensor HxW]
    C3 --> D3[Cropped and Resized Tensor HxW]
    C4 --> D4[Convolved Tensor HxW]
    C5 --> D5[Noise-Perturbed Tensor HxW]
    D1 --> E[Concat Augmented Batch]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[CNN Forward Pass]
    F --> G[Loss Computation]
    G --> H[Backpropagation and Weight Update]
    H --> I[Updated Model Weights]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Choosing Augmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Augmentation Strategy] --> B{What Invariance is Needed?}
    B -->|Scale| C[Apply Zooming z in 0.8 to 1.2]
    B -->|Orientation| D[Apply Rotation theta in -30 to 30 deg]
    B -->|Translation| E[Apply Random Crop w_c 0.6W to 0.9W]
    B -->|Defocus| F[Apply Gaussian Blur sigma in 0.5 to 2.0]
    B -->|Sensor Noise| G[Apply Gaussian Noise sigma_n in 0.01 to 0.1]
    C --> H[Combine into Compose Pipeline]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Validate on Held-Out Set]
    I --> J{Validation Accuracy Improved?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Keep Augmentation Config]
    J -->|No| L[Reduce Augmentation Intensity]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Pixel Coordinate Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Pixel xs, ys] --> B[Translate to Center]
    B --> C[Apply Rotation Matrix R of theta]
    C --> D[Translate Back to Origin]
    D --> E[Destination Pixel xd, yd]
    E --> F{In Bounds?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Assign Pixel Value]
    F -->|No| H[Apply Fill Mode]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Pipeline]
        raw[Raw Image Batch]
        norm[Normalize to 0-1]
    end
    subgraph GeoOps[Geometric Augmentations]
        zoom[Zooming Rescale]
        rot[Rotation Affine]
        crop[Cropping Resize]
    end
    subgraph PhotoOps[Photometric Augmentations]
        blur[Blurring Convolution]
        noise[Noise Addition]
    end
    subgraph TrainLoop[Training Loop]
        fwd[Forward Pass]
        loss[Loss Computation]
        bwd[Backpropagation]
        opt[Optimizer Step]
    end
    raw --> norm
    norm --> zoom
    norm --> rot
    norm --> crop
    norm --> blur
    norm --> noise
    zoom --> fwd
    rot --> fwd
    crop --> fwd
    blur --> fwd
    noise --> fwd
    fwd --> loss
    loss --> bwd
    bwd --> opt
    opt --> fwd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define data augmentation. List any four commonly used image augmentation techniques.*

**Model Answer:**

Data augmentation is a regularization strategy that artificially expands the training dataset by applying label-preserving transformations to the original samples, thereby improving model generalization and reducing overfitting.

Four commonly used image augmentation techniques are:
1. **Zooming** — modifies the scale of the object.
2. **Rotation** — rotates the image by an angle.
3. **Cropping** — extracts a sub-region and resizes it.
4. **Blurring** — applies a low-pass filter to the image.

*(A 5th valid answer is Noise Addition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of Gaussian noise injection during training? State the PDF of Gaussian noise.*

**Model Answer:**

Gaussian noise injection during training acts as a form of **data-driven regularization**. It forces the model to learn smoother decision boundaries, reduces overfitting, and improves robustness to real-world sensor noise.

The Probability Density Function (PDF) of Gaussian noise is:

$$p(\eta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_n} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\eta^2}{2\sigma_n^2}\right)$$

where $\sigma_n$ is the noise standard deviation and $\eta$ is the noise sample.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided as per KTU ESE Module Pattern**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the inverse mapping equations for image rotation around the image center. Explain why inverse mapping is preferred over forward mapping with a forward mapping diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of Gaussian blurring as a convolution operation. Derive the normalized Gaussian kernel and discuss the effect of the standard deviation $\sigma$ on the degree of blur. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Define the rotation matrix:**
For an angle $\theta$, the standard 2D rotation matrix is:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Translate to image-centered coordinates:**
Let the image center be $(c_x, c_y) = \left(\frac{W-1}{2}, \frac{H-1}{2}\right)$. For a destination pixel $(x_d, y_d)$:

$$x_c = x_d - c_x, \quad y_c = y_d - c_y$$

**Step 3 — Apply inverse rotation:**
To find the source pixel that maps to this destination, we use $R(-\theta)$:

$$R(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute source coordinates:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{s, c} &= x_c \cos\theta + y_c \sin\theta \\
y_{s, c} &= -x_c \sin\theta + y_c \cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Translate back to image origin:**

$$x_s = x_{s, c} + c_x, \quad y_s = y_{s, c} + c_y$$

**Why Inverse Mapping is Preferred:**
In forward mapping, we scan each source pixel and compute its destination — but due to rounding and the non-invertible nature of rotation, **holes** appear in the output (pixels that are never written). In inverse mapping, we scan every destination pixel and pull from a source — **every output pixel gets a defined value** (using padding for out-of-bounds). This guarantees a **complete, hole-free rotated image**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Convolution Definition:**
For an image $\mathbf{X}$ and kernel $\mathbf{K}$ of size $(2k+1) \times (2k+1)$:

$$\mathbf{X}'(i, j) = \sum_{m=-k}^{k} \sum_{n=-k}^{k} \mathbf{K}(m, n) \cdot \mathbf{X}(i+m, j+n)$$

**Step 2 — Raw Gaussian Function:**

$$G(m, n) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Normalization Sum:**

$$S = \sum_{m=-k}^{k} \sum_{n=-k}^{k} \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Normalized Kernel:**

$$\mathbf{K}(m, n) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2 \cdot S_{unit}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

with $\sum_{m,n} \mathbf{K}(m, n) = 1$.

**Step 5 — Effect of $\sigma$:**
- **Small $\sigma$ ($\sigma < 1$):** Kernel is sharply peaked — weak blur, almost identity.
- **Medium $\sigma$ ($\sigma \approx 1.5$):** Balanced blur — preserves edges while removing noise.
- **Large $\sigma$ ($\sigma > 3$):** Kernel becomes wide — heavy blur, significant edge loss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the five major image augmentation techniques: Zooming, Rotation, Cropping, Blurring, and Noise Addition. For each technique, state the type of invariance it imparts and the typical parameter range used in practice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, derive the effect of adding Gaussian noise to a training image. Compute the SNR (in dB) for a normalized image with $\sigma_{signal} = 0.5$ and $\sigma_{noise} = 0.05$. Justify why SNR in the range 20–30 dB is recommended for augmentation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Technique | Type of Invariance | Typical Parameter Range | Effect |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|--------|
| **Zooming** | Scale invariance | $z \in [0.8, 1.2]$ | Forces feature detection at multiple scales |
| **Rotation** | Rotation invariance | $\theta \in [-30°, 30°]$ | Forces orientation-independent recognition |
| **Cropping** | Translation invariance | $w_c \in [0.6W, 0.9W]$ | Forces robustness to partial visibility |
| **Blurring** | Defocus invariance | $\sigma \in [0.5, 2.0]$ | Forces reliance on macro-structures |
| **Noise Addition** | Sensor noise invariance | $\sigma_n \in [0.01, 0.1]$ | Regularizes the loss landscape |

**Detailed Discussion:**

1. **Zooming** — The zoom factor $z$ rescales a sub-region. A factor $z > 1$ zooms out (object smaller); $z < 1$ zooms in (object larger). The rescaled region is then padded or cropped to match the original canvas size.

2. **Rotation** — Applied via the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$. Beyond $\pm 30°$, the semantic content (e.g., digits, faces) may invert and confuse the network. Therefore, mild rotations are preferred for fine-grained invariance.

3. **Cropping** — Random crop extracts a sub-region of size $w_c \times h_c$, then resizes to $H \times W$. This simulates objects appearing at different positions and partial occlusions.

4. **Blurring** — Gaussian blurring convolves the image with a 2D Gaussian kernel. Larger $\sigma$ produces stronger blur, attenuating high-frequency content.

5. **Noise Addition** — Gaussian noise $\boldsymbol{\eta} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2)$ is added per-pixel. Acts as input-layer regularization, smoothing the loss landscape.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Noise Addition Equation:**

$$\mathbf{X}'(i, j) = \mathbf{X}(i, j) + \boldsymbol{\eta}(i, j), \quad \boldsymbol{\eta} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2)$$

**Step 2 — SNR Definition (Power form):**

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{\sigma_{signal}^2}{\sigma_{noise}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Given Values:**

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{(0.5)^2}{(0.05)^2} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{0.25}{0.0025} = 10 \log_{10}(100)$$

**Step 4 — Final Computation:**

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \times 2 = \mathbf{20 \; dB}$$

**Justification for 20–30 dB Range:**
- **Below 20 dB:** The noise is perceptually heavy; the network may struggle to learn meaningful patterns because the signal is corrupted.
- **Above 30 dB:** The noise is too mild to provide meaningful regularization — the model behaves as if trained on clean data.
- **In the 20–30 dB range:** Noise is visible enough to force the model to learn denoising implicitly, but not so severe that it destroys semantic content. This is the **sweet spot** for data augmentation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Data Augmentation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting normalization** of the Gaussian kernel — if $\sum \mathbf{K} \neq 1$, the image brightness will shift. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if normalization is skipped.
> 2. **Confusing forward and inverse mapping** in rotation — writing $R(\theta)$ instead of $R(-\theta)$ in the source coordinate computation is a **fatal error**, costing up to **3 marks**.
> 3. **Missing the boundary handling step** — students often forget to address out-of-bounds pixels. Mentioning fill mode (constant, reflect, replicate) is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 4. **Not stating the parameter range** — for each augmentation, examiners expect the typical range (e.g., $z \in [0.8, 1.2]$). Omitting this loses **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing SNR formula** — using amplitude form ($20\log$) when power form ($10\log$) is required (or vice versa) costs **1 mark**.
> 6. **No mention of invariance** — augmentation questions always carry a sub-part on "what invariance is gained". Skipping this loses **2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Preparation**

- ✅ **Data Augmentation** = Label-preserving transformations to expand dataset diversity and improve generalization.
- ✅ **Five core augmentations:** Zooming, Rotation, Cropping, Blurring, Noise Addition.
- ✅ **Zooming** uses factor $z$; zoom-out pads, zoom-in crops. Standard range $z \in [0.8, 1.2]$.
- ✅ **Rotation** uses matrix $R(\theta)$; **inverse mapping** is preferred to avoid holes.
- ✅ **Cropping** extracts $(x_0, y_0, w_c, h_c)$; resized to maintain output dimensions.
- ✅ **Blurring** is a 2D convolution; Gaussian kernel must be **normalized** ($\sum \mathbf{K} = 1$).
- ✅ **Noise Addition** is $\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{X} + \boldsymbol{\eta}$; Gaussian PDF: $p(\eta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_n} e^{-\eta^2/2\sigma_n^2}$.
- ✅ **Invariance mapping:** Zooming → Scale, Rotation → Orientation, Cropping → Translation, Blurring → Defocus, Noise → Sensor robustness.
- ✅ **SNR formula:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma_{signal}^2 / \sigma_{noise}^2)$; recommended range **20–30 dB**.
- ✅ **Parameter ranges to memorize:** $z \in [0.8, 1.2]$, $\theta \in [-30°, 30°]$, $w_c \in [0.6W, 0.9W]$, $\sigma \in [0.5, 2.0]$, $\sigma_n \in [0.01, 0.1]$.
- ✅ **Inverse mapping** is essential in rotation to prevent holes; forward mapping is rejected.
- ✅ **Bilinear interpolation** is the workhorse for both zoom and rotation resampling.
- ✅ Augmentation acts as **input-layer regularization** — analogous in spirit to dropout but applied at the data level.
- ✅ Always mention **fill mode** for rotation/zoom-out (constant, reflect, replicate, wrap).
- ✅ Real-world applications: medical imaging, autonomous driving, satellite imagery, industrial defect detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-Supervision Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Self-Supervision Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)** is a representation learning paradigm in which a deep neural network is trained on **unlabeled data** by automatically generating **pseudo-labels (supervisory signals)** from the raw input itself. The model formulates an auxiliary *pretext task* whose targets are derived from the structure of the data, and in solving that task, the network is forced to learn rich, transferable feature representations that can later be fine-tuned for downstream supervised tasks (classification, detection, segmentation, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> *Self-supervised learning is the practice of learning useful representations of data by solving auxiliary "pretext" tasks whose labels are generated automatically from the data, without requiring human annotation.*

Formally, given an unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$, SSL constructs a pseudo-label function $f_{\text{label}}(\cdot)$ such that:

$$\hat{y}_i = f_{\text{label}}(x_i, \tilde{x}_i)$$

where $\tilde{x}_i$ is a transformed/augmented view of $x_i$, and the model learns parameters $\theta$ by minimizing:

$$\min_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}\big(g_\theta(f_{\text{label}}(x_i, \tilde{x}_i)), \; \hat{y}_i\big)$$

### 1.2 Position in the Learning Landscape

| Learning Paradigm | Data Labels Required | Source of Supervision |
|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Supervised Learning** | Yes (manual annotation) | Human-provided labels $y$ |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | No | No explicit objective (e.g., clustering) |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | No (uses rewards) | Environment reward signal |
| **Self-Supervised Learning** | **No human labels** | **Pseudo-labels from data itself** |

> [!NOTE]
> Self-supervised learning is a **sub-category of unsupervised learning**, distinguished by the use of a structured pretext objective — it is not "label-free" in spirit, only in human-effort.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Curious Toddler" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a child who has never been taught what a "cat" is by an adult (no labels), but who observes millions of objects in the world.**

The child naturally plays **games with the world** to build understanding:
- **Rotation Game**: "If I rotate this toy car, it still looks like the same car." → Learns **invariance**.
- **Jigsaw Game**: "These puzzle pieces must fit together to form a coherent picture." → Learns **spatial coherence**.
- **Colorization Game**: "This grayscale photo of a sunset should have orange and red tones." → Learns **semantic priors**.
- **Prediction Game**: "If I cover half a dog's face, the other half should still be a dog." → Learns **contextual reasoning**.

A self-supervised model is exactly this **curious toddler**: it creates its own "questions" and "answers" from raw data, and through millions of such self-generated puzzles, it builds a deep, general understanding that later makes it extremely sample-efficient when finally given a few real labels (the *linear evaluation protocol*).

### 1.4 The Two Pillars of Modern SSL

Modern self-supervision in computer vision and language domains rests on two foundational pillars:

1. **Pretext-Task Based SSL (Generative / Predictive)**
   - The model solves a hand-crafted auxiliary task.
   - Examples: image rotation prediction, jigsaw puzzle solving, masked patch prediction (MAE), colorization, context encoders.
   - Pseudo-labels are the *attributes* of the transformation applied.

2. **Contrastive / Invariance-Based SSL**
   - The model learns an **embedding space** where augmented views of the *same* sample are pulled together (positive pairs) and views of *different* samples are pushed apart (negative pairs).
   - Examples: **SimCLR, MoCo, MoCo-v2/v3, PIRL, CPC**.
   - Objective is typically the **InfoNCE** or **NT-Xent** loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Non-contrastive methods (BYOL, SimSiam, DINO, Barlow Twins, VICReg) form a modern third category that **removes the explicit negatives** and still achieves strong performance, often surpassing contrastive baselines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pretext Tasks — The Classical Self-Supervision Toolkit

A **pretext task** is a surrogate objective whose solution forces the network to learn semantically meaningful features. The pseudo-labels are produced by a deterministic transformation $T(x)$ of the input.

#### 2.1.1 Image Rotation Prediction (Gidaris et al., 2018)
- Input image $x$ is rotated by $\{0^\circ, 90^\circ, 180^\circ, 270^\circ\}$.
- The model must predict the rotation angle as a **4-class classification** problem.
- **Why it works**: To know the angle, the network must understand the *semantic orientation* of objects (a face is upright, the sky is on top).

#### 2.1.2 Jigsaw Puzzle Solving (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016)
- Image is divided into a $3 \times 3$ grid; tiles are shuffled.
- The model predicts the **permutation index** of the shuffled arrangement (one of $9!$ permutations).
- **Why it works**: Forces the network to learn **spatial relationships** between object parts.

#### 2.1.3 Colorization (Zhang et al., 2016)
- Convert RGB image to Lab color space; provide only the L (lightness) channel.
- Network predicts the **a, b** color channels.
- **Why it works**: Correct colorization requires understanding object semantics (grass is green, sky is blue).

#### 2.1.4 Masked Image Modeling — Masked Autoencoder (MAE, He et al., 2022)
- Randomly mask $\sim 75\%$ of image patches.
- Encoder processes visible patches; a lightweight decoder reconstructs the masked pixels.
- Loss is **Mean Squared Error** on masked pixels.
- **Why it works**: Reconstruction requires holistic scene understanding.

#### 2.1.5 Context Encoders (Pathak et al., 2016)
- Remove a central region of the image.
- Network inpaints the missing region using surrounding context.
- Loss combines **reconstruction L2** + **adversarial loss**.

### 2.2 Contrastive Learning — The Invariance Principle

The **core principle** of contrastive learning is the **Invariance Hypothesis**:

> *Two augmented views of the same underlying image should produce similar representations in the embedding space, while views from different images should be dissimilar.*

Mathematically, given a batch of $N$ images $\{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$, we apply two stochastic augmentations $t \sim \mathcal{T}$ and $t' \sim \mathcal{T}$ to produce $2N$ views:

$$x_i^1 = t(x_i), \qquad x_i^2 = t'(x_i)$$

Each view is encoded by a shared backbone $f(\cdot)$ and projected by a head $g(\cdot)$ to embeddings $z_i^1 = g(f(x_i^1))$ and $z_i^2 = g(f(x_i^2))$.

### 2.3 The InfoNCE Family of Losses

#### 2.3.1 Cosine Similarity
The standard similarity measure between two $L_2$-normalized embeddings $u, v$:

$$\text{sim}(u, v) = \frac{u^\top v}{\Vert u \Vert_2 \, \Vert v \Vert_2} = u^\top v \quad \text{(after normalization)}$$

#### 2.3.2 InfoNCE Loss (van den Oord et al., 2018)
For an anchor $z_i$, a positive $z_j$, and $K$ negatives $\{z_k\}_{k=1}^{K}$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{InfoNCE}}^{(i,j)} = -\log \frac{\exp\!\big(\text{sim}(z_i, z_j) / \tau\big)}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]} \exp\!\big(\text{sim}(z_i, z_k) / \tau\big)}$$

where $\tau$ is the **temperature hyperparameter** (typically $0.05 \leq \tau \leq 0.5$).

#### 2.3.3 SimCLR's NT-Xent Loss (Normalized Temperature-scaled Cross-Entropy)
With $2N$ augmented views in a batch, the loss for the pair $(i, j)$ is:

$$\ell_{i,j} = -\log \frac{\exp\!\big(\text{sim}(z_i, z_j) / \tau\big)}{\sum_{k=1}^{2N} \mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]} \exp\!\big(\text{sim}(z_i, z_k) / \tau\big)}$$

The total SimCLR loss is the **mean over all positive pairs**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SimCLR}} = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big[\ell_{i, 2i-1} + \ell_{2i-1, i}\Big]$$

### 2.4 Non-Contrastive Methods — Removing the Negatives

| Method | Key Innovation | Negative Samples? |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| **BYOL** (Grill et al., 2020) | Asymmetric online + EMA target network with predictor | **No** |
| **SimSiam** (Chen & He, 2021) | Siamese net + stop-grad + predictor MLP | **No** |
| **DINO** (Caron et al., 2021) | Self-distillation with centering & sharpening | **No** |
| **Barlow Twins** (Zbontar et al., 2021) | Cross-correlation matrix → identity matrix | **No** |
| **VICReg** (Bardes & Ponce, 2022) | Variance + Invariance + Covariance terms | **No** |

**BYOL Target Update Rule** (Exponential Moving Average):

$$\theta_{\text{target}} \leftarrow \tau \cdot \theta_{\text{target}} + (1 - \tau) \cdot \theta_{\text{online}}$$

where $\tau$ is typically close to **1 (e.g., 0.99 → 0.9999 cosine schedule)**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Cosine similarity | $\text{sim}(u,v) = \dfrac{u^\top v}{\Vert u \Vert \Vert v \Vert}$ | Range $[-1, +1]$ |
| 2 | InfoNCE loss | $-\log \dfrac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i,z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_{k} \mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]} \exp(\text{sim}(z_i,z_k)/\tau)}$ | $\tau$ = temperature |
| 3 | NT-Xent (SimCLR) | Same as InfoNCE with $2N$ views | Symmetrized over batch |
| 4 | SimCLR total loss | $\frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(\ell_{i,2i-1} + \ell_{2i-1,i}\big)$ | Bidirectional sum |
| 5 | BYOL target update | $\xi \leftarrow \tau \xi + (1-\tau) \theta$ | $\tau$ = EMA decay |
| 6 | Barlow Twins objective | $\mathcal{L} = \underbrace{\sum_i (1 - \mathcal{C}_{ii})^2}_{\text{invariance}} + \lambda \underbrace{\sum_i \sum_{j \neq i} \mathcal{C}_{ij}^2}_{\text{redundancy reduction}}$ | $\mathcal{C}$ = cross-corr |
| 7 | DINO centering | $c \leftarrow m c + (1-m) \cdot \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} g_\theta(x_i)$ | Prevents collapse |
| 8 | Reconstruction loss (MAE) | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{\vert M \vert} \sum_{i \in M} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert_2^2$ | $\vert M \vert$ = masked pixels |
| 9 | Effective negatives in SimCLR | $2(N-1)$ | Per positive pair |
| 10 | Linear evaluation accuracy | $\text{Top-1 acc. on frozen features}$ | SSL benchmark metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **temperature $\tau$** in InfoNCE is the single most sensitive hyperparameter. Low $\tau$ sharpens the distribution (stronger contrast between positives and negatives); high $\tau$ smooths it. SimCLR sets $\tau = 0.1$ by default; MoCo uses $\tau = 0.07$.

### 2.6 Why Self-Supervision Matters in Engineering

- **Medical Imaging**: Annotated medical scans are extremely expensive; SSL pre-training on unlabeled scans + fine-tuning on a few thousand labels yields state-of-the-art results.
- **Autonomous Driving**: Petabytes of unannotated camera/LiDAR data can be exploited via SSL.
- **Industrial Defect Detection**: Where defects are rare and labeled, SSL leverages abundant normal samples.
- **Foundation Models**: LLMs (BERT, GPT) and Vision Transformers (DINO, MAE) are pre-trained self-supervised on web-scale data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SimCLR (NT-Xent) Loss from First Principles

We derive the SimCLR objective starting from probabilistic reasoning on similarity scores.

**Step 1 — Set up the similarity matrix.**

Given $2N$ normalized embeddings $\{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_{2N}\}$ in a batch, compute the pairwise similarity matrix $S \in \mathbb{R}^{2N \times 2N}$:

$$S_{ij} = \frac{z_i^\top z_j}{\Vert z_i \Vert_2 \Vert z_j \Vert_2} = z_i^\top z_j$$

Since the embeddings are $L_2$-normalized, the cosine similarity reduces to the dot product.

**Step 2 — Apply temperature scaling.**

Scale all similarities by a temperature $\tau > 0$:

$$A_{ij} = \frac{S_{ij}}{\tau}$$

This controls the "sharpness" of the resulting softmax distribution. Smaller $\tau$ → sharper distribution.

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to get unnormalized probabilities.**

$$P_{ij} = \exp(A_{ij}) = \exp\!\left(\frac{z_i^\top z_j}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Form a softmax distribution over all possible positive candidates.**

For anchor $i$, the denominator sums over all $2N$ views except $i$ itself (using the indicator $\mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]}$):

$$D_i = \sum_{k=1}^{2N} \mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]} \exp\!\left(\frac{z_i^\top z_k}{\tau}\right)$$

The probability that $j$ is the *positive* of $i$ under this distribution is:

$$P(\text{positive} = j \mid i) = \frac{\exp(z_i^\top z_j / \tau)}{\sum_{k=1,\, k \neq i}^{2N} \exp(z_i^\top z_k / \tau)}$$

**Step 5 — Take the negative log-likelihood.**

We maximize the log-probability of the correct positive pair, equivalently minimize the **negative log-likelihood**:

$$\ell_{i,j} = -\log \frac{\exp(z_i^\top z_j / \tau)}{\sum_{k=1,\, k \neq i}^{2N} \exp(z_i^\top z_k / \tau)}$$

This is precisely the **categorical cross-entropy** of classifying the positive sample among $2N - 1$ candidates.

**Step 6 — Symmetrize the loss.**

Since the pair $(i, j)$ and $(j, i)$ are two valid anchor choices, sum both directions:

$$\ell_{i,j}^{\text{sym}} = \ell_{i,j} + \ell_{j,i}$$

**Step 7 — Average over all $N$ positive pairs in the batch.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SimCLR}} = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big(\ell_{i, 2i-1} + \ell_{2i-1, i}\Big)$$

This is the final SimCLR objective used in practice.

### 3.2 Analytical Closed-Form: Relationship Between $\tau$ and Gradient Magnitude

Compute the gradient of $\ell_{i,j}$ w.r.t. the similarity $S_{ij} = z_i^\top z_j$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell_{i,j}}{\partial S_{ij}} = \frac{1}{\tau}\left[\frac{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(S_{ik}/\tau) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{[k = j]} \cdot \exp(S_{ij}/\tau)}{\big(\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(S_{ik}/\tau)\big)^2} \cdot \tau - \frac{\exp(S_{ij}/\tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(S_{ik}/\tau)}\right]$$

The compact form:

$$\frac{\partial \ell_{i,j}}{\partial S_{ij}} = \frac{1}{\tau}\left[P_{ij} - P(\text{positive} = j \mid i)\right]$$

$$\frac{\partial \ell_{i,j}}{\partial S_{ij}} = \frac{1}{\tau}\left[\frac{\exp(S_{ij}/\tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(S_{ik}/\tau)} - \frac{\exp(S_{ij}/\tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(S_{ik}/\tau)}\right]$$

More transparently:

$$\frac{\partial \ell_{i,j}}{\partial S_{ij}} = \frac{1}{\tau}\bigg[\underbrace{\text{softmax}_k(S_{ik}/\tau)}_{\text{current prob.}} - \underbrace{\mathbb{1}_{[k=j]}}_{\text{target}}\bigg]$$

**Implication:** Lowering $\tau$ **amplifies the gradient** by a factor of $1/\tau$, which is why SimCLR's $\tau = 0.1$ (vs. MoCo's $0.07$) gives well-conditioned but informative updates.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SSL Methods

| Property | SimCLR | MoCo-v2 | BYOL | SimSiam | MAE | DINO |
|----------|--------|---------|------|---------|-----|------|
| **Requires negatives?** | Yes | Yes | **No** | **No** | N/A (generative) | **No** |
| **Large batch needed?** | Yes (4096+) | No (memory bank) | No | No | No | No |
| **EMA target network?** | No | Yes (queue) | **Yes** | No (stop-grad) | No | Yes (teacher) |
| **Predictor head?** | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | Decoder only | Yes (centroid) |
| **Augmentation strength** | Strong (crop+color) | Strong | Strong + mild | Mild–Moderate | N/A | Multi-crop |
| **Collapse prevention** | Negatives | Negatives | EMA + predictor | Stop-grad | Pixel loss | Centering + sharpening |
| **Backbone** | ResNet-50 | ResNet-50 | ResNet-50 | ResNet-50 | ViT | ViT |
| **Linear eval. (ImageNet)** | 69.3% | 71.1% | 74.3% | 71.3% | 85.9% (ViT-L) | 80.1% (ViT-B) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — SimCLR Loss for a Mini-Batch

**Setup:** $N = 2$ images, producing $2N = 4$ embeddings after augmentation and projection. Let the $L_2$-normalized embeddings be:

$$z_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad z_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ \sqrt{0.75} \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.866 \end{bmatrix}, \quad z_3 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad z_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ -0.714 \end{bmatrix}$$

Positive pairs: $(1, 3)$ and $(2, 4)$. Temperature: $\tau = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Compute similarity matrix.**

$$S = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 0.793 & -0.000 & -0.151 \\ 0.793 & 1.000 & 0.119 & -0.969 \\ -0.000 & 0.119 & 1.000 & -0.988 \\ -0.151 & -0.969 & -0.988 & 1.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Scale by $\tau = 0.1$.**

$$A_{ij} = S_{ij} / 0.1 = 10 \cdot S_{ij}$$

For the positive pair $(1, 3)$:

$$A_{13} = 10 \cdot (-0.000) = 0.000$$

**Step 3 — Compute exponentiated values for anchor 1 (row 1, excluding diagonal):**

$$\exp(A_{12}) = \exp(7.93) \approx 2781.5, \quad \exp(A_{13}) = \exp(0) = 1.000$$

$$\exp(A_{14}) = \exp(-1.51) \approx 0.221$$

Sum: $D_1 = 2781.5 + 1.0 + 0.221 = 2782.72$

**Step 4 — Loss for pair $(1, 3)$:**

$$\ell_{1,3} = -\log \frac{1.000}{2782.72} = -\log(3.594 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 7.93$$

**Step 5 — Symmetrize and average** (compute $\ell_{3,1}, \ell_{2,4}, \ell_{4,2}$ analogously and take the mean).

This numerical example shows that the SimCLR loss is naturally **high** when the positive pair is not well separated from negatives — driving the network to push positives closer in embedding space.

### 3.5 The Connection: InfoNCE $\leftrightarrow$ Mutual Information

A celebrated result (Poole et al., 2019) shows that the InfoNCE loss is a **lower bound on the mutual information** $I(z_i; z_j)$ between anchor and positive:

$$I(z_i; z_j) \geq \log(K) - \mathcal{L}_{\text{InfoNCE}}$$

where $K$ is the number of negative samples. This means **minimizing InfoNCE implicitly maximizes mutual information**, providing a principled information-theoretic justification for the contrastive objective.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
