## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model QP 2024 Scheme, PBCAT604]`
**Define self-supervised learning. Distinguish it clearly from supervised and unsupervised learning with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a representation learning paradigm in which a neural network is trained on **unlabeled data** by automatically generating **pseudo-labels** from the data's own structure, typically via a *pretext task* or *contrastive objective*.
>
> **Distinction (1.5 marks):**
> - *Supervised learning* requires human-annotated labels (e.g., classifying cats vs. dogs given 1000 labeled images per class). SSL uses **no human labels**.
> - *Unsupervised learning* (e.g., K-Means clustering) has no specific prediction objective; SSL has a **well-defined objective** derived from the data.
> - *Self-supervised learning* sits between them — it has an objective like supervised learning, but the labels are *auto-generated*.
>
> **Example (0.5 mark):** In **SimCLR**, the pseudo-label is the *identity* of the augmented view — the model is told "view A and view B came from the same source image" and must confirm this in embedding space. In **BERT**, the pseudo-label is the *masked word* — the model predicts hidden tokens from context.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Model QP 2024 Scheme, PBCAT604]`
**Explain the concept of a "pretext task" in self-supervised learning. List any three classical pretext tasks used in computer vision.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A **pretext task** is a surrogate learning objective whose **pseudo-labels are deterministically generated** from raw unlabeled data, designed so that solving it forces the model to learn semantically meaningful features useful for downstream tasks.
>
> **Three classical pretext tasks (2 marks):**
> 1. **Rotation Prediction** (Gidaris et al., 2018): The image is rotated by one of $\{0^\circ, 90^\circ, 180^\circ, 270^\circ\}$ and the model must classify the rotation angle.
> 2. **Jigsaw Puzzle Solving** (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016): The image is split into a $3 \times 3$ grid; the model must predict the permutation of shuffled tiles.
> 3. **Colorization** (Zhang et al., 2016): Given the grayscale (L channel) input, the model must predict the a, b color channels in CIE Lab space.
> 4. *(Bonus)* **Masked Image Modeling (MAE)**: Random patches are masked; the model reconstructs the missing pixels.
>
> **Key insight (0 mark – supplementary):** The pretext task need not be useful *itself* — its only purpose is to drive the network toward a useful representation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBCAT604 — Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **SimCLR framework** for contrastive self-supervised learning. Describe the role of each component: stochastic augmentation, encoder, projection head, and contrastive loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **NT-Xent (InfoNCE) loss** used in SimCLR from first principles. Show how the temperature parameter $\tau$ affects the gradient magnitude and discuss the role of large batch size. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SimCLR Framework — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
x → [Aug t] → x_i → [Encoder f] → h_i → [Projector g] → z_i ─┐
                                                                   ├── [NT-Xent Loss]
x → [Aug t']→ x_j → [Encoder f] → h_j → [Projector g] → z_j ─┘
```

(The shared encoder $f$ and projector $g$ are *Siamese* — same weights for both branches.)

**Component Roles (5 marks):**

1. **Stochastic Data Augmentation Module** $\mathcal{T}$ (1 mark): A composition of three augmentations — *random crop + color jitter + Gaussian blur* (in SimCLR v1); *crops, color distortion, Gaussian blur, and solarization* (in SimCLR v2). Two augmentations $t, t' \sim \mathcal{T}$ are sampled independently, producing two views $x_i, x_j$ of the same image. **This is the only source of variation** defining the "positive pair."

2. **Base Encoder $f(\cdot)$** (1 mark): A standard CNN backbone (typically ResNet-50, though Vision Transformers are now common). Extracts **representations** $h_i = f(x_i) \in \mathbb{R}^d$. These representations are used for **downstream tasks** after pre-training.

3. **Projection Head $g(\cdot)$** (1.5 marks): A small **MLP** (2 hidden layers in SimCLR v1) with ReLU activations that maps $h \mapsto z = g(h) \in \mathbb{R}^{128}$. The projection head is **discarded** after pre-training; only the encoder $f$ is retained. Empirically, this separation is crucial — the projector prevents the encoder from discarding information that is useful only for the contrastive objective.

4. **Contrastive Loss Function (NT-Xent)** (1.5 marks): For a batch of $N$ images, generate $2N$ embeddings. For each anchor, the *positive* is its twin view, and the remaining $2N - 2$ embeddings are *negatives*. The loss is a softmax cross-entropy that maximizes similarity to the positive and minimizes it to negatives, scaled by temperature $\tau$.

---

**(b) NT-Xent Loss Derivation — 7 marks**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cosine similarity matrix** (0.5 mark):
$$S_{ij} = \frac{z_i^\top z_j}{\Vert z_i \Vert_2 \, \Vert z_j \Vert_2}$$

After $L_2$-normalization, $S_{ij} = z_i^\top z_j$.

**Step 2 — Temperature scaling** (0.5 mark):
$$A_{ij} = \frac{z_i^\top z_j}{\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Softmax over $2N - 1$ candidates** (1 mark):
$$P(\text{pos} = j \mid i) = \frac{\exp(z_i^\top z_j / \tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(z_i^\top z_k / \tau)}$$

**Step 4 — Negative log-likelihood** (1 mark):
$$\ell_{i,j} = -\log \frac{\exp(z_i^\top z_j / \tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(z_i^\top z_k / \tau)}$$

**Step 5 — Symmetrize and average** (1 mark):
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SimCLR}} = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(\ell_{i, 2i-1} + \ell_{2i-1, i}\big)$$

**Effect of Temperature $\tau$ (1.5 marks):**

The gradient w.r.t. similarity $S_{ij}$ is:
$$\frac{\partial \ell_{i,j}}{\partial S_{ij}} = \frac{1}{\tau}\big[\text{softmax}_k(S_{ik}/\tau) - \mathbb{1}_{[k=j]}\big]$$

- **Small $\tau$** (e.g., 0.05): Gradient amplified by $1/\tau = 20\times$; sharper softmax → harder negatives are weighted more → stronger learning signal.
- **Large $\tau$** (e.g., 1.0): Softmax approaches uniform distribution → weak learning signal.

**Role of Large Batch Size (1.5 marks):**

- Each anchor needs $2(N-1)$ negative samples to form a useful contrastive task.
- **Large $N$ (e.g., 4096 in SimCLR)** gives $\sim 8190$ negatives per anchor — drastically improving the quality of the softmax denominator.
- **Limitation:** Requires large GPU memory; this motivated **MoCo**, which uses a *momentum queue* to decouple batch size from negative count.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PBCAT604 — Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **BYOL (Bootstrap Your Own Latent)** framework. With a block diagram, describe its online and target networks, the predictor head, and the EMA update rule. How does BYOL avoid representational collapse **without using negative samples**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **contrastive (SimCLR)** and **non-contrastive (BYOL, SimSiam, DINO)** self-supervised methods. Discuss the role of negatives, the predictor head, stop-gradient, and the practical trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BYOL Framework — 7 marks**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Online Network** (parameterized by $\theta$): Encoder $f_\theta$, projector $g_\theta$, **predictor** $q_\theta$ — a *third* MLP that predicts the target's projection from the online's projection.
- **Target Network** (parameterized by $\xi$): Encoder $f_\xi$, projector $g_\xi$ — *no* predictor.

Both networks receive two different augmented views $x_i, x_j$ of the same image. The online branch processes $x_i$ and produces $p_\theta = q_\theta(g_\theta(f_\theta(x_i)))$. The target branch processes $x_j$ to produce $z'_\xi = g_\xi(f_\xi(x_j))$.

**Loss (1.5 marks):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\theta,\xi}^{\text{BYOL}} = \Vert \bar{p}_\theta - \bar{z}'_\xi \Vert_2^2 = 2 - 2 \cdot \frac{\langle p_\theta, z'_\xi \rangle}{\Vert p_\theta \Vert_2 \Vert z'_\xi \Vert_2}$$

where $\bar{p}_\theta, \bar{z}'_\xi$ are $L_2$-normalized. The loss is **symmetrized** by swapping $x_i \leftrightarrow x_j$.

**EMA Update Rule (1.5 marks):**
$$\xi \leftarrow \tau \xi + (1 - \tau) \theta$$

where $\tau$ starts at $0.99$ and is **cosine-annealed** to $0.9999$ during training. **Only $\theta$ is updated by backpropagation; $\xi$ is updated as an exponential moving average.**

**How BYOL Avoids Collapse (1 mark):**
- The **asymmetric predictor** $q_\theta$ breaks the trivial constant-solution (the online network cannot trivially match the target because it must pass through an extra MLP that the target lacks).
- The **slow-moving target** (high $\tau$) prevents the target from "chasing" the online and collapsing.
- Theoretical analysis (Tian et al., 2021) shows that the predictor induces an implicit contrastive effect even without explicit negatives.

---

**(b) Contrastive vs Non-Contrastive SSL — 7 marks**

| Dimension | **Contrastive (SimCLR, MoCo)** | **Non-Contrastive (BYOL, SimSiam, DINO)** |
|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Negative samples** | Required ($2N-2$ per anchor) | Not required |
| **Mechanism to prevent collapse** | Repulsion from negatives | Predictor + stop-grad / EMA target / centering |
| **Batch size requirement** | Large (4096+) for SimCLR | Can be small (256 in SimSiam) |
| **Computational cost** | High (large batch parallelism) | Lower (smaller batches sufficient) |
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric (Siamese) | Often asymmetric (predictor only on one side) |
| **Linear eval performance** | 69–71% (ResNet-50, ImageNet) | 71–74% (ResNet-50, ImageNet) |
| **Theoretical justification** | Mutual information lower bound (InfoNCE) | Implicit contrast / variance–covariance regularization |

**Key trade-offs (1 mark):**
- Contrastive methods are **well-understood theoretically** but **computationally expensive** due to large batch sizes.
- Non-contrastive methods are **cheaper and often better**, but their theoretical analysis is **more subtle** — they rely on architectural asymmetries to avoid collapse.
- Both families can match or exceed supervised pre-training on downstream tasks, confirming SSL as the dominant pre-training paradigm.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing self-supervised with unsupervised learning.** Examiners specifically look for the phrase *"pseudo-labels generated from the data."* Writing only "no labels used" loses a mark — you must mention the *automatic generation* of supervision.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the projection head in SimCLR diagrams.** The projector MLP $g$ is essential — it prevents the encoder from throwing away augmentation-relevant information. Drawing the encoder feeding directly into the loss is a **frequent mark-deducting error**.
>
> 3. **Miswriting the InfoNCE loss — sign errors and missing the temperature.** The loss is **negative log** of the *positive* in the *denominator*; the indicator $\mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]}$ excludes the anchor from negatives. Missing the minus sign or forgetting $\tau$ costs full marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing BYOL's predictor with the projector.** The predictor $q_\theta$ exists *only* in the online network; the target network has no predictor. This asymmetry is the key to BYOL's non-collapse guarantee.
>
> 5. **Stating "BYOL uses no negatives" without explaining *why* it works.** You must mention the **asymmetric predictor + EMA target** as the collapse-prevention mechanism. Stating "no negatives" alone is **incomplete** for full marks.
>
> 6. **In derivation questions, skipping steps.** KTU examiners reward *every algebraic transition*. Even if you know the final formula, **show the softmax, the log, and the symmetrization steps explicitly**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Self-Supervision Techniques (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Self-supervised learning = unsupervised learning with an *auto-generated* supervised objective (pretext task or contrastive loss).
>
> ✅ **Pretext Tasks (must-know 3):** Rotation Prediction, Jigsaw Puzzle, Colorization. *(Bonus: MAE, Context Encoders.)*
>
> ✅ **Contrastive Principle:** Pull positive pairs (augmented views of same image) together, push negative pairs (different images) apart in embedding space.
>
> ✅ **SimCLR Pipeline:** Augmentation → Encoder $f$ → Projector $g$ → NT-Xent loss. **Projector is discarded after pre-training.**
>
> ✅ **InfoNCE / NT-Xent Loss:**
> $$\mathcal{L} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)}$$
> - $\tau \in [0.05, 0.5]$ — **lower $\tau$ → sharper contrast.**
> - **InfoNCE is a lower bound on mutual information.**
>
> ✅ **BYOL Components:** Online net ($\theta$, with predictor $q_\theta$) + Target net ($\xi$, no predictor) + EMA update $\xi \leftarrow \tau \xi + (1-\tau)\theta$.
>
> ✅ **SimSiam Trick:** Siamese network + stop-gradient on one branch + predictor MLP. No negatives, no EMA, no momentum queue.
>
> ✅ **MAE (Masked Autoencoder):** Mask 75% patches → ViT encoder on visible patches → small decoder reconstructs masked pixels → MSE loss.
>
> ✅ **DINO:** Self-distillation with **centering** (prevents one-dim collapse) + **sharpening** (prevents uniform collapse); uses ViT backbone.
>
> ✅ **Barlow Twins:** Minimize off-diagonal of cross-correlation matrix (redundancy reduction) + make diagonal close to 1 (invariance).
>
> ✅ **Practical consideration:** Large batch size (e.g., 4096) is critical for SimCLR; non-contrastive methods relax this requirement.
>
> ✅ **Linear Evaluation Protocol:** The standard SSL benchmark — train a linear classifier on frozen pre-trained features; report top-1 accuracy.
>
> ✅ **Two-stage training:** (1) SSL pre-training on large unlabeled set, (2) Supervised fine-tuning (or linear probe) on small labeled set.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semi-Supervised and Weakly Supervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) — Formal Definition

**Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL)** is a paradigm in machine learning that lies strictly between **Supervised Learning** (which uses only labeled data) and **Unsupervised Learning** (which uses only unlabeled data). It exploits the geometric and distributional structure of **unlabeled samples** alongside a typically **small set of labeled samples** to build a model whose generalization performance exceeds what could be obtained from the labeled data alone.

Mathematically, the training set is partitioned as:

$$
\mathcal{D} \;=\; \mathcal{D}_L \;\cup\; \mathcal{D}_U
$$

where $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_l}$ is the labeled set with $N_l$ examples, and $\mathcal{D}_U = \{x_j\}_{j=1}^{N_u}$ is the unlabeled set with $N_u$ examples, and typically $N_u \gg N_l$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SSL is treated as a *data-efficient* learning strategy — the central promise is *"can a model perform as well as a fully-supervised one while seeing only a fraction of the labels?"*

### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine teaching a child the concept of a **"cat"** by showing her **10 labeled photographs** of cats and dogs. Then, you hand her a **library of 1,000,000 unlabeled internet images**. She cannot ask you "what is this?", but she notices that the labeled cats share fur textures, ear shapes, and facial geometry — and the labeled dogs share very different geometry. When she sees the 1M unlabeled images, she clusters them by similarity, and confidently says *"this cluster looks like the cat cluster; that cluster looks like the dog cluster."* This **cluster-assisted generalization** from very few labels is the heart of SSL.

## 1.2 Weakly Supervised Learning (WSL) — Formal Definition

**Weakly Supervised Learning (WSL)** is a learning paradigm in which the supervision signal is **incomplete**, **inexact**, or **inaccurate**, but **cheaper to obtain** than strong, ground-truth labels. The model is trained to recover the strong supervision as an implicit output.

According to the canonical taxonomy by **Zhou (2018)**, weakly supervised learning has **three sub-types**:

| Type | Definition | Example |
|:-----|:-----------|:--------|
| **Incomplete Supervision** | Only a subset of training data is labeled; rest is unlabeled. | SSL is a special case. |
| **Inexact Supervision** | Only coarse / global labels are available, not the precise target. | An image is labeled "tiger" but the tiger's bounding box is unknown. |
| **Inaccurate Supervision** | Labels are noisy / partially wrong. | Crowdsourced labels where some annotators are unreliable. |

### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of **Weak Supervision** as a courtroom where the judge is given **clues** instead of an eyewitness. The clues (noisy labels, coarse labels, partial labels) are individually weak, but **collectively informative**. A skilled lawyer (the model) can still arrive at the correct verdict by triangulating weak signals.

## 1.3 Self-Supervised Learning (SSL*) — Adjacent Paradigm

For completeness, **Self-Supervised Learning** is a related paradigm in which supervision is **generated from the data itself** (via a *pretext task*), with **no human labels at all**. It is often grouped with weakly-supervised techniques because the label is "weak" in the sense that it is auto-generated.

## 1.4 Why These Paradigms Matter — Engineering Motivation

In real-world KTU-relevant domains:

- **Medical imaging:** Radiologist labels are **expensive**; SSL dramatically reduces labeling cost.
- **Autonomous driving:** Pixel-level segmentation labels are **coarse and inexact**; WSL recovers them.
- **Web-scale text:** Most web text is **unlabeled**; SSL leverages it for better language models.
- **Crowdsourcing:** Labels are **noisy**; WSL methods like label-noise modeling clean them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constant of Interest:** Labeling cost is the dominant economic factor in modern deep learning. Reducing labels by **10× to 1000×** while keeping accuracy within **1–3%** of the fully-supervised model is the **headline metric** of SSL/WSL research.

# 🧪 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Assumptions of SSL

SSL is not magic — it works **only** when the data satisfies structural assumptions. The three classical assumptions are:

1. **Smoothness Assumption (Manifold Assumption):** If two points $x_1$ and $x_2$ are *close* in a high-density region of the input manifold, then their labels $y_1$ and $y_2$ should be *close* as well. Formally, $d_{\mathcal{X}}(x_1, x_2) \approx 0 \;\Rightarrow\; d_{\mathcal{Y}}(y_1, y_2) \approx 0$.

2. **Cluster Assumption:** If points lie in the same cluster, they likely share the same class label. The decision boundary should **avoid high-density regions** and lie in low-density gaps.

3. **Low-Density Assumption:** The decision boundary between classes should pass through regions of **low data density**, maximizing the margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** All three assumptions are equivalent in practice — they all require that the decision boundary lie in a **low-density region** of input space.

## 2.2 The SSL Optimization Objective

The general semi-supervised objective is a **composite loss** with a weighted unsupervised regularizer:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}}(\theta) \;+\; \lambda\,\mathcal{R}_{\text{unsup}}(\theta)
$$

where:

- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}}(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{N_l} \sum_{i=1}^{N_l} \ell\big(f_\theta(x_i), y_i\big)$ is the standard supervised cross-entropy.
- $\mathcal{R}_{\text{unsup}}(\theta)$ is an **unsupervised regularizer** that exploits the structure of $\mathcal{D}_U$.
- $\lambda$ is a scalar trade-off hyperparameter (called the *unsupervised loss weight*).

## 2.3 Five Families of SSL Methods

| Family | Core Idea | Canonical Example |
|:-------|:----------|:------------------|
| **Self-Training** | Model predicts on $\mathcal{D}_U$, takes confident predictions as pseudo-labels, retrains. | Pseudo-Label (Lee, 2013) |
| **Co-Training** | Two views of the data train two models; each labels the other. | Blum & Mitchell (1998) |
| **Consistency Regularization** | Output should be invariant to small input perturbations. | $\Pi$-Model, Mean Teacher, FixMatch |
| **Graph-Based Methods** | Propagate labels along a similarity graph. | Label Propagation, Label Spreading |
| **Generative Methods** | Model $p(x, y)$ via VAEs / GANs; latent codes share structure. | Kingma et al. (2014), Semi-Supervised GAN |

## 2.4 Consistency Regularization — Mathematical Formulation

Given an input $x$, a stochastic augmentation function $\text{Aug}(\cdot)$, and the model $f_\theta$, the **consistency loss** penalizes differences between predictions on two stochastic augmentations of the same input:

$$
\mathcal{R}_{\text{consistency}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}_U}\,\mathbb{E}_{\xi \sim \mathcal{A}}\,\Big[\, d\big(\, f_\theta(x),\; f_\theta(\text{Aug}(x;\xi)) \,\big)\,\Big]
$$

where $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is typically **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** or **KL-divergence**.

## 2.5 Entropy Minimization

A key principle introduced by **Grandvalet & Bengio (2005)**: push the model to make **confident predictions** on unlabeled data. The **conditional entropy** of $p_\theta(y \mid x)$ is:

$$
H(p_\theta(y \mid x)) \;=\; -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_\theta(y=c \mid x)\,\log p_\theta(y=c \mid x)
$$

Minimizing $H$ encourages low-entropy (peaked) output distributions on unlabeled data.

## 2.6 Pseudo-Label Sharpening

Given raw model probabilities $q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_C)$ from a weakly-augmented view, the **pseudo-label** $\hat{q}$ is the one-hot encoding of the argmax, taken only when the confidence exceeds threshold $\tau$:

$$
\hat{q}_c \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } c = \arg\max_c q_c \;\text{ and }\; \max_c q_c \;\geq\; \tau \\[4pt]
\text{(discard)} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

A *soft* alternative is **temperature sharpening** with temperature $T$:

$$
\hat{q}_c \;=\; \frac{q_c^{1/T}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} q_k^{1/T}}
$$

## 2.7 FixMatch — Unified Modern SSL Algorithm

**FixMatch (Sohn et al., 2020)** combines consistency regularization and pseudo-labeling:

1. Generate a **weakly-augmented** view $\alpha(x)$ and a **strongly-augmented** view $\mathcal{A}(x)$.
2. Compute the model's prediction on the weak view: $q = p_\theta(y \mid \alpha(x))$.
3. Form a **pseudo-label** $\hat{q} = \text{onehot}(\arg\max q)$ if $\max q \geq \tau$; else discard.
4. Compute the consistency loss on the **strong** view: $\ell\big(p_\theta(y \mid \mathcal{A}(x)), \hat{q}\big)$.

The composite loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{FixMatch}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}}(\theta) \;+\; \lambda_u \cdot \frac{1}{\mu B}\sum_{i=1}^{\mu B} \mathbb{1}\big\{\max q_i \geq \tau\big\}\,\cdot\,\text{H}\big(\hat{q}_i,\,p_\theta(y \mid \mathcal{A}(x_i))\big)
$$

where $\mu$ is the ratio of unlabeled to labeled examples per batch, and $\text{H}$ is cross-entropy.

## 2.8 Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) — WSL for Inexact Supervision

In MIL, training data is organized as **bags** $\mathcal{B}_i = \{x_{i,1}, \ldots, x_{i,k_i}\}$ with a single **bag-level label** $Y_i \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$
Y_i \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } \sum_{j=1}^{k_i} y_{i,j} = 0 \\
1 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The instance-level labels $\{y_{i,j}\}$ are unknown. The bag-level prediction is typically computed via a permutation-invariant aggregator:

$$
p(Y_i = 1 \mid \mathcal{B}_i) \;=\; g\!\left(\bigoplus_{j=1}^{k_i} h(x_{i,j})\right)
$$

where $h$ is an instance-level network, $g$ is the bag-level classifier, and $\oplus$ is a symmetric aggregator (e.g., $\max$, $\text{mean}$, or *attention-based* pooling).

## 2.9 Label Noise Modeling — WSL for Inaccurate Supervision

For a noisy-label distribution $p(\tilde{y} \mid y, x)$ corrupted from the clean posterior $p(y \mid x)$, the **forward-correction** loss uses a noise transition matrix $T$:

$$
p(\tilde{y} = c \mid x) \;=\; \sum_{c'} T_{c,c'}\;p(y = c' \mid x)
$$

Training minimizes the loss under the **observed** noisy labels but is consistent for the **clean** posterior if $T$ is correctly estimated.

## 2.10 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Memorize all of these.

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Key Variable | Typical Range / Unit |
|:-:|:--------|:----------------------|:-------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Labeled + Unlabeled set union | $\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D}_L \cup \mathcal{D}_U$ | $N_l, N_u$ | $N_u \gg N_l$ |
| 2 | Total SSL loss | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda\,\mathcal{R}_{\text{unsup}}$ | $\lambda$ | $1.0$ – $10.0$ |
| 3 | Cross-entropy (supervised) | $\ell = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | $C$ classes | nats or bits |
| 4 | Consistency loss (MSE) | $\mathcal{R} = \mathbb{E}\left[\,\Vert f_\theta(x) - f_\theta(\tilde{x})\Vert^2\,\right]$ | $f_\theta$ | non-negative |
| 5 | Conditional entropy | $H = -\sum_c p_c \log p_c$ | $p_c$ | $[0, \log C]$ |
| 6 | Pseudo-label thresholding | $\hat{q} = \mathbb{1}\{\max p \geq \tau\}$ | $\tau$ | $0.90$ – $0.95$ |
| 7 | Temperature sharpening | $q_c^{1/T} \big/ \sum_k q_k^{1/T}$ | $T$ | $0.5$ – $2.0$ |
| 8 | FixMatch total loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda_u \cdot \text{H}(\hat{q}, p_{\text{strong}})$ | $\lambda_u$ | $1$ – $10$ |
| 9 | MIL bag definition | $Y_i = 0 \iff \sum_j y_{i,j} = 0$ | bag size $k_i$ | varies |
| 10 | MIL aggregator | $g(\oplus_{j=1}^{k} h(x_{i,j}))$ | $h, g, \oplus$ | $\max$/$\text{mean}$/attn |
| 11 | Noise transition | $p(\tilde{y}\mid x) = T\cdot p(y\mid x)$ | $T \in [0,1]^{C \times C}$ | row-stochastic |
| 12 | Label-spreading affinity | $W_{ij} = \exp\!\left(-\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2 \big/ 2\sigma^2\right)$ | $\sigma$ bandwidth | $> 0$ |
| 13 | KL consistency | $\text{KL}(p \,\Vert\, q) = \sum_c p_c \log(p_c / q_c)$ | divergence | nats |
| 14 | RandAugment magnitude | $N$ ops × $M$ magnitude | $M \in [1, 30]$ | discrete |
| 15 | Unlabeled batch ratio | $\mu = N_u / N_l$ | $\mu$ | $5$ – $7$ (FixMatch) |

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Consistency Regularization Works Under the Cluster Assumption

We will derive the *cluster assumption* of SSL from first principles of density estimation. Consider a mixture-of-Gaussians data distribution with $C$ components, one per class:

$$
p(x) \;=\; \sum_{c=1}^{C} \pi_c\,\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_c, \Sigma_c)
$$

with class priors $\pi_c > 0$ and $\sum_c \pi_c = 1$. The Bayes-optimal classifier assigns $x$ to class $c^\star = \arg\max_c p(y=c \mid x)$, where:

$$
p(y=c \mid x) \;=\; \frac{\pi_c\,\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_c, \Sigma_c)}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} \pi_k\,\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}
$$

**Step 1.** Define the decision boundary as the set of $x$ where the top two posteriors are equal:

$$
B \;=\; \big\{x \;:\; p(y=c_1 \mid x) \;=\; p(y=c_2 \mid x) \;=\; \max_{c\neq c_1}p(y=c \mid x)\big\}
$$

**Step 2.** Under the cluster assumption, the data density $p(x)$ along $B$ is **low** because the boundary lies in a gap between Gaussians. Formally, if the Gaussians are *well-separated* ($\Vert \mu_{c_1} - \mu_{c_2}\Vert \gg \sqrt{\text{tr}(\Sigma)}$), then for $x \in B$:

$$
p(x \mid y=c_1) \;\approx\; p(x \mid y=c_2) \;\ll\; \max_{c}\,p(x \mid y=c)
$$

**Step 3.** Enforce that the model's decision boundary $B_\theta$ **stays in low-density regions** by adding a penalty on predictions where $p_\theta(y \mid x)$ is uncertain. Uncertainty is measured by the **predictive entropy**:

$$
H(p_\theta(y \mid x)) \;=\; -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_\theta(y=c \mid x)\,\log p_\theta(y=c \mid x)
$$

The unsupervised regularizer is the *expected entropy* over unlabeled data:

$$
\mathcal{R}_{\text{unsup}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}_U}\big[\,H(p_\theta(y \mid x))\,\big]
$$

**Step 4.** The composite objective becomes:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}(\theta) \;=\; \underbrace{-\frac{1}{N_l}\sum_{i=1}^{N_l}\log p_\theta(y_i \mid x_i)}_{\text{supervised fit}} \;+\; \lambda\;\underbrace{\frac{1}{N_u}\sum_{j=1}^{N_u} H(p_\theta(y \mid x_j))}_{\text{entropy minimization}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Minimizing the entropy term pushes $p_\theta(y \mid x)$ to be **peaked** for unlabeled $x$, which forces the boundary to **avoid** regions where it is forced to be uncertain. This is mathematically consistent with placing the boundary in a low-density gap — i.e., the **cluster assumption is satisfied** as a side effect of entropy minimization. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pseudo-Label Cross-Entropy Gradient

The FixMatch-style cross-entropy between a pseudo-label $\hat{q}$ (one-hot vector with $\hat{q}_{c^\star} = 1$) and the model's predicted distribution $p_\theta(y \mid x)$ is:

$$
\text{H}\big(\hat{q},\;p_\theta(y\mid x)\big) \;=\; -\sum_{c=1}^{C} \hat{q}_c \log p_\theta(y=c \mid x)
$$

**Step 1.** Since $\hat{q}$ is one-hot at $c^\star = \arg\max_c p_\theta(y=c \mid \alpha(x))$, only the term $c = c^\star$ survives:

$$
\text{H} \;=\; -\log p_\theta(y = c^\star \mid x)
$$

**Step 2.** Assume a softmax output: $p_\theta(y=c \mid x) = \dfrac{\exp(z_c)}{\sum_k \exp(z_k)}$, where $z_k$ are the logits. Therefore:

$$
\log p_\theta(y = c^\star \mid x) \;=\; z_{c^\star} \;-\; \log\sum_{k=1}^{C}\exp(z_k)
$$

**Step 3.** The gradient with respect to the target-class logit $z_{c^\star}$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{c^\star}}\text{H} \;=\; -\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{c^\star}}\!\left[\,z_{c^\star} - \log\sum_k\exp(z_k)\,\right] \;=\; -\Big[\,1 \;-\; p_\theta(y=c^\star \mid x)\,\Big] \;=\; p_\theta(c^\star \mid x) - 1
$$

**Step 4.** The gradient with respect to a *non-target* logit $z_{c'}$ where $c' \neq c^\star$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{c'}}\text{H} \;=\; -\Big[\,0 \;-\; p_\theta(y=c' \mid x)\,\Big] \;=\; p_\theta(c' \mid x)
$$

**Step 5.** Combining, the **full gradient** of H with respect to the logit vector $z$ is:

$$
\nabla_{z}\,\text{H} \;=\; p_\theta(y \mid x) \;-\; \hat{q}
$$

This is the famous **"softmax minus one-hot"** gradient, identical in form to standard supervised cross-entropy. The only difference is that $\hat{q}$ is **machine-generated**, not human-provided. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Graph-Based Label Propagation Closed-Form

Given a similarity graph $W$ of size $(N_l + N_u) \times (N_l + N_u)$ over the union of labeled and unlabeled data, and an initial label matrix $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{(N_l + N_u) \times C}$ (with non-zero rows only for labeled points), the **Label Propagation** algorithm computes a soft-label matrix $F$ satisfying:

$$
F \;=\; (1 - \alpha)\,S\,F \;+\; \alpha\,Y
$$

where $S = D^{-1/2} W D^{-1/2}$ is the symmetric normalized adjacency, and $D$ is the degree matrix.

**Step 1.** Move all $F$ terms to the left:

$$
F - (1-\alpha)\,S\,F \;=\; \alpha\,Y
$$

$$
\big[\,I \;-\; (1-\alpha)\,S\,\big]\,F \;=\; \alpha\,Y
$$

**Step 2.** Solve for $F$ in closed form:

$$
F \;=\; \alpha\,\big[\,I - (1-\alpha)\,S\,\big]^{-1}\,Y
$$

**Step 3.** For $K$ iterations of the iterative scheme, substitute $S \to S^K$ (lazy random-walk kernel) and the closed-form generalizes to:

$$
F^{(K)} \;=\; \big[\,\alpha\,I \;+\; (1-\alpha)\,S\,\big]^{K}\,Y
$$

**Step 4.** The asymptotic limit $K \to \infty$ exists and equals:

$$
F^{\infty} \;=\; \alpha\,\big[\,I - (1-\alpha)\,S\,\big]^{-1}\,Y
$$

This matrix is **dense** — every unlabeled point receives a soft label influenced by all other points, with influence decaying through the graph's random-walk transition kernel. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MIL Gradient via Permutation-Invariant Pooling

In a max-pooling MIL setup, the bag-level probability is:

$$
p(Y = 1 \mid \mathcal{B}) \;=\; \max_{j \in \{1,\ldots,k\}} \sigma\big(h(x_j)\big)
$$

where $h(x_j)$ is the instance score and $\sigma$ is the sigmoid. The loss is the binary cross-entropy:

$$
\mathcal{L} \;=\; -Y \log p - (1-Y)\log(1-p)
$$

**Step 1.** Define $j^\star = \arg\max_j \sigma(h(x_j))$. Only this instance contributes to the gradient.

**Step 2.** Gradient w.r.t. $h(x_{j^\star})$ using the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h(x_{j^\star})} \;=\; \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p}\cdot\frac{\partial p}{\partial h(x_{j^\star})}
$$

**Step 3.** $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p} = -\dfrac{Y}{p} + \dfrac{1-Y}{1-p}$ and $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial h(x_{j^\star})} = \sigma(h(x_{j^\star}))\big(1 - \sigma(h(x_{j^\star}))\big)$.

**Step 4.** For all $j \neq j^\star$, $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial h(x_j)} = 0$ because of the max operation. Thus only $x_{j^\star}$ receives a learning signal. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SSL vs WSL vs Self-Supervised

| Axis | Supervised | **Semi-Supervised** | **Weakly Supervised** | **Self-Supervised** |
|:-----|:-----------|:--------------------|:----------------------|:---------------------|
| Labeled data $N_l$ | $N_l = N$ | small | coarse/noisy/partial | $0$ |
| Unlabeled data $N_u$ | $0$ | $N_u \gg N_l$ | varies | $N = N_u$ |
| Label source | human | human + model | coarse / crowdsource | auto (pretext) |
| Core assumption | IID | Cluster/Manifold | Noise model | Data structure |
| Key loss | $\text{CE}(y, f(x))$ | $\text{CE} + \lambda \cdot \text{Consistency}$ | $\text{CE}(\text{noisy} y, f(x)) + \text{regularizer}$ | Pretext task loss |
| Canonical method | CNN / Transformer | FixMatch, MixMatch | MIL, Co-teaching | SimCLR, MoCo, BYOL |
| Typical gain | baseline | $+$ **5–25% accuracy** | recovers clean perf | strong representations |

```mermaid
flowchart TB

```

## 4.2 FixMatch Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Unlabeled Image x] --> B1[Weak Augmentation alpha]
    A1 --> C1[Strong Augmentation A]
    B1 --> D1[Model f theta]
    C1 --> E1[Model f theta]
    D1 --> F1[Soft Prediction q]
    F1 --> G1{max q greater tau?}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Pseudo Label onehot argmax]
    G1 -- No --> I1[Discard sample]
    H1 --> J1[Cross Entropy with strong view prediction]
    E1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Unsupervised Loss]
    L1[Labeled Image xy] --> M1[Model f theta] --> N1[Supervised Cross Entropy Loss]
    K1 --> O1[Total Loss]
    N1 --> O1
```

## 4.3 Three Types of Weak Supervision (Zhou Taxonomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[Weak Supervision] --> B2[Incomplete]
    A2 --> C2[Inexact]
    A2 --> D2[Inaccurate]
    B2 --> B3[Only some data has labels]
    B2 --> B4[Solution: SSL pseudo labeling]
    C2 --> C3[Only coarse global labels known]
    C2 --> C4[Solution: Multiple Instance Learning]
    D2 --> D3[Labels are noisy or partially wrong]
    D2 --> D4[Solution: Noise transition matrix or co teaching]
```

## 4.4 Self-Training Loop (Pseudo-Labeling Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A3[Start with small labeled set D_L] --> B3[Train model f theta on D_L]
    B3 --> C3[Predict on unlabeled set D_U]
    C3 --> D3{Confidence greater tau?}
    D3 -- Yes --> E3[Add high confidence predictions to labeled set as pseudo labels]
    D3 -- No --> F3[Keep in unlabeled set]
    E3 --> G3[Retrain f theta on expanded labeled set]
    F3 --> G3
    G3 --> H3{Stopping criterion met?}
    H3 -- No --> C3
    H3 -- Yes --> I3[Final Model]
```

## 4.5 Graph-Based Label Propagation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A4[All samples X_L plus X_U] --> B4[Build similarity graph W with exp kernel]
    B4 --> C4[Compute normalized adjacency S equals D inv half W D inv half]
    C4 --> D4[Initialize label matrix Y with one hot for labeled]
    D4 --> E4[Iterate F equals 1 minus alpha S F plus alpha Y]
    E4 --> F4[Convergence check]
    F4 -- Not Converged --> E4
    F4 -- Converged --> G4[Read soft labels F for unlabeled points]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B5 --> B6[Instance Scores h x1 to h xk]
    B6 --> C5[Permutation Invariant Pool: max or mean or attention]
    C5 --> D5[Bag Level Feature Vector]
    D5 --> E5[Bag Classifier g]
    E5 --> F5[Bag Label Prediction Y hat]
    G5[Ground truth bag label Y] --> H5[Bag Level Loss CE]
    F5 --> H5
```

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Distinguish between Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) and Weakly Supervised Learning (WSL). List the three sub-types of weak supervision as per Zhou's taxonomy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Semi-Supervised Learning | Weakly Supervised Learning |
|:-------|:-------------------------|:---------------------------|
| **Definition** | Uses a small labeled set and a large unlabeled set | Uses labels that are coarser, noisier, or partial |
| **Label availability** | A *subset* is fully labeled; the rest is unlabeled | Labels are weak — incomplete, inexact, or inaccurate |
| **Goal** | Exploit data structure to improve over labeled-only training | Recover strong supervision from weak cues |
| **Example** | 100 labeled + 10,000 unlabeled medical images | Image labeled "tumor present" without bounding box |

**Zhou's three sub-types of weak supervision (1 mark each):**

1. **Incomplete supervision** — Only some training samples have labels; rest are unlabeled.
2. **Inexact supervision** — Labels are coarser than the desired output (e.g., image-level vs. pixel-level).
3. **Inaccurate supervision** — Given labels may be partially or entirely wrong (e.g., crowdsourced labels).

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]`

**Explain the *consistency regularization* principle used in semi-supervised learning. Why is it expected to improve generalization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Consistency regularization is the SSL principle that a *good* model should produce **similar predictions** for two different stochastic augmentations (or perturbations) of the same unlabeled input. The unsupervised loss is:

$$
\mathcal{R}_{\text{consistency}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x\sim\mathcal{D}_U}\big[\,\Vert f_\theta(x) - f_\theta(\tilde{x})\Vert^2\,\big]
$$

**Why it improves generalization (2 marks):**

1. **Smoothness on the data manifold:** By enforcing prediction invariance under input perturbations, the model learns smoother functions that respect the data's intrinsic structure (satisfies the *smoothness/cluster assumption*).
2. **Effective data augmentation:** The model is forced to behave robustly across a much larger effective training distribution than the labeled set alone provides.
3. **Decision-boundary placement:** Consistency pushes the boundary into low-density regions, maximizing the margin — a classical regularization effect.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

#### **A(a).** `[7 Marks]`

**Describe in detail the *FixMatch* algorithm for semi-supervised learning. Draw its pipeline and write the loss function.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Algorithmic Pipeline (3 marks):**

For each unlabeled input $x$:

- **Step 1 — Weak augmentation:** Generate $\alpha(x)$ using a weak augmentation (e.g., random horizontal flip + crop).
- **Step 2 — Strong augmentation:** Generate $\mathcal{A}(x)$ using a strong augmentation (e.g., RandAugment, CutOut, CTAugment).
- **Step 3 — Pseudo-label generation:** Compute $q = p_\theta(y \mid \alpha(x))$. If $\max_c q_c \geq \tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 0.95$), form the one-hot pseudo-label $\hat{q}_c = \mathbb{1}[c = \arg\max_c q_c]$. Otherwise, **discard** the sample.
- **Step 4 — Consistency loss:** Apply cross-entropy between the strong-view prediction $p_\theta(y \mid \mathcal{A}(x))$ and $\hat{q}$.

**2. Loss Function (2 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{FixMatch}}(\theta) \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{1}{B}\sum_{i=1}^{B} \text{H}(y_i, p_\theta(y \mid x_i))}_{\text{supervised}} \;+\; \lambda_u\;\underbrace{\frac{1}{\mu B}\sum_{i=1}^{\mu B} \mathbb{1}\{\max q_i \geq \tau\}\,\text{H}(\hat{q}_i, p_\theta(y \mid \mathcal{A}(x_i)))}_{\text{unsupervised}}
$$

where $B$ = labeled batch size, $\mu B$ = unlabeled batch size, and $\lambda_u$ is a fixed weight (typically 1 or 10).

**3. Why FixMatch Works (2 marks):**

- Combines **two SSL principles**: pseudo-labeling + consistency regularization.
- The **confidence threshold** $\tau$ filters noisy pseudo-labels, preventing confirmation bias.
- **Strong augmentation** ensures the consistency target is non-trivial — the weak view is easy, the strong view is hard, and matching them enforces strong generalization.
- State-of-the-art results: on **CIFAR-10 with only 40 labels**, FixMatch reaches **88–95% accuracy**, comparable to fully-supervised training with 50,000 labels.

---

#### **A(b).** `[7 Marks]`

**Derive the gradient of the FixMatch cross-entropy loss with respect to the model logits. Explain why the resulting gradient form is identical to that of standard supervised cross-entropy.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Setup (2 marks):** Let $\hat{q}$ be a one-hot pseudo-label at class $c^\star$ and $p_\theta(y=c \mid x) = \dfrac{\exp(z_c)}{\sum_k \exp(z_k)}$ be the softmax of logits $z$.

The cross-entropy is:

$$
\text{H}(\hat{q}, p_\theta) \;=\; -\sum_{c} \hat{q}_c \log p_\theta(c \mid x) \;=\; -\log p_\theta(c^\star \mid x)
$$

**2. Log-probability expansion (2 marks):**

$$
\log p_\theta(c^\star \mid x) \;=\; z_{c^\star} \;-\; \log\sum_{k=1}^{C}\exp(z_k)
$$

**3. Differentiation (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{\partial\,\text{H}}{\partial z_{c^\star}} \;=\; -\big(1 - p_\theta(c^\star \mid x)\big) \;=\; p_\theta(c^\star \mid x) - 1
$$

$$
\frac{\partial\,\text{H}}{\partial z_{c'}}\,,\;c' \neq c^\star \;=\; 0 - \big(-p_\theta(c' \mid x)\big) \;=\; p_\theta(c' \mid x)
$$

**4. Final gradient form (1 mark):** The gradient vector is:

$$
\nabla_{z}\,\text{H} \;=\; p_\theta(y \mid x) \;-\; \hat{q}
$$

This is **mathematically identical** to the supervised CE gradient, where $\hat{q}$ is replaced by the human one-hot label $y$. The only difference is **provenance**: in supervised training, $\hat{q}$ is human; in FixMatch, $\hat{q}$ is **machine-generated** by a confidence-thresholded pseudo-label. Hence FixMatch re-uses the **same backpropagation machinery** as supervised learning. $\blacksquare$

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **B(a).** `[7 Marks]`

**Explain Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) as a weakly supervised framework. Derive the bag-level probability for a max-pooling MIL network and discuss its gradient properties.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. MIL Setup (2 marks):** Training data consists of $M$ bags $\mathcal{B}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{B}_M$ where each bag $\mathcal{B}_i = \{x_{i,1}, \ldots, x_{i,k_i}\}$ contains $k_i$ instances. Only the bag-level label $Y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is given; instance labels $\{y_{i,j}\}$ are unknown. The relationship is:

$$
Y_i = 0 \iff \sum_{j=1}^{k_i} y_{i,j} = 0
$$

**2. Max-Pooling MIL Formulation (2 marks):** The instance network $h$ produces scores $h(x_{i,j}) \in \mathbb{R}$, and the bag-level probability is:

$$
p(Y_i = 1 \mid \mathcal{B}_i) \;=\; \max_{j\,\in\,\{1,\ldots,k_i\}} \sigma\big(h(x_{i,j})\big)
$$

The max operator is **permutation-invariant**, so the bag prediction is independent of the order of instances.

**3. Gradient Properties (3 marks):** Let $j^\star = \arg\max_j \sigma(h(x_{i,j}))$. The bag-level BCE loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L} \;=\; -Y_i \log p_i - (1 - Y_i) \log(1 - p_i)
$$

The gradient with respect to $h(x_{i,j^\star})$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h(x_{i,j^\star})} \;=\; \left(-\frac{Y_i}{p_i} + \frac{1-Y_i}{1-p_i}\right)\cdot \sigma(h(x_{i,j^\star}))\big(1 - \sigma(h(x_{i,j^\star}))\big)
$$

For all $j \neq j^\star$, $\dfrac{\partial p_i}{\partial h(x_{i,j})} = 0$, so **only the witness instance** receives gradient signal. This leads to a well-known MIL pathology: the network over-specializes to a single "witness" instance per bag, ignoring context. **Modern remedies** include *attention-based* pooling, *gated attention*, and *soft* max-approximations (e.g., $\text{log-sum-exp}$).

---

#### **B(b).** `[7 Marks]`

**Discuss label noise modeling for weakly supervised learning. Define the noise transition matrix and derive the corrected loss assuming the matrix is known.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Problem Setup (2 marks):** In many real applications, the observed label $\tilde{y}$ is a noisy version of the true label $y$. The corruption is characterized by a class-dependent transition matrix $T \in [0,1]^{C \times C}$:

$$
T_{i,j} \;=\; p(\tilde{y} = j \mid y = i)
$$

Each row of $T$ is a probability distribution: $\sum_j T_{i,j} = 1$ and $T_{i,j} \geq 0$.

**2. Marginal Decomposition (2 marks):** The noisy label distribution is:

$$
p(\tilde{y} = j \mid x) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{C} p(\tilde{y} = j \mid y = i)\,p(y = i \mid x) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{C} T_{i,j}\,p(y = i \mid x)
$$

In matrix form: $p(\tilde{y}\mid x) = T^{\top}\,p(y\mid x)$.

**3. Forward Correction Loss (2 marks):** Instead of training on $p(y\mid x)$ against $\tilde{y}$, we train on $T^{\top}\,p(y\mid x)$ against $\tilde{y}$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{corrected}} \;=\; -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \log\big[\,T^{\top}\,p_\theta(y\mid x_n)\,\big]_{\tilde{y}_n}
$$

**4. Key Result (1 mark):** If $T$ is correctly estimated and **invertible**, then the minimizer of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{corrected}}$ coincides with the minimizer of the clean cross-entropy loss, even though the network never sees the clean labels. This is the *backward-correction* result of Patrini et al. (2017). $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic (KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Conflating SSL with Self-Supervised Learning.** SSL = *labeled* + *unlabeled* data. Self-supervised = *only data, no labels at all*. Examiners specifically look for this distinction; losing 1–2 marks here is common.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the cluster / manifold assumption.** Whenever asked *"why does SSL work?"*, students often answer with vague platitudes. The model answer must invoke the **cluster assumption** (decision boundary lies in low-density regions) explicitly.
>
> 3. **Confusing the three types of weak supervision.** Incomplete ≠ Inexact ≠ Inaccurate. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Omitting the pseudo-label threshold $\tau$** in FixMatch-style derivations. The threshold is the *key* innovation that prevents confirmation bias; omitting it loses a mark.
>
> 5. **Wrong aggregation in MIL.** Many students write $p(Y \mid \mathcal{B}) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_j \sigma(h(x_j))$ and call it "MIL". That is *mean-pooling* MIL, which is **not** the original definition. The classical MIL uses the **OR-rule / max** because of the binary constraint $Y = 0 \iff \sum y_j = 0$.
>
> 6. **Mathematical sloppiness in the loss derivation.** Forgetting that $\hat{q}$ is one-hot and dropping the summation early is a common error. Show every algebraic step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE on this Topic:**

- ☐ SSL training set: $\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D}_L \cup \mathcal{D}_U$, with $N_u \gg N_l$.
- ☐ Composite SSL loss: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda\,\mathcal{R}_{\text{unsup}}$.
- ☐ Three assumptions of SSL: **Smoothness**, **Cluster**, **Low-Density** (all are equivalent in practice).
- ☐ Five SSL families: **Self-Training, Co-Training, Consistency Regularization, Graph-Based, Generative**.
- ☐ Consistency loss: $\mathbb{E}\left[\,\Vert f_\theta(x) - f_\theta(\tilde{x})\Vert^2\,\right]$.
- ☐ Entropy minimization pushes the decision boundary to low-density regions.
- ☐ FixMatch = pseudo-labeling + consistency + confidence threshold $\tau$ + strong augmentation.
- ☐ Pseudo-label gradient equals **softmax − one-hot** (identical to supervised CE gradient).
- ☐ Weak supervision has **three** sub-types: **Incomplete, Inexact, Inaccurate** (Zhou, 2018).
- ☐ MIL uses **permutation-invariant pooling** (max, mean, or attention) over a bag of instances.
- ☐ Max-pooling MIL gradient flows **only through the witness instance** $j^\star$.
- ☐ Label noise transition matrix $T$ is **row-stochastic**; the corrected loss uses $T^{\top}\,p(y\mid x)$.
- ☐ Graph-based SSL: $F = (1-\alpha)\,S\,F + \alpha\,Y$, with closed form $F = \alpha\,[I - (1-\alpha)S]^{-1}Y$.
- ☐ **Pseudo-label threshold** $\tau \in [0.90, 0.95]$ is critical to avoid confirmation bias.
- ☐ **Self-supervised learning** uses *no* human labels — it generates its own pretext labels.
- ☐ Be ready to **derive** the consistency loss gradient, the pseudo-label gradient, and the label-propagation closed form.
- ☐ Be ready to **contrast** the three types of weak supervision with **concrete examples**.

> **End of Topic Notes — Semi-Supervised and Weakly Supervised Learning (Module 3, PBCAT604)**
