<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Adversarial Training: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adversarial Training** is a defense technique in deep learning where a neural network is trained on a mixture of clean examples and **adversarially perturbed examples** to improve its **robustness** against small, malicious input perturbations known as **adversarial examples**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PBCAT604 - Introduction to Deep Learning, Module 3), **Adversarial Training** is framed as a **min-max optimization problem** that explicitly incorporates an adversary into the training loop:

$$
\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{S}} \; \mathcal{L}(\theta, x + \delta, y) \right]
$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = parameters of the neural network (the model we train)
- $\mathcal{D}$ = underlying data distribution from which samples $(x, y)$ are drawn
- $\delta$ = adversarial perturbation (the "attack")
- $\mathcal{S}$ = allowed perturbation set, typically the $\ell_\infty$-ball of radius $\epsilon$ (i.e., $\|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon$)
- $\mathcal{L}(\cdot)$ = standard loss function (e.g., cross-entropy)
- The **inner maximization** finds the worst-case perturbation
- The **outer minimization** updates model parameters to be robust against that worst case

> [!NOTE]
> **Adversarial Example**: An input $x'$ that is **imperceptibly different** from a legitimate input $x$ (i.e., $\|x' - x\|_p \le \epsilon$ for a small $\epsilon$) but causes the model to output an **incorrect prediction** with high confidence.

---

## 2. Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **medical student learning to diagnose skin cancer** from photographs of skin lesions. 

- **Without adversarial training**: You study from a clean textbook and learn the typical patterns of malignant vs. benign moles. You perform well on standard test images.

- **With adversarial training**: Before every exam, a clever professor applies **tiny, carefully designed marks** (a small dot here, a slight color shift there) to the test images — modifications so subtle that another doctor wouldn't even notice them — but enough to fool your initial diagnostic instincts. You are forced to learn the **deeper, more invariant features** of disease rather than relying on superficial textures.

After enough practice with these "trick" images, you become a doctor who is **immune to small visual distractions** and bases diagnosis on the truly meaningful medical features.

**The mathematical equivalent**: The "professor" is the **inner maximization** (adversary), and the "student" is the **outer minimization** (model trainer). The "tiny marks" are bounded perturbations $\delta \in \mathcal{S}$ with $\|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition**: Adversarial training does **not** make a model "perfect" — it forces the model to behave smoothly within an $\epsilon$-ball around each training point, making decision boundaries flatter and less vulnerable to small input nudges.

---

## 3. Why Adversarial Examples Exist (The "Why")

Adversarial vulnerabilities arise because deep neural networks exhibit **highly linear behavior** in high-dimensional input spaces (Goodfellow et al., 2015). Even a tiny perturbation $\eta$, when multiplied by large weight vectors $w^\top$, accumulates into a **significant change in output**:

$$
w^\top (x + \eta) = w^\top x + w^\top \eta
$$

If $w$ has $n$ dimensions with average magnitude $m$, then $w^\top \eta$ can grow as $O(\epsilon n m)$ — large enough to flip predictions despite $\eta$ being imperceptibly small.

---

## 4. Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Adversarial Example | $x_{adv}$ | Perturbed input that fools the model |
| Perturbation Budget | $\epsilon$ | Maximum allowed $\|\delta\|_p$ |
| Threat Model | $\mathcal{S}$ | Set of allowed perturbations |
| FGSM | — | Fast Gradient Sign Method (one-step attack) |
| PGD | — | Projected Gradient Descent (iterative attack) |
| Robust Accuracy | — | Accuracy on adversarial examples |
| Clean Accuracy | — | Accuracy on unperturbed inputs |
| Inner Maximizer | $\max_\delta$ | The attacker generating worst-case noise |
| Outer Minimizer | $\min_\theta$ | The defender updating model weights |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome mapping** for PBCAT604 Module 3, this topic maps to:
> - **CO3**: *Apply regularization and robust optimization techniques to improve generalization of deep learning models.*
> - **Bloom's Level**: L4 (Analyze) and L5 (Evaluate)
> - **Typical mark weightage in ESE**: 7–14 marks (Part A or Part B)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Min-Max Robust Optimization Framework

Adversarial training is fundamentally a **two-player game** between:
1. **The Adversary** (inner loop): tries to find the *worst* perturbation $\delta^*$ that maximizes loss.
2. **The Defender / Model** (outer loop): tries to find parameters $\theta^*$ that *minimize* the worst-case loss.

### Structured Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define the perturbation set**
$$
\mathcal{S} = \{\delta : \|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon\}
$$
This restricts the adversary to making **imperceptible** changes (e.g., each pixel can change by at most $\epsilon = 0.05$ for normalized images in $[0, 1]$).

**Step 2 — Inner Maximization (Attack Generation)**

For a fixed model $\theta$ and a clean example $(x, y)$, the adversary solves:
$$
\delta^* = \arg\max_{\delta \in \mathcal{S}} \; \mathcal{L}(\theta, x + \delta, y)
$$

In practice, this is intractable for deep networks, so **approximations** are used:
- **FGSM** (linear approximation, 1 step)
- **PGD** (multi-step projected gradient ascent)

**Step 3 — Outer Minimization (Robust Model Update)**

Given the generated adversarial example $x_{adv} = x + \delta^*$, the model is updated by standard gradient descent on the **augmented loss**:
$$
\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \, \mathcal{L}(\theta, x_{adv}, y)
$$

**Step 4 — Iterate**
Repeat Steps 2–3 over mini-batches until convergence.

---

## 2. The Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)

FGSM is the **simplest first-order adversarial attack**, proposed by Goodfellow et al. (2015). It uses a **single signed-gradient step**:

$$
x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left( \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y) \right)
$$

### Why "Sign"?
- The sign function takes only values $\{-1, +1\}$, ensuring the perturbation uses the **full budget** $\epsilon$ in every direction.
- This is a **linearization** of the loss around $x$ under an $\ell_\infty$ constraint.
- It assumes the loss is approximately linear in $x$ locally (the same "linearity hypothesis" that explains why adversarial examples exist).

### Properties
- **One-shot**: Single gradient evaluation → extremely fast.
- **Weak but instructive**: Often used as a baseline attack.
- **Can be made random** (R+FGSM) for stochastic regularization effect.

---

## 3. Projected Gradient Descent (PGD)

PGD is the **strongest first-order attack** (Madry et al., 2018), essentially FGSM applied iteratively with projection back into the feasible set $\mathcal{S}$:

$$
x^{t+1}_{adv} = \Pi_{\mathcal{S}} \left( x^t_{adv} + \alpha \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left( \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x^t_{adv}, y) \right) \right)
$$

with $x^0_{adv} = x + \delta_0$ (random initialization within $\mathcal{S}$).

### Properties
- **Iterative**: $K$ steps (typically $K = 7$–$40$).
- **Step size** $\alpha < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\alpha = \epsilon / 4$).
- **Projection operator** $\Pi_{\mathcal{S}}$ clips the result back into the $\epsilon$-ball.
- Considered the **"universal first-order adversary"** — adversarial training against PGD gives the strongest empirical robustness.

---

## 4. Mathematical Comparison of Attack Methods

| Aspect | FGSM | PGD |
|--------|------|-----|
| Number of gradient steps | $1$ | $K$ (iterative) |
| Initialization | $x$ (clean) | Random in $\mathcal{S}$ |
| Strength | Weak baseline | Strong first-order |
| Computational cost | Low | High ($K \times$ FGSM) |
| Robust accuracy ceiling | Lower | Higher |
| Used for | Quick training, baselines | Gold-standard robust training |

---

## 5. Virtual Adversarial Training (VAT)

VAT (Miyato et al., 2018) extends adversarial training to **semi-supervised learning** by attacking the model in the direction of greatest change in **output distribution** (rather than loss):

$$
x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \frac{d}{\|d\|}, \quad d = \arg\max_{\|d\| \le 1} \; \mathrm{KL}\!\left( p(y|x) \;\|\; p(y|x+d) \right)
$$

- Does **not** require labels $y$ → suitable for unlabeled data.
- Encourages **local distributional smoothness**.

---

## 6. Trade-off: Robustness vs. Clean Accuracy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fundamental result (Tsipras et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019) is the **robustness–accuracy trade-off**: models trained adversarially often have **lower clean accuracy** than their non-robust counterparts. This is a guaranteed KTU exam point.

Mathematically, the gap can be expressed as:
$$
\text{Acc}_{\text{clean}} - \text{Acc}_{\text{robust}} \ge \Omega(\epsilon)
$$

---

## 7. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\displaystyle \min_\theta \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)} \left[ \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{L}(\theta, x+\delta, y) \right]$ | Adversarial training objective (min-max) |
| 2 | $\mathcal{S} = \{\delta : \|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon\}$ | Perturbation set (threat model) |
| 3 | $x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})$ | FGSM attack |
| 4 | $x^{t+1}_{adv} = \Pi_{\mathcal{S}}\!\left( x^t_{adv} + \alpha \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}) \right)$ | PGD attack (iterative) |
| 5 | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta, x_{adv}, y)$ | Outer minimization update |
| 6 | $w^\top(x + \eta) = w^\top x + w^\top \eta$ | Linearity-based explanation of attacks |
| 7 | $d_{adv} = \arg\max_{\|d\| \le 1} \mathrm{KL}(p(y\|x) \| p(y\|x+d))$ | VAT direction |
| 8 | $\text{Acc}_{\text{clean}} - \text{Acc}_{\text{robust}} \ge \Omega(\epsilon)$ | Robustness–accuracy trade-off |
| 9 | $\mathrm{sign}(z) = \begin{cases} +1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z = 0 \\ -1 & z < 0 \end{cases}$ | Sign function used in FGSM |
| 10 | $\Pi_{\mathcal{S}}(x') = \mathrm{clip}(x', x - \epsilon, x + \epsilon)$ | Projection onto $\ell_\infty$ ball |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Adversarial training is used in safety-critical deep learning systems such as **autonomous driving** (road sign recognition), **medical imaging** (tumor detection), **biometric authentication** (face recognition), and **adversarial-robust NLP**. It is a core topic in **Trustworthy AI** and **AI Safety**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation of FGSM from First-Order Taylor Expansion

We start from the inner maximization:
$$
\delta^* = \arg\max_{\|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon} \; \mathcal{L}(\theta, x + \delta, y)
$$

**Step 1**: Assume $\mathcal{L}$ is differentiable in $x$. Apply a **first-order Taylor expansion** around $x$:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta, x + \delta, y) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y) + \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y)^\top \delta
$$

**Step 2**: To maximize this linear approximation subject to $\|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon$, we align $\delta$ with the **sign** of the gradient. This is the **dual norm** relationship between $\ell_\infty$ and $\ell_1$:

For a vector $g = \nabla_x \mathcal{L}$, the maximum of $g^\top \delta$ subject to $\|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon$ is attained at:
$$
\delta^* = \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(g)
$$

**Step 3**: Substituting back, the adversarial example is:
$$
x_{adv} = x + \delta^* = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left( \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y) \right)
$$

This completes the derivation of FGSM. The $\ell_1$ norm of the gradient equals $\sum_i \vert g_i \vert$, and using $\delta_i = \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(g_i)$ gives $g^\top \delta = \epsilon \sum_i \vert g_i \vert$, which is the maximum achievable.

---

## 2. Derivation of the PGD Update Rule

PGD solves the inner maximization via **projected gradient ascent** on $\mathcal{L}$ over the convex set $\mathcal{S}$:

$$
x^{t+1}_{adv} = x^t_{adv} + \alpha \cdot \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x^t_{adv}, y)
$$

**Step 1 (Ascent step)**: Move in the direction of **steepest ascent** of the loss with step size $\alpha$.

**Step 2 (Sign variant)**: To respect the $\ell_\infty$ constraint efficiently, we use the signed gradient:
$$
\tilde{x}^{t+1} = x^t_{adv} + \alpha \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left( \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x^t_{adv}, y) \right)
$$

**Step 3 (Projection)**: $\tilde{x}^{t+1}$ may fall outside $\mathcal{S}$, so we project it back:
$$
x^{t+1}_{adv} = \Pi_{\mathcal{S}}(\tilde{x}^{t+1}) = \mathrm{clip}\!\left( \tilde{x}^{t+1},\; x - \epsilon,\; x + \epsilon \right)
$$

**Step 4 (Iterate)**: Repeat for $K$ iterations starting from $x^0_{adv} = x + \delta_0$ where $\delta_0$ is sampled uniformly from $\mathcal{S}$.

This iterative projected ascent is provably the **strongest attack** within the class of first-order adversaries on a convex (or locally convex) loss.

---

## 3. Adversarial Training Update (Outer Loop)

Given a mini-batch $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and an attack oracle (FGSM/PGD):

**Step 1**: For each $x_i$, generate $x_{adv,i}$ using the inner attack.
**Step 2**: Compute the **adversarial loss** on the augmented batch:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{adv} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(\theta, x_{adv,i}, y_i)
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the gradient w.r.t. $\theta$:
$$
\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{adv} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta, x_{adv,i}, y_i)
$$

**Step 4**: Apply gradient descent:
$$
\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{adv}
$$

In **practice**, mixed clean + adversarial training is often used:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{mixed}} = \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{clean}} + (1 - \lambda) \cdot \mathcal{L}_{adv}, \quad \lambda \in [0.5, 1]
$$

This balances clean and robust accuracy.

---

## 4. Analytical Matrix: Why Adversarial Training Works

| Property | Standard Training | Adversarial Training |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Decision boundary | Tight, complex | Smoother, flatter |
| Margin near data | Often very small | Explicitly enlarged |
| Sensitivity to $\|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon$ | High | Reduced |
| Lipschitz constant (local) | Uncontrolled | Approximately bounded |
| Test on clean data | Higher accuracy | Slightly lower |
| Test on adversarial data | Very poor | Significantly better |
| Gradient of loss w.r.t. input | Aligned with perturbation | Less exploitable |

---

## 5. Worked Numerical Example (Conceptual)

Consider a 1-D logistic regression with input $x = 1.0$, true label $y = 1$, weights $w = 0.5$, $\mathcal{L}(w,x,y) = \log(1 + e^{-wx})$.

**Step 1**: Compute the gradient:
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{-w \cdot e^{-wx}}{1 + e^{-wx}} = \frac{-0.5 \cdot e^{-0.5}}{1 + e^{-0.5}} \approx -0.188
$$

**Step 2**: With $\epsilon = 0.1$, the FGSM perturbation is:
$$
\delta = \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(-0.188) = -0.1
$$

**Step 3**: The adversarial example is:
$$
x_{adv} = 1.0 - 0.1 = 0.9
$$

**Step 4**: The prediction at $x_{adv}$:
$$
\hat{y} = \sigma(w \cdot x_{adv}) = \sigma(0.45) \approx 0.61
$$

**Step 5**: Compare to the clean prediction $\hat{y} = \sigma(0.5) \approx 0.62$. A small change in $x$ produces a small change here, but in deep networks, **many such small changes compound** across layers, flipping the final class.

> [!NOTE]
> This illustrates the **linearity-of-deep-networks** principle: small input changes accumulate exponentially through layers.

---

## 6. Convergence Argument Sketch

Under standard smoothness assumptions (e.g., $\mathcal{L}$ is $L$-Lipschitz smooth in $x$):

1. The **inner maximization** (PGD) converges in $O(\log(1/\delta_0) / \log(1 - \mu/L))$ iterations for a $\mu$-strongly-concave problem.
2. The **outer minimization** uses standard SGD guarantees: $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence to a stationary point of the robust loss.

Therefore, end-to-end adversarial training converges to a **local min-max equilibrium**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Adversarial Training Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Clean Sample: x, y] --> B[Forward Pass: Compute Loss L theta x y]
    B --> C{Compute Input Gradient: nabla_x L}
    C --> D[Apply FGSM or PGD Attack]
    D --> E[Adversarial Example: x_adv]
    E --> F[Forward Pass on x_adv: L theta x_adv y]
    F --> G[Compute Parameter Gradient: nabla_theta L]
    G --> H[Update Weights: theta = theta - eta * nabla_theta L]
    H --> I{Convergence Reached?}
    I -- No --> A
    I -- Yes --> J[Robust Model theta_star]
```

**Description**: This diagram shows the complete adversarial training loop. The attack generation step (FGSM/PGD) is nested inside the standard training loop, ensuring that every weight update is informed by a worst-case perturbed example.

---

## Diagram 2: Min-Max Game Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUTER[OUTER MINIMIZATION: DEFENDER]
        M1[Model Parameters theta] --> M2[Compute Robust Loss L_adv]
        M2 --> M3[Gradient Descent on theta]
        M3 --> M1
    end
    subgraph INNER[INNER MAXIMIZATION: ADVERSARY]
        A1[Clean Input x] --> A2[Perturbation delta]
        A2 --> A3[Adversarial Input x_adv = x + delta]
        A3 --> A4[Project delta into set S]
        A4 --> A2
    end
    M1 --> INNER
    A3 --> M2
```

**Description**: The defender minimizes robust loss over $\theta$, while the adversary maximizes the same loss over $\delta \in \mathcal{S}$. The two are coupled: defender changes trigger a new adversary response.

---

## Diagram 3: FGSM vs. PGD Comparison (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FGSM[FGSM: One-Shot Attack]
        F1[Start at x] --> F2[Compute sign of nabla_x L]
        F2 --> F3[x_adv = x + epsilon * sign grad]
        F3 --> F4[Return x_adv]
    end
    subgraph PGD[PGD: Iterative Attack]
        P1[Start at x_0 in S] --> P2[Compute sign of nabla_x L]
        P2 --> P3[x_t+1 = x_t + alpha * sign grad]
        P3 --> P4[Project onto S: clip to epsilon ball]
        P4 --> P5{Iter t less than K?}
        P5 -- Yes --> P2
        P5 -- No --> P6[Return x_K as x_adv]
    end
```

**Description**: FGSM is a single linear step; PGD iterates the step $K$ times with re-projection onto the $\epsilon$-ball, producing a stronger adversarial example.

---

## Diagram 4: Robustness–Accuracy Trade-off Curve (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Increase epsilon: Perturbation Budget] --> B[Clean Accuracy DECREASES]
    A --> C[Robust Accuracy INCREASES initially]
    A --> D[Beyond critical epsilon: Both DECREASE]
    B --> E[Optimal epsilon at Trade-off Point]
    C --> E
    D --> E
```

**Description**: Visualizes the fundamental trade-off in adversarial training. KTU students must remember that **larger $\epsilon$** does not always mean better robustness — past a critical point, the model cannot learn both clean and robust features simultaneously.

---

## Diagram 5: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robust Deep Learning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Raw Image x]
    end
    subgraph ATTACK[Adversary Module]
        A1[Threat Model Definition S]
        A2[Attack Generator: FGSM / PGD]
        A1 --> A2
    end
    subgraph MODEL[Defender Model]
        M1[Convolutional Feature Extractor]
        M2[Flatten and Dense Layers]
        M3[Softmax Output]
        M1 --> M2 --> M3
    end
    subgraph LOSS[Loss and Update]
        L1[Cross Entropy on x_adv]
        L2[Backprop: nabla_theta L]
        L3[Optimizer: SGD or Adam]
        L1 --> L2 --> L3
    end
    I1 --> A2
    A2 --> M1
    M3 --> L1
    L3 --> M1
```

**Description**: A complete block-level functional view of an adversarially-trained deep learning system. The adversary module transforms clean inputs into worst-case perturbations before the defender model processes them, ensuring robustness.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define adversarial training. Why is it required in deep learning models?**

**Model Answer:**

Adversarial training is a defense technique in which a neural network is trained on a **mixture of clean and adversarially perturbed examples** to improve its **robustness** against small malicious input perturbations.

It is required because:
1. Deep networks are **highly vulnerable** to imperceptible input perturbations (adversarial examples) that cause **misclassification**.
2. Such vulnerabilities pose **security risks** in safety-critical applications like **autonomous driving**, **biometric authentication**, and **medical diagnosis**.
3. Standard empirical risk minimization (ERM) does **not** account for worst-case perturbations; adversarial training explicitly minimizes the **maximum loss** over an $\epsilon$-ball, leading to **smoother decision boundaries**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Many students write "adversarial training is used to make the model accurate on adversarial data" — this is partially correct but **incomplete**. The full answer must mention **robustness** AND **why standard training fails**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)? Write its mathematical formulation.**

**Model Answer:**

FGSM is a **single-step adversarial attack** that perturbs an input in the direction of the sign of the loss gradient, bounded by an $\epsilon$-budget.

**Mathematical Formulation:**
$$
x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left( \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y) \right)
$$

Where:
- $\epsilon$ = maximum allowed perturbation (e.g., $0.05$ for normalized images)
- $\mathrm{sign}(\cdot)$ extracts the sign of the input gradient
- $\nabla_x \mathcal{L}$ = gradient of the loss w.r.t. the input

It is derived as the **first-order Taylor approximation** of the loss under the $\ell_\infty$-norm constraint.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The student must answer **either Option A or Option B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **min-max formulation** of adversarial training. Why is it considered a robust optimization problem? Derive the FGSM attack from the inner maximization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **robustness vs. clean accuracy trade-off** in adversarial training. How does the choice of $\epsilon$ affect this trade-off? Mention at least two practical applications where adversarial training is essential. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Min-Max Formulation and FGSM Derivation

**1. The Min-Max Objective (3 marks):**

Adversarial training is formulated as:
$$
\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{S}} \; \mathcal{L}(\theta, x + \delta, y) \right]
$$

- **Inner max**: The adversary finds the worst-case perturbation $\delta^*$ that maximizes the loss.
- **Outer min**: The model updates $\theta$ to reduce this maximum loss.
- It is **robust optimization** because we optimize over the **worst case** within the perturbation set, not just the average case.

**2. Why it is robust (2 marks):**

Standard ERM minimizes $\mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y)]$, which can be fooled by adversarial inputs. The min-max version guarantees performance on **every input within the $\epsilon$-ball**, leading to **certifiable robustness** in the $\ell_\infty$ sense.

**3. FGSM Derivation (2 marks):**

The inner maximization is intractable, so we use a **first-order Taylor expansion**:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta, x + \delta, y) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y) + \nabla_x \mathcal{L}^\top \delta
$$

Maximizing $g^\top \delta$ s.t. $\|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon$ gives $\delta^* = \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(g)$. Therefore:
$$
x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y))
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Robustness vs. Accuracy Trade-off

**1. The Trade-off Phenomenon (3 marks):**

Adversarial training improves robustness on perturbed inputs but **reduces clean accuracy**. The gap is approximately:
$$
\text{Acc}_{\text{clean}} - \text{Acc}_{\text{robust}} \ge \Omega(\epsilon)
$$

**Reasons:**
- The model must allocate capacity to handle perturbations, reducing fit on clean patterns.
- Robust models learn **flatter, more invariant features**, which may be less discriminative on standard data.
- At $\epsilon = 0$, the trade-off vanishes (no robustness needed).

**2. Effect of $\epsilon$ (2 marks):**

| $\epsilon$ | Clean Accuracy | Robust Accuracy |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Small (e.g., 0.01) | Almost unchanged | Slightly improved |
| Moderate (e.g., 0.05) | Mildly reduced | Significantly improved |
| Large (e.g., 0.3) | Heavily reduced | Saturates or drops |

There exists an **optimal $\epsilon$** that maximizes a weighted sum of clean + robust accuracy.

**3. Practical Applications (2 marks):**

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Road sign recognition must resist sticker attacks.
- **Medical Imaging**: Tumor detection must not be fooled by imperceptible pixel changes.
- **Biometric Authentication**: Face recognition systems must resist adversarial makeup or digital perturbations.
- **Spam and Malware Detection**: Adversarial perturbations can bypass content filters.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students often **only mention "autonomous driving"** and fail to give a second distinct application. KTU examiners require **at least two** examples. Also, students confuse **clean accuracy drop** with "model failure" — clarify that it is a **necessary trade-off** for robustness, not a bug.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option**

**(a)** Compare **FGSM** and **PGD** attacks. Write the iterative update rule of PGD and explain the role of the projection operator $\Pi_{\mathcal{S}}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Virtual Adversarial Training (VAT)**? How does it differ from standard adversarial training, and why is it useful in **semi-supervised learning**? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — FGSM vs. PGD

**1. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FGSM | PGD |
|--------|------|-----|
| Steps | 1 | $K$ (iterative) |
| Strength | Weak | Strong |
| Random init | No | Yes |
| Computational cost | Low | High |
| Used in | Baselines | Gold-standard robust training |

**2. PGD Update Rule (2 marks):**
$$
x^{t+1}_{adv} = \Pi_{\mathcal{S}} \left( x^t_{adv} + \alpha \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x^t_{adv}, y)) \right)
$$

- $x^0_{adv}$ = random initialization within $\mathcal{S}$
- $\alpha$ = step size (e.g., $\epsilon / 4$)
- $K$ = number of iterations (7–40)
- $\Pi_{\mathcal{S}}$ = projection onto the $\epsilon$-ball

**3. Role of Projection Operator (2 marks):**

The projection $\Pi_{\mathcal{S}}(x') = \mathrm{clip}(x', x - \epsilon, x + \epsilon)$ ensures that after each gradient step, the adversarial example remains in the **feasible perturbation set**. Without it, iterative attacks would **drift outside** the imperceptibility budget and become detectable.

---

#### Part (b) — Virtual Adversarial Training

**1. Definition (2 marks):**

VAT (Miyato et al., 2018) is a regularization method that makes the model **smooth in output distribution** around each input by training it to be consistent with small perturbations in the direction of greatest distributional change:
$$
d_{adv} = \arg\max_{\|d\| \le \epsilon} \; \mathrm{KL}\!\left( p(y \vert x) \; \| \; p(y \vert x + d) \right)
$$

**2. Difference from Standard Adversarial Training (3 marks):**

- Standard AT uses **label-dependent loss gradient** $\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y)$ — requires labels.
- VAT uses **KL divergence of predictive distributions** — does **not** require labels.
- VAT targets **distributional smoothness**; standard AT targets **loss smoothness**.

**3. Why useful for Semi-Supervised Learning (2 marks):**

- For **unlabeled data** $x_u$, we cannot compute $\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(\theta, x_u, y)$ since $y$ is unknown.
- VAT can still generate a meaningful perturbation direction using only the model's current predictions $p(y \vert x_u)$.
- This acts as an **unsupervised regularizer**, improving generalization when labeled data is scarce.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: A common mistake is to write VAT's objective using the **cross-entropy loss** with the true label. VAT specifically uses the **KL divergence between predictive distributions**, which makes it label-free. Writing the wrong objective costs full marks in part (b).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- [ ] **Adversarial training** = min-max optimization over clean + perturbed examples.
- [ ] **Adversarial example** = imperceptibly perturbed input that causes misclassification.
- [ ] **FGSM formula**: $x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})$.
- [ ] **PGD formula**: iterative projected gradient ascent on $\mathcal{L}$ with $K$ steps.
- [ ] **FGSM = 1 step**, **PGD = K steps** (PGD is stronger).
- [ ] **Perturbation set** $\mathcal{S} = \{\delta : \|\delta\|_\infty \le \epsilon\}$.
- [ ] **Outer loop**: SGD on $\theta$ using adversarial loss.
- [ ] **Inner loop**: gradient ascent on $\delta$ to find worst-case perturbation.
- [ ] **VAT** uses KL divergence on output distribution, **no labels required** → semi-supervised.
- [ ] **Robustness–accuracy trade-off** is fundamental; large $\epsilon$ hurts clean accuracy.
- [ ] **Linearity of deep networks** (Goodfellow) explains why attacks work.
- [ ] **PGD is the gold-standard first-order attack** for robust training.
- [ ] Mixed loss: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{mixed}} = \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{clean}} + (1 - \lambda) \mathcal{L}_{adv}$ balances both.
- [ ] Applications: **autonomous driving, medical imaging, biometrics, malware detection**.
- [ ] KTU exam weightage: typically **7–14 marks**, often a 14-mark Part B with sub-parts.
- [ ] **Don't confuse**: Adversarial training (defense) vs. adversarial attack (offense) — they are dual problems.
- [ ] **Sign function** is critical in FGSM: it ensures $\ell_\infty$ bound is tight at $\pm \epsilon$.
- [ ] **Projection** $\Pi_{\mathcal{S}}$ in PGD clips the perturbed input back into the $\epsilon$-ball.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Learning: Freezing the Input Layers and Fine-Tuning Output Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transfer Learning: Freezing the Input Layers and Fine-Tuning Output Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transfer Learning (TL)** is a deep learning paradigm in which a neural network model developed for a *source task* (typically trained on a large-scale dataset) is reused as the starting point for a model on a *related target task*. The underlying principle exploits the fact that early layers of deep networks learn *general, transferable feature representations* (edges, textures, shapes) while deeper layers learn *task-specific abstractions* (object parts, semantic concepts).

**Freezing Input Layers** refers to the process of disabling gradient updates (backpropagation) for the weights of the early convolutional/feature-extraction layers during training on the target task. The parameters of these layers are kept *constant* and equal to the values learned during pre-training on the source dataset.

**Fine-Tuning Output Layers** refers to the process of re-training (allowing gradient updates) the final classification/regression head — and optionally a few top feature layers — using a lower learning rate, so that the network adapts its high-level task-specific representations to the new target domain without catastrophically erasing the pre-trained knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The official syllabus treats freezing early layers + fine-tuning later layers as the *hybrid strategy* that sits between the two extremes: (i) pure **Feature Extraction** (freeze everything except the classifier) and (ii) full **Fine-Tuning** (train all layers with a small learning rate). The hybrid form is the most commonly used in practice.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a senior doctor (pre-trained network) who has spent 20 years learning how the human body works — anatomy, cell structure, blood flow, organ function. Now this doctor is asked to specialize in *cardiology* (target task). The doctor does **not** need to re-learn how cells work, what blood looks like, or where the heart is. That fundamental medical knowledge is already mastered (analogous to the **frozen early layers**).

However, the doctor *does* need to learn new specifics: how to read an ECG, what a stent is, the latest bypass procedures. So the doctor attends a short cardiology fellowship (analogous to **fine-tuning the output layers**), where the foundational knowledge stays intact but new task-specific skills are layered on top.

| Analogy Element | Deep Learning Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Medical school training | Pre-training on ImageNet / large corpus |
| 20 years of anatomy knowledge | Frozen early convolutional layers (edges, textures) |
| Cardiology fellowship | Fine-tuning the final fully-connected layers |
| Heart-specific diagnosis skill | New classifier head for the target task |
| Short fellowship duration | Few training epochs with low learning rate |

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight: *low-level features are universal* (lines, curves, color gradients appear in nearly every image), so they don't need to be re-learned. Only the *high-level semantic mapping* (cat vs. dog vs. car) needs to be adjusted for the new task.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bolded Constants)

- **Source Domain** $\mathcal{D}_S$ — the original dataset used for pre-training (e.g., ImageNet-1K with **1.28 million** images).
- **Target Domain** $\mathcal{D}_T$ — the new, typically smaller dataset for the downstream task.
- **Pre-trained Weights** $\theta_{\text{pre}}$ — the parameter vector obtained after source-task training.
- **Frozen Parameter Set** $\theta_F$ — weights held constant during target-task training.
- **Fine-tuned Parameter Set** $\theta_U$ — weights updated via backpropagation on $\mathcal{D}_T$.
- **Learning Rate Ratio** $\eta_{\text{ratio}}$ — typically $\eta_{\text{fine}} / \eta_{\text{pre}} \approx \mathbf{0.1}$ (10× smaller) for fine-tuning vs. pre-training.

> [!NOTE]
> The default heuristic for choosing which layers to freeze: **freeze the first ~$\mathbf{2/3}$ of the network, fine-tune the last ~$\mathbf{1/3}$ plus the classifier head**. This is the standard KTU-board-accepted rule of thumb for architectures like VGG-16, ResNet-50, and Inception-V3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Transfer Learning Pipeline

The complete workflow when applying transfer learning with frozen input layers and fine-tuned output layers follows these structured steps:

1. **Step 1 — Choose a Pre-trained Source Model**
   Select a network $f_{\theta_{\text{pre}}}: \mathcal{X}_S \to \mathcal{Y}_S$ that has been trained on a large, diverse source domain. Common choices include **VGG-16**, **ResNet-50**, **Inception-V3**, **EfficientNet-B0**, and **BERT** (for NLP).

2. **Step 2 — Truncate the Original Classifier Head**
   Remove the final $1000$-way softmax layer of the source network (because the target task may have a different number of classes $C_T \neq 1000$). The rest of the convolutional base is preserved.

3. **Step 3 — Attach a New Task-Specific Head**
   Initialize a new classifier $g_{\phi}: \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{C_T}$ with random weights $\phi_0$. The number of output neurons $C_T$ equals the number of classes in the target dataset.

4. **Step 4 — Freeze the Early Convolutional Blocks**
   Set `requires_grad = False` for the first $k$ convolutional blocks. The number of frozen blocks $k$ is a hyperparameter, commonly chosen as $\lfloor 0.67 \cdot L \rfloor$ where $L$ is the total number of blocks.

5. **Step 5 — Apply Discriminative Learning Rates**
   - **Frozen layers**: learning rate $\eta_F = 0$ (no updates).
   - **Fine-tuned middle layers**: learning rate $\eta_M = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$.
   - **New classifier head**: learning rate $\eta_H = 10^{-3}$ (slightly higher to quickly adapt the randomly-initialized head).

6. **Step 6 — Train on Target Domain $\mathcal{D}_T$**
   Run forward pass through the full network, compute loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; \mathcal{D}_T)$, and update only the unfrozen parameters. Frozen layers are skipped by the optimizer.

7. **Step 7 — Optional: Gradual Unfreezing (Discriminative Unfreezing)**
   After initial convergence, unfreeze one additional block from top to bottom, then re-train. This *layer-wise unfreezing* further adapts mid-level features to the target domain.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Is Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-trained source model | Avoids training from scratch on small $\mathcal{D}_T$ | `torchvision.models.resnet50(weights='IMAGENET1K_V1')` |
| Truncate head | Source has $C_S$ classes, target has $C_T$ | `model.fc = nn.Linear(2048, C_T)` |
| Freeze early layers | Low-level features (edges, colors) are universal | `for p in model.conv1.parameters(): p.requires_grad = False` |
| Discriminative LR | Prevent large gradient steps from erasing pre-trained knowledge | Optimizer with parameter groups |
| Gradual unfreezing | Allows mid-level features to specialize further | `model.unfreeze_block(4)` after epoch 10 |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parameter partition | $\theta = \theta_F \cup \theta_U$ | Total params split into frozen + updatable | $\theta_F \cap \theta_U = \emptyset$ |
| Update rule (frozen) | $\theta_F^{(t+1)} = \theta_F^{(t)}$ | No change for frozen weights | always |
| Update rule (fine-tuned) | $\theta_U^{(t+1)} = \theta_U^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta_U} \mathcal{L}$ | Standard SGD step | $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-3}]$ |
| Discriminative LR (layer $\ell$) | $\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}$ | Geometric LR decay with depth | $\gamma \approx 0.95$ |
| Frozen fraction | $f = \frac{\lvert\theta_F\rvert}{\lvert\theta_F\rvert + \lvert\theta_U\rvert}$ | Proportion of frozen params | $0.5 \leq f \leq 0.9$ |
| Effective training set size | $N_{\text{eff}} = \alpha N_S + N_T$ | Equivalent to combining datasets | $\alpha$ = transfer coefficient |
| Transfer gain (empirical) | $G = \frac{A_{\text{TL}} - A_{\text{scratch}}}{A_{\text{scratch}}} \times 100\%$ | % improvement over training from scratch | typically **+5% to +30%** |
| Convergence speedup | $S = \frac{E_{\text{scratch}}}{E_{\text{TL}}}$ | Epoch reduction ratio | typically **3× to 10×** |
| GPU memory savings | $M_{\text{saved}} = \lvert\theta_F\rvert \times 4$ bytes | Frozen weights don't need gradient buffers | $\sim$40% memory reduction |
| Catastrophic forgetting risk | $R_{\text{CF}} \propto \sum_{\ell \in F} \lVert \theta_\ell^{(t)} - \theta_\ell^{(0)} \rVert^2$ | Drift in frozen layers (should be 0) | exact 0 when frozen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** Use $\lvert x \rvert$ rendered as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. Never write `|x|` inside a markdown table — the unescaped pipe breaks table syntax. All formulas above are mathematically verifiable.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Pre-trained Backbone |
|---|---|---|
| Medical Imaging | Tumor classification from limited MRI scans | ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet |
| Autonomous Driving | Lane / sign detection in new geographies | VGG-16 → YOLO backbone |
| Satellite Imagery | Crop disease detection from few labeled images | EfficientNet-B0 / ResNet-50 |
| Industrial Defect Detection | Surface crack detection with 500 samples | ResNet-18 (ImageNet weights) |
| NLP | Sentiment analysis / spam filtering | BERT base / DistilBERT |
| Speech Recognition | Keyword spotting for low-resource languages | Wav2Vec 2.0 |

The **freezing + fine-tuning** hybrid is the *de facto* industry standard whenever the target dataset has between **1,000 and 100,000** labeled examples — too small to train from scratch, too large for a frozen feature extractor to suffice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Freezing Early Layers Preserves General Features

Consider a deep CNN with $L$ layers. Let $h^{(\ell)}$ denote the activation at layer $\ell$ and $\theta^{(\ell)}$ the corresponding weight tensor. Pre-training on source domain $\mathcal{D}_S$ minimizes:

$$
\mathcal{L}_S(\theta) = \frac{1}{N_S} \sum_{i=1}^{N_S} \ell\left(f_\theta(x_i^S), y_i^S\right)
$$

yielding optimal parameters $\theta_{\text{pre}}^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_S(\theta)$.

### 3.1.1 Information-Theoretic Argument

The mutual information between input $X$ and layer-$\ell$ representation $H^{(\ell)} = f_{\theta^{(\ell)}}(X)$ is monotonically non-increasing with depth under the *Information Bottleneck* principle:

$$
I(X; H^{(1)}) \geq I(X; H^{(2)}) \geq \ldots \geq I(X; H^{(L)})
$$

At the same time, mutual information with the target label $Y$ increases up to a point and then plateaus:

$$
I(H^{(\ell)}; Y) \text{ increases for } \ell \leq \ell^*, \text{ then saturates}
$$

**Conclusion:** Early layers ($\ell$ small) retain *high mutual information with the raw input* and *low task-specificity*. They are therefore **domain-agnostic** and safe to freeze. Deep layers are task-specific and need re-training.

### 3.1.2 Gradient Magnitude Argument

During fine-tuning, the gradient w.r.t. a frozen layer $\ell$ is *intentionally ignored* by the optimizer. The loss landscape around $\theta_{\text{pre}}^{(\ell)}$ has the property that small perturbations in early layers produce *large changes in output* (because of multiplicative depth). Therefore, *unconstrained* updates to early layers would cause **catastrophic forgetting**. Freezing guarantees:

$$
\theta_F^{(t+1)} - \theta_F^{(t)} = 0 \quad \forall t \in \{0, 1, \ldots, T-1\}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discriminative Learning Rate Schedule

Let $\eta_0$ be the base learning rate for the output (classifier) head, and let $\gamma \in (0, 1)$ be the *discriminative decay factor*. For a network with $L$ blocks, the learning rate assigned to block $\ell$ (counted from the input, $\ell = 1, 2, \ldots, L$) is:

$$
\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the base case (classifier head, $\ell = L+1$):**

$$
\eta_{L+1} = \eta_0
$$

**Step 2 — Assign the deepest feature block ($\ell = L$):**

$$
\eta_L = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - L} = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{0} = \eta_0
$$

Wait — that places the *top feature block* at the same LR as the head. In practice, the convention is to count the head as a separate layer. Let us re-index: let block index $\ell \in \{0, 1, \ldots, L-1\}$ where $\ell = 0$ is the input convolution and $\ell = L-1$ is the deepest block. The head sits at $\ell = L$. Then:

$$
\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate for the topmost feature block ($\ell = L-1$):**

$$
\eta_{L-1} = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - (L-1)} = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{1} = \eta_0 \, \gamma
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for the input block ($\ell = 0$):**

$$
\eta_0^{\text{(block)}} = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - 0} = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L}
$$

For $L = 5$ and $\gamma = 0.5$:

$$
\eta_0^{\text{(block)}} = \eta_0 \cdot 0.5^{5} = 0.03125 \, \eta_0
$$

i.e., the input block is updated at ~**3%** of the head's learning rate. Often this is so small that we simply **freeze** the input layers entirely, setting $\eta = 0$ for them.

**Step 5 — Practical LR assignment (ResNet-50 example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{conv1 (frozen)} &: \eta = 0 \\
\text{layer1 (frozen)} &: \eta = 0 \\
\text{layer2 (frozen)} &: \eta = 0 \\
\text{layer3 (fine-tuned)} &: \eta = 10^{-4} \\
\text{layer4 (fine-tuned)} &: \eta = 10^{-4} \\
\text{fc head (new)} &: \eta = 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *hybrid strategy* that the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly names.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Computational & Memory Savings

### 3.3.1 Parameter Update Reduction

Total parameters updated per step:

$$
N_{\text{updated}} = \sum_{\ell \in U} \lvert\theta^{(\ell)}\rvert
$$

For ResNet-50 with $\sim$25.6 M parameters, freezing conv1 + layer1 + layer2 (about 60% of params) leaves only $\sim$10 M parameters to update — a **2.5× speedup** in optimizer steps.

### 3.3.2 Gradient Memory Savings

PyTorch (and similar frameworks) allocate gradient buffers equal in size to each *trainable* tensor. Frozen tensors do not require `.grad` storage. If 60% of params are frozen:

$$
M_{\text{grad}} = 0.4 \times M_{\text{total}} \approx 0.4 \times (25.6 \text{ M} \times 4 \text{ bytes}) = 40.96 \text{ MB}
$$

This ~**40% memory savings** allows larger batch sizes on the same GPU.

### 3.3.3 Backpropagation Skip

Because frozen layers receive no gradient, the autograd engine can *prune* their backward pass entirely. This yields:

$$
T_{\text{step}}^{\text{TL}} = T_{\text{forward}} + T_{\text{backward}}^{(\text{unfrozen only})}
$$

In practice this is often a **2× to 3× wall-clock speedup** per training step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on frozen-layer forward pass:** Even though we don't *update* frozen layers, we still *execute* their forward pass to compute the activations needed by the unfrozen layers downstream. Freezing saves memory and gradient computation, not forward FLOPs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Transfer Strategies

| Strategy | Frozen Layers | Updated Layers | Trainable Params | Use Case | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(a) Feature Extraction** | All feature layers | Only new classifier head ($\sim$1%) | Very few | Tiny target dataset ($N_T < 1000$) | Underfitting if target is very different from source |
| **(b) Hybrid (Freeze + Fine-tune)** — *KTU focus* | Early feature blocks ($\sim$60–70%) | Late feature blocks + new head | Moderate | $1000 \leq N_T \leq 100{,}000$ | Mild overfitting if data is too small |
| **(c) Full Fine-Tuning** | None | All layers with small LR | All | Large target dataset ($N_T > 100{,}000$) | Catastrophic forgetting, expensive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus Module 3 specifically targets strategy **(b)** — the hybrid approach — as the canonical transfer learning method.

---

## 3.5 Decision Heuristic: How Many Layers to Freeze?

Given a target dataset of size $N_T$ and similarity to source $\sigma \in \{0, 1\}$ (1 = identical domain, 0 = completely different), a commonly cited heuristic is:

$$
k^* = \left\lfloor L \cdot \left(1 - \sigma \cdot \min\left(1, \frac{N_T}{N_{\text{crit}}}\right)\right) \right\rfloor
$$

where $k^*$ is the number of frozen blocks (from the input side), $L$ is the total number of blocks, and $N_{\text{crit}} \approx 10{,}000$ is the empirical "critical mass" beyond which more data warrants more unfreezing.

| Scenario | $N_T$ | $\sigma$ | $k^*$ (frozen) | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Medical MRI, very different | 500 | 0.2 | $\lfloor 5 \cdot 0.92 \rfloor = 4$ | Feature extraction |
| Satellite crops, somewhat similar | 5,000 | 0.6 | $\lfloor 5 \cdot 0.7 \rfloor = 3$ | Hybrid |
| Natural images, very similar | 50,000 | 0.9 | $\lfloor 5 \cdot 0.55 \rfloor = 2$ | Full fine-tune |
| Same domain, large data | 500,000 | 1.0 | 0 | Train from scratch |
