# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: What is transfer learning? Explain the strategy of "freezing input layers and fine-tuning output layers" in 3-4 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Transfer learning is a deep learning technique where a model pre-trained on a large source dataset (e.g., ImageNet) is reused for a related target task to reduce training time, computational cost, and data requirements. In the freeze-and-fine-tune strategy, the early convolutional layers — which learn generic low-level features such as edges, colors, and textures — have their weights **frozen** (gradient updates disabled) to preserve the pre-trained knowledge. The final fully-connected output layers are then **fine-tuned** with a small learning rate on the target dataset so that the network learns the new task-specific class boundaries while leveraging the pre-learned feature hierarchy.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]

**Q: List any three advantages of freezing input layers during transfer learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**
1. **Prevents catastrophic forgetting** of the universal low-level features learned during pre-training, because the frozen weights are guaranteed not to drift from their pre-trained values.
2. **Reduces computational cost and memory** — gradient buffers are not allocated for frozen parameters and the autograd engine prunes their backward pass, yielding roughly 2× to 3× faster training steps and ~40% memory savings.
3. **Mitigates overfitting** on small target datasets, since the number of trainable parameters is drastically reduced, lowering the effective model capacity and the variance of the learned function.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Choose **either** Question B1 **or** Question B2.

---

### Question B1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Module 3 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the conceptual motivation behind transfer learning, the role of pre-trained models, and the difference between the source domain $\mathcal{D}_S$ and target domain $\mathcal{D}_T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the hybrid transfer learning strategy of *freezing input layers and fine-tuning output layers*. Mention at least three benefits and one limitation of this approach. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The motivation for transfer learning stems from three practical observations in deep learning:

- **Data scarcity:** Most real-world tasks have only hundreds to thousands of labeled examples, while modern deep networks require millions of samples to train from scratch without overfitting.
- **Computational cost:** Pre-training a state-of-the-art CNN like ResNet-50 on ImageNet requires days of multi-GPU computation, which is infeasible for most practitioners and academic projects.
- **Feature universality:** Empirical research (Yosinski et al., 2014) has shown that the first layers of a CNN learn *transferable* features (Gabor filters, color blobs, edge detectors) regardless of the dataset or task.

The **source domain** $\mathcal{D}_S = \{x_i^S, y_i^S\}_{i=1}^{N_S}$ refers to the large dataset (e.g., ImageNet with $N_S = 1.28$ million images) on which a base model $f_{\theta_{\text{pre}}}$ is originally trained. The **target domain** $\mathcal{D}_T = \{x_j^T, y_j^T\}_{j=1}^{N_T}$ is the smaller, downstream dataset for the new task (e.g., 5,000 medical X-rays). Transfer learning assumes $\mathcal{D}_S$ and $\mathcal{D}_T$ share *low-level* structure (e.g., both are natural RGB images) even if their high-level semantics differ.

Formally, transfer learning seeks parameters $\theta^*$ such that:

$$
\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_T(\theta; \mathcal{D}_T) \quad \text{with} \quad \theta^{(0)} = \theta_{\text{pre}} \neq \theta_{\text{random}}
$$

i.e., we initialize from pre-trained weights rather than from random initialization.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The hybrid strategy is implemented as follows (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. Load a pre-trained network (e.g., ResNet-50 with ImageNet weights).
2. Replace the final fully-connected layer with a new randomly-initialized head matching $C_T$ target classes.
3. Freeze the weights of the first $k$ convolutional blocks (typically $k = 3$ out of 5 in ResNet-50).
4. Keep the remaining top blocks and the new head trainable, with a small learning rate (e.g., $10^{-4}$).
5. Train on $\mathcal{D}_T$ for a small number of epochs (often 5–20, vs. 100+ from scratch).

**Three benefits:**
- **(i)** Drastically reduced training time — typically 3× to 10× fewer epochs to convergence.
- **(ii)** Lower GPU memory and compute footprint.
- **(iii)** Better generalization on small target datasets.

**One limitation:** If the target domain is *very dissimilar* from the source (e.g., satellite SAR images vs. natural photos), the pre-trained low-level features may be a poor match, and freezing them can hurt accuracy. In such cases, full fine-tuning or self-supervised pre-training is preferred.

---

### Question B2 (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice - KTU 2024 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Define *discriminative learning rates* in the context of transfer learning. Derive the formula $\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}$ and explain how it is applied in practice to assign different learning rates to frozen vs. fine-tuned layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A research group uses a ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet (5 convolutional blocks, $\gamma = 0.5$, base LR $\eta_0 = 10^{-3}$) for a 4-class histopathology classification task with 8,000 training images. They decide to **freeze blocks 1, 2, 3** and **fine-tune blocks 4, 5 plus the new FC head**. Calculate the learning rate assigned to each unfrozen block and the head. Also, estimate the percentage of trainable parameters if ResNet-50 has 25.6 M parameters with the distribution: conv1+layer1+layer2 = 15.4 M, layer3+layer4 = 8.2 M, FC head = 2.0 M. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Discriminative learning rates (DLR) is a *layer-wise learning rate schedule* used in transfer learning where different layers of the network receive different learning rates based on their depth and proximity to the output. The intuition: layers closer to the output are *more task-specific* and can tolerate (or require) larger updates, while layers closer to the input are *more general* and need gentler updates to avoid catastrophic forgetting.

**Derivation:**

Let $L$ be the total number of trainable blocks (excluding the head). The head is indexed as $\ell = L$ and receives the base rate $\eta_0$. The deepest feature block is indexed as $\ell = L - 1$ and receives $\eta_0 \cdot \gamma$, the next as $\eta_0 \cdot \gamma^2$, and so on, down to the input block at $\ell = 0$ which receives $\eta_0 \cdot \gamma^L$. Generalizing:

$$
\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}, \quad \ell \in \{0, 1, \ldots, L\}
$$

where $\gamma \in (0, 1)$ is the discriminative decay factor (commonly $\gamma = 0.95$ in NLP and $\gamma = 0.5$ in CV).

In practice, the implementation uses PyTorch *parameter groups*:

- For frozen blocks: $\eta_\ell = 0$ (set `requires_grad = False`).
- For fine-tuned blocks: small $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$.
- For the new head: $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $L = 5$ blocks, $\gamma = 0.5$, $\eta_0 = 10^{-3}$, frozen = blocks 1, 2, 3, fine-tuned = blocks 4, 5 + FC head.

**Step 1 — Re-index with $\ell = 0$ for the input block and $\ell = L = 5$ for the head:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1} \ (\ell = 0) &: \eta = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{5} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.03125 = 3.125 \times 10^{-5} \\
\text{Block 2} \ (\ell = 1) &: \eta = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{4} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.0625 = 6.25 \times 10^{-5} \\
\text{Block 3} \ (\ell = 2) &: \eta = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{3} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.125 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \\
\text{Block 4} \ (\ell = 3) &: \eta = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{2} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.25 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
\text{Block 5} \ (\ell = 4) &: \eta = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{1} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \\
\text{FC Head} \ (\ell = 5) &: \eta = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{0} = 10^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Since blocks 1, 2, 3 are frozen, their learning rates are overridden to $\eta = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1} &: \eta = 0 \quad \text{(frozen)} \\
\text{Block 2} &: \eta = 0 \quad \text{(frozen)} \\
\text{Block 3} &: \eta = 0 \quad \text{(frozen)} \\
\text{Block 4} &: \eta = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \quad \text{(fine-tuned)} \\
\text{Block 5} &: \eta = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \quad \text{(fine-tuned)} \\
\text{FC Head} &: \eta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(fine-tuned)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage of trainable parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frozen} &= 15.4 \text{ M} \\
\text{Fine-tuned (blocks 4, 5)} &= 8.2 \text{ M} \\
\text{New FC head} &= 2.0 \text{ M} \\
\text{Trainable} &= 8.2 + 2.0 = 10.2 \text{ M}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Trainable \%} = \frac{10.2 \text{ M}}{25.6 \text{ M}} \times 100\% = 39.84\% \approx 39.8\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Blocks 1, 2, 3 are frozen ($\eta = 0$); block 4 trains at $\eta = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$; block 5 at $\eta = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$; the new FC head at $\eta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$. Approximately **39.8%** of the parameters are trainable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to override frozen-block LR to 0:** Many students compute the geometric schedule but forget that frozen layers should *not* be passed to the optimizer at all. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Confusing "freezing" with "zero initialization":** Freezing means *using the pre-trained values as constants*. It does *not* mean re-initializing to zero (that would destroy the network's feature extraction).
> 3. **Wrong re-indexing of layers:** Off-by-one errors in $\ell$ when applying $\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}$. Always state the indexing convention explicitly at the start of the answer.
> 4. **Omitting the new FC head from the diagram:** The KTU board expects the new classifier head to be drawn *explicitly* as a separate block with its own LR, not merged with the rest of the network.
> 5. **Stating that freezing "saves forward-pass time":** This is **incorrect** — frozen layers still execute forward computation. Only the backward pass and gradient storage are saved.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **Transfer Learning** — reusing a pre-trained model on a related target task.
- ✅ **Source Domain $\mathcal{D}_S$** — large dataset for pre-training (e.g., ImageNet).
- ✅ **Target Domain $\mathcal{D}_T$** — smaller dataset for the downstream task.
- ✅ **Freezing** — setting `requires_grad = False` so weights are not updated.
- ✅ **Fine-Tuning** — continuing training on $\mathcal{D}_T$ with a smaller learning rate.
- ✅ **Discriminative Learning Rates** — per-layer LR schedule $\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}$.
- ✅ **Catastrophic Forgetting** — sudden loss of pre-trained knowledge when training on new data without freezing.

### Must-Know Heuristics
- ✅ Freeze the first $\sim 2/3$ of the network; fine-tune the last $\sim 1/3$ + new head.
- ✅ Use $\eta_{\text{fine}} \approx 0.1 \times \eta_{\text{pre}}$ (10× smaller).
- ✅ Freeze when $N_T < 10{,}000$; full fine-tune when $N_T > 100{,}000$.
- ✅ Replace the final FC layer to match $C_T$ target classes.
- ✅ Always verify domain similarity $\sigma$ between source and target before choosing a strategy.

### Must-Know Benefits
- ✅ **Speedup** — 3× to 10× fewer training epochs.
- ✅ **Memory savings** — ~40% reduction in gradient buffer size.
- ✅ **Better generalization** on small datasets.
- ✅ **Prevents catastrophic forgetting** of low-level features.

### Must-Know Limitations
- ✅ Domain mismatch between $\mathcal{D}_S$ and $\mathcal{D}_T$ can hurt accuracy.
- ✅ Frozen layers cannot adapt to new low-level statistics (e.g., grayscale medical images).
- ✅ Requires careful LR tuning; a single high LR can destroy pre-trained weights even in fine-tuned layers.

### Must-Know Formulas (Quick Recall)
- ✅ Update rule (frozen): $\theta_F^{(t+1)} = \theta_F^{(t)}$
- ✅ Update rule (fine-tuned): $\theta_U^{(t+1)} = \theta_U^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta_U} \mathcal{L}$
- ✅ Discriminative LR: $\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L - \ell}$
- ✅ Trainable fraction: $f_{\text{trainable}} = \frac{\lvert\theta_U\rvert}{\lvert\theta_F\rvert + \lvert\theta_U\rvert}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoencoders: Standard, Sparse, Denoising, Contractive, and Variational Autoencoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autoencoders: Standard, Sparse, Denoising, Contractive, and Variational Autoencoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Autoencoder (AE)?

An **Autoencoder** is a special class of **unsupervised neural network** designed to learn a compressed, distributed representation (encoding) of input data and then reconstruct the original input from that representation. It is composed of two symmetric sub-networks: an **Encoder** $f_\phi$ that maps high-dimensional input $x$ to a low-dimensional latent vector $h$, and a **Decoder** $g_\theta$ that maps the latent vector back to a reconstruction $r$ in the original input space.

Formally, the network learns two functions:

$$
h = f_\phi(x) = \sigma(W_e x + b_e) \quad \text{(Encoder)}
$$

$$
r = g_\theta(h) = \sigma'(W_d h + b_d) \quad \text{(Decoder)}
$$

The training objective minimizes a **reconstruction loss** $L(x, r)$ — typically the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** for continuous inputs or **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** for binary inputs:

$$
L_{\text{rec}}(x, r) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - r_i \rVert^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCAT604 — Module 4):** An autoencoder is essentially a **dimensionality reduction and feature learning** algorithm. The bottleneck layer (latent space) forces the network to learn only the most salient, invariant features of the data — analogous to PCA, but **non-linear and data-adaptive**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Art Restorer" Metaphor

Imagine a **restorer of Renaissance paintings**:

1. The **Encoder** is like a detective who studies a damaged painting and writes a *concise description* on a notecard (the **latent code**): "Oil on canvas, 16th century, depicts a woman in a blue dress holding a flower." This notecard is a **bottleneck** — far less information than the full painting.
2. The **Decoder** is the restorer who reads that notecard and **reconstructs the painting** from the description alone.

If the description is *too verbose* (large latent code), the restorer just memorizes the painting — this is **overfitting**. If the description is *too terse* (too small a bottleneck), the restorer cannot reproduce meaningful detail — this is **underfitting**. The **art of autoencoders** is finding the right balance, with the additional constraint that different variations (sparse, denoising, contractive, variational) change the *rules* the detective uses to write the notecard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** All five variants discussed in this module share the same **encoder–decoder skeleton** but differ in *what additional constraint* is imposed on the latent code $h$. This constraint determines what kind of features the network learns.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Autoencoders Covered in KTU Module 4

| # | Autoencoder Type | Core Constraint / Inductive Bias | Primary Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Standard (Vanilla) AE** | Bottleneck dimension $d_h < d_x$ | Non-linear PCA, feature extraction |
| 2 | **Sparse AE** | Most latent activations $\approx 0$ | Interpretable features, dictionary learning |
| 3 | **Denoising AE (DAE)** | Reconstruct clean $x$ from corrupted $\tilde{x}$ | Robust feature learning, imputation |
| 4 | **Contractive AE (CAE)** | Encoder Jacobian has small Frobenius norm | Locally invariant feature learning |
| 5 | **Variational AE (VAE)** | Latent $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$; KL-regularized | Generative modeling, sampling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Autoencoder Pipeline

Every autoencoder — regardless of variant — follows the same **three-stage forward pass**:

1. **Encoding Stage** — compress $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ into $h \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ where typically $d_h \ll d_x$.
2. **Latent Constraint Stage** — apply a *regularizer* $\Omega(h)$ that shapes the properties of the latent space.
3. **Decoding Stage** — reconstruct $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ from $h$ and compute the **reconstruction error**.

The **generalized loss function** for *any* regularized autoencoder is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell\bigl(x_i, g_\theta(f_\phi(x_i))\bigr)}_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} + \lambda \cdot \underbrace{\Omega(h; x)}_{\text{Regularizer}}
$$

where $\lambda \geq 0$ is a **hyperparameter** controlling the strength of regularization, and $\ell(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a per-sample loss (MSE or BCE).

## 2.2 Why a Bottleneck? The Information-Theoretic View

Without a bottleneck, the network would trivially learn the **identity function** $g_\theta(f_\phi(x)) = x$ by memorizing the input. The bottleneck forces the network to learn a **lower-dimensional manifold** $\mathcal{M}$ on which the data approximately lies. For data lying on a $k$-dimensional manifold in $\mathbb{R}^{d_x}$, the optimal latent dimension is $d_h \approx k$.

## 2.3 The Five Variants — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Standard (Vanilla) Autoencoder

**Encoder:** A stack of fully-connected (or convolutional) layers producing $h = f_\phi(x)$.

**Decoder:** A mirror-image stack that upsamples $h$ back to $\hat{x}$.

**Loss:** Pure reconstruction — there is *no* regularizer ($\lambda = 0$ for the standard variant when $d_h < d_x$):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{AE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $d_h \geq d_x$ and there is **no other regularizer**, the autoencoder can learn the identity. Therefore, either $d_h < d_x$ (undercomplete) or some other regularizer is required.

**Intuition:** It is the **non-linear generalization of PCA** — same idea (project to lower dimension, reconstruct), but the projection is learned via a deep non-linear map.

---

### 2.3.2 Sparse Autoencoder

**Key Idea:** Even when $d_h$ is *large* (overcomplete), enforce that **only a small fraction of latent units are active** for any given input. This forces the network to discover **disentangled, interpretable features** — similar to independent components.

**Sparsity Parameter $\rho$:** A small number (e.g., $\rho = 0.05$) representing the *desired* average activation of each hidden unit across the training set.

**Actual Activation:** For hidden unit $j$, the empirical mean activation over a batch of $N$ samples is:

$$
\hat{\rho}_j = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} h_j(x_i) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_j^{(2)}\bigl(x_i\bigr)
$$

**KL-Divergence Penalty:** The regularizer penalizes the divergence between $\hat{\rho}_j$ and $\rho$ for every hidden unit $j$:

$$
\Omega_{\text{sparse}} = \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \text{KL}\bigl(\rho \;\Vert\; \hat{\rho}_j\bigr)
$$

The **Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence** between two Bernoulli distributions with parameters $\rho$ and $\hat{\rho}_j$ is:

$$
\text{KL}\bigl(\rho \;\Vert\; \hat{\rho}_j\bigr) = \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1-\rho) \log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j}
$$

**Total Loss:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2 + \beta \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \text{KL}\bigl(\rho \;\Vert\; \hat{\rho}_j\bigr)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KL-Divergence?** It is **asymmetric**, equals zero when $\hat{\rho}_j = \rho$, and diverges as $\hat{\rho}_j \to 0$ or $\hat{\rho}_j \to 1$. This produces a **strong, smooth penalty** for any hidden unit whose average activation drifts away from the target sparsity $\rho$.

---

### 2.3.3 Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)

**Key Idea:** Instead of feeding the *clean* input $x$ into the encoder, feed a **stochastically corrupted** version $\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x} \mid x)$. The decoder must still reconstruct the *clean* $x$. This forces the encoder to learn features that capture the **stable, high-level structure** of $x$ rather than memorize pixel-level noise.

**Corruption Process $q(\tilde{x} \mid x)$ — Common Choices:**

| Corruption Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Masking Noise** | A fraction $\nu$ of inputs are randomly set to 0 | General-purpose |
| **Gaussian Noise** | $\tilde{x} = x + \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$ | Continuous data (images, audio) |
| **Salt-and-Pepper** | A fraction of pixels set to min or max value | Image data |
| **Dropout-style** | Random feature dropout | Tabular data |

**Loss:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x} \mid x_i)} \Bigl[ \;\lVert x_i - g_\theta\bigl(f_\phi(\tilde{x})\bigr) \rVert^2 \Bigr]
$$

The expectation is approximated by sampling one or a few corrupted versions per input per training step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Result (Vincent 2011):** A DAE with infinitesimal Gaussian corruption and small $\sigma$ is **analytically equivalent** to a Contractive Autoencoder up to second-order Taylor expansion terms. Both learn a **tangent space** to the data manifold.

---

### 2.3.4 Contractive Autoencoder (CAE)

**Key Idea:** Directly penalize the **sensitivity of the encoder** to small input perturbations. The encoder is encouraged to have a **small Jacobian** at the training points, meaning $f_\phi(x)$ changes slowly as $x$ moves a little — i.e., the features are **locally invariant**.

**Jacobian Matrix of the Encoder:**

$$
J_f(x) = \frac{\partial f_\phi(x)}{\partial x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}
$$

**Frobenius Norm of the Jacobian:**

$$
\lVert J_f(x) \rVert_F^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \left( \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial x_i} \right)^2
$$

**Total Loss:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Bigl[ \;\lVert x_i - g_\theta(f_\phi(x_i)) \rVert^2 + \lambda \cdot \lVert J_f(x_i) \rVert_F^2 \Bigr]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Frobenius Norm?** Squaring the partial derivatives and summing them produces a **smooth, differentiable, convex-in-derivatives** penalty that biases the encoder to be **locally contractive** (a Lipschitz constant < 1 in feature space). This is the geometric meaning of "contractive."

**Comparison with DAE:** The DAE penalty is *stochastic* (averaged over corruption noise), while the CAE penalty is *deterministic* (evaluated exactly at $x$). The CAE is a **higher-order, pointwise** version of the DAE penalty.

---

### 2.3.5 Variational Autoencoder (VAE)

**Key Idea:** This is the most profound variant. Instead of encoding $x$ as a *single point* $h$, the encoder outputs the **parameters $(\mu, \sigma)$ of a probability distribution** over the latent space, and we *sample* a latent code $z$ from that distribution. The decoder then reconstructs $x$ from the sampled $z$.

**This makes the VAE a *generative model*:** once trained, we can sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and decode it to produce *new* samples that look like the training data.

**Encoder Outputs (the "inference network"):**

$$
\mu = W_\mu h_1 + b_\mu, \qquad \log \sigma^2 = W_\sigma h_1 + b_\sigma
$$

where $h_1$ is the output of an intermediate fully-connected layer from $x$.

**Sampling with the Reparameterization Trick:**

$$
z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon, \quad \text{where} \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

**Decoder Output:**

$$
\hat{x} = g_\theta(z) = \sigma_{\text{dec}}(W_d z + b_d)
$$

**Loss Function — The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO):**

The VAE minimizes the **negative ELBO**, which decomposes into a **reconstruction term** and a **KL-regularization term**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\bigl[ \log p_\theta(x \mid z) \bigr] + \text{KL}\bigl( q_\phi(z \mid x) \;\Vert\; p(z) \bigr)
$$

For **continuous $x$ with Gaussian likelihood** $p_\theta(x \mid z) = \mathcal{N}(x; g_\theta(z), I)$ and **standard normal prior** $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} \bigl( x_i - \hat{x}_i \bigr)^2}_{\text{Reconstruction (MSE)}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \bigl( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \bigr)}_{\text{KL Regularizer (closed form)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KL term for two Gaussians $q(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$ and $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ has a **closed-form** expression. This avoids expensive sampling for that part of the loss.

**Why the Reparameterization Trick?** Sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is a **non-differentiable** operation. Writing $z = \mu + \sigma \epsilon$ with $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ externalizes the randomness into $\epsilon$, making $z$ a **deterministic, differentiable function** of $(\mu, \sigma)$ and a noise variable $\epsilon$. Gradients now flow freely through the sampling step.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important table for the KTU ESE — memorize it before the exam.**

| # | AE Variant | Encoder Output | Latent Constraint | Total Loss $\mathcal{L}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Standard AE** | $h = f_\phi(x)$ | $d_h < d_x$ (bottleneck) | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_i \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2$ |
| 2 | **Sparse AE** | $h = f_\phi(x)$ | $\hat{\rho}_j \to \rho$ (KL sparsity) | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_i \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2 + \beta \sum_j \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$ |
| 3 | **Denoising AE** | $h = f_\phi(\tilde{x})$ | $\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x} \mid x)$ (corruption) | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_i \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{x}} \lVert x_i - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x})) \rVert^2$ |
| 4 | **Contractive AE** | $h = f_\phi(x)$ | $\lVert J_f(x) \rVert_F^2$ small | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_i \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2 + \lambda \lVert J_f(x_i) \rVert_F^2$ |
| 5 | **Variational AE** | $\mu, \sigma = f_\phi(x)$ | $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $-\mathbb{E}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] + \text{KL}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))$ |

**Additional Compact Formulas (for direct exam use):**

**KL Divergence between two univariate Gaussians** $\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$:

$$
\text{KL}\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2) \;\Vert\; \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)\bigr) = \log \frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} + \frac{\sigma_1^2 + (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}{2\sigma_2^2} - \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Reparameterization (compact):**

$$
z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

**Jacobian Frobenius penalty (component form):**

$$
\lVert J_f(x) \rVert_F^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} \left( h_j \cdot (1 - h_j) \cdot w_{ij}^{(1)} \right)^2
$$

(for sigmoid activations, where $h_j(1 - h_j)$ is the derivative and $w_{ij}^{(1)}$ is the encoder weight connecting $x_i$ to $h_j$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Variant | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **Standard AE** | Anomaly detection in IoT sensor streams; non-linear feature extraction for downstream classifiers |
| **Sparse AE** | EEG/fMRI signal decomposition; dictionary learning for medical imaging; interpretable feature discovery in genomics |
| **Denoising AE** | Image super-resolution; audio denoising; MRI artifact removal; document inpainting |
| **Contractive AE** | Domain-adversarial features; domain-invariant representation for transfer learning |
| **Variational AE** | Drug molecule generation; text-to-image synthesis precursors; data augmentation; anomaly detection via reconstruction probability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard AE: Equivalence to PCA in the Linear Case

When the encoder is a single linear layer $h = W_e x$ and the decoder is a single linear layer $\hat{x} = W_d h$, and we use MSE loss, the optimal solution recovers the **PCA subspace**.

**Step 1 — Set up the optimization.**

$$
\min_{W_e, W_d} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - W_d W_e x_i \rVert^2
$$

**Step 2 — Assume centered data** ($\bar{x} = 0$, $C = \frac{1}{N} X X^\top$ is the covariance matrix).

**Step 3 — Set the gradient with respect to $W_d$ to zero.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_d} = -2 W_e X X^\top + 2 W_d W_e X X^\top W_e^\top = 0
$$

Solving gives:

$$
W_d = W_e^\top \quad \text{(when } W_e \text{ is orthonormal)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.** The loss becomes:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \lVert x_i - W_e^\top W_e x_i \rVert^2
$$

This is the **projection error onto the subspace spanned by the rows of $W_e$**, minimized when $W_e$ consists of the **top $d_h$ eigenvectors of $C$** — i.e., **Principal Component Analysis**. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A *linear* autoencoder with bottleneck $d_h$ is mathematically equivalent to **PCA** with $d_h$ components. Non-linear encoders generalize this to a **non-linear manifold**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sparse AE: Derivation of the KL Sparsity Term

**Step 1 — Model each hidden unit's activation as a Bernoulli random variable.**

Treat $h_j$ as a binary random variable (active = 1, inactive = 0) with probability $p(h_j = 1) = \hat{\rho}_j$.

**Step 2 — Define the target distribution.**

We want $p(h_j = 1) = \rho$ for all $j$, where $\rho$ is a small number (e.g., 0.05).

**Step 3 — Measure divergence with KL.**

$$
\text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j) = \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1 - \rho) \log \frac{1 - \rho}{1 - \hat{\rho}_j}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the four corner cases.**

- $\hat{\rho}_j = \rho$ → $\text{KL} = 0$ (no penalty).
- $\hat{\rho}_j \to 0$ → second term $\to \log 1 = 0$, first term $\to -\infty \cdot \rho$ (penalty blows up).
- $\hat{\rho}_j \to 1$ → first term $\to 0$, second term $\to -\infty \cdot (1 - \rho)$ (penalty blows up).
- $\hat{\rho}_j = 0.5, \rho = 0.05$ → finite positive penalty.

**Step 5 — Total regularizer.**

$$
\Omega_{\text{sparse}} = \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)
$$

**Step 6 — Final loss:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SAE}} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2}_{\text{Reconstruction}} + \beta \cdot \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)}_{\text{Sparsity penalty}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** With $d_h = 100$, $\rho = 0.05$, suppose $\hat{\rho}_j = 0.5$ for one unit. Then $\text{KL}(0.05 \Vert 0.5) = 0.05 \log(0.1) + 0.95 \log(1.9) = 0.05 \cdot (-2.303) + 0.95 \cdot 0.642 = -0.115 + 0.610 = 0.495$. The penalty for that one unit alone is **0.495 nats**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Denoising AE: Stochastic Corruption as Implicit Regularizer

**Step 1 — Define the corruption distribution.**

For masking noise with fraction $\nu$:

$$
q(\tilde{x} \mid x) = \prod_{i=1}^{d_x} (1-\nu)^{\mathbb{1}[\tilde{x}_i = x_i]} \cdot \nu^{\mathbb{1}[\tilde{x}_i = 0]}
$$

**Step 2 — Approximate the expectation in the loss.**

For each $x_i$, sample $K$ corrupted versions $\{\tilde{x}_i^{(k)}\}_{k=1}^{K}$ and average the loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} \approx \frac{1}{NK} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \lVert x_i - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x}_i^{(k)})) \rVert^2
$$

In practice, $K = 1$ suffices because stochastic gradient descent already provides the noise.

**Step 3 — Score matching interpretation (Vincent 2011).**

Expand the encoder around $x$ via Taylor:

$$
f_\phi(\tilde{x}) = f_\phi(x) + J_f(x)(\tilde{x} - x) + \frac{1}{2}(\tilde{x} - x)^\top H_f(\tilde{x} - x) + \cdots
$$

The denoising loss is minimized when $f_\phi$ learns the **score function** $\nabla_x \log p(x)$ — the gradient of the log data density. This means the DAE implicitly learns the **data-generating distribution** through denoising.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Contractive AE: Frobenius Jacobian Penalty

**Step 1 — Recall the encoder.**

For a single-layer sigmoid encoder $h = \sigma(W x + b)$:

$$
h_j = \sigma(s_j), \quad s_j = \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} W_{ji} x_i + b_j
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian entries.**

$$
\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial x_i} = h_j(1 - h_j) \cdot W_{ji}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Frobenius norm.**

$$
\lVert J_f(x) \rVert_F^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \left( h_j(1-h_j) W_{ji} \right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Final CAE loss.**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} h_j(1-h_j)^2 \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} W_{ji}^2 \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Geometric interpretation.**

The Frobenius norm of the Jacobian is the **operator norm squared** in expectation over input directions. Penalizing it forces the encoder to map all inputs in a small neighborhood of $x$ to **approximately the same latent point** $h$. This is **local contractivity** — a Lipschitz constant of the encoder is reduced below 1.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — VAE: The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)

This is the **most important derivation in this module** — it is asked in nearly every KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Goal: maximize $\log p_\theta(x)$.**

We cannot compute it directly because $p_\theta(x) = \int p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z) \, dz$ is intractable. Use variational inference.

**Step 2 — Introduce a variational posterior $q_\phi(z \mid x)$.**

$$
\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z) \, dz = \log \int q_\phi(z \mid x) \cdot \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)} \, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Jensen's inequality.**

Since $\log$ is concave:

$$
\log p_\theta(x) \geq \int q_\phi(z \mid x) \log \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)} \, dz = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side.**

$$
\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] + \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\left[ \log \frac{p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the two terms.**

$$
= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{\text{KL}\bigl(q_\phi(z \mid x) \;\Vert\; p(z)\bigr)}_{\text{Regularization}}
$$

This is the **ELBO** — a lower bound on $\log p_\theta(x)$. Maximizing it is equivalent to minimizing the **negative ELBO**, which is the VAE loss.

**Step 6 — Closed-form KL for Gaussian prior and posterior.**

With $q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$ and $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$
\text{KL}\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I) \;\Vert\; \mathcal{N}(0, I)\bigr) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \left( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Stochastic gradient estimation via reparameterization.**

The reconstruction term requires sampling $z \sim q_\phi(z \mid x)$. The Monte Carlo estimate with one sample $z = \mu + \sigma \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] \approx \log p_\theta(x \mid z = \mu + \sigma \epsilon)
$$

**Step 8 — Final VAE loss (negative ELBO, per sample).**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}}(x) = -\log p_\theta(x \mid \mu + \sigma \epsilon) + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \left( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 9 — Practical form (Gaussian decoder, unit variance).**

For continuous $x$ with $\hat{x} = g_\theta(z)$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}}(x) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \left( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When the KTU ESE asks for the VAE loss, **always state the two-component structure**: reconstruction + KL. Then write the **closed-form Gaussian KL** explicitly. Most students lose marks by forgetting the constant term $-1$ in the KL.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Autoencoder

| Decision Criterion | Recommended AE | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Need a generative model (sample new data) | **VAE** | Latent space is a smooth probabilistic manifold |
| Need robust features under noise | **DAE** or **CAE** | Both penalize sensitivity to perturbations |
| Need interpretable, disentangled features | **Sparse AE** | Each unit activates for a specific concept |
| Need non-linear PCA baseline | **Standard AE** | Simplest, no extra regularizer |
| Have a very small dataset | **Contractive AE** | Stronger regularization reduces overfitting |
| Need to detect anomalies | **VAE** (reconstruction probability) or **Sparse AE** | Out-of-distribution inputs yield high reconstruction error |
| Need to handle missing data | **Denoising AE** | Trained on partial inputs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — All Five Variants on a Common Canvas

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the five autoencoders relate to each other structurally, with the **encoder–decoder skeleton** shared by all and the **regularization module** varying by variant.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inpX["Input X"]:::inputNode
    corrModule["Corruption Module qXtilde_given_X"]:::corrNode
    encShared["Encoder Network f_phi"]:::encNode
    latentConstraint["Latent Constraint Module variant-specific"]:::latNode
    decShared["Decoder Network g_theta"]:::decNode
    reconX["Reconstruction X_hat"]:::outputNode

    lossRecon["Reconstruction Loss L_recon"]:::lossNode
    regPenalty["Regularizer Omega variant-specific"]:::regNode
    totalLoss["Total Loss L"]:::finalNode

    inpX -->|Optional: DAE only| corrModule
    corrModule -->|Corrupted X_tilde| encShared
    inpX -->|Standard / Sparse / CAE / VAE| encShared

    encShared --> latentConstraint
    latentConstraint --> decShared
    decShared --> reconX

    reconX --> lossRecon
    inpX --> lossRecon

    latentConstraint --> regPenalty
    regPenalty --> totalLoss
    lossRecon --> totalLoss
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    enc1["Encoder Layer 1: W_e1 x + b_e1"]:::stdEnc
    enc2["Encoder Layer 2: W_e2 h1 + b_e2"]:::stdEnc
    bottleneck["Latent h in R^d_h bottleneck"]:::stdBottle
    dec1["Decoder Layer 1: W_d1 h + b_d1"]:::stdDec
    dec2["Decoder Layer 2: W_d2 h1 + b_d2"]:::stdDec
    outNode["Reconstruction x_hat in R^d_x"]:::stdOutput

    inNode --> enc1 --> enc2 --> bottleneck
    bottleneck --> dec1 --> dec2 --> outNode
```

```mermaid

    meanAct["Compute mean activation rho_j per unit"]:::spMean
    klPen["KL Divergence Penalty KL rho_||_rho_j summed over j"]:::spKL
    spLoss["Sparsity Loss beta * Omega_sparse"]:::spLoss
    decSp["Decoder g_theta"]:::spDec
    recSp["Reconstruction x_hat"]:::spRec

    inSp --> encSp --> latentActivations
    latentActivations --> meanAct --> klPen --> spLoss
    latentActivations --> decSp --> recSp
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    latentDAE["Latent h"]:::daeLat

    cleanX -.->|target label| reconDAE
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    encCA["Encoder f_phi"]:::caeEnc
    hCA["Latent h"]:::caeLat
    jacCA["Jacobian J_f of encoder w.r.t. x"]:::caeJac
    fNorm["Frobenius Norm squared"]:::caeFN

    decCA["Decoder g_theta"]:::caeDec
    recCA["Reconstruction x_hat"]:::caeRec

    encCA --> hCA
    hCA --> jacCA --> fNorm --> jacPen
    encCA --> decCA --> recCA --> mseCA
```

```mermaid
    encSharedVAE["Encoder Backbone h_pre"]:::vaeEncShared

    inVAE --> encSharedVAE
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define an autoencoder. Write the general loss function used to train it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **autoencoder** is an unsupervised neural network that learns to compress input data into a lower-dimensional latent representation and then reconstruct the original input from that representation. It consists of an **encoder** $f_\phi$ that maps $x \to h$ and a **decoder** $g_\theta$ that maps $h \to \hat{x}$.

The general loss function combines a **reconstruction term** and a **regularization term**:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - g_\theta(f_\phi(x_i)) \rVert^2 + \lambda \, \Omega(h; x)
$$

where $\Omega(h; x)$ is the regularizer specific to the autoencoder variant (e.g., sparsity, Jacobian, or KL term).

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for encoder–decoder description, 1 mark for loss formula.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the reparameterization trick in a Variational Autoencoder? Why is it needed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **reparameterization trick** rewrites the sampling of the latent variable $z$ from $q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$ in a differentiable form:

$$
z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

**Why it is needed:** Direct sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is a **non-differentiable stochastic operation**, which blocks gradient backpropagation through the encoder. By isolating the randomness into the noise variable $\epsilon$, the latent code $z$ becomes a **deterministic, differentiable** function of the encoder's outputs $(\mu, \sigma)$. This allows end-to-end training via standard backpropagation while preserving the stochastic behavior of the latent space.

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for problem statement, 1 mark for solution.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice 1 (14 Marks) — Question A

### **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Question A:**  
**(a)** Explain the architecture and training procedure of a **Sparse Autoencoder**. Derive the KL-divergence based sparsity penalty used in its loss function. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)**. Derive the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** loss function used to train it. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Architecture (2 marks):**

A **Sparse Autoencoder** has the same encoder–decoder structure as a standard autoencoder but typically uses a **larger hidden layer** ($d_h \geq d_x$, i.e., overcomplete). The key addition is a **sparsity constraint** applied to the hidden activations. A typical configuration:

- **Input layer:** dimension $d_x$
- **Hidden layer:** dimension $d_h \geq d_x$ with **sigmoid activation** (so that activations are bounded in $[0, 1]$ and can be interpreted as firing rates)
- **Output layer:** dimension $d_x$

**Training Procedure (2 marks):**

The network is trained via **stochastic gradient descent** to minimize the combined reconstruction + sparsity loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SAE}} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2}_{\text{Reconstruction}} + \beta \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)}_{\text{Sparsity penalty}}
$$

**Derivation of KL Sparsity Penalty (3 marks):**

Let $\rho$ be the **target sparsity** (e.g., 0.05) and $\hat{\rho}_j = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i h_j(x_i)$ be the **empirical mean activation** of unit $j$. Modeling the activation as a Bernoulli random variable with probability $\hat{\rho}_j$, the divergence from the target Bernoulli($\rho$) is:

$$
\text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j) = \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1 - \rho) \log \frac{1 - \rho}{1 - \hat{\rho}_j}
$$

This equals zero when $\hat{\rho}_j = \rho$ and diverges as $\hat{\rho}_j \to 0$ or $\hat{\rho}_j \to 1$. Summing over all hidden units gives the total sparsity penalty $\Omega_{\text{sparse}}$.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The VAE consists of:
1. An **encoder network** with two output heads: a $\mu$-head and a $\log\sigma^2$-head, both producing vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{d_z}$.
2. A **sampling layer** that draws $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and computes $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ (reparameterization).
3. A **decoder network** $g_\theta$ that takes $z$ and outputs the reconstruction $\hat{x}$.

**Working (1 mark):**

The encoder maps $x$ to the **parameters of a Gaussian posterior** $q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$. A latent code $z$ is sampled from this distribution, and the decoder attempts to reconstruct $x$ from $z$. The training objective balances reconstruction fidelity with proximity of $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ to the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

**ELBO Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** We want to maximize the marginal log-likelihood $\log p_\theta(x)$, but the integral $\int p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z) \, dz$ is intractable.

**Step 2:** Introduce the variational posterior $q_\phi(z \mid x)$:

$$
\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z) \, dz = \log \int q_\phi(z \mid x) \cdot \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)} \, dz
$$

**Step 3:** Apply **Jensen's inequality** ($\log$ is concave):

$$
\log p_\theta(x) \geq \int q_\phi(z \mid x) \log \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)} \, dz
$$

**Step 4:** Expand:

$$
= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] - \text{KL}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))
$$

**Step 5:** The right-hand side is the **ELBO**. The VAE loss is the negative ELBO:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] + \text{KL}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))
$$

**Step 6:** For Gaussian assumptions, the KL term has the closed form:

$$
\text{KL}(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I) \Vert \mathcal{N}(0, I)) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} (\mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1)
$$

The reconstruction term is estimated via the **reparameterization trick** with a single Monte Carlo sample $z = \mu + \sigma \epsilon$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Question B

### **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Question B:**  
**(a)** Explain the concept of a **Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)**. Discuss the various noise corruption strategies used and derive the loss function. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** What is a **Contractive Autoencoder (CAE)**? Derive the Jacobian-based penalty term and explain its geometric meaning. Compare it with the Denoising Autoencoder. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Concept (2 marks):**

A **Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)** is a regularized autoencoder trained to reconstruct the **clean** original input $x$ from a **stochastically corrupted** version $\tilde{x}$. The corruption process $q(\tilde{x} \mid x)$ is a conditional distribution that perturbs $x$ in some way. By forcing the network to recover $x$ from $\tilde{x}$, the encoder is prevented from learning a trivial identity mapping and is instead compelled to capture the **stable, high-level structure** of the data distribution.

**Corruption Strategies (2 marks):**

| Strategy | Formula / Description |
|---|---|
| **Masking noise** | A fraction $\nu$ of input components are set to 0; the rest are unchanged. |
| **Gaussian noise** | $\tilde{x} = x + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$. |
| **Salt-and-pepper noise** | A fraction of pixels is set to the minimum or maximum value. |

**Loss Function Derivation (3 marks):**

The DAE loss is the expected reconstruction error under the corruption distribution:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x} \mid x_i)} \Bigl[ \;\lVert x_i - g_\theta\bigl(f_\phi(\tilde{x})\bigr) \rVert^2 \Bigr]
$$

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1:** Define the corruption distribution $q(\tilde{x} \mid x)$. For masking noise with fraction $\nu$:

$$
q(\tilde{x} \mid x) = \prod_{i=1}^{d_x} (1-\nu)^{\mathbb{1}[\tilde{x}_i = x_i]} \cdot \nu^{\mathbb{1}[\tilde{x}_i = 0]}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the expectation:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{\tilde{x}} q(\tilde{x} \mid x_i) \lVert x_i - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x})) \rVert^2
$$

**Step 3:** Approximate via Monte Carlo — for each $x_i$, sample $K$ corrupted versions and average:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} \approx \frac{1}{NK} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \lVert x_i - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x}_i^{(k)})) \rVert^2
$$

In practice, $K = 1$ suffices because each minibatch provides fresh samples.

**Step 4:** Use **score matching interpretation** (Vincent 2011) — the optimal DAE learns the **score function** $\nabla_x \log p(x)$, equivalent to identifying the data manifold.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**

A **Contractive Autoencoder (CAE)** is a regularized autoencoder that adds an **analytic penalty on the sensitivity** of the encoder to small changes in the input. The penalty is the squared **Frobenius norm** of the encoder's Jacobian matrix.

**Jacobian Penalty Derivation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** For a single-layer sigmoid encoder $h = \sigma(Wx + b)$, the activation of unit $j$ is:

$$
h_j = \sigma(s_j), \quad s_j = \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} W_{ji} x_i + b_j
$$

**Step 2:** The derivative with respect to $x_i$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial x_i} = h_j(1 - h_j) \cdot W_{ji}
$$

**Step 3:** The Jacobian is the $d_h \times d_x$ matrix $J_f(x)$ with entries $\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial x_i}$.

**Step 4:** The Frobenius norm squared is:

$$
\lVert J_f(x) \rVert_F^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d_x} \sum_{j=1}^{d_h} \left( \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial x_i} \right)^2 = \sum_{i,j} \bigl( h_j(1 - h_j) W_{ji} \bigr)^2
$$

**Step 5:** The full CAE loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2 + \lambda \lVert J_f(x_i) \rVert_F^2 \right]
$$

**Geometric Meaning (1 mark):**

The Frobenius norm of the Jacobian is the **sum of squared directional derivatives** of the encoder. Penalizing it forces the encoder to map all inputs in a small input-space neighborhood of $x$ to **approximately the same latent point**. Geometrically, this means the encoder is **locally contractive** — a small ball in input space maps to a smaller ball in latent space. This biases the encoder to learn the **tangent plane** of the data manifold at $x$, producing locally invariant features.

**Comparison with DAE (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Denoising AE | Contractive AE |
|---|---|---|
| **Penalty type** | Stochastic (over random corruption) | Deterministic (analytic at $x$) |
| **Penalty computation** | Expected reconstruction error | Frobenius norm of Jacobian |
| **Noise level** | Controlled by $\nu$ or $\sigma$ | Controlled by $\lambda$ |
| **Equivalence** | Equivalent to CAE for **small** Gaussian noise (Vincent 2011) | Higher-order, pointwise version of DAE |
| **Differentiability** | Requires Monte Carlo approximation | Exact, closed-form derivative |

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU ESE:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Sparse AE and DAE** — Sparse AE constrains the **latent activations** to be sparse; DAE corrupts the **input** and reconstructs the clean version. These are entirely different regularizers.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the constant term $-1$ in the VAE KL** — The closed-form Gaussian KL is $\frac{1}{2} \sum (\mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1)$. Students routinely omit the $-1$.
> 
> 3. **Writing $\log p(z)$ instead of $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ in ELBO** — Be explicit: the prior is a **standard normal**.
> 
> 4. **Confusing $\sigma$ with $\sigma^2$ in the VAE reparameterization** — The encoder outputs $\log \sigma^2$, then $\sigma = \exp(0.5 \cdot \log \sigma^2)$. Be careful with this two-step transform.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting that the DAE target is the *clean* $x$**, not the corrupted $\tilde{x}$. The loss compares $\hat{x} = g(f(\tilde{x}))$ against the **original** $x$, not $\tilde{x}$.
> 
> 6. **CAEs use $J_f(x)$ of the *encoder only*, not the decoder.** A common error is computing the Jacobian of the full network.
> 
> 7. **Sparse AE's KL term is summed over hidden units** — do not forget the $\sum_{j=1}^{d_h}$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the KTU ESE:**

- ☐ **Standard AE** = encoder + decoder + bottleneck + MSE loss. Linear case is **PCA**.
- ☐ **Sparse AE** = Standard AE + **KL sparsity penalty** on hidden activations. Bernoulli divergence.
- ☐ **Denoising AE** = encoder takes **corrupted** $\tilde{x}$, decoder reconstructs **clean** $x$. Penalty is **stochastic**.
- ☐ **Contractive AE** = Standard AE + $\lambda \lVert J_f(x) \rVert_F^2$. Penalty is **deterministic** and pointwise.
- ☐ **Variational AE** = encoder outputs $(\mu, \sigma)$; sample $z = \mu + \sigma \epsilon$; loss = **negative ELBO**.
- ☐ **Reparameterization trick** = $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Makes sampling differentiable.
- ☐ **ELBO** = $\mathbb{E}_{q(z \mid x)}[\log p(x \mid z)] - \text{KL}(q(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))$. Derived via Jensen's inequality.
- ☐ **Closed-form Gaussian KL** = $\frac{1}{2} \sum_j (\mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1)$.
- ☐ **Frobenius Jacobian** = $\sum_{i,j} (h_j(1-h_j) W_{ji})^2$ for sigmoid encoder.
- ☐ **DAE and CAE are related** — both penalize sensitivity to input perturbations; DAE is stochastic, CAE is analytic.
- ☐ **VAE is the only generative model** among the five — sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and decode to get new data.
- ☐ **All five variants share the same encoder–decoder skeleton** — they differ only in the *regularizer* $\Omega(h; x)$.
- ☐ **Trade-off parameter** $\lambda$ (or $\beta$) controls reconstruction vs. regularization — always tune on a validation set.
- ☐ **Hyperparameters to remember**: $\rho$ (target sparsity), $\nu$ (corruption fraction), $\lambda$ (regularization strength), $d_z$ (latent dimension).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic carries high weightage in Module 4. It is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in End Semester Examinations (ESE) and a regular 3-mark question under Part A. Mastery of the **min-max objective function** and the **derivation of the optimal discriminator** is mandatory.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a class of deep learning frameworks introduced by **Ian Goodfellow and colleagues in 2014**, designed for **unsupervised machine learning**. It consists of two neural networks — a **Generator (G)** and a **Discriminator (D)** — that are trained simultaneously in an **adversarial (min-max) game**.

- The **Generator** $G$ maps a latent noise vector $z \sim p_z(z)$ (sampled from a simple prior distribution such as a Gaussian) to a synthetic data sample $G(z)$, attempting to mimic the true data distribution $p_{data}(x)$.
- The **Discriminator** $D$ is a binary classifier that outputs the probability $D(x)$ that an input sample $x$ is *real* (drawn from $p_{data}$) rather than *fake* (produced by $G$).
- The two networks compete in a **two-player zero-sum game**: $G$ tries to *fool* $D$, while $D$ tries to *distinguish* real from fake.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Definition (Bankable Answer):**
> A GAN is a generative model trained via an adversarial process in which two neural networks — a generator $G$ and a discriminator $D$ — contest with each other in a min-max optimization problem until the generator produces samples indistinguishable from real data (i.e., Nash Equilibrium is reached).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Counterfeiter vs. the Police

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**

Imagine a **counterfeiter (Generator)** trying to forge rare, valuable paintings, and a **police detective (Discriminator)** trying to catch forgeries.

| Role in GAN | Real-World Analogy | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Generator (G)** | Counterfeiter producing fake currency | Fool the police by making fakes indistinguishable from real notes |
| **Discriminator (D)** | Police detective examining notes | Correctly classify a note as "real" or "fake" |
| **Latent Vector $z$** | The blueprint or inspiration | Random starting point driving each forgery |
| **Adversarial Loss** | The escalating cat-and-mouse dynamic | Improves *both* the forger's skill and the detective's vigilance |

In the beginning, the forger is clumsy and the detective easily catches him. But each time the detective catches a fake, the forger learns from his mistakes and improves. Each time the forger produces a convincing fake, the detective must sharpen his detection skills. This **continuous adversarial pressure** drives both networks to mastery — and the eventual equilibrium is when the forger's notes are *so perfect* that the detective is essentially *guessing* (accuracy = 50%).

## 1.3 Why GANs Matter — Real-World Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of GANs**

- **Image Synthesis:** Photorealistic face generation (StyleGAN), art creation.
- **Super-Resolution:** Enhancing low-resolution medical scans (SRGAN).
- **Data Augmentation:** Generating synthetic training data for medical/rare-class problems.
- **Drug Discovery:** Generating novel molecular structures.
- **Style Transfer & Image-to-Image Translation:** Pix2Pix, CycleGAN.
- **Anomaly Detection:** In manufacturing quality control.

The **standard evaluation metrics** relevant to KTU are:

- **Inception Score (IS):** Measures both the *quality* and *diversity* of generated images. Higher is better.
- **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID):** Measures the distance between generated and real feature distributions. **Lower** FID indicates better quality.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adversarial Min-Max Game — Operational Walkthrough

The training of a GAN is governed by the **value function** $V(D, G)$, and the optimization is formulated as a **min-max problem**:

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Why a `max` over $D$?** The discriminator wants to *maximize* its classification accuracy. It outputs $D(x) \to 1$ for real samples and $D(G(z)) \to 0$ for fake samples, so $\log D(x) \to 0$ (good) and $\log(1 - D(G(z))) \to 0$ (good). Both terms are maximized when $D$ is optimal.

- **Why a `min` over $G$?** The generator wants to *minimize* $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. Since $D$ is fixed during the $G$-update, the only way to reduce this term is to make $D(G(z)) \to 1$ (i.e., produce samples the discriminator classifies as real). The generator thus "wins" by reducing the second expectation.

- **Why alternation, not joint update?** Because $D$ and $G$ are adversaries, joint training causes oscillation. The standard **Algorithm 1 (Goodfellow et al., 2014)** alternates between $k$ discriminator updates (typically $k=1$) and one generator update per minibatch.

- **Why a separate $G$ loss in practice?** Early in training, $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturates (its gradient is tiny when $D$ easily rejects $G$'s poor samples). Goodfellow's heuristic is to instead train $G$ to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ — non-saturating alternative. This keeps gradients strong.

- **Convergence — Nash Equilibrium:** The game theoretically converges to a **saddle point** (Nash equilibrium) where $p_g = p_{data}$ and $D(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere (the discriminator cannot distinguish real from fake).

## 2.2 The Discriminator's Decision Boundary

For a **fixed generator** $G$, the optimal discriminator $D^*_G(x)$ is given by:

$$D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

This is a **Bayes-optimal classifier** under the 0-1 loss given equal class priors in the joint real/fake mixture. It means: at any point $x$, the discriminator's confidence is the **ratio of real density to total density** at that point.

- When $p_{data}(x) \gg p_g(x)$, then $D^* \to 1$ (clearly real).
- When $p_g(x) \gg p_{data}(x)$, then $D^* \to 0$ (clearly fake).
- At equilibrium $p_g = p_{data}$, we get $D^* = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 ESE**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Value Function | $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Core min-max objective |
| Min-Max Game | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$ | $D$ maximizes, $G$ minimizes |
| Latent Prior | $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or $z \sim \mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$ | Standard Gaussian or uniform |
| Optimal Discriminator | $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Derivable by calculus of variations |
| Global Optimum Value | $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$ | At $p_g = p_{data}$ |
| CG* at Equilibrium | $C(G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot \mathrm{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$ | $\mathrm{JSD}$ = Jensen-Shannon Divergence |
| Non-Saturating G-Loss | $L_G = -\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))]$ | Used in practice for stable gradients |
| FID Metric | $\mathrm{FID} = \vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert^2 + \mathrm{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2})$ | Lower is better; uses Inception features |
| Inception Score | $\mathrm{IS} = \exp(\mathbb{E}_x \, D_{KL}(p(y \vert x) \parallel p(y)))$ | Higher is better |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The min-max formulation of GANs generalizes to any **two-player adversarial scenario**, e.g., **Adversarial Training in Cybersecurity** (defender vs. attacker networks), **Robust ML** against adversarial examples, and **Mechanism Design** in economics.
- The mathematical structure of $V(D,G)$ directly mirrors **Generative Replay in Continual Learning** and **Actor-Critic** formulations in Reinforcement Learning — making it a unifying concept across modern AI.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandate:** This derivation of the **optimal discriminator** and the **global optimum value** is a **direct, full-mark question** in KTU ESE. Master every step.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$

**Given:** The value function for a fixed generator $G$ is

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Goal:** Find $D^*(x)$ that maximizes $V(D, G)$ for a fixed $G$.

### Step 1 — Convert expectations to integrals

The expectations can be expressed as integrals over the data and generator distributions respectively:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

**Logic:** The expectation $\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[f(x)]$ is equivalent to $\int_x p_{data}(x) f(x) \, dx$ by definition.

### Step 2 — Change of variables in the second integral

Substitute $x = G(z)$. Since $G$ is a deterministic function, $dx$ corresponds to the measure induced by $G$ on the data space. Let $p_g(x)$ denote the induced generator distribution over data space. Then:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

**Logic:** By change of variables, integration over $z$ with density $p_z$ is converted to integration over $x$ with induced density $p_g$.

### Step 3 — Combine integrals

Both terms now share the same integration variable $x$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

**Logic:** The integrand is the sum of the two density-weighted log-probabilities; integration is over the data space $x$.

### Step 4 — Maximize the integrand point-wise

For any fixed $x$, the integrand is a function of $D(x)$ alone. Therefore, we can maximize the integral by maximizing the integrand at *each* $x$ independently. Define:

$$f(D) = p_{data}(x) \log D + p_g(x) \log(1 - D)$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $D$ and set to zero

$$\frac{df}{dD} = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{D} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D} = 0$$

**Logic:** Standard derivative of $\log D$ is $1/D$, and derivative of $\log(1-D)$ is $-1/(1-D)$.

### Step 6 — Solve for $D^*$

$$\frac{p_{data}(x)}{D} = \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D}$$

$$p_{data}(x)(1 - D) = p_g(x) \, D$$

$$p_{data}(x) = D \left[ p_{data}(x) + p_g(x) \right]$$

$$\boxed{D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}}$$

**Verification:** Second derivative $f''(D) = -\frac{p_{data}(x)}{D^2} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1-D)^2} < 0$, confirming a **maximum**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Global Optimum Value $V(D^*, G^*)$

**Given:** At the optimal discriminator $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$, substitute back into $V(D^*, G)$.

### Step 1 — Plug $D^*$ into the value function

$$C(G) = \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \right]$$

**Logic:** Substitute $D^*(x)$ into the integrand; the first expectation uses the data density, the second uses the generator density.

### Step 2 — Algebraic rearrangement

Factor out $\log 2$ from both terms. Note that $p_{data} + p_g$ in the denominator gives:

$$C(G) = -\log 4 + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}} \left[ \log \frac{2 \cdot p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \left[ \log \frac{2 \cdot p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \right]$$

**Logic:** This is the standard algebraic step that converts the expression into a form recognizable as a divergence.

### Step 3 — Identify the Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD)

The two expectations are exactly the definition of the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence** between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$:

$$C(G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \mathrm{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$$

Where

$$\mathrm{JSD}(P \parallel Q) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P}\left[ \log \frac{2 P(x)}{P(x) + Q(x)} \right] + \frac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim Q}\left[ \log \frac{2 Q(x)}{P(x) + Q(x)} \right]$$

**Logic:** The JSD is a symmetrized, bounded version of the KL divergence, taking values in $[0, \log 2]$.

### Step 4 — Find the global minimum

The **JSD is non-negative** and equals zero **if and only if** $p_{data} = p_g$. Therefore:

$$\min_G C(G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot 0 = -\log 4$$

$$\boxed{V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4 \quad \text{at} \quad p_g = p_{data}}$$

This is the **global optimum** of the GAN training objective. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** The generator's training objective under the optimal discriminator is equivalent (up to an additive constant and scaling) to **minimizing the JSD between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$**. This is the theoretical reason why GANs perform distribution matching.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Convergence Properties

| Property | Statement | Implication for Training |
|---|---|---|
| **Nash Equilibrium** | $(D^*, G^*)$ where $D^* = \tfrac{1}{2}$ and $p_g = p_{data}$ | Theoretical convergence target; not always reached in practice |
| **Bounded Objective** | $\mathrm{JSD} \in [0, \log 2]$ | $C(G) \in [-\log 4, 0]$; provides a clear convergence target |
| **Gradient Saturation** | $\nabla_G \log(1 - D(G(z)))$ vanishes when $D$ is far from $0.5$ | Use non-saturating loss $L_G = -\log D(G(z))$ |
| **Mode Collapse** | $G$ maps all $z$ to a few samples | Minimize JSD collapses to a few modes; needs architectural fixes |
| **Vanishing Gradients** | When $D$ is too strong early in training | Balances $D$ and $G$ updates; e.g., limit $D$ updates |

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse the **min-max order** — the **discriminator** is the *maximizer* and the **generator** is the *minimizer*. Reversing this loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. In derivations, students skip the **change of variables** step $z \to x$. Examiners *specifically test* this substitution; missing it costs full marks on the question.
> 3. The **non-saturating loss** and the **original minimax loss** are *not* the same — make sure you state which one is used and why.
> 4. Do **not** write $p_{data}(x)$ in place of $p_g(x)$ in the optimal discriminator formula — they are *different distributions*.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define GAN. Explain the roles of the Generator and Discriminator. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a deep generative model consisting of two neural networks — the **Generator $G$** and the **Discriminator $D$** — trained in opposition to each other.

- **Generator $G$:** Takes a random noise vector $z \sim p_z(z)$ as input and produces a synthetic sample $G(z)$ that aims to mimic the real data distribution $p_{data}(x)$. Its objective is to *fool* the discriminator.
- **Discriminator $D$:** Acts as a binary classifier. Given an input $x$, it outputs $D(x) = P(x \text{ is real})$. It is trained to correctly classify real samples as real ($D(x) \to 1$) and fake samples $G(z)$ as fake ($D(G(z)) \to 0$).

The two networks play a **min-max game** $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$, reaching a **Nash Equilibrium** when $G$ produces samples indistinguishable from real data and $D$ outputs $0.5$ everywhere. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: What is mode collapse in GANs? Mention any two techniques to overcome it. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Mode collapse** is a training failure in GANs where the **Generator produces a limited variety of samples** (often collapsing to a single mode of the data distribution), regardless of the input noise $z$. The generator finds a few "winning" samples that consistently fool the discriminator and exploits them.

**Two techniques to overcome mode collapse:**

1. **Mini-batch Discrimination:** Allow the discriminator to compare samples within a minibatch so that similar (collapsed) outputs can be detected and penalized.
2. **Unrolled GANs:** Unroll the discriminator's update steps when computing the generator's gradient, giving the generator a more accurate signal about how the discriminator would respond. Other methods include **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)**, which uses the Earth-Mover distance instead of JSD, and adding **noise** to discriminator inputs. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** — answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Each question has two sub-parts typically carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A: Derive the Optimal Discriminator and Discuss GAN Training Challenges `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q(a) Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ for a fixed generator $G$ in a GAN. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks — full step-by-step):**

We start with the GAN value function for a fixed $G$:

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 1 — Express as integrals (1 mark):**

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

**Step 2 — Change of variables: substitute $x = G(z)$ (1 mark).** Let $p_g(x)$ denote the distribution induced by $G$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Combine the integrals (1 mark):**

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

**Step 4 — Maximize the integrand point-wise. Differentiate w.r.t. $D$ and set to zero (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D} \left[ p_{data}(x) \log D + p_g(x) \log(1 - D) \right] = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{D} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $D^*$ (2 marks):**

$$p_{data}(x)(1 - D) = p_g(x) D \implies p_{data}(x) = D[p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)]$$

$$\boxed{D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}}$$

Second derivative is negative, confirming a maximum. At equilibrium $p_g = p_{data}$, $D^* = \tfrac{1}{2}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q(b) Explain the major training challenges in GANs and the techniques to overcome them. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Challenge | Cause | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Mode Collapse (2 marks)** | Generator maps all $z$ to a few samples that fool $D$ | Mini-batch discrimination; WGAN (EM distance); unrolled GANs |
| **2. Vanishing Gradients (2 marks)** | $D$ becomes too strong, $G$'s $\log(1-D(G(z)))$ saturates | Use non-saturating loss $-\log D(G(z))$; balance $D$/$G$ updates; leaky ReLU |
| **3. Training Instability / Oscillation (1.5 marks)** | Adversarial dynamics cause oscillating losses | Use Wasserstein loss with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP); spectral normalization |
| **4. Lack of Convergence Metric (1.5 marks)** | No clear stopping criterion | Use **FID** and **Inception Score** on a held-out test set for periodic evaluation |

A robust training recipe includes: (i) alternate $k$ discriminator and $1$ generator updates, (ii) use batch normalization, (iii) use Adam optimizer with low learning rate ($2 \times 10^{-4}$), and (iv) monitor loss and generated samples qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B: Architecture, Training Procedure & Variants of GANs `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q(a) Describe the architecture and step-by-step training procedure of a vanilla GAN. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Architecture (3.5 marks):**

- **Generator $G$:** A neural network (typically MLP for vanilla GAN, but DCNN for image tasks). Input: a noise vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d_z}$ (e.g., $d_z = 100$). Output: a synthetic sample $G(z)$ of the same shape as a real data sample (e.g., a $28 \times 28$ image flattened to a $784$-D vector for MNIST).
- **Discriminator $D$:** A binary classifier. Input: a sample $x$ (real or fake). Output: a scalar $D(x) \in [0, 1]$ representing $P(\text{real} \vert x)$. Final layer uses **sigmoid activation** for binary classification.
- **Loss Function:** Binary cross-entropy is used:

$$L_D = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ \log D(x_i) + \log(1 - D(G(z_i))) \right]$$

$$L_G = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log D(G(z_i)) \quad \text{(non-saturating form)}$$

**Training Procedure (3.5 marks):**

**For each minibatch:**

1. Sample $m$ noise vectors $\{z_1, \dots, z_m\}$ from $p_z(z)$.
2. Sample $m$ real samples $\{x_1, \dots, x_m\}$ from $p_{data}$.
3. **Update Discriminator:** Freeze $G$. Compute the gradient of $L_D$ w.r.t. $D$'s parameters and take an ascent step (e.g., Adam).
4. Sample another minibatch of $m$ noise vectors.
5. **Update Generator:** Freeze $D$. Compute the gradient of $L_G$ w.r.t. $G$'s parameters and take a descent step.
6. Repeat for a fixed number of epochs; periodically evaluate on validation samples.

The theoretical convergence target is $D(x) = 0.5$ (Nash Equilibrium). **(7 marks)**

---

**Q(b) Discuss three important variants of GANs and their applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN) — (2.5 marks)**
- **Architecture:** Replaces MLPs with **transposed convolutions** (in $G$) and **strided convolutions** (in $D$).
- **Key innovations:** Batch normalization in both networks, ReLU in $G$ (except output tanh), LeakyReLU in $D$, no fully connected hidden layers.
- **Application:** High-quality image generation; foundation for many subsequent GANs.

**2. Conditional GAN (cGAN) — (2.5 marks)**
- **Architecture:** Both $G$ and $D$ receive an additional input $y$ (e.g., a class label or conditioning vector) typically via concatenation with $z$ or $x$.
- **Objective:**

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}[\log D(x,y)] + \mathbb{E}_{z,y}[\log(1 - D(G(z,y), y))]$$

- **Application:** Class-conditional image synthesis, text-to-image generation, image inpainting with desired content.

**3. CycleGAN — (2 marks)**
- **Architecture:** Two generators $G_{AB}, G_{BA}$ and two discriminators $D_A, D_B$ that translate between two domains $A$ and $B$ (e.g., horses ↔ zebras, summer ↔ winter).
- **Key innovation:** **Cycle-consistency loss** $\vert G_{BA}(G_{AB}(x)) - x \vert$ ensures the round-trip mapping is identity, enabling **unpaired image-to-image translation**.
- **Application:** Style transfer without paired training data; domain adaptation; artistic style transfer.

> *Other notable variants:* **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** (EM distance for stable training), **StyleGAN** (style-based generator for face synthesis), **Pix2Pix** (paired image translation), **InfoGAN** (unsupervised disentangled representation learning). **(7 marks)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Print This Section**

- **GAN = Generator + Discriminator** trained adversarially. The discriminator wants to *maximize* the value function, the generator wants to *minimize* it.
- The **value function** is $V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- The **optimal discriminator** is $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$ — **must** be derived with the change-of-variables step.
- The **global optimum** is $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$, occurring when $p_g = p_{data}$ and $D^* = 0.5$ everywhere.
- The generator's training objective under optimal $D$ reduces to **minimizing the JSD** between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$.
- In practice, use the **non-saturating loss** $-\log D(G(z))$ to avoid early-training gradient saturation.
- **Mode collapse** is when the generator produces a few samples regardless of $z$ — addressed by WGAN, mini-batch discrimination, unrolled GANs.
- **Vanishing gradients** occur when $D$ is too strong — fixed by balancing updates and using non-saturating loss.
- **Evaluation metrics:** **FID** (lower is better) and **Inception Score** (higher is better).
- **Standard training recipe:** Adam optimizer, learning rate $\approx 2 \times 10^{-4}$, alternate 1 $D$-update and 1 $G$-update per minibatch, batch normalization.
- **Variants to remember for KTU:** DCGAN (convolutional), cGAN (class-conditional), CycleGAN (unpaired translation), WGAN (Wasserstein loss).
- **Final KTU Pitfall:** Always state the **min-max order** correctly — $D$ is the *maximizer*, $G$ is the *minimizer*. Reversing them is an instant mark-deduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoencoder and DBM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCAT604 - INTRODUCTION TO DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Autoencoder and DBM # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Autoencoder — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Autoencoder):** > An **Autoencoder (AE)** is an **unsupervised feedforward neural network** trained to reconstruct its own input at the output layer. It consists of two coupled sub-networks: an **Encoder** that maps a high-dimensional input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ into a lower-dimensional latent (code) representation $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ (where typically $p \ll d$), and a **Decoder** that maps $\mathbf{z}$ back to a reconstruction $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ in the original input space.
